name: al-'Ababsah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and pastoral territorial rights
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Abbas, part of the broader tribal confederations of eastern Arabia. The tribe traces its lineage through traditional Bedouin genealogical systems.
description: Al-'Ababsah is a tribal branch historically present in eastern Arabia, particularly in areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who moved between seasonal grazing grounds in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle and social structures, with some sections settling in oasis communities. Today, members of the tribe are integrated into modern Gulf societies while maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: Both tribes shared overlapping migration routes in eastern Arabia
Historical cooperation in eastern Arabian territories

history: The research reveals that al-'Ababsah exists as a clan within the Sudanese Manasir tribe, specifically listed among the Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil). According to Sudanese tribal genealogy, they trace their lineage to Mansur, who according to oral tradition belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. However, critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-'Ababsah, should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. The al-'Ababsah clan, along with other Riverain Manasir clans like al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, and al-Kabanah, inhabits the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan, known as Dar al-Manasir. As of 1993, the total Manasir population was approximately 30,000 residents with an additional 17,000 non-resident members. The clan pursues small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on the national market.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration from central Arabia to coastal regions of what is now Abu Dhabi emirate
Settlement in the Liwa Oasis system for agricultural activities while maintaining pastoral nomadism

timelineEvents.title: Eastern Settlement
timelineEvents.description: Establishment of al-'Ababsah settlements in the coastal regions of eastern Arabia

timelineEvents.title: Portuguese Resistance
timelineEvents.description: Participation in tribal resistance against Portuguese colonial presence in the Gulf

timelineEvents.title: UAE Formation
timelineEvents.description: Integration into the newly formed United Arab Emirates while maintaining tribal identity

name: Abd al-Qays
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribe with established genealogical records and historical presence in eastern Arabia
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Abd al-Qays ibn Afsa ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir. The tribe traces its lineage to the ancient Rabi'ah branch of Adnan.
description: Abd al-Qays was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arab tribe based in eastern Arabia, particularly in the region of Bahrain (historical Bahrain province) and parts of modern-day eastern Saudi Arabia. They were among the first tribes to embrace Islam during Prophet Muhammad's lifetime, sending a delegation to Medina. The tribe was known for their involvement in maritime trade across the Arabian Gulf and their agricultural settlements in the oasis regions. While historically significant, they largely merged into other tribal formations over the centuries.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical connections to Bahrain region
Both involved in early Arabian Gulf maritime activities

history: Abd al-Qays was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, originally based in the al-Arid region of South Arabia before migrating northwest to areas extending from Sudayr to al-Kharj. The tribe's name means 'servant of the god Qays' and they belonged to the Rabi'ah confederation within the northern Arab Ma'add group. By the 5th century, Abd al-Qays had shifted to nomadism in the southern Najd outside the Tuwaiq escarpment. In the 6th century, they migrated northeast to the oases of al-Ahsa, Tarout Island, and Qatif in eastern Arabia. In pre-Islamic times, the tribe frequently raided Iran, prompting Sasanian king Shapur II (309-379 CE) to lead an expedition against them, massacring most of the tribe and relocating survivors to the Iranian province of Kirman. The tribe was predominantly Christian before Islam's advent. During the Islamic period, Abd al-Qays participated extensively in the Arab conquests of Iran, with considerable numbers entering Fars and advancing toward Istakhr. They settled near Tavvaz in Iranian coastal mountains and Basra in lower Mesopotamia. In the early 8th century, 4,000 Abd al-Qays warriors joined Qutayba ibn Muslim's campaign into Khorasan. The tribe later established the Uyunid Emirate (1076-1238 CE) in al-Hasa after overthrowing Qarmatian rule, becoming a significant regional power that controlled most of eastern Arabia and Najd until being overthrown by the Usfurids. The majority of modern Baharna are descendants of Abd al-Qays, along with minorities from cousin tribes Bakr and Taghlib. Today, most tribal descendants reside throughout the Gulf Cooperation Council countries.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from South Arabian homeland to central Arabian regions
Shift to nomadism outside Tuwaiq escarpment
Major migration to eastern Arabian oases including al-Ahsa, Qatif, and Tarout Island
Participation in Islamic conquests, settlement in Iranian territories

timelineEvents.title: Shapur II's Campaign
timelineEvents.description: Sasanian king Shapur II massacred most of Abd al-Qays tribe and relocated survivors to Kirman province

timelineEvents.title: Settlement in Eastern Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Abd al-Qays established themselves in al-Ahsa, Qatif, and Tarout Island oases

timelineEvents.title: Islamic Conquests Participation
timelineEvents.description: Considerable numbers of Abd al-Qays entered Fars during Arab conquest of Iran

timelineEvents.title: Khorasan Campaign
timelineEvents.description: 4,000 Abd al-Qays warriors joined Qutayba ibn Muslim's army in Khorasan campaign

timelineEvents.title: Uyunid Emirate Founded
timelineEvents.description: Abdullah ibn Ali Al-Uyuni of Abd al-Qays established emirate after defeating Qarmatians

timelineEvents.title: Uyunid Emirate Falls
timelineEvents.description: Uyunid dynasty overthrown by Usfurids, ending Abd al-Qays political dominance

name: Abdah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent from Shammar confederation
ancestorStory: Abdah is traced as a descendant of the greater Shammar tribal confederation. The branch established itself as a distinct lineage within the broader Shammar genealogical structure.
description: Abdah Shammar is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups with Adnani lineage. Historically based in northern Arabia and Najd regions, they practiced nomadic pastoralism and participated in the complex tribal politics of central Arabia. As part of the broader Shammar network, they maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle and social structures. The tribe remains active with members present across the Gulf region, particularly in Saudi Arabia and other GCC states.

relations.context: Historical competition for influence in Najd region between Shammar confederation and Saudi state

history: The Abdah represent one of the three main branches of the Shammar tribal confederation, alongside Al-Aslam and Sinjarah. According to Wikipedia sources, Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula in the second century CE. The tribe settled in the mountains of Ajā and Salma (later known as Jabal Shammar) after displacing Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in 115 CE. The Abdah branch is organized into several major clans: Al Jaafar (from whom the rulers of Jabal Shammar come), Al Awad (influential in central Iraq), Al Rubaeya (including sub-clans Al Webbar, Al Ata, Zagareet, Al Jadi, Al Mohissen), and Al Yihya (including families Al Sleet, Al Mufadal and Al Jundah). The research reveals that in 1764, individuals from the Alzagareet clan of Abdah migrated to Southern Iraq under Yousef bin Mohammed bin Nasrallah's leadership, establishing a presence there. By 1932, population estimates showed that Abdah comprised 37% of the Shammar Badu population of 150,000-200,000. The existing data's description of Abdah as having 'Adnani lineage' conflicts with the Wikipedia research showing Shammar's Qahtanite descent from Tayy, indicating the need for genealogical correction. Modern members of Abdah are found throughout the Gulf region, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to contemporary lifestyles.

migrationPath.description: Tayy migration from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula under Usma bin Luai, settling in mountains of Ajā and Salma
Migration of Alzagareet clan of Abdah to Southern Iraq under Yousef bin Mohammed bin Nasrallah

timelineEvents.title: Tayy Settlement in Northern Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Led by Usma bin Luai, Tayy invaded mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim

timelineEvents.title: Abdah Joins Saladin
timelineEvents.description: Abdah of Shammar tribe joins Saladin against the crusaders

timelineEvents.title: Alzagareet Migration to Iraq
timelineEvents.description: Migration of individuals of Alzagareet of Abdah to Southern Iraq under leadership of Yousef bin Mohammed bin Nasrallah

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Abdah comprised 37% of Badu Shammar population estimated at 150,000-200,000

name: Abida
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani lineage with established tribal territory and recognized genealogy
ancestorStory: Descended from Abida ibn Muhalhal, a prominent figure in early Arabian tribal genealogy. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Qahtani confederation.
description: Abida is an ancient Arabian tribe of Qahtani origin that has been present in the Arabian Peninsula since pre-Islamic times. They are traditionally based in the central and southern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with some branches extending into modern-day Saudi Arabia. The tribe played a role in early Islamic conquests and maintained their tribal structure through various historical periods. Today, members of Abida can be found across the GCC states, though they maintain stronger presence in Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Both tribes trace descent to Qahtani origins

history: The Abida tribe is an ancient Qahtanite confederation with documented presence in the Arabian Peninsula since pre-Islamic times. According to genealogical records, they descended from Abida ibn Muhalhal and trace their lineage to the broader Qahtani tribal system. The tribe's historical significance is enhanced by their role as the parent tribe of the influential Bani Hajer (al-Hawajir), who emerged as a major sub-tribal confederation. The Bani Hajer, led by Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an in the early 13th century, migrated from the Abida homeland near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja') near Thahran Al Janoub to Lower Najd. Under subsequent leadership of Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, they migrated further eastward to Al Ahsa'a region. The Bani Hajer achieved particular prominence during the early 20th century Saudi unification period, playing a decisive role in the Battle of Kinzan in Al Ahsa'a against the Al Ajman tribe in 1333 AH (1914-1915 AD). Their intervention under Sheikh Shafi bin Salim Al Sahfi Al Hajri helped turn the tide in favor of King Abdulaziz Al Saud, who had been ambushed and wounded in the battle that also claimed his brother Sa'ad Bin Abdulrahman. In recognition of their crucial support, King Abdulaziz granted the Bani Hajer the honorary title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) and awarded them territorial rights from Ain Dar to the Qatar border, known as 'Jouf Bani Hajer,' along with certain judicial privileges. Today, descendants of the broader Abida confederation, particularly the Bani Hajer branch, are distributed across the GCC states, with concentrations in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, Qatar, UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Oman, where they are known by the surname Al-Hajri.

migrationPath.description: Bani Hajer migration under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an from ancestral Abida lands
Second migration under Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an to eastern regions
Expansion of Bani Hajer branches across the Persian Gulf region

timelineEvents.title: First Bani Hajer Migration
timelineEvents.description: Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an leads Bani Hajer migration from Sarat Abida to Lower Najd

timelineEvents.title: Eastern Migration
timelineEvents.description: Second major migration under Shafi bin Safar to Al Ahsa'a region

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Kinzan
timelineEvents.description: Bani Hajer forces under Sheikh Shafi bin Salim Al Sahfi support King Abdulaziz against Al Ajman tribe, earning title 'Khaznat Thafar'

timelineEvents.title: Royal Recognition
timelineEvents.description: King Abdulaziz grants Bani Hajer territorial rights from Ain Dar to Qatar border and judicial privileges

name: Abookara
description: Abookara is a tribal clan or family group in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation is available regarding their specific origins, territorial base, or role in Gulf state formation. Their current status and activities within the GCC region require further genealogical research to establish their lineage connections and historical significance.
history: The Abookara (Al Kuwari) emerge in historical records as one of the most significant tribal groups of northern Qatar during the 19th century. In the 1820s British survey by George Barnes Brucks, they were described as 'formerly one of the most powerful tribes in these parts, but now incorporated with the Uttoobees (Bani Utbah).' The tribe had a substantial presence across multiple settlements: approximately 450 members in Al Huwaila (the peninsula's principal town at the time), 150 in Fuwayrit, 100 in Al Ruwais, and 50 in Abu Dhalouf. They were noted as being 'subject to Bahrein' and primarily engaged in fishing, pearling, and coastal trade. By the early 20th century, J.G. Lorimer's Gazetteer confirms their continued presence in these northern settlements, with the Bu Kuwara maintaining approximately 100 houses in Fuwayrit and being recorded in Al Ruwais before 1856. The tribe appears to have maintained their traditional maritime occupations throughout this period, with significant involvement in the pearl fishing industry that was central to the Gulf economy. By 1950, anthropological fieldwork recorded approximately 600 tents belonging to the Bu Kuwara tribe in the Fuwayrit area, indicating their continued presence and adaptation to changing circumstances in northern Qatar.

timelineEvents.title: British Survey Records Abookara Presence
timelineEvents.description: George Barnes Brucks' survey records 450 Abookara tribe members in Al Huwaila, 150 in Fuwayrit, 100 in Al Ruwais, and 50 in Abu Dhalouf, describing them as formerly very powerful but now incorporated with the Uttoobees

timelineEvents.title: Mass Migration to Fuwayrit
timelineEvents.description: Mass exodus of Bu Kuwara tribe members from Doha to Fuwayrit due to tensions with Emir Jassim bin Mohammed, reportedly instigated by Bahraini sheikh

timelineEvents.title: Tribal Census
timelineEvents.description: Anthropological fieldwork records approximately 600 tents belonging to the Bu Kuwara tribe in the Fuwayrit area

name: Abu Karaki
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
ancestorStory: Descended from Karak, an ancestor whose lineage traces to the broader tribal confederations of the Arabian Peninsula. The name reflects their connection to this founding figure.
description: Abu Karaki is a tribal branch found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula, primarily engaged in traditional pastoral activities. They maintain their tribal structure and identity within the broader social fabric of the region. Like many smaller tribal groups, they have adapted to modern conditions while preserving their cultural heritage. Their current influence and exact historical significance in Gulf affairs remains limited in documented sources.
history: The research provides no direct information about the Abu Karaki tribe's origins, genealogy, or historical development. The only potential connection found in the research is a reference to the Abu Karaki family among branches of the Mahamid tribe in Jordan, described as 'the Mahamid who came from Daraa, immigrated from them to Umm al-Fahm, after which to the Karak governorate.' However, this connection is unclear and may not relate to the Arabian Peninsula tribal branch known as Abu Karaki. The research does not provide verified information about their founding ancestor Karak, their migration patterns within the Arabian Peninsula, their political alignments, or their role in Gulf affairs. Without additional documented sources specifically addressing the Abu Karaki tribal branch of the Arabian Peninsula, their exact origins, historical significance, and genealogical connections remain undetermined.

name: Adnan (Adnanite lineage)
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical authority as progenitor of northern Arabian tribes
ancestorStory: Descended from Ishmael, son of Abraham
description: Northern Arabs / Arabized Arabs / al-Arab al-Mustarabah. Origin: Hejaz, Central/North Arabia.

subTribes.name: Mudar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
subTribes.ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
subTribes.description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Rabi'ah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
subTribes.ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - bedouin pastoralism and sedentary agriculture, with some branches being mostly sedentary while others remained nomadic
subTribes.description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Qays Aylan
subTribes.description: Key Adnanite branch

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

subTribes.name: Bani Tamim
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claims direct Adnanite lineage through a well-documented genealogical tradition tracing back to Abraham through Ishmael. This appears to be a genuine blood lineage claim rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the traditional family tree, Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan - claimed to be a direct descendant of Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham).
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Early Islamic period through Abbasid era
subTribes.description: The Banū Tamīm are one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. They are an Adnanite tribe with a claimed direct lineage to Abraham through Ishmael. The tribe occupied numerous Wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century and played an important role in the beginning of Islam. They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. The word Tamim in Arabic means strong and solid, and can also mean those who strive for perfection.

subTribes.name: Bani Yas
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a genuine tribal confederation that formed through political alliance and shared settlement patterns, with the Al Bu Falah clan holding the most power. The confederation structure allowed for integration of associated tribes under Abu Dhabi's authority while maintaining some autonomous characteristics.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a.
subTribes.description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Otaibah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
subTribes.description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

subTribes.name: Harb
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The text only briefly mentions 'Harb tribe in the Arabian peninsula' without providing details about its formation, lineage claims, or historical context.
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.description: Harb is an Arabian Peninsula tribe mentioned in the research text, though specific details about the tribe itself are not provided in the source material.

subTribes.name: Mutayr
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
subTribes.description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

subTribes.name: Banu Khalid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with established chieftainship structure through the Al Humaid clan
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: desert nomadism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.

subTribes.name: Subay'
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
subTribes.ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
subTribes.description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

relations.context: Al Saud trace Adnanite descent
Qasimi lineage traces to Adnanite origins
Al Nahyan claim Adnanite descent

history: The Adnanites (عَدْنَانِيُّون) represent one of the two major tribal confederations of Arabia, tracing their lineage through Adnan to Ishmael, son of Abraham and Hagar. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they are classified as 'al-Arab al-Mustarabah' (الأرب المستعربة) - the 'Arabized Arabs' - originating from the Hejaz region of northwestern Arabia. The lineage connects Adnan through his son Ma'add to numerous prominent tribes including Mudar, Rabi'ah, and Qays Aylān, with the Islamic prophet Muhammad belonging to the Quraysh tribe of this confederation through the Banu Hashim clan. Pre-Islamic inscriptions, particularly Thamudic and Nabataean texts, reference Adnan as a venerated ancestral figure, with some Nabataeans taking names like 'Abd Adnon' (servant of Adnan). Following Adnan's death after Nebuchadnezzar II's return to Babylon, his descendants spread throughout central and northern Arabia. The rise of Islam in the 7th century marked a pivotal transformation, as Adnanite tribes became central to the early Muslim conquests and subsequent Arabization of vast territories. Major Adnanite tribal groups like the Anizah settled across the Levant and Mesopotamia during the Islamic conquests, while others migrated to Egypt, the Maghreb, and beyond. Modern historiography questions the traditional Adnanite-Qahtanite distinction, suggesting it may have developed during later Umayyad factional conflicts, though the genealogical framework remains culturally significant across the contemporary Arab world.

migrationPath.description: Adnanite tribes participated in the Muslim conquest of Syria, with many settling in isolated areas to avoid conflict with local populations
Arab tribal migration to Mesopotamia following Muslim conquest, with Adnanite tribes constituting major portion of settlers
Migration of Adnanite Bedouin tribes to Egypt under Umayyad Caliphate, reaching peak during Fatimid era
Major Adnanite tribal migration including Banu Hilal and Banu Sulaym sent by Fatimids, advancing as far as Morocco

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Siffin Participation
timelineEvents.description: Adnanite tribes including Anizah participated alongside Ali ibn Abi Talib, with 4,000 armored Anizah warriors joining Ali's forces

timelineEvents.title: Abbasid Caliphate Establishment
timelineEvents.description: Banu Hashim, the Adnanite clan of Prophet Muhammad, established the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad through Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib's descendants

timelineEvents.title: Anizah Settlement in Khaybar
timelineEvents.description: Anizah tribe settled in Khaybar at end of 10th century, fighting and expelling descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region

timelineEvents.title: Anizah Expansion into Syria
timelineEvents.description: First Anizah tribe (Hasana) entered Syria in mid-18th century, establishing dominance in plains around Hama and Homs, followed by other clans

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Al Shimasiyah

name: Adwan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on Hawazin lineage and historical presence in Hejaz
ancestorName: Adwan ibn Amr ibn Qays Aylan
ancestorStory: Descended from Adwan ibn Amr, part of the Hawazin tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through Qays Aylan to Adnan.
description: Adwan was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Hawazin confederation, traditionally based in the Hejaz region. They were known as one of the major Adnani tribes and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. The tribe was particularly active in the Najd and western Arabian Peninsula regions. While historically important, their influence diminished over time and they are now primarily of genealogical significance to modern Arabian tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace Adnani origins through different branches

history: The Adwan tribe traces its descent from the Al Dhafir confederation, specifically from two brothers Fayez and Fauzan of the Al Dhafir tribe who sought refuge with the Al Kinda tribe near Jebel Samik around 1640. After Fauzan's early death, Fayez integrated into the local tribal hierarchy through marriage to the daughter of the Sheikh of Kinda. Their son Adwan forged an alliance through marriage with a daughter of the Mihdawiya sheikhs, establishing the foundation for the tribal confederation that would bear his name. Hamdan, Adwan's son, became notable through a successful raid on a caravan traveling from northern Iraq to Mecca at Al Zarqa stream, acquiring a she-camel named 'Al Zabta' that became a war cry for the tribe. Over two decades of tensions with the Mihdawiya Emir Jaudat culminated in revolt when Hamdan's followers, known as Al Qradha ('The Borrowed'), supported his resistance against oppressive demands. Following Hamdan's death, his sons continued the struggle, ultimately killing Jaudat in an ambush at Al Fheis around 1750. Under Adwan bin Hamdan's leadership, the tribe consolidated control over the Balqa region, driving the remnant Mihdawiya forces north beyond Beisan into the Ajlun District. The tribe faced significant challenges from Zahir Al Omar who captured Al-Salt in 1760, forcing temporary retreat to Al-Karak. Leadership struggles between brothers Diyab and Salih marked the late 18th century, with Salih's execution leading to temporary exile in Jabal Al Druze before returning under the famous poet Nimr Al-Adwan. The 19th century saw the Adwan form strategic alliances, notably the 1860s marriage alliance between Ali ibn Diyab Al Adwan's sister Alia and Sattam Al-Fayez of the Bani Sakher, ending centuries of hostility between the tribes. This alliance posed sufficient threat to Ottoman authority that multiple military campaigns were launched against Adwan lands in 1867 and 1869, resulting in heavy fines and the installation of Ottoman governors. The establishment of Circassian settlements in Amman significantly impacted Adwan prestige and territorial control, marking the beginning of their decline as a regional power in the late Ottoman period.

name: Afar
legitimacyNotes: recognized sub-section of the larger Awamir tribal confederation
description: The Afar are a sub-tribe of the larger Awamir confederation, part of the Adnani Arab lineage. They have historically been nomadic pastoralists moving across the borders of what is now the UAE, Oman, and Saudi Arabia. Like other Awamir sections, they have maintained their tribal identity while some members have settled in modern Gulf states. They remain an active part of the broader Awamir tribal structure in the contemporary period.

relations.context: both tribes traditionally migrated across similar desert territories in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: The research provided reveals a critical error in the existing data. The Afar referenced in the Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy database appears to be confused with the Afar people (Qafár/Danakil) of the Horn of Africa, who are a Cushitic ethnic group inhabiting Ethiopia, Eritrea, and Djibouti - not an Arab tribe. The Afar people speak the Afar language, part of the East Cushitic branch of the Afroasiatic family, and have been mentioned in historical records since the 13th century by writers like Ibn Sa'id. They traditionally organized into independent sultanates including Aussa, Tadjourah, and Rahaito, and were known for their salt trade and pastoral lifestyle. The Afar have had complex relationships with neighboring Somali clans like the Issa, with whom they frequently clashed over territory and trade routes. In the 19th century, they resisted Egyptian expansion and later faced Italian and French colonization of their coastal territories. The confusion may stem from historical interactions between Afar and Arab traders, or from the presence of some Afar communities who worked as mercenaries, but the Afar are definitively not an Arab tribal group from the Arabian Peninsula. This entry requires complete revision to reflect accurate tribal genealogy.

name: Ahl al-Buwait
description: Ahl al-Buwait is a clan-level tribal group in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins, territorial base, or major historical roles. Their current status and activities within the GCC region remain unclear from available historical sources.
history: Ahl al-Buwait is a clan-level subdivision within the larger Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to tribal genealogists including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari, the ancestral homes of Al-Dhafeer trace back to al-Dhaghn, a place opposite Medina in the Hejaz. The Ahl al-Buwait designation originated as a laqab (nickname) for the al-Suwait branch due to their habit of defending refugees. Within the Al-Dhafeer tribal structure, Ahl al-Buwait represents one of two main divisions and encompasses four sub-branches: Al-Suwait, Al-Sa'id, Bani Hussein, and Al Katheer. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe historically inhabited the deserts of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), corresponding to areas of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known for their mobile lifestyle, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration rather than maintaining permanent settlements, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans.' In 1782, Dhafeer fighters participated in the Battle of Zubarah, helping the House of Khalifa defeat Persian forces in Bahrain. The tribe later faced conflict with Ikhwan forces at the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, successfully defending themselves in the southern Iraqi desert.

name: Ahl al-Sumada
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Ahl al-Sumada is a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific territorial base, major historical roles, or current status within the GCC region. Further research would be needed to confirm their lineage connections and historical significance.
history: Ahl al-Sumada is a major branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to genealogical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the Al-Dhafeer tribe originated from areas near Medina, specifically from a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina. The tribe later migrated to central Najd, then to north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), encompassing the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. As part of Al-Dhafeer, Ahl al-Sumada comprises several sub-branches: Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. The Al-Dhafeer tribe was known for their mobility and raiding lifestyle, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children. In 1782, fighters from the Dhafeer tribe participated in the Battle of Zubarah, helping the House of Khalifa defeat Persian forces in Bahrain. The tribe later defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe at the Battle of Qaysar in 1924. Around 1926, Ibn Saud allowed the Saudi branch of the tribe to settle permanently after securing the roads. Today, most Al-Dhafeer members, including Ahl al-Sumada, live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait in professional roles, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey.

name: Ajman
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and historical control of territory in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from Ajman ibn Yasir of the Yam tribe. The confederation formed through alliance of related clans in the eastern Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Ajman are a major Bedouin tribal confederation historically based in eastern Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were one of the most powerful nomadic tribes in the region during the 18th and 19th centuries, controlling vast territories and trade routes. The tribe played significant roles in regional conflicts and the formation of modern Gulf states. Today they remain influential with members holding citizenship across GCC countries, particularly in Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

Conflicts during Saudi state expansion
Cooperation in Gulf maritime activities

history: The Ajman are a Qahtanite Arab tribal confederation descended from Banu Yam, specifically tracing their lineage to Ajman ibn Yasir of the Yam tribe from southwestern Arabia. The confederation formed through alliances of related clans in eastern Arabia, with most members abandoning nomadic life to settle in northeastern Saudi Arabia. The Ajman became major players in eastern Arabian politics during the 18th and early 20th centuries. Their most famous leader was Rakan bin Hithlain in the 19th-20th century, noted for his poetry and recognized as the maternal grandfather of Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. In 1861, the Ajman were defeated by Faisal bin Turki of the Second Saudi State after challenging his rule in the 1850s, though Faisal later married into the tribe. The Ajman supported the Saud Al Kabir branch against Abdulaziz bin Saud during the founding of modern Saudi Arabia. A section led by Dhaydan bin Hithlain joined the Ikhwan movement in 1900, initially supporting Ibn Saud before rebelling. The Ajman, alongside allies from Utaybah and Mutayr tribes, were decisively defeated by Ibn Saud at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929, ending the Ikhwan rebellion. Today, nearly all Ajman have settled permanently, primarily in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia in their traditional territory of Wadi Al-Ajman spanning from Al Nairyah to Judah, with members also holding citizenship across other GCC states. The tribe maintains a distinctive southern Najdi Arabic dialect with south Arabian features, reflecting their Banu Yam heritage.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Ajman clans from ancestral Banu Yam territories to form confederation in eastern Arabia

The Ajman were noted for being important players in the politics of eastern Arabia in the 18th and early 20th centuries. Nearly all the Ajman have abandoned nomadic life and have settled in the Persian Gulf states, particularly in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia.

history: Al-Ajman is a Qahtanite Arab tribal confederation descended from Banu Yam, with their traditional territory spanning from northeastern Saudi Arabia across the Gulf states. The tribe emerged as important players in eastern Arabian politics during the 18th and early 20th centuries. Their most prominent leader was Rakan bin Hithlain (19th-20th century), renowned for his poetry and noted as the maternal grandfather of Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. In 1861, the Ajman challenged the rule of Faisal bin Turki, Imam of the Second Saudi State, but were defeated, leading to Faisal's subsequent marriage into the tribe. The Ajman later supported the Saud Al Kabir branch against Abdulaziz bin Saud, the founder of modern Saudi Arabia. A faction led by Dhaydan bin Hithlain joined the Ikhwan movement in 1900, initially supporting Ibn Saud before rebelling. The tribe, alongside allies from Utaybah and Mutayr, was decisively defeated by Ibn Saud in the 1929 Battle of Sabilla, which ended the Ikhwan rebellion. Nearly all Ajman have since abandoned nomadic life, settling primarily in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia. Their main tribal territory became known as Wadi Al-Ajman, extending from Al Nairyah governorate to the town of Judah. The Ajman dialect, shared with their cousin tribe Al-Murrah, belongs to the southern Najdi group characteristic of their parent tribe Banu Yam, distinguishing them from neighboring eastern Arabian Bedouins through features like the '-ish' feminine suffix, reflecting their South Arabian origins.

name: Al Bin Ali
legitimacyNotes: Established merchant family with historical ruling authority in Zubara and involvement in Gulf politics
ancestorStory: The Al Bin Ali trace their lineage to Ali bin Sulaiman, who established the family's presence in the Gulf region. They became prominent merchants and political figures in Qatar and Bahrain.
description: The Al Bin Ali are a prominent merchant family originally from Najd who settled in the Gulf region and became influential in Qatar and Bahrain. They established the town of Zubara in northern Qatar in the 18th century, which became a major pearling and trading center. The family played significant roles in Gulf politics and commerce, with branches in both Qatar and Bahrain. They remain active today with continued presence in business and government positions across the Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical conflicts over Bahrain and Zubara

history: The Al Bin Ali are a Sunni Arab sub-tribal confederation descended from the original Utub tribe, with the vast majority of members stemming from either the Bani Sulaim or Al-Maadeed tribes. Historical texts and maps indicate that over 1,500 years ago, the tribe originated from the center of Arabia – Najd, currently known as Riyadh. The Al Bin Ali settled at Zubarah in 1732, founding and ruling the town and its port, making it one of the most important port and pearl trading centers in the Persian Gulf in the 18th century. They expanded their settlements and constructed walls and a fort outside Zubarah called Qal'at Murair (Murair Castle), named after a water spring in the Bani Sulaim area next to Madina Al Munawara. The Al Bin Ali had practically independent status in Bahrain and Qatar as a self-governing tribe, using their distinctive Al-Sulami flag with four red and three white stripes with seven triangles facing west. On 17 May 1783, war broke out between the Al Bin Ali Bani Utbah tribe and the army of Nasr Al-Madhkur. About 2,000 Persian troops attacked Zubarah, but after suffering defeat, the Persians withdrew. The strategist Shaikh Nasr Al-Madhkur's sword fell into the hands of Salama Bin Saif Al Bin Ali and was later given as a gift to King Abdul Aziz Al Saud. Sheikh Isa bin Hamad bin Tarif Al Bin Ali was chief of Al Bidda (modern Doha) and the Al Bin Ali tribe from the early 19th century until his death in 1847. On 5 March 1837, the Al Bin Ali under Isa bin Tarif attacked Mombasa with help from Said bin Sultan of Oman, capturing Fort Jesus from the Portuguese on 12 March. According to J.G. Lorimer, the tribe owned upwards of 500 houses in Bahrain, concentrated in Muharraq and Al Hidd. A 1904 population estimate recorded 1,750 Al Bin Ali members in Qatar, most concentrated in Doha. The Al Bin Ali family became related by marriage to Bahrain's royal family and successfully fought off an attempted invasion by a maritime Al Binali force in 1895.

history: The research provided contains no verified historical information about the tribal grouping Al Abdul Dayem. The only reference appears in the Wikipedia article about the Mahamid tribe, which lists 'Al Abdul Dayem' as one of the branches that emerged from the Mahamid in Jordan. According to this source, the Mahamid are described as an Arab tribe tracing origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with original residences in Yemen and the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina. The Mahamid allegedly established an emirate called 'Emirate of Mahamid' in the Karak region of Jordan, though the article notes that some historians described their rule as harsh, leading to opposition from other clans. However, the specific historical details, genealogical connections, geographical distribution, and role of Al Abdul Dayem as a distinct branch remain undocumented in the available research. Without additional verified sources about their particular lineage, migrations, political activities, or modern status within the Arabian Peninsula tribal networks, their exact significance in GCC regional context cannot be established from the research provided.

name: Al'ahmad
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through patrilineal descent
ancestorStory: Named after their progenitor Ahmad, though specific genealogical details vary among different branches.
description: Al Ahmad is a widespread clan name found across various tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. Multiple unrelated tribal branches carry this patronymic, making it one of the more common clan designations. Different Al Ahmad groups have varying origins and historical backgrounds, with some integrated into larger tribal confederations throughout the GCC states. Their current status and specific roles vary significantly depending on their particular lineage and geographic location.
history: Al'ahmad represents one of the branches that emerged from the Mahamid tribe, according to genealogical records tracing their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before undertaking extensive migrations across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the tribe grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, they made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb tribal clans. The Mahamid migration took them northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant where they established themselves in the Karak region. There they formed the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily led by the Al-Amru clan, though their rule was eventually challenged by other clans who united against them due to reported harsh governance. Through subsequent dispersals, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the Levant, with Al'ahmad specifically documented as one of the Palestinian branches that emerged from this tribal matrix.

Movement toward the Levant and establishment in Karak

name: Al-'Aljanat
description: Al-'Aljanat is a tribal clan whose specific historical details and geographical origins within the Arabian Peninsula remain unclear in available sources. The name suggests a tribal grouping that may have been part of the broader tribal confederation system of the region. Limited documentation makes it difficult to establish their definitive role in Gulf state formation or their current status within the GCC tribal landscape.
history: Al-'Aljanat is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, specifically part of the Ahl al-Sumada division within the broader tribal structure. The Al-Dhafeer tribe descends from Tayy through Banu Lam, with ancestral origins tracing back to al-Dhaghn, a place opposite Medina in the Hejaz region. The tribe's early settlements were near Medina before spreading throughout Arabia through migration and raiding activities. Al-Dhafeer historically occupied the deserts of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), encompassing areas of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their distinctive raiding practices involving entire families. In 1782, Al-Dhafeer fighters participated alongside other tribal cavalry in supporting Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa and the House of Khalifa against Persian control of Bahrain, contributing to the victory in the Battle of Zubarah. The tribe later faced conflict with Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe in 1924 at the Battle of Qaysar in southern Iraq, where they successfully defended themselves. Around 1926, Ibn Saud's conquest of Arabia led to the settlement of the Saudi branch in established communities. Today, members of Al-Dhafeer, including the Al-'Aljanat branch, primarily reside in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey.

Expansion northward into desert regions spanning north Saudi Arabia, south Iraq, and north Kuwait

description: Al Araytham is a small tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional tribal structure with limited documented historical prominence. Current status and specific regional distribution within the GCC states requires further documentation. Their role in modern Gulf society appears to be primarily at the local community level.
history: Al Araytham is documented as one of the sections within the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged as a powerful alliance in 18th century Iraq. The Albu Salih originated from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation and transformed into a tribal confederation due to their opposition to Ottoman Empire influence in the region. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the confederation became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The confederation consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes of southern Iraq and semi-nomadic branches who migrated seasonally. The nomadic branches, including Al Araytham members, would migrate south into the desert annually during autumn, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, where they traded with local villages and towns before returning north for summer. Al Araytham fought alongside other Albu Salih sections in the Battle of Shaiba in 1915 against British forces, supporting the Ottoman Empire despite their historical rivalry with the Ottomans. During the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of nomadic members settled permanently in various locations. Today, members of the broader Albu Salih confederation, including Al Araytham, are located across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia.

description: Al Asead is a tribal grouping found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with branches present in different Gulf states. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and social structures in modern times, though specific historical records about their major political or military activities are limited. Various branches of Al Asead have integrated into the social fabric of contemporary Gulf societies.
history: Al'asead appears as a branch of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When their original territories became overcrowded, the tribe made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans. The Mahamid moved north to Dumat Al-Jandal, then continued toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. According to the research, Al'asead emerged as one of the Palestinian branches of this larger Mahamid tribal confederation, alongside other branches like alkhudur, alsawalima, and al'ahmad. The specific circumstances and timeline of Al'asead's establishment in Palestine, or their potential presence in other Gulf regions, are not detailed in the available genealogical records.

ancestorStory: Al-Aslam traces its lineage to Aslam, a prominent ancestor within the broader Shammar tribal structure. They established themselves as a distinct branch while maintaining ties to the greater Shammar confederation.
description: Al-Aslam is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the most prominent tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula. Historically based in the northern Arabian Peninsula, they participated in the traditional nomadic pastoralist economy and seasonal migrations. As part of Shammar, they were involved in the complex tribal politics of the region during the rise of the Saudi state. Today, members of Al-Aslam are found across the Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, where they maintain their tribal identity while adapting to modern sedentary life.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Historical alliances in northern Arabian Peninsula tribal conflicts

history: Al-Aslam traces its lineage to the ancient Arabian tribe Banu Aslam, which played a significant role during the early Islamic period in the 7th century CE. The Banu Aslam were active participants in the formative events of Islamic history, notably during the Prophet Muhammad's era. During the Invasion of Hamra al-Asad, Muhammad sent three spies from this tribe to track the departing Meccan Army, demonstrating their trusted position within the early Muslim community. The tribe formed part of a confederation that participated in the crucial Battle of the Trench (Battle of Khandaq), one of the defining conflicts of early Islam. Following Muhammad's death, the Banu Aslam proved their loyalty during the turbulent Ridda Wars by pledging allegiance to Abu Bakr, the first Caliph. Abu Bakr reportedly noted that upon seeing the Banu Aslam's support, he became certain of victory against the rebel tribes. The tribe's genealogical connection extends through the broader Azd tribal structure, as evidenced by the lineage chart showing Aslam as a descendant of Haritha bin Amr, who was part of the Azd confederation. According to tribal genealogies, Haritha bin Amr led his branch of the Azd into the Hijaz and eventually to the Tihamah region, where his son Afsa became the father of Aslam. This places the Al-Aslam within the ancient Qahtanite (southern Arabian) tribal system rather than the Adnanite northern Arabian lineage. The tribe's historical significance is further emphasized by prominent figures like Buraydah ibn al-Husayb, a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who hailed from the Sahm subdivision of the Aslam tribe. Buraydah participated in major early Islamic campaigns including the expedition to Hudhaybiyah, the Battle of Khaybar, and the conquest of Mecca, later settling in Basra and traveling throughout Khurasan.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Haritha bin Amr's branch of Azd from Yemen to Tihamah region following the collapse of Ma'rib Dam

name: Al Aunan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and alliance networks
description: Al Aunan is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional alliance networks with other Gulf tribes, notably Al Bu Falah. Their current status and specific regional influence within the GCC states remains documented through their ongoing tribal connections and social structures.

history: Al Aunan is documented as part of the Al-Muntafiq tribal confederation, a large Arab tribal confederation that historically controlled southern Iraq and northern Kuwait. According to the research, Al Aunan belongs to the Bani Huchaim division of the Muntafiq confederation. The Muntafiq confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests in the 7th century, with most of the tribe tracing its genealogy to Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. During Ottoman rule, the confederation held control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. In 1521, they successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif in eastern Saudi Arabia on behalf of the Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid. From the late 18th century onwards, most of the confederation converted to Shia Islam and transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. The research specifically mentions that nomadic branches of related Muntafiq tribes, including Al Aunan, would migrate south into the desert annually during autumn, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, before returning north for summer. During the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of these nomads settled permanently. Today, many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation, indicating Al Aunan's continued presence in the region.

traditionalEconomy: nomadic pastoralism and later urban settlement
description: Al-Awazem is a large tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan and Egypt, with the majority of its current settled population residing in Kuwait, northeastern Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain. They are among the largest tribes in Kuwait and were historically known for their military prowess, having fought the Ottomans, Achaemenids (Iranians), and Bani Rasheed. They once fought seven tribes to protect the Emirate of Diriyah.

history: Al-Awazim is a large tribal confederation tracing descent from Azem bin Hind bin Hilal bin Nufayl bin Rabiah bin Kilab bin Rabiah bin Amer bin Sa'sa bin Muawiyah bin Bakr bin Hawazin bin Mansour bin Ikrimah bin Khasfa bin Qais Aylan bin Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'd bin Adnan, connecting to Ishmael son of Abraham. The tribe predominantly originated from the Hejaz in western Arabia and migrated to the eastern Arabian Peninsula by 1700 CE, driven by war with the Hejaz vilayet and the large-scale famine of Najd in 1703 CE. Al-Awazim historically maintained strong military alliances with Bani Khalid and Al Kathiri in various conflicts. They were among the only tribes to fight the Ottomans, Achaemenids (Iranians), and Bani Rasheed, and once fought seven tribes to protect the Emirate of Diriyah. As the largest tribe in Kuwait, Al-Awazim were among the first tribal groups to inhabit Kuwait City, providing commanders like Mubarak Al Duraya, Falah bin Jamea, and Khalf ibn Athbiya. The earliest recorded presence of Al-Awazim includes Sheikh Musaeed Al-Azmi, who was born on Failaka Island and published a copy of Muwatta Imam Malik in 1682, considered the oldest document in Kuwait's modern history. During the Ikhwan revolt, Al-Awazim were attacked by Ikhwan tribesmen on October 5, 1929, resulting in approximately 250 deaths. According to University of Sheffield genetic studies of Kuwaiti Bedouin, Al-Awazim show 73% J-M267, 24% E-M123, and 3% R-M124 paternal lineage markers. Wadi al-Fara, which once belonged to the Awazim according to Charles Doughty's records, later came under Harb tribe control. Today, Al-Awazim populations are distributed across Kuwait, northeastern Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Egypt.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Hejaz to eastern Arabia due to war with Hejaz vilayet and Najd famine of 1703

name: Al-Azaznah tribe
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Azaznah is a tribal group historically present in the eastern Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists about this particular tribal branch, which appears to have been part of the broader tribal confederation system in the Gulf region. Their historical presence is noted in some genealogical records but detailed accounts of their activities and territorial control remain scarce. Current status and modern presence require further verification through tribal genealogical sources.
history: Al-Azaznah tribe was one of the tribal groups that held territorial control in the Al-Buraimi oasis region of eastern Arabia. According to historical records from the Al-Buraimi area, the tribe exercised authority over Al Hailah village within the broader Buraimi oasis system during the pre-Saudi era. The oasis region, historically known as 'Tawam' and 'Al Jaw,' consisted of nine villages and was originally inhabited by Al Nuaimi tribal groups. Al-Azaznah's rule over Al Hailah was part of a complex tribal governance system where different families and tribes controlled various settlements - Al-Zar'a family governed Al-Buraimi proper, Al-Surur family held authority over Hamasah, and Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara. The tribe's political significance became evident during the power struggles of the region when they played a decisive role in local succession disputes. In a critical political betrayal around 1290 AH (approximately 1873 CE), Al-Azaznah tribe turned against Mohammed bin Ali Al Nuaimi, who had strong ties with the Bani Jaber tribe and had assumed leadership of the broader region. This betrayal led directly to Mohammed bin Ali's assassination, after which Al-Azaznah appointed themselves as rulers of Al-Buraimi, demonstrating their political ambitions and capacity for decisive action in the complex tribal politics of the eastern Arabian Peninsula.

timelineEvents.title: Al-Azaznah Betrayal and Assassination of Mohammed bin Ali

name: Al-Bahri
legitimacyNotes: maritime expertise and coastal territorial control
description: Al-Bahri is a maritime tribal group historically associated with coastal areas of the Arabian Gulf. The name 'Bahri' derives from 'bahr' meaning sea, indicating their traditional connection to maritime activities. They were historically involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade across various Gulf emirates. The tribe maintains presence in multiple Gulf states today, though with diminished traditional maritime focus.

history: The research provided discusses the Bahri Mamluks (al-Mamalik al-Baḥariyya), a medieval Egyptian dynasty that ruled from 1250-1382 CE, rather than the Arabian tribal group Al-Bahri. The Bahri Mamluks were named 'Bahri' (meaning 'of the river') after their original barracks on Roda Island in the Nile River in Cairo, not due to any maritime activities. This dynasty consisted primarily of Kipchak Turkic mamluks (freed slave soldiers) who established the Mamluk Sultanate after overthrowing the Ayyubid dynasty in Egypt. The research contains no information about the Arabian Peninsula tribal group Al-Bahri mentioned in the existing data, their genealogy, maritime activities, pearling traditions, or presence in Gulf emirates. The two entities - the medieval Egyptian Bahri Mamluks and the Arabian Gulf Al-Bahri tribe - appear to be completely distinct groups that happen to share a similar name derived from the Arabic word 'bahr' (sea/river).

name: Al Bu Falah

description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

history: The Al Bu Falah represents a small but influential subsection of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, historically numbering only 15-20 households according to J.G. Lorimer. Also known as the Al Nahyan, this clan has traditionally supplied the paramount shaykh of the Bani Yas and served as rulers of Abu Dhabi since 1793. The dynasty was founded by Sheikh Dhiyab ibn Isa Al Nahyan, who led the Bani Yas from Liwa Oasis. In 1761, Dhiyab sent a hunting party from Liwa that tracked a gazelle to a brackish spring on an island, which became Abu Dhabi (literally 'Father of the Gazelle'). In 1793, he ordered his son Shakbut to move to the island and establish a settlement of about 20 houses and a fort. The village expanded rapidly to 400 houses within two years, becoming the Bani Yas capital. Dhiyab's rule ended tragically in 1793 when his cousin Hazza bin Zayed bin Muhammad bin Falah killed him during an argument. The Bani Yas elders supported Dhiyab's son Shakhbut as successor. The Al Bu Falah owned extensive date gardens across seven villages in the Liwa region and were the first among the Bani Yas to acquire property in Buraimi Oasis. They maintained a seasonal lifestyle, living in the desert with camels during winter and participating in pearling expeditions in summer. The family developed close relationships with other tribal groups, particularly the Al Bu Shamis section of the Na'im tribe. Today, the House of Al Nahyan, as a branch of the Al Bu Falah, rules Abu Dhabi and controls sovereign wealth funds worth approximately $1 trillion, making them the second richest family globally as of 2024.

They were considered a subsection of the Manasir under the Bani Yas structure. The tribe comprised approximately 60 houses in Abu Dhabi around the turn of the 20th century, where they appear to have settled. Members of the tribe have traditionally enjoyed close relationships with the Al Nahyan ruling family, with several holding important positions in Sheikh Zayed Bin Sultan Al Nahyan's administration and Royal court. history: The Al Bu Hamir (Arabic: آل بو حمير) is a small tribal section within the Bani Yas confederation, originating from the Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi emirate. Within the hierarchical structure of the Bani Yas, they were classified as a subsection of the Manasir. The tribe followed Maliki Sunni Islam and politically aligned with the Hinawi faction. Around the turn of the 20th century, the Al Bu Hamir comprised approximately 60 houses in Abu Dhabi, where they had settled from their original Al Ain homeland. The Bani Yas confederation itself originated from the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 before the Al Bu Falah branch migrated to Abu Dhabi island in 1793-1795. Members of the Al Bu Hamir maintained notably close relationships with the Al Nahyan ruling family throughout their history, with several tribe members holding important positions in Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's administration and royal court. However, the tribe appears to have experienced demographic challenges, as they were among the sections recorded by early 20th century British sources but were not subsequently mentioned in later comprehensive tribal surveys, suggesting possible integration into other sections or migration during the mid-20th century period when many Bani Yas families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment opportunities.

description: The Al Bu Muhair (singular Al Muhairi or Al Mheiri or Al Mehairi) are a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi but settled throughout the western coastal areas of the Emirates. At the turn of the 20th century, they were mostly settled with a minority of some 20 households in Abu Dhabi still following a Bedouin lifestyle. The tribe has been distributed across various Emirates including Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al Quwain, and Ras Al Khaimah.

history:
The Al Bu Muhair (singular Al Muhairi, Al Mheiri, or Al Mehairi) are a tribe of the United Arab Emirates closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. While their specific origins are not detailed in available records, they settled throughout the western coastal areas of the Emirates and became one of the most widely distributed tribes in the region. The Al Bu Muhair first settled in Ras Al Khaimah from Abu Dhabi with the establishment of Al Qawasim rule in the 1750s, marking their early documented migration pattern. By the early 19th century, they had become a significant presence across multiple Emirates, with substantial populations in Dubai under the rule of the Al Bu Falasah.

Political tensions arose in the 1840s when the Al Bu Muhair became dissatisfied with the rule of Maktoum bin Butti, who had led the Al Bu Falasah from Abu Dhabi to establish Dubai in 1833. In 1841, approximately 500 members of the Al Bu Muhair left Dubai and settled in Sharjah, followed by another contingent in 1852. This migration, combined with a plague outbreak in Dubai, led to the temporary abandonment of Dubai and its subsequent sacking by forces from Abu Dhabi under Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan. By the turn of the 20th century, the Al Bu Muhair were mostly settled people, with only a minority of about 20 households in Abu Dhabi maintaining a Bedouin lifestyle. Their population was distributed across the Emirates: 500 in Abu Dhabi, 500 in Al Bateen, 400 houses in Dubai, and smaller communities in Al Khan (60 houses), Sharjah (60 houses), Ajman (80 houses), Umm Al Quwain (30 houses), and 120-200 houses in Ras Al Khaimah. Historical records also mention the Al Bu Muhair's military activities outside the Gulf region, including a conflict with the Niquilus tribe in Oman over pearl grounds that resulted in the Niquilus being forced to migrate to Portuguese territory in the early 1570s.

name: Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty
legitimacyNotes: Ruling dynasty of Oman since 1744, established through imam election and military conquest
ancestorStory: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi was elected Imam of Oman in 1744 after expelling Persian invaders and unifying the country. He established the dynasty that continues to rule Oman today.
description: The Al Bu Sa'id is the ruling dynasty of Oman, founded by Imam Ahmad bin Said in 1744. Based in Muscat, they established a maritime empire that included territories in East Africa, particularly Zanzibar, and controlled important trade routes in the Indian Ocean. The dynasty continues to rule modern Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, making it one of the longest-serving ruling families in the Arabian Peninsula. They played a crucial role in Gulf maritime trade and regional politics for over two centuries.

history: The Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty represents one of the most enduring ruling families in Arabian Peninsula history, tracing their origins to the Azd tribe through their patrilineal ancestor al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba (Dabá). Like other Qahtani tribes, the Azd originally migrated from Yemen following the destruction of the Marib Dam, establishing themselves as a leading force in early Islamic conquests under the celebrated general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. The dynasty's rise to power began with Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi, born in Adam, Oman in 1694 to a small Hinawi tribe from the interior. Working his way up from reportedly being a wood vendor to becoming a leading merchant and governor of Sohar in 1737, Ahmad demonstrated exceptional military and political acumen during the Persian occupation under Nader Shah. After enduring a nine-month siege of Sohar and negotiating terms with Persian forces, Ahmad gradually consolidated power across Oman. Following the defeat and death of rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar in 1749, Ahmad was elected Imam by the Ibadi tribes at Rustaq on June 9, 1749, marking the foundation of the Al Bu Sa'id dynasty. His 39-year reign established Oman as a major maritime power, maintaining permanent army and navy forces and encouraging agricultural and maritime trade development. The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great' and the 'Lion of Oman.' After eliminating pretender Badr bin Saif in 1806 through a dramatic duel at Bait al Nu'man castle, Said expanded Omani influence from Iran to Zanzibar, establishing a vast maritime empire. He moved his capital to Zanzibar in 1832, initiated large-scale clove cultivation, and signed historic treaties with Britain (1798) and the United States (1833). Upon Said's death in 1856, the empire divided into two sultanates: Thuwaini bin Said ruling Muscat and Oman, while Majid bin Said governed Zanzibar. The Omani branch continued under British protection through various succession struggles, including the brief imamate of Azzan bin Qais (1868-1871) before power returned to the Al Bu Sa'id line under Turki bin Said. The dynasty modernized under Sultan Qaboos bin Said (1970-2020), who overthrew his father in a palace coup and ruled for 50 years, transforming Oman into a modern state while maintaining the family's 280-year rule. Today, Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said continues the dynasty's reign, having been designated successor by Qaboos himself, with his son Theyazin bin Haitham named as the first Crown Prince in 2021.

name: Al Bu Shamis
legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

relations.context: Both are subsections of the Na'im tribe
Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi took Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis tribe in a short conflict
Al Bu Shamis is one of three sections of the Na'im confederation, but has become virtually independent
Close association with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, despite both being Ghafiri faction
