history: The Al Bu Shamis is one of three sections of the Na'im tribal confederation, alongside Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir, following the Ghafiri tradition. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals, including around 400 armed men. Leadership was held by Muhammad bin Salmin bin Rahmah, who resided at either Sunaynah or Qabil, south of the Buraimi Oasis, exercising authority jointly with his cousins Mani and Hamad bin Ali bin Rahmah. The Al Bu Shamis emigrated in Eastern Arabia to settle around the Dhahirah and Sunaynah areas, with later migrations leading some to Al Ain and the Buraimi Oasis. They occupied strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera. Unlike other Na'im sections, the Al Bu Shamis developed a close alliance with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, possibly originating from assistance provided by Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut in helping them regain control of Qabil and Sunaynah prior to 1833. The Al Bu Shamis are believed to have settled along the coast after being temporarily displaced from Dhahirah in the early 19th century. By 1816-1817, they lost control of Ajman to Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and his followers from the Al Bu Kharaiban section. The tribe's most prominent leader, Sheikh Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi of Al Heera, was frequently in conflict with the Na'imi rulers of Ajman, seizing Ajman fort in June 1920 before being removed by British intervention. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1940s-1950s, the Al Bu Shamis, led by Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad, supported Saudi Arabia and actively fought British forces alongside the Bani Kaab. Following the conflict, some members chose deportation to Saudi Arabia while the majority settled in Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi. With the continuing decline of the Na'im tribal federation, the Al Bu Shamis maintained an almost completely separate identity and became closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, with Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan marrying Alia, daughter of Muhammad bin Salmin bin Rahmah.

migrationPath.description: Early settlement in Eastern Arabia around Dhahirah and Sunaynah areas
Later migrations to Al Ain and Buraimi Oasis
Settlement along coast after temporary displacement from Dhahirah in early 19th century
Settlement in Al Heera as headmen

name: Al-Dhafeer
legitimacyNotes: Multiple historical sources confirm their lineage from Banu Lam through Tayy, making this a well-documented blood lineage rather than a political formation.
ancestorStory: The tribe genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari, they came from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn.
description: Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. Known historically as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their habit of raiding alongside women and children and their enthusiasm for victories. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration without proper settlement until 1926 when Ibn Saud allowed their Saudi branch to settle in several settlements after securing roads. They originated from areas near Medina and migrated through central Najd to northern Najd, northern Bahrain, and the deserts of southern Iraq and northern Kuwait.

relations.context: Persian-Bahrain conflict of 1782
Battle of Qaysar 1924

history: Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe of Qahtani lineage that descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari, they originated from a place opposite Medina called al-Dhaghn. Known historically as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans,' they were characterized by their practice of raiding alongside women and children and their enthusiasm for victories. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration patterns, moving from their original areas near Medina through central Najd to northern Najd, northern Bahrain, and the deserts of southern Iraq and northern Kuwait, living as nomads without proper settlement. A significant administrative development occurred when the area that became Al-Baha Province was initially known as the Emirate of Al-Dhafeer during King Abdulaziz's reign, indicating the tribe's historical administrative presence in southwestern Saudi Arabia. In 1926, Ibn Saud allowed the Saudi branch of Al-Dhafeer to settle in several settlements after securing the roads, marking the end of their purely nomadic lifestyle. The administrative center was later moved from Al-Dhafeer to Baljurashi following requests from the local population, and eventually King Faisal established the modern Al-Baha Province, replacing the earlier Emirate of Al-Dhafeer.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from ancestral homeland near Medina into central Arabian territories
Expansion northward through raiding and nomadic movement patterns

timelineEvents.title: Settlement Authorization by Ibn Saud
timelineEvents.description: Ibn Saud allowed the Saudi branch of Al-Dhafeer to settle in several settlements after securing roads, ending their nomadic lifestyle

name: Al Fadl
legitimacyNotes: Established Bedouin tribal authority through traditional shaykh system and alliance networks
ancestorStory: Al Fadl traces their lineage to Fadl ibn Rabi'ah from the Tayy tribal confederation. They became prominent as a nomadic group controlling traditional migration routes in northern Arabia.
description: Al Fadl is a prominent Bedouin tribal branch originally from the Tayy confederation, historically based in northern Arabia and the Syrian desert regions. They were known for their nomadic lifestyle and control of caravan routes between Syria and the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained significant influence through their alliance networks, particularly with the Anizzah confederation. While historically powerful in their traditional territories, their influence has diminished in modern GCC states as sedentarization occurred.

relations.context: The 14th-century historian Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari notes that the Anazah were one of the tribes allied with the Al Fadl, along with Khath'am, Adwan, and Dhafeer.
Both trace ancestry to the broader Tayy tribal confederation

history: The Al Fadl trace their origins to Fadl ibn Rabi'ah ibn Hazim ibn Ali ibn Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah, descending from the Banu Tayy confederation through the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine. The tribe emerged as a major force in the 12th century, assisting the Burids and Zengids against the Crusaders in Syria. By 1107, Fadl was noted as an emir commanding mounted auxiliary troops for Damascus rulers. The Ayyubids appointed Al Fadl leaders to the prestigious office of Amir al-ʿarab, giving them command over northern Syria's Bedouin tribes. Under Mamluk rule from 1260, beginning with Emir Isa ibn Muhanna, the Al Fadl became hereditary holders of this office and received substantial iqtaʿat (fiefs) in Salamiyah and Palmyra. Their territory spanned from Homs westward to Qal'at Ja'bar eastward, and from the Euphrates valley north to central Arabia south. The Ottomans preserved their hereditary leadership after 1516, but by the mid-16th century, leading emirs joined the Mawali confederation and became known as Al Abu Risha, while their rivals were driven to the Beqaa Valley. During Ottoman times, the remaining Al Fadl split into Hourrouk and Fa'our branches, with the latter settling in the Golan Heights. The tribe became semi-sedentarized by the late 19th century but were displaced during the 1948 and 1967 Arab-Israeli wars, with most settling around Damascus. By the 1990s, an estimated 20,000-30,000 Al Fadl tribesmen lived in Syria and Lebanon, having transitioned from powerful medieval rulers to symbolic leadership.

migrationPath.description: Al Fadl driven out of Hauran by Al Mira, migrated north to steppe regions around Homs
Rival Al Fadl sheikhs driven out by Al Abu Risha, migrated to Beqaa Valley
Fa'our branch migrated to Golan Heights following feud with Bani Khalid
Al Fadl displaced from Golan after Israeli occupation, settled around Damascus

name: Al al-Ghawth
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Tayy confederation with traditional Bedouin authority structures
ancestorStory: Al-Ghawth descends from Ghawth, a notable figure within the Tayy tribal lineage. The family established itself as a recognized branch maintaining traditional Arabian tribal customs.
description: Al al-Ghawth is a tribal branch within the larger Tayy confederation, tracing their lineage to Qahtani origins. They are historically associated with the nomadic pastoralist traditions of the Arabian Peninsula. As part of the Tayy tribal network, they maintain connections across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe continues to exist as an active genealogical and social unit within modern Arabian society.

relations.context: Both branches of the greater Tayy confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches with common ancestry

history: Al al-Ghawth represents one of the two main branches of the ancient Tayy tribe, descended from al-Ghawth, son of Julhumah ibn Udad (known as 'Tayy'). The Tayy genealogy traces through Julhumah's ancestry to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan, establishing clear Qahtani lineage. Originally based in Yemen, the Al al-Ghawth migrated with the broader Tayy confederation to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling primarily in the mountain ranges of Aja and Salma (collectively known as Jabal Tayy, later Jabal Shammar). The immediate offspring of al-Ghawth's son 'Amr included Thu'al, Aswadan (known as Nabhan), Hani, Bawlan and Salaman, with the Banu Thu'al and Banu Nabhan becoming leading sub-branches in northern Arabia. The late 6th century brought the devastating 25-year Fasad War between Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila, resulting in significant tribal displacement. While Al Jadila migrated to Syria and converted to Christianity, Al al-Ghawth remained in Jabal Tayy, maintaining traditional beliefs until Islam's emergence. The tribe produced notable figures including the legendary poet and chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, whose reputation for generosity became emblematic throughout Arabia. During Islam's rise, prominent Al al-Ghawth leaders including Adi ibn Hatim (from Banu Thu'ayl) and Zayd al-Khayr (from Banu Nabhan) converted in 629-630, becoming companions of Prophet Muhammad. The tribe participated actively in the Ridda Wars, with Adi ibn Hatim playing a crucial diplomatic role in preventing tribal rebellion and securing Al al-Ghawth's allegiance to the nascent Islamic state. Throughout the Umayyad and Abbasid periods, Al al-Ghawth maintained their traditional territories while various sub-branches dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond, with descendants forming major tribal confederations including elements of the later Shammar and continuing to exist as active genealogical units in modern Arabian society.

migrationPath.description: Major migration from Yemen to northern Arabian mountain ranges following dispersion of Banu Azd

name: Halalmah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and customary law
description: Al Halalmah is a tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Like many smaller tribal branches, they have maintained their tribal identity while adapting to modern state structures. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to trace their specific origins or major historical roles. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal framework in the region.
history: The Halalmah (Al Halami) are documented as one of the branches within the Bani Yas tribal confederation, according to J.C. Lorimer's early 20th century Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. They were identified by Lorimer as a distinct group that was already in the process of integration into the larger Bani Yas confederation and are now generally regarded as a minor subsection of the Bani Yas. Like other Bani Yas branches, they would have been part of the broader tribal movements and settlements associated with the confederation, which had its origins in the Najd region of Central Arabia and descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. The Halalmah's integration into the Bani Yas structure reflects the fluid nature of tribal affiliations in the region, where smaller groups often became incorporated into larger confederations for political and economic advantages. Their current status as a recognized branch of the Bani Yas places them within the broader tribal framework that spans the UAE and parts of Qatar.

timelineEvents.description: The Bani Yas clans, including the Halalmah as a constituent branch, settled together in the Liwa oasis

timelineEvents.title: Lorimer's Documentation

name: Al Haramsha of Bani Idaid Shihuh
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage within Shihuh confederation
ancestorStory: Descended from Haramsh, a notable figure within the Bani Idaid branch of the Shihuh. The lineage traces back to the broader Shihuh tribal confederation of the northern UAE and Oman.
description: Al Haramsha are a sub-tribe of the Bani Idaid branch within the larger Shihuh tribal confederation. They are traditionally based in the mountainous regions of northern UAE and southeastern Oman, particularly in areas around Ras Al Khaimah and the Musandam Peninsula. As part of the Shihuh confederation, they have historically controlled strategic mountain passes and engaged in agriculture, pastoralism, and trade. The tribe remains active today as part of the broader Shihuh identity in both UAE and Oman.

relations.context: Historical cooperation in Ras Al Khaimah region
Both tribes present in northern UAE mountains

history: The research reveals significant historical connections between Al Haramsha of Bani Idaid Shihuh and the neighboring Habus tribe of Ras Al Khaimah. According to documented sources, intermarriage between the Shihuh and Habus was common, particularly between the Al Haramsha of the Bani Idaid Shihuh and the Habus people. This indicates close social and cultural ties between these mountain tribes of the northern UAE and southeastern Oman region. The Habus, who settled areas around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis east of Ras Al Khaimah city, were frequently neighbors with the Shihuh confederation and shared numerous cultural similarities and traditions. Both groups spoke the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, believed to have Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. The territorial boundaries were clearly defined, with the area north of Wadi Bih being traditionally Shihuh territory, while the area south of the wadi was considered Habus domain. This geographical proximity and the documented intermarriage patterns, especially with Al Haramsha, suggests the sub-tribe played an important role in maintaining diplomatic and kinship relations between the larger Shihuh confederation and their Habus neighbors in the strategic mountain regions of the northern emirates.

migrationPath.description: Early Qahtani migration to Hajar Mountains as part of Shihuh confederation settlement
Al Haramsha branch establishes settlements in strategic mountain passes of Musandam

name: Al Bu Hemair
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs and consensus-based decision making
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Hemair, the tribe established itself in the lower Gulf region. They are considered part of the broader Arab tribal migrations to the eastern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Bu Hemair is a tribal branch traditionally based in the lower Gulf region, particularly in areas of present-day UAE. They were historically involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade activities that characterized Gulf society before the oil era. The tribe maintained traditional settlement patterns along the coast and participated in the complex tribal alliances that shaped early Gulf politics. They continue to maintain their tribal identity within the modern UAE federal structure.

relations.context: Historical cooperation in Gulf coastal regions
Both tribes part of broader Gulf maritime tribal networks

history: Al Bu Hemair (الحميري) is documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. According to historical sources, the Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700. The Al Bu Hemair, as part of this confederation, would have participated in the broader Bani Yas migrations and settlements. The Bani Yas confederation was noted by colonial administrator J.C. Lorimer in his early 20th century gazetteer, though the specific mention of Al Bu Hemair (listed as Al Bu Hamir) appears in his 1907 documentation but was not subsequently mentioned in later sources by other observers like J.B. Kelly, suggesting possible changes in tribal organization or nomenclature over time. As part of the larger Bani Yas group, Al Bu Hemair would have been subject to the same demographic and economic pressures that affected the confederation, including population decline through emigration to other Gulf states during the 1950s in search of employment in oil and construction sectors, followed by return migration after Sheikh Zayed's accession accelerated development in Abu Dhabi.

migrationPath.description: Settlement with other Bani Yas clans in Liwa oasis as part of the broader tribal confederation migration from central Arabia

name: Al Humaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Humaid, the tribal branch established itself as part of the broader tribal confederation system in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Humaid is a tribal branch found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They are traditionally associated with nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal identity through the modern era. The tribe has integrated into the contemporary Gulf state systems while preserving their traditional social structures. They remain active in the region's tribal networks and continue to play a role in local governance and social organization.

relations.context: Historical cooperation in nomadic territories

history:
Al Humaid emerged as a dominant tribal branch within the Bani Khalid confederation, holding traditional chieftainship over the larger tribal group. The tribe's most significant historical period came under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid, who in 1669-1670 expelled Ottoman forces from eastern Arabian cities and established the Bani Khalid Emirate, also known as the Emirate of Al Humaid. Ibn Ghurayr made his capital in Al-Mubarraz in present-day eastern Saudi Arabia, where remnants of his castle still stand today. The Al Humaid dominated the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif during the 15th-18th centuries, controlling a domain that extended from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south.

The tribe's power began declining in the late 18th century when they opposed the Saudi-Wahhabi expansion. After the Khalidi chief ordered the emir of al-Uyayna to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the religious reformer moved to Dir'iyyah and allied with the Al Saud. The Al Humaid remained staunch enemies of the Saudis, attempting multiple invasions of Nejd and Dir'iyyah. However, their efforts failed, and the Saudis conquered their domain in al-Hasa, deposing the Al 'Ura'yir branch in 1789-1793. The Emirate of Al Humaid officially ceased to exist in 1796 after the defeat of Barak bin Abdul Mohsen. Though the Ottomans briefly reinstated Bani Khalid rulers after overthrowing the First Saudi State in 1818, internal quarrels and encroachment by tribes like the Ajman, Dawasir, and Subay' led to their final defeat in battles during 1823 and 1830. Today, Al Humaid members are found across the Arabian Peninsula, with significant populations in eastern and central Saudi Arabia, as well as in Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE, maintaining both Shia and Sunni Muslim communities.

migrationPath.description: Barrak ibn Ghurayr established the Emirate of Al Humaid capital in Al-Mubarraz after expelling Ottoman forces
Migration to Iraq, Lebanon and Palestine following military defeats against Ibn Saud and tribal alliances

name: Al-Huwaitat
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin confederation with established tribal leadership and territorial control
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Huwaitat ibn Ali, a prominent ancestor who established the tribal confederation in the northwestern Arabian Peninsula. They became known as skilled warriors and guardians of pilgrimage routes.
description: Al-Huwaitat is a major Bedouin tribal confederation historically based in northwestern Saudi Arabia, southern Jordan, and parts of the Sinai Peninsula. They were traditionally known as powerful camel herders and controllers of important trade and pilgrimage routes, particularly those leading to Mecca. The tribe played a significant role during the Arab Revolt in World War I, with some sections supporting the Hashemite cause. Today, various branches of Al-Huwaitat remain active across the region, with some settled in modern Saudi Arabia and maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: The Mahameed alliance with Al-Huwaitat
Modern political alignment within Saudi Arabia
Historical territorial and grazing rights disputes

history: The Al-Huwaitat trace their lineage to Alayan al-Jammazi al-Husayni al-Hashimi, a Hashemite descendant of Husayn ibn Ali who traveled from Medina to the Syrian desert accompanied by other ashraf. When Alayan fell sick during his journey, he was taken in by Atiyyah, chief of the Ma'azah tribe at al-Aqaba. Being the only literate person in the vicinity, he supervised accounts for the Hajj pilgrimage and was called 'al-Huwayt' ('the little wall') for preventing fraudulent activities. He married his benefactor's daughter, became sheikh after his father-in-law's death, and drove out the Ma'azah tribe. The Huwaytat developed into a large confederation with major branches including Ibn Jazi, Abu Tayi, Anjaddat, and Sulaymanniyin, inhabiting areas of present-day southern Jordan, Sinai Peninsula, parts of Egypt, the Negev, and northwestern Saudi Arabia. During the Ottoman period, they were recorded as the only tribesmen in the southern Karak Sanjak. In the early 20th century, rivalry between sheikhs Abtan ibn Jazi and Auda Abu Tayi rendered them more nomadic. During the Arab Revolt, the Abu-Tayi section supported the Hashemite cause under Auda Abu Tayi, helping Prince Feisal capture Aqaba, while the Ibn-Jazi section remained loyal to the Ottomans under Hamad ibn Jazi. In recent decades, the tribe has largely settled into villages but faced displacement for the NEOM project. In April 2020, Abdul Rahim al-Huwaiti was killed by Saudi security forces after refusing eviction orders, and in May 2023, three tribe members received death sentences for opposing the development.

migrationPath.description: Alayan al-Jammazi traveled from Medina with other ashraf, fell sick and was taken in by the Ma'azah tribe chief at al-Aqaba

timelineEvents.title: Arab Revolt Participation
timelineEvents.description: Abu-Tayi section under Auda Abu Tayi supported Hashemite forces in capturing Aqaba, while Ibn-Jazi section under Hamad ibn Jazi remained loyal to Ottoman Empire

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Huwaytat tribe in Transjordan estimated at around 1,000 tents, residing in Ma'an, Aqaba, Tafilah regions

timelineEvents.description: Abdul Rahim al-Huwaiti killed by Saudi security forces after refusing eviction orders for NEOM development project

name: Al Jabr (Qatar section)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Na'im tribal confederation with established presence in Qatar
ancestorStory: Al Jabr traces its lineage to Jabir, a prominent ancestor within the Na'im tribal structure. They migrated to Qatar as part of the broader Na'im settlement in the peninsula.
description: Al Jabr is a prominent section of the Na'im tribe that settled in Qatar during the 18th century. They established themselves as part of Qatar's tribal structure and participated in the traditional Gulf economy of pearling and maritime trade. The tribe has maintained its presence in Qatar and continues to be part of the country's established tribal framework. They are recognized as one of the constituent groups within Qatar's Na'im community.

relations.context: Both are sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation

history: Al Jabr represents a section of the broader Na'im tribal confederation that settled in Qatar. According to British sources from 1904, the Na'im were described as 'a Bedouin tribe who grazed their cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah in 1873,' with 60-70 members of the tribe's Qatar branch having hereditary attachment to the Bahraini emir. The Al Jabr section gained particular prominence during a significant tribal dispute in 1937 over Zubarah between Bahrain and Qatar. During this conflict, the Na'im in Qatar split into factions - the Ramzan section supported Qatari emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, while Al Jabr pledged allegiance to the ruler of Bahrain. In July 1937, tensions escalated when Sheikh Abdullah Al Thani moved against Na'im groups residing in Ath Thaqab over perceived violations of Qatar's laws. The Na'im leadership claimed Abdullah Al Thani had 'stolen their cattle' and burned their houses and villages. The conflict reached its climax when Abdullah Al Thani sent an armed force numbering in the hundreds to confront around 60 armed Na'im men encamped in Ath Thaqab Fort. Despite surrendering, four Na'im fighters were killed, and Abdullah's forces captured the fort and occupied the villages of Ath Thaqab, Freiha, Al-ʽArish and Al Khuwayr. Following their defeat, Abdullah Al Thani confiscated 40 rifles from Ath Thaqab residents and livestock, leading to approximately 1,000 Na'im tribal members, including Al Jabr, going into exile in Bahrain. Some members returned after the Second World War, re-establishing their presence in Qatar's tribal structure.

Return of some Na'im members to Qatar after Second World War

name: Al-Jadara

description: Al-Jadara is a tribal name mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about this specific tribal group is limited. Like many smaller tribal branches, they would have been part of the broader tribal confederation system that characterized Arabian Peninsula society. Their current status and specific regional affiliations within the GCC states require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The provided research focuses extensively on Saddam Hussein and Iraqi political history but contains no information about the Al-Jadara tribe mentioned in the query. The research details Saddam's tribal origins from the Al-Bu Nasir tribe, specifically the Al-Bejat clan and Al-Majid branch in the Tikrit region of Iraq. While the document mentions various Iraqi tribal affiliations and political developments from 1937-2006, there is no reference to Al-Jadara as a tribal entity, nor any genealogical connections, migration patterns, or historical events related to this specific tribal group. The research does not provide sufficient data to establish any verified historical narrative about Al-Jadara beyond what was already known from the existing description.

name: Al Jadila
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Tayy confederation with traditional grazing and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Jadila was a descendant of Tayy ibn Udad, establishing this branch within the greater Tayy tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the pre-Islamic Arabian tribal system.
description: Al Jadila is a tribal branch of the ancient Tayy confederation, one of the major Qahtani tribal groups of pre-Islamic Arabia. They traditionally inhabited parts of the northern Arabian Peninsula and maintained nomadic pastoral traditions. As part of the Tayy confederation, they participated in the broader tribal movements and alliances that shaped Arabian Peninsula history. The tribe maintains its identity within the modern Gulf region while adapting to contemporary social structures.

relations.context: Both branches of Tayy confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches

history: The research indicates that Al Jadila appears to be referenced as a tribal branch within the broader Arabian Peninsula tribal system, specifically mentioned in the context of the Ridda Wars following Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. During the campaigns led by Khalid ibn al-Walid against rebel tribes in central Arabia, the sources mention that 'Khalid next marched against another rebel tribe, Jadila' and that 'Adi ibn Hatim offered his services to persuade the tribe to submit without bloodshed. Bani Jadila submitted, and their 1000 warriors joined Khalid's army.' This indicates that Al Jadila was initially among the tribes that rebelled against the nascent Islamic state during the Ridda Wars but ultimately submitted to Khalid's forces and contributed warriors to the Muslim army. The fact that they fielded 1000 warriors suggests they were a tribe of considerable size and military capability. Their submission without bloodshed through negotiation rather than conquest indicates they may have had political pragmatism or existing connections to the Muslim leadership. However, beyond this specific historical episode, the research provides limited additional details about their origins, subsequent history, or geographic territories within the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Al Janah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and customary leadership
description: Al Janah is a tribal branch found in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and social structure within the contemporary Gulf context. Their current status and specific regional influence remain documented primarily through local tribal records.
history: Al Janah is confirmed as one of the main branches of the powerful Bani Khalid tribal confederation that dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation consisted of multiple branches including Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood, with the Al Humaid traditionally holding chieftainship. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions and established the Bani Khalid Emirate with its capital in Al-Mubarraz. The confederation controlled vast territories from the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases, wielding significant political influence across Eastern Arabia. The Bani Khalid, including the Al Janah branch, maintained strong opposition to the emerging Saudi-Wahhabi alliance from the mid-18th century. When Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab sought support, the Khalidi chief ordered his expulsion, leading to lasting enmity with the Al Saud. Military confrontations intensified in 1763 with Saudi attacks led by Abdulaziz bin Muhammad, culminating in the Saudis deposing the Al 'Ura'yir rulers of Al-Hasa in 1789-1793. After brief Ottoman restoration in 1818, the Bani Khalid faced final defeats in battles against Mutayr-Ajman alliance in 1823 and Subay'-Saudi coalition in 1830, ending their political dominance. Many tribal members, including Al Janah families, subsequently dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant, with some settling in Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, Syria, Palestine, and Lebanon.

migrationPath.description: Dispersal following final military defeats and end of Bani Khalid political rule in Eastern Arabia

name: Al-Jawasim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional maritime authority and historical rulership of Gulf emirates
ancestorStory: Descended from Qasim ibn Raziq of the Huwalah Arabs. The tribe established itself as a major maritime power in the Gulf during the 18th century.
description: The Al-Jawasim (also known as Al-Qawasim) are a prominent Arab tribe that became one of the most powerful maritime confederations in the Gulf. Historically based in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, they dominated Gulf waters and trade routes during the 18th and 19th centuries. They established the ruling dynasties of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah in the UAE, and maintain significant influence in Gulf maritime and commercial affairs. The tribe played a crucial role in the political formation of the modern UAE.

history: According to the research, Al-Jawasim appears as a branch of the larger Al-Dhafeer tribal confederation. Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy, with genealogical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari agreeing that the tribe came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. The ancestral homes can be traced to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their habit of raiding alongside women and children. Al-Jawasim is listed among the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. The broader Al-Dhafeer confederation participated in significant conflicts, including supporting the House of Khalifa against Persian control of Bahrain in 1782 and defeating Ikhwan forces at the Battle of Qaysar in 1924.

name: Al Katheer
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Hadramawt and eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from Kathir ibn Malikan of the Kinda tribal confederation. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient South Arabian kingdoms.
description: Al Katheer is a prominent Qahtani tribe historically based in Hadramawt, Yemen, with branches extending into the eastern Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. They were known for their role in trans-Arabian trade routes and had significant influence in Hadramawt's political landscape during the medieval and early modern periods. The tribe established various settlements and maintained trading connections across the Arabian Peninsula. Today, Al Katheer descendants are found across the GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and the UAE, where they maintain their tribal identity while participating in modern Gulf society.

history: The research reveals a critical genealogical correction for Al Katheer: according to Wikipedia's Al-Dhafeer article, Al Katheer (Arabic: آل كثير) is actually a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribal confederation, not the previously recorded Qahtani lineage. Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy, with ancestral homes traced back to areas near Medina in the Hejaz. The tribe historically lived in al-Dhaghn, opposite Medina, before spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration as they lacked permanent settlements. Al Katheer specifically belongs to the Ahl al-Buwait division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside Al-Suwait, Al-Sa'id, and Bani Hussein branches. The Al-Dhafeer confederation was nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their distinctive raiding practices that included women and children, and their enthusiasm for victories. The tribe played significant military roles in regional conflicts, including supporting the House of Khalifa against Persian control in the 1782 Battle of Zubarah in Bahrain, where Dhafeer cavalry helped end Persian rule. In 1924, during the Battle of Qaysar in southern Iraq, the Dhafeer tribe successfully defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe, killing 72 fighters including notable commander Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish. Around 1926, Emir Ibn Saud allowed the Saudi branch of the tribe to settle in several permanent settlements after securing regional roads. Today, most Al-Dhafeer descendants, including the Al Katheer branch, live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, holding positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain and Turkey.

name: Al Kathiri
legitimacyNotes: Ruling dynasty of Hadhramaut with claimed Qahtani lineage and historical sultanate authority
ancestorStory: The Al Kathiri trace their lineage to Kathir ibn Ahmad, establishing themselves as rulers in Hadhramaut. They became one of the dominant ruling houses of the region through military conquest and tribal alliances.
description: The Al Kathiri were a major ruling dynasty of Hadhramaut in present-day Yemen, controlling much of the interior regions from the 15th century onwards. They established the Kathiri Sultanate with its capital at Sayun, competing with the Qu'aiti Sultanate for control over Hadhramaut's trade routes and frankincense commerce. The dynasty maintained power through tribal alliances and control of caravan routes connecting the Arabian interior with Indian Ocean ports. Their rule ended with the establishment of South Yemen in 1967, though tribal identity persists among their descendants.

relations.context: Al Awazem historically had very strong alliances with Al Kathiri in most of the wars

history: The Al Kathiri are a major Yemeni tribe tracing their origins to the southern Arabian Peninsula, with genetic studies suggesting they belong to the larger Banu Quda'a confederation. Modern genetic analysis of haplogroup J1 L222.2 indicates Al Kathiri tribes may be more closely aligned with Adnanite rather than traditional Qahtanite origins, challenging earlier Islamic-period genealogical claims. The Al Kathiri established themselves as a dominant ruling dynasty in Hadhramaut, founding the Kathiri Sultanate in 1379 (according to some sources) or 1395 (per other accounts) under Badr as-Sahab ibn al-Habrali Bu Tuwairik. The dynasty's power extended from Dhofar in the east to Shabwa in the west, north into the Empty Quarter, and south to the Arabian Sea coast, with their capital established at Seiyun. The Al Kathiri conquered the important port of Ash-Shihr in the 1460s and later seized Dhofar in 1414 under Sultan Ali bin Omar bin Jaafar bin Badr al-Kathiri. The sultanate faced significant challenges beginning in the 19th century with the emergence of the British-supported Qu'aiti Sultanate, leading to a century-long conflict that divided Hadhramaut between the two powers. A peace treaty was finally signed in 1937 at the Sultan Al Kathiri's palace in Seiyun under British supervision. The Kathiri State was forced to join the British Protectorate of South Arabia in the mid-1950s, maintaining autonomy until the 1967 revolution that established South Yemen and ended the sultanate. Sultan Al-Husayn ibn Ali, who had ruled since 1949, was overthrown in October 1967. The Al Kathiri diaspora spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula, Southeast Asia, and East Africa, with notable descendants including Mari Alkatiri, who became East Timor's first Prime Minister, demonstrating the tribe's significant 19th and 20th century migration to Indonesia and other regions.

Sultan Ali bin Omar conquest of Dhofar region
Al Kathiri conquest of the important coastal port of Ash-Shihr
Major Hadhrami migration wave to Indonesia and Southeast Asia

name: Al Khlawi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and local recognition
description: Al Khlawi is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula region. Due to limited historical documentation, specific details about their origins, traditional territories, and historical significance remain unclear. They maintain tribal identity within the broader Arabian tribal system. Their current status and geographical distribution require further research for comprehensive documentation.
history: Al Khlawi is documented as one of the main sections of the Albu Salih tribal confederation, with origins tracing to Bani A'ridh lineage. The Albu Salih confederation emerged in the 18th century as a warfare alliance opposing Ottoman Empire influence in the region, transforming from the original Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the confederation became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. Al Khlawi, as part of this confederation, participated in the complex tribal dynamics of southern Iraq and northern Arabia. The Albu Salih territory was centered around the great marshes in what is now the district of Al Islah in Dhi Qar province, established in 1918 by Shaikh Sulaiman bin Mansur Al Nasrallah. During the early 20th century, Al Khlawi members, alongside other Albu Salih sections, were involved in resistance against British colonial forces, including participation in the 1915 Battle of Shaiba and the 1920 Revolution. The confederation included both settled agricultural communities and semi-nomadic branches who migrated seasonally between the marshlands of southern Iraq and the northeastern Arabian desert, trading with Kuwaiti settlements. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, maintaining their tribal identity within the modern state system.

name: Al Kurdi
legitimacyNotes: descent-based tribal identity with historical presence in Gulf region
description: Al Kurdi is a tribal family name found across various Gulf states, particularly in Kuwait, UAE, and eastern Saudi Arabia. The name suggests possible Kurdish origins or connection to the Kurdish regions, though many families bearing this name have been established in the Arabian Peninsula for generations. They are typically integrated into the broader tribal structure of their respective regions and maintain traditional Gulf Arab customs and practices.
history: The research provides limited direct information about Al Kurdi as a Gulf tribal entity, instead focusing primarily on Kurdish historical figures and the broader Kurdish experience. The name 'Al Kurdi' (meaning 'the Kurd' or 'of Kurdish origin') suggests connections to Kurdish populations, but the specific genealogical and historical trajectory of this family in the Arabian Peninsula cannot be definitively established from the available sources. The research does reveal important context about Kurdish interactions with the Arab world, particularly through early Islamic converts like Jaban al-Kurdi, who is believed to be among the first Kurds to embrace Islam during the Prophet Muhammad's time, and his son Meymun al-Kurdi. The mass conversion of Kurdish populations occurred primarily during the reign of Umar ibn Al-Khattab (634-644 CE) following the Arab conquest of Mesopotamia in 637 CE. Kurdish tribes initially supported the Sasanian Empire against Muslim armies but eventually submitted as the empire fell. The research indicates that various individuals bearing the 'al-Kurdi' designation have appeared throughout Islamic history, from medieval scholars like Muhammad Amin al-Kurdi al-Irbili (d. 1914), a Shafi'i jurist and Sufi who lived in Ottoman Iraq and later Egypt, to modern figures. However, the specific connection between these historical Kurdish figures and the contemporary Gulf Arab families known as Al Kurdi remains unclear from the available documentation.

name: Al-Ma'adin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical lineage and customary leadership
description: Al-Ma'adin is a tribal group that appears in historical records of the Arabian Peninsula. They are believed to have been involved in traditional Bedouin activities including pastoralism and regional trade networks. Their current status and presence in modern GCC states requires further historical verification.
history: Al-Ma'adin (المعادين) is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from the Tayy confederation. According to multiple historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the Al-Dhafeer tribe genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy, originating from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn. As one of the branches under the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, Al-Ma'adin participated in the broader tribal migrations and activities of their parent tribe. The Al-Dhafeer confederation spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and immigration, as they did not have a proper settlement, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their habit of raiding alongside women and children. Al-Ma'adin would have been part of the tribal territories in the desert of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), which encompassed the desert of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. When Ibn Saud conquered large portions of Arabia around 1926, the Al-Dhafeer branches, including Al-Ma'adin, were allowed to settle in several settlements after securing roads. Today, Al-Ma'adin members, like other Al-Dhafeer branches, are found primarily in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, with smaller numbers in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers.

description: Al-Ma'alem is a tribal group found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Gulf region. history: Al-Ma'alem is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy Arabs. According to the research, Al-Ma'alem (Arabic: المعاليم) is listed among the branches of Ahl al-Sumada, one of the two main divisions of Al-Dhafeer. The Al-Dhafeer tribe's ancestral homes can be traced back to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration, as they did not have a proper permanent settlement. Historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd' confirm that the Dhafeer genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. The tribe was nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their habit of raiding alongside women and children. They lived in the Desert of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), which encompasses the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia and north Kuwait. Around 1926, Emir Ibn Saud allowed the tribe's Saudi branch to settle in several settlements after securing roads. As a branch of Al-Dhafeer, Al-Ma'alem would have been part of these historical movements and settlements, maintaining their traditional social structure while becoming active in contemporary Gulf society.

name: Mishagin
description: Al Mashghouni (المشاغين) is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or major historical role in Gulf affairs. They appear to be a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories.
history: The Mishagin (Al Mashghouni) are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation. According to the Bani Yas genealogical structure, the Al Mashghouni/Mishagin formed part of this larger confederation that originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. As members of the Bani Yas confederation, they would have participated in the major tribal migrations, including the settlement in the Liwa oasis around 1700, and the subsequent establishment of Abu Dhabi in 1795 when the Al Bu Falah discovered fresh water on Abu Dhabi island. The Mishagin are listed among the more than 20 sections that contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi identify as forming part of the confederation. Like other Bani Yas sections, they experienced the demographic changes of the 20th century, when the confederation's population declined from approximately 12,000 in the early 1900s to about 8,000 by the 1950s due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment opportunities in oil and construction sectors.

name: Maharibah
legitimacyNotes: Established tribal lineage with traditional leadership structure
description: Al Maharibah is a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe has maintained its traditional structure and continues to exist in modern times. Like many Arabian tribes, they have adapted to contemporary Gulf society while preserving their tribal identity and customs.
history: The Maharibah are a tribal section that became incorporated into the Bani Yas confederation, though historical sources indicate they originated from the Na'im tribe rather than being originally part of the Bani Yas. By the early 19th century, they were established as one of the constituent sections of the Bani Yas under Abu Dhabi's authority. During the 1820s, the tribe was led by Suwaidan bin Za'al, who was described by British sources as 'the wild head of the Maharibah tribe.' His leadership proved problematic for regional stability, as he and his followers continued to engage in piracy despite the 1820 General Maritime Treaty between the Trucial States and Britain. The British Resident at Bushire specifically pressured Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan, the ruler of Abu Dhabi from 1818-1833, to control Suwaidan bin Za'al's activities. As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Maharibah shared in the territorial domain that largely corresponded to what became the Emirate of Abu Dhabi, though their specific settlements and population numbers within the confederation are not detailed in available records.

name: Al Mira
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Al Mira traces their lineage to an ancestor named Mira who settled in the eastern regions of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe developed as a distinct branch with pastoral and trading activities.
description: Al Mira is a tribal branch historically based in the eastern Arabian Peninsula with traditional pastoral and trading activities. The tribe has maintained its identity through centuries of settlement and migration patterns common to the region. They have been part of the broader tribal confederation networks that characterized Gulf society. The tribe remains active today with members dispersed across various Gulf states.

relations.context: both tribes practiced nomadic pastoralism in eastern Arabia
historical cooperation in tribal affairs

history: Al Mira is one of two main branches of the Banu Rabi'ah, a sub-branch of the ancient Arab tribe Tayy (also known as Tayyid). The Al Mira descended from Mira (also spelled Al Murrah), son of Rabi'ah ibn Hazim ibn Ali ibn Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah. The Banu Rabi'ah were descendants of the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine and became prominent in Syria through their participation in the Muslim war effort against the Crusaders after 1099. By 1107, both Al Mira and their rival branch Al Fadl were noted as emirs providing mounted auxiliary troops for the Burid ruler of Damascus. At some point during Ayyubid rule in the late 12th or early 13th century, Al Mira drove their kinsmen Al Fadl out of Hauran in southern Syria, forcing the latter to migrate north. During the Mamluk period, Al Mira controlled southern Syria while Al Fadl dominated northern Syria. Al Mira's territory extended from the Golan Heights (Jawlan) southward to the al-Harrah hot springs region north of Mecca in the Hejaz. Their emir held the title of malik al-ʿarab (king of the Bedouin), a position lower in rank than the amir al-ʿarab title held by Al Fadl. Al Mira's strongest opposition came from their Al Fadl kinsmen under Ahmad ibn Hajji, though this enmity gradually dissipated as Ahmad was given virtual independence in the southern desert while remaining under overall Mamluk suzerainty.

migrationPath.description: Al Mira drove Al Fadl out of Hauran and established control over southern Syrian territories

name: Al-Muntafiq
legitimacyNotes: Saadoun family leadership over confederation of tribes in southern Iraq
ancestorStory: Named after Muntafiq ibn Uqayl of the Banu Uqayl tribe. The confederation was later dominated by the Saadoun family who claimed Sharifian descent.
description: Al-Muntafiq was a major tribal confederation based in southern Iraq, primarily in the areas around Basra and along the lower Euphrates. Led by the Saadoun family from the 17th century onwards, they controlled vast territories and numerous smaller tribes, becoming one of the most powerful tribal entities in Ottoman Iraq. The confederation had significant influence over trade routes and tribal movements between Iraq and the Arabian Peninsula. Their power declined in the late 19th and early 20th centuries under Ottoman centralization efforts.

relations.context: Albu Salih were originally part of the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation

history: Al-Muntafiq was a major Arab tribal confederation that emerged in the 15th century, named after Muntafiq ibn Uqayl of the Banu Uqayl tribe from Najd. The confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and became one of the most powerful tribal entities in Ottoman Iraq, controlling vast territories in southern Iraq around Basra and along the lower Euphrates. From the 17th century onwards, the confederation was dominated by the Saadoun family (Al-Saadun), who claimed Sharifian descent from Mecca and served as the traditional leaders, though they remained Sunni while most confederation members converted to Shia Islam from the late 18th century. The confederation was divided into three main branches: Bani Malik, al-Ajwad (partially descended from the ancient Arab tribe of Tayy), and Bani Sa'id, with most tracing genealogy to Banu Uqayl of the Banu Amir confederation. In 1521, they successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif in eastern Saudi Arabia on behalf of the Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid. During the Ottoman era, most members settled into sedentary agricultural life in southern and western Iraq. The city of Nasiriya was named after one of their sheikhs, and the surrounding province was known as 'Al-Muntafiq Province' until 1976. Led by Yusuf Beg of the Saadun clan, they were traditional rivals of the Dhufir confederation and Ibn Saud. Their power declined in the late 19th and early 20th centuries under Ottoman centralization efforts, and today their tribal bonds have weakened significantly, with many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belonging to this confederation.

name: Al Murrah
description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

history: The Al Murrah are a Qahtanite Arab tribe descended from the larger Banu Yam tribe, who historically dominated southwestern Arabia. As camel-herding nomads, they controlled and traveled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, including the formidable Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali), covering up to 3,000 kilometers annually in their migrations. The tribe comprises approximately 15,000 individuals, with seven clans forming the Saudi branch. Historically, the Al Murrah constituted a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population and played significant roles in the country's political affairs. In 1885, a number of Al Murrah tribespeople, along with members from the Bani Hajer tribe, renounced their allegiance to Qatar and departed the country, joining with the hostile Ajman tribe. This led to a military confrontation where 1,200 partisans composed of loyal Al Murrah and Bani Hajer branches engaged the rebel coalition of 2,000 men, inflicting heavy losses on the rebels despite being outnumbered. The tribe's modern political involvement in Qatar proved tumultuous - they were strong supporters of Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir deposed in 1995. Eight months after the coup, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996 but failed. In February 2000, 19 alleged perpetrators were sentenced to death and 33 to life imprisonment, though none were executed. In 2017, Qatar revoked citizenship of the sheikh and 54 tribespeople, and Al Murrah members were barred from the 2021 Qatari general election, leading to protests. The Al Murrah maintain friendly relations with UAE and Saudi Arabia while opposing the current Iranian regime.

name: Al Musallam
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on descent and consensus among sub-clans
ancestorStory: Descended from Musallam, a tribal progenitor who established the lineage in the eastern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe traces its origins to the broader tribal confederations of the region.
description: Al Musallam is a tribal branch with historical presence in the eastern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in areas that now constitute parts of the UAE and eastern Saudi Arabia. They have maintained traditional pastoral and trading activities across the Gulf region. The tribe has had historical tensions with the Bani Utbah confederation, reflecting broader tribal competition for resources and territory. Today they remain active as part of the broader tribal structure in the GCC states.

relations.context: Fought military skirmishes in Qatar, Al Musallam had Ottoman support

history: Al Musallam emerged as a dominant tribal force in the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the eastern regions that now constitute parts of Qatar and the UAE. The tribe held significant territorial control in northwestern Qatar, ruling over settlements including Al Huwaila and exercising authority over the broader Bani Khalid confederation's coastal territories. Historical records from the mid-18th century document Al Musallam's role as local representatives of the Bani Khalid, acting under nominal Ottoman support while maintaining practical autonomy over their domain. The tribe's power peaked during the 1760s when they controlled key coastal areas and demanded tribute from new arrivals, including the emerging Bani Utbah confederation. Al Musallam's territorial disputes with the Bani Utbah, particularly the Al Khalifa branch, led to a series of military confrontations. The tribe initially resisted the Al Khalifa's expansion in the Zubarah region, demanding taxes and tribute, but Sheikh Muhammad bin Khalifa's refusal to pay led to escalating skirmishes. Despite their established position and Ottoman backing, Al Musallam ultimately lost control of their coastal strongholds following successful Al Khalifa assaults on Al Huwaila, forcing their leadership to flee to Najd. This defeat marked the end of Al Musallam's dominance in the region, as the Bani Utbah consolidated control over the northwestern Qatar coast and established Zubarah as a major commercial center. The expulsion of Al Musallam represented a significant shift in the regional balance of power, contributing to the broader decline of Bani Khalid influence in favor of emerging tribal confederations that would shape the future political landscape of the Gulf states.

migrationPath.description: Al Musallam leadership fled to Najd after successive defeats by Al Khalifa forces and the loss of their coastal strongholds

name: al-Nammir ibn Qasit
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afsa ibn Du'mi ibn Jadilah
ancestorStory: Al-Nammir was the son of Qasit from the ancient Adnanite lineage. The tribe traces its origins to the pre-Islamic Arabian Peninsula as part of the larger Rabi'ah confederation.
description: Al-Nammir ibn Qasit was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Adnanite branch through Rabi'ah. They were primarily nomadic pastoralists who roamed the northern Arabian Peninsula and had connections to the broader Qasit tribal confederation. The tribe is mentioned in classical Arab genealogical sources as part of the ancient tribal structure of Arabia. Like many pre-Islamic tribes, their influence waned with the rise of Islam and the subsequent tribal reorganizations, though their genealogical legacy remains documented in Arab tribal histories.

relations.context: Both tribes trace descent through the Rabi'ah lineage

history: Al-Nammir ibn Qasit was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Rabi'ah branch of the Adnanite tribal confederation. According to classical Arab genealogists, they were listed among the important branches of Rabīʿa ibn Nizar, alongside major tribes like Banu Bakr, Taghlib, Anizah, and Abd al-Qays. The tribe's origins trace back to the legendary patriarch Qasit, placing them within the ancient tribal structure of northern Arabia. Geographically, al-Nammir inhabited eastern Najd and are specifically mentioned as being clients of the powerful Taghlib tribe, who resided on the eastern banks of the Euphrates River. This client relationship suggests al-Nammir held a subordinate but allied position within the broader Rabi'ah confederation. Like many tribes of the Rabi'ah branch, including Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, and some sections of Bakr, al-Nammir were predominantly Christian before the advent of Islam, reflecting the religious diversity of pre-Islamic Arabian Peninsula. The tribe shared the general migration pattern of Rabi'ah tribes, who according to legend originated in the Tihamah region of western Arabia before migrating northwards and eastwards. As clients of Taghlib, their fate would have been closely tied to the fortunes of their patron tribe, and like many smaller tribal units of the pre-Islamic period, al-Nammir's independent political significance appears to have diminished with the rise of Islam and subsequent tribal reorganizations that favored larger, more powerful confederations.

migrationPath.description: Migration northwards and eastwards from original Rabi'ah homelands in Tihamah as part of broader tribal movements

name: Al Nasrallah
legitimacyNotes: Religious appellative name suggesting divine favor and blessing
description: Al Nasrallah is a tribal name found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf states. The name, meaning 'Victory of Allah,' appears among different tribal groupings and may represent multiple unrelated clans sharing the same appellative name. Without specific geographical or genealogical context, it is difficult to determine their exact origins, historical significance, or current tribal affiliations in the GCC region.
history: The Al Nasrallah name, meaning 'Victory of Allah,' appears across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf states as both a given name and surname. According to Wikipedia sources, the surname often indicates that the family adopted the name Nasrallah from one of its patrilineal ancestors. In Kuwait specifically, the most notable family carrying this name is known as Al-Nasrallah, who trace back to the Anazzah tribe and migrated from Sudair region. They reside mainly in Jiblah and Jahrah areas of Kuwait. Research reveals that Al Nasrallah families exist as part of larger tribal confederations - notably appearing in the Al-Musawi family genealogical records as a related family branch, and significantly within the Al Faiz family of Karbala. The Al Faiz family, descendants of Ibrahim al-Mujab (9th century grandson of the seventh Shi'ite Imam Musa al-Kadhim), includes Al Nasrallah as one of their six main family branches that emerged from their eponymous ancestor Muhammad Abu al-Faiz. The Karbala-based Al Nasrallah branch owns vast lands across Ayn al-Tamur and Karbala, with their grandfather Yunis al-Faizi having purchased the muqata'a Maal Yunis extending from Arba' Nahran to al-Jayya. The family has held custodianship of both Husayn and Abbas shrines and is noted as the only Alid family with the right to serve in both shrines, possessing three designated crypts in the Husayn shrine. The name appears among different tribal groupings across the region, and without specific geographical context, represents multiple potentially unrelated clans sharing the same appellative name.

name: Al Nasur/Nassour
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage with historical presence in Gulf region
description: Al Nasur (Al Nassour) is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. The name suggests connections to the concept of 'victory' or 'assistance' in Arabic nomenclature. Limited historical documentation makes specific details about their origins and traditional territories unclear. They appear to maintain tribal identity in the contemporary Gulf states.
history: Al Nasur (Nassour) is identified as one of the authentic Arab Huwala tribes, part of a tribal confederation that migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Iranian coast between the 13th and 19th centuries. The Huwala were Arabian Iranians of tribal lineage who settled primarily in Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, particularly around Bandar Abbas, Qishm, and the mainland near Bandar Lengeh. Al Nasur, along with other Arab Huwala tribes including the Qawasem, Hammadi, Obaidli, and Bani Tamim, were collectively referred to as 'Arab El-Sahel' or 'the Arabs of the Eastern Coast.' These tribes maintained their Sunni Islamic faith, distinguishing them from the majority Twelver Shia Persian population. The Al Nasur, like other Huwala tribes, experienced significant return migration to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the final major wave occurring in the 1930s due to restrictive economic policies imposed by Reza Shah Pahlavi. Contemporary historians such as Niebuhr, Lorimer, and David Seton documented that the true Huwala Arabs, including Al Nasur, inhabited a specific geographical region from Bandar Kanj in the south to Bandar Kangan in the north, extending from the Arabian Gulf coast to the Shibkoh mountains. As part of the first type of Huwalas identified by Saudi historian Jalal Al-Haroon, Al Nasur represents the original Arabs who migrated to Southern Iran during the 16th and 17th centuries and later returned to Arabia in the 20th century.

legitimacyNotes: Lineage through descent from Albu Salih tribal confederation
ancestorStory: Descended from Omar, a prominent member of the Albu Salih confederation. The family established themselves as a recognized tribal branch through traditional genealogical ties.
description: Al Omar is a tribal branch within the larger Albu Salih confederation, historically active in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintained traditional pastoral economies and tribal governance structures typical of Qahtani lineages. The tribe has preserved its identity through genealogical records and traditional practices. Today they remain an active tribal group with members across various Gulf states.

history: The research reveals no direct connection between the Al Omar tribal branch of the Albu Salih confederation and the historical figures mentioned in the sources. Omar al-Mukhtar (1858-1931) was a Libyan resistance leader from the Arab Mnifa tribe in Cyrenaica, belonging to the Senussi movement. He led the native resistance against Italian colonization of Libya and was executed in 1931. Omar al-Bashir (born 1944) was a Sudanese military officer and politician from the Ja'alin tribe who served as Sudan's head of state from 1989-2019 before being ousted and later convicted of corruption. Umar ibn al-Khattab (c. 584-644) was the second Rashidun caliph from the Banu Adi clan of Quraysh in Mecca, who converted to Islam in 616 and later ruled the expanding Islamic empire until his assassination in 644. None of these historical Omar/Umar figures show any documented genealogical or geographical connection to the Al Omar branch of the Albu Salih confederation in the Arabian Peninsula context described in the existing tribal data.

name: Al-Omaro Bani Uqba
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage from Bani Uqba
ancestorStory: Descended from Uqba, a tribal leader whose descendants formed the Al-Omaro branch. The tribe traces its origins to ancient Arabian tribal confederations.
description: Al-Omaro Bani Uqba is a tribal branch descended from the ancient Bani Uqba confederation. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who moved across the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained alliance relationships with other tribal groups including the Mahamid. Their current status and geographic concentration in the modern Gulf states remains unclear from historical records.

relations.context: Allied with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and other Karak clans when they established their emirate
Both trace descent from Qahtani tribal roots

history: The Al-Omaro Bani Uqba are documented as key allies of the Mahamid tribe in the Levant, specifically in the Karak region of present-day Jordan. According to historical records, the Mahamid tribe, which originated from the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation and initially resided in Yemen and the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, underwent significant migrations northward. When the Mahamid expanded beyond their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, they first moved to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia before continuing their migration toward the Levant. Upon reaching the Karak region, the Mahamid found themselves in such large numbers that they outnumbered the existing major tribes in the area. It was in this context that they formed a strategic alliance with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, among other local Karak clans. This alliance proved significant as the Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid in the region, with the majority rule held by the Al-Amru clan. The emirate maintained control for an extended period, though historical sources suggest their rule became increasingly harsh, eventually prompting various clans to unite against them. The specific role of Al-Omaro Bani Uqba within this political structure and their subsequent fate following the emirate's challenges remains unclear from available records.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration following conquest routes and seeking new grazing lands

name: Al-Oraif
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and local consensus
description: Al-Oraif is a smaller tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Like many Arabian clans, they maintain their tribal identity and genealogical connections. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins or major historical roles in Gulf politics. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal structure in the region.
history: Al-Oraif (Arabic: العريف) is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, one of the major Bedouin Arab tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Al-Dhafeer tribe descends from Tayy and traces its genealogical origins to Banu Lam from the Arabs of the Hejaz. According to multiple historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes were near Medina, specifically from a place called al-Dhaghn. Al-Oraif belongs to the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe historically lived in the desert of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), spanning the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known for its mobile lifestyle, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration rather than maintaining fixed settlements. They were nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children. In 1782, Al-Dhafeer warriors participated in the Persian-Bahrain conflict, joining forces sent by the Emir of Kuwait to help Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa confront Persian rule in Bahrain, culminating in the victory at the Battle of Zubarah. The tribe also fought in the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, where they successfully defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe in the southern desert of Iraq. Around 1926, when Emir Ibn Saud consolidated control over much of Arabia, the tribe's branches in Saudi Arabia were allowed to settle in various settlements after the securing of regional roads.

Expansion to northern regions including areas of modern Kuwait and eastern Saudi Arabia

name: Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah
legitimacyNotes: Ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah emirate, hereditary sheikhdom established through maritime dominance
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Razzaq, who established the family's prominence in the coastal regions. The dynasty rose to power through control of maritime trade routes and pearling operations in the 18th century.
description: The Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah are the ruling family of the emirate and one of the most prominent maritime dynasties of the Arabian Gulf. Historically based in Ras Al Khaimah, they established a powerful confederation controlling much of the lower Gulf's sea lanes from the 18th century. They faced conflicts with British forces in the early 19th century over maritime activities, leading to various treaties. Today, they continue as the ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, one of the seven emirates of the UAE.

relations.context: The Zaab became dependents of the Al Qasimi rulers of Ras Al Khaimah by 1853, but this relationship deteriorated leading to the 1968 migration
Both branches of the Al Qasimi dynasty ruling separate emirates
Part of the broader Qawasim tribal confederation

history: The Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah trace their origins to the Qawasim tribal confederation, descended from Huwala tribes who migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia. They claim Hashemite ancestry through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki, descending from Husayn ibn Ali. During the 18th century, they established themselves as rulers of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, embracing the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement and becoming allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. The first recorded ruler was Sheikh Rahma Al Qasimi (1708-1731), described by the Dutch as the 'Emir of Julfar' and one of the richest Arab merchants. By the early 19th century, the Qawasim had emerged as a powerful maritime force controlling territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah, Sharjah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh on the Persian coast. Their strategic position near the Strait of Hormuz brought them into conflict with the Omani Empire and eventually with Britain. The British branded them as 'pirates' due to their allegiance to the Wahhabi movement and attacks on British-allied shipping. This led to two major British military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819, culminating in their defeat and the signing of the General Maritime Treaty of 1820. Following the 1819 campaign, Sheikh Sultan Bin Saqr Al Qasimi was reinstated by the British and ruled until 1866. The family continued to rule through various successions, with periods where Ras Al Khaimah was incorporated into Sharjah (1900-1921). Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi ruled for 62 years (1948-2010), initially opposing UAE formation due to disputes over the Tunb islands, but eventually joining the federation on February 10, 1972. His son Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi has ruled since 2010, with his son Mohammed bin Saud designated as heir presumptive.

description: The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates, descended from the broader Qawasim confederation. Historically based along the Trucial Coast, they were major maritime powers controlling trade routes in the lower Gulf from the 17th-19th centuries. They faced conflicts with British forces in the early 19th century but later signed treaties establishing the Trucial States system. Today they remain the ruling family of Sharjah under Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.

history: The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian Peninsula, tracing their origins to Qasim ibn Rahmah who established their power in the lower Gulf in the 17th century. According to genealogical sources, the Qawasim tribe from which the dynasty originates are Huwala (Arab migrants to Persia), of Hashemite origin descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors maintained connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly ruling the town of Lengeh on the Hormozgan coast. By the early 18th century, the Huwayla tribe of the Qawasim clan established themselves in Sharjah around 1727, declaring independence. The first recorded ruler was Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi (1722-1747). During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they had emerged as a powerful maritime confederation based in both Ras Al Khaimah on the southern Persian Gulf shore and Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh on the northern shore. Their control of Gulf trade routes led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself, resulting in the Qawasim being labeled as 'pirates' by the British. This led to two major British military campaigns - the Persian Gulf campaign of 1809 and a more decisive 1819 expedition that sacked Ras Al Khaimah and other Qawasim strongholds. Following their defeat, Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi signed the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, leading to the establishment of the Trucial States system. The dynasty split, with separate branches ruling Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. On December 2, 1971, Sharjah joined the United Arab Emirates as one of the founding seven emirates. Today, Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi continues the Al Qasimi rule over Sharjah, while Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi rules Ras Al Khaimah.
