description: The Al Qawasim are a major tribal confederation that became the ruling families of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah in the UAE. Historically based in the northern Emirates and southern Iran, they were a dominant maritime power controlling pearling and trade routes in the Arabian Gulf during the 18th and 19th centuries. They engaged in conflicts with British forces during the so-called 'Piracy Wars' before signing treaties in the early 19th century. Today, branches of Al Qawasim continue to rule as the Al Qasimi families in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, playing important roles in UAE federal politics and development.

history: The Al Qawasim (also spelled Al Qasimi) are a tribal confederation of Hashemite origin, claiming descent from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The confederation traces its specific origins to figures like Qasim ibn Rahmah and later rulers such as Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi (1722-1747). The Qawasim are classified as Huwala, maintaining ancestral connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly ruling over the town of Lengeh on the Hormozgan province coast until its annexation by Iran in 1887. During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, becoming close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they had emerged as a formidable maritime power controlling both southern shores (Ras Al Khaimah) and northern Persian Gulf territories (Qishm, Bandar Abbas, Lingeh). Their dominance over Gulf trade routes brought them into conflict with the British-allied Omani Empire over border disputes, religious differences, and naval supremacy. Beginning in 1804, increased Qawasim naval attacks on trading vessels led the British to label them as 'pirates,' resulting in two major military campaigns - the 1809 Persian Gulf campaign and the more successful 1819 expedition under William Keir Grant. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty forced the Qawasim to cease their maritime activities and carry a distinctive peace flag. Modern scholarship, particularly by Sultan bin Mohammed Al Qasimi (current Ruler of Sharjah), contests the piracy charges as a British casus belli to eliminate Arab trade competition. Following the treaties, the Al Qawasim evolved into the ruling dynasties of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, becoming founding members of the United Arab Emirates in 1971, with branches continuing to rule both emirates today.

ancestorStory: The Al Qemzi trace their lineage to Qamzi, who established the family as skilled pearl divers and seafarers along the UAE coast. They became prominent in the pearling trade and developed strong maritime traditions.
description: The Al Qemzi are a prominent UAE maritime tribe historically based along the coastal areas of the Emirates, particularly associated with Dubai and the northern emirates. They gained prominence as skilled pearl divers, boat builders, and maritime traders during the height of the Gulf pearling industry. The tribe maintained strong relationships with other coastal trading families and played an important role in the pre-oil economy of the UAE. Today, they remain an active and respected family within UAE society, with members involved in business and public service.

history: The Al Qemzi (Arabic: القمزي/القمزان) are one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia. As part of the larger Bani Yas confederation, they descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, sharing this lineage with other branches including the Al Nahyan and Al Maktoum ruling families. The Al Qemzi, along with other Bani Yas clans, settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah house. The confederation later expanded their territory when they acquired property in the Buraimi Oasis and established settlements in Al Ain. Following the discovery of fresh water on Abu Dhabi island in 1793, many Bani Yas families, likely including the Al Qemzi, participated in the migration to the coastal areas, with Abu Dhabi town being formally established in 1795. The Bani Yas confederation maintained close relations with the Dhawahir tribe while traditionally opposing the Na'im and Bani Ka'ab in the Buraimi region. As maritime-oriented members of the confederation, the Al Qemzi would have been part of the broader Bani Yas population that Lorimer estimated at around 12,000 in the early 1900s, including approximately 2,000 in Dubai and 2,800 in Abu Dhabi town, though specific population figures for the Al Qemzi branch are not recorded in the available sources.

name: Al-Rabbab
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with established genealogical records
ancestorStory: Al-Rabbab traces its lineage to Rabab ibn Malik, an ancient Arabian ancestor. The tribe has maintained its genealogical traditions through oral and written records.
description: Al-Rabbab is an ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic origins, traditionally associated with the northern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe has historical presence in areas that overlap with modern Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia. They maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle centered on camel herding and trade routes. Today, members of Al-Rabbab are integrated into modern Gulf societies while maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: Al-Rabbab is confirmed through the research as one of the four main branches of the powerful Banu Tamim tribe, alongside Amr, Banu Hanzala, and Banu Sa'd. The Banu Tamim trace their lineage through an established genealogical chain: Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan, connecting them directly to Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham). As an Adnanite tribe descended from Adnan, Al-Rabbab shares this ancient northern Arabian heritage. The Banu Tamim were historically concentrated in the central and northern parts of Najd before the spread of Islam in the 6th century, playing an important role at the beginning of Islam when they came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah. Following the Islamic conquests, the tribe spread across the Arabian Peninsula and then migrated to areas ruled by subsequent caliphates, extending their presence from Morocco in the west to Khuzestan in the east. The research shows that Banu Tamim held significant power for centuries in various regions through dynasties like the Aghlabids, and continue to maintain a strong presence today in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Kuwait, Iraq, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, and North African countries including Algeria, Morocco, Palestine, Tunisia, and Libya.

name: Al Rumaiyadh
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and recognized lineage
ancestorStory: Al Rumaiyadh trace their lineage to their eponymous ancestor Rumaidh. The tribe established itself as part of the broader tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Rumaiyadh is a tribal branch with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They are part of the Qahtani tribal lineage system and have maintained their tribal identity through traditional genealogical connections. The tribe has historically been involved in the pastoral economy typical of Arabian tribes. Their current status and specific regional influence within the GCC states requires further documentation.
history: The Al Rumaiyadh are a clan within the Bani Malik section of the broader Albu Salih tribal confederation, with their lineage tracing to their eponymous ancestor Rumaidh. During the 18th century, they became part of a significant transformation when the Albu Salih evolved into a powerful tribal confederation opposing Ottoman influence in the region. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the Al Rumaiyadh became the dominant force within the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate, with Shaikh Badr rising to become the paramount Shaikh of the entire Bani Malik region. The tribe's territory was centered in what is now southern Iraq, particularly around the great marshes in the Al Islah district of Dhi Qar province. The Al Rumaiyadh practiced both settled agriculture and semi-nomadic pastoralism, with their nomadic branches migrating seasonally south through the Wadi Batin valley into northeastern Arabia, including northern and western Kuwaiti territories, before returning north for summer. Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh became renowned for his fierce resistance against British colonial forces during World War I and the subsequent British Mandate of Mesopotamia. He fought alongside Ottoman forces at the Battle of Shaiba in 1915, and after losing two sons in battle, vowed never to surrender to British occupation. His resistance included successfully reclaiming tribal lands from British forces and renaming their fort from 'Qalat Al-Fatihi' to 'Qalat Al-Nasri.' He remained a wanted outlaw for years and played a major role in the 1920 Iraqi Revolution. Today, members of the Al Rumaiyadh are found across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with the current leader being Shaikh Sabah bin Fares bin Mohsen bin Bader Al Rumaiyadh.

name: Al-Sa'id
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Sa'id, though specific genealogical details vary by regional branch.
description: Al-Sa'id is a tribal name found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with different branches having distinct histories and locations. Some branches are found in the Gulf region, particularly in parts of Saudi Arabia and other GCC states. The name is relatively common among Arab tribes, with various unrelated groups sharing this designation. Current branches remain active in their respective regions, maintaining traditional tribal structures alongside modern state integration.
history: Al-Sa'id represents a diverse collection of tribal branches across the Arabian Peninsula, with the most prominent being the Al Bu Said dynasty of Oman. The Omani Al Bu Said trace their lineage through the Azd tribe, specifically to al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally migrated from Yemen following the destruction of the Marib Dam and established themselves as a leading force in early Islamic conquests. The dynasty's founder, Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi, rose to power in 1744 after successfully defending Sohar against Persian forces for nine months. He was elected Imam on November 20, 1744, establishing one of Arabia's oldest surviving royal dynasties. Under Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' the dynasty reached its zenith, controlling territories from Oman to Zanzibar, parts of the East African coast, southern Iran including Bandar-Abbas, and Gwadar in Balochistan. The empire was divided in 1856 after Said's death, creating separate sultanates in Oman and Zanzibar. The Zanzibar branch ended with the 1964 revolution, while the Omani branch continues under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq. Other Al-Sa'id branches exist across the Gulf region with distinct histories, including connections to various tribal confederations like Anizah, though comprehensive genealogical documentation for these branches remains limited.

name: House of Saud
legitimacyNotes: Religious legitimacy through Wahhabi alliance, conquest and unification of Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Al Saud trace their origins to Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin who settled in Diriyah in the 15th century. The family rose to prominence through alliance with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in the 18th century.
description: The House of Saud is the ruling royal family of Saudi Arabia, originally from Najd region. They established three successive Saudi states, with the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia founded in 1932 by Abdulaziz ibn Saud. Through alliance with the Wahhabi religious movement and military conquest, they unified most of the Arabian Peninsula. They remain the absolute monarchs of Saudi Arabia and custodians of Islam's holiest sites.

history: The House of Saud (Al Saud) traces its origins to Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi, who settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 with his clan, the Mrudah. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the larger Rabi'ah tribal confederation, which played an important role in shaping Arabia from the 6th century. Mani' was invited to Diriyah by a relative named Ibn Dir, who handed him two estates called al-Mulaybeed and Ghusayba. The region was renamed Al Diriyah after their benefactor. The clan's name derives from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725. The First Saudi State was founded in 1727 by Muhammad bin Saud through alliance with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, establishing a strict Islamic polity. This state expanded across most of the Arabian Peninsula until its destruction by Ottoman-Egyptian forces in 1818, when Diriyah was razed and the Saudi leadership executed or exiled. The Second Saudi State emerged in 1824 under Turki ibn Abdallah but was characterized by internal conflicts and eventually fell to the Rashidis at the Battle of Mulayda in 1891, forcing the family into exile in Kuwait. In 1902, Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud) recaptured Riyadh with 40 followers, beginning the third Saudi state. Through three decades of conquest (1902-1932), he unified Najd, conquered Al-Hasa from the Ottomans (1913), defeated the Rashidis (1921), and captured the Hejaz including Mecca and Medina (1925). The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia was proclaimed in 1932, with oil discovered in 1938 transforming the kingdom into a global power. The family has ruled as absolute monarchs, with succession passing from Ibn Saud to his sons: Saud (1953-1964), Faisal (1964-1975), Khalid (1975-1982), Fahd (1982-2005), Abdullah (2005-2015), and currently Salman (2015-present). The royal family is estimated to comprise 15,000 members with vast wealth including control of Saudi Aramco.

name: Al-Shaleikh
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority and historical settlement in Gulf region
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Shaleikh. They established themselves as part of the broader tribal confederation system in the Arabian Gulf region.
description: Al-Shaleikh is a tribal group found in the Arabian Gulf region, particularly associated with the broader tribal systems of the GCC states. They maintain traditional tribal structures and have been part of the historical settlement patterns in the Gulf. The tribe continues to exist as an active social unit within the modern Gulf states, preserving their tribal identity and customs. Their specific historical role and current distribution across Gulf states requires further documentation.
history: Al-Shaleikh is documented as a branch that descended from the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد, al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The parent Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original areas became crowded in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz, tribal leaders gathered to decide their course of action, ultimately choosing migration over conquest of neighboring Harb sub-clans. The Mahamid undertook extensive migrations, moving north to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid and became one of the largest tribes in the area. Al-Shaleikh emerged as one of the documented branches of the Mahamid specifically in Jordan, alongside other branches including Al Abdul Dayem, Al-Bahri, Ayal Abdo, and the Abu Karaki family. The research indicates that various Mahamid branches formed alliances with local tribal groups including Al-Huwaitat, Bani Abbad, Bani Hamida, and Zoubi, suggesting Al-Shaleikh likely participated in the broader tribal confederation systems that characterized the region's political landscape.

name: Al Subaih
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical settlement patterns
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Subayh. They are considered part of the broader tribal confederation structure of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Subaih is a tribal branch found primarily in the Arabian Peninsula region. They are traditionally associated with nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal identity through genealogical connections. The tribe has historical presence in areas that are now part of modern GCC states. They continue to exist as an active tribal group with traditional social structures intact.

relations.context: Historical migration patterns in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: Al Subaih is identified in the historical sources as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation. The research documents Al Subaih among the core branches of Bani Khalid, specifically listed alongside Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, and Mahashir & Nahood. As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, Al Subaih participated in the broader tribal political dynamics of Eastern Arabia from the 15th through 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid dominated the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif during this period, establishing the Khalidi Emirate under Barrak ibn Ghurayr in 1670 after expelling Ottoman forces. The confederation faced significant challenges from the expanding Saudi-Wahhabi state, with the Khalidi chief ordering the emir of Uyayna to cease support for Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and expel him, leading to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's move to Diriyyah where he allied with the Al Saud. The Bani Khalid, including Al Subaih, remained staunch enemies of the Saudis and attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion. After Saudi conquest of Nejd, the confederation's domain in Al-Hasa was invaded in 1789, marking the decline of Khalidi power. Final defeats came through battles with tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, ending Bani Khalid rule. Members of the confederation, including Al Subaih, subsequently dispersed across the region, with many settling in Iraq, Kuwait, Palestine, Lebanon, and other areas of the Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from central Arabian homeland toward eastern coastal regions
Settlement along the Trucial Coast for pearling and fishing opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Bani Khalid Expel Ottomans
timelineEvents.description: Barrak ibn Ghurayr of Al Humaid leads Bani Khalid confederation, including Al Subaih branch, in expelling Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabian cities and establishing Khalidi rule

timelineEvents.description: Saudi forces invade Bani Khalid domain in Al-Hasa, deposing Al-'Ura'yir rulers and beginning the decline of Khalidi confederation power

timelineEvents.description: Bani Khalid confederation faces defeat in battle against alliance led by Mutayr and Ajman tribes, marking major setback for tribal power

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat by Subay' and Saudis
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' tribe and Al Saud forces brings the rule of Bani Khalid confederation to a close, ending Al Subaih's participation in regional governance

name: Sabais
description: Al Sabais (السبايس) is a tribal group with presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They appear to maintain tribal identity in the modern era, though their historical role and influence in Gulf affairs requires further research.
history: The Sabais, also known as Al Subousi (السبايس/السبوسي), are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. According to the UK Memorial on the Buraimi dispute, the Bedouin group referred to as the Jabais is generally identified with the Sabais, indicating their nomadic heritage within the broader Bani Yas structure. As part of the Bani Yas confederation, the Sabais shared in the tribal movement from the Najd region of Central Arabia, where the Bani Yas originated, descending along with the Awamir from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. Historical records from J.C. Lorimer's Gazetteer (1908-1915) estimated the total Bani Yas population at around 12,000, including approximately 2,000 nomadic members who generally remained within Abu Dhabi territory throughout the year. The Sabais would have been part of this nomadic element of the confederation. By the early 1950s, the overall Bani Yas population had declined to approximately 8,000, including about 1,700 Bedouin, due to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors. As members of the Bani Yas confederation, the Sabais fell under the authority of the Al Bu Falah (Al Nahyan) leadership and were part of the political structure that established the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.

migrationPath.description: Migration with Bani Yas clans from Najd to Liwa Oasis under Al Bu Falah leadership

timelineEvents.title: Settlement in Liwa Oasis
timelineEvents.description: Sabais settle with other Bani Yas clans in Liwa Oasis under Al Bu Falah leadership

timelineEvents.title: Population Decline
timelineEvents.description: Sabais affected by broader Bani Yas population decline as families emigrate to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment

name: Al Suhoob
description: Al Suhoob is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula, though specific historical documentation about their origins and activities is limited in available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain their genealogical identity while being integrated into modern Gulf society. Their current status and geographic distribution within the GCC region requires further research for accurate characterization.
history: Al Suhoob is one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob, traditionally claim descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, though Arab genealogists have questioned this lineage, suggesting alternative origins from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe. As part of Bani Khalid, Al Suhoob participated in the confederation's rule over al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions, with the chieftainship traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan while other branches like Al Suhoob maintained their distinct tribal identity within the larger confederation. Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces and established the Khalidi Emirate with its capital in Al-Mubarraz. The confederation, including Al Suhoob, became staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement, attempting to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion. However, their efforts failed, and the Saudis conquered al-Hasa in 1789, ending Khalidi rule. After periods of Ottoman restoration and internal conflicts with tribes like the Ajman, Dawasir, and Subay', the Bani Khalid's political power definitively ended by 1830. Today, members of Al Suhoob and other Bani Khalid branches are found throughout the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in eastern and central Saudi Arabia, as well as in Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE.

timelineEvents.title: Establishment of Bani Khalid Emirate
timelineEvents.description: Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr established the Khalidi Emirate with capital in Al-Mubarraz, with Al Suhoob as one of the main tribal branches

timelineEvents.description: Bani Khalid including Al Suhoob defeated by alliance of Mutayr and Ajman tribes

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat by Saudi-Subay Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay and Al Saud brought the rule of Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob influence, to a definitive close

name: Al-Surur family
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and established presence in Gulf region
ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to an ancestor named Surur. They established themselves as a notable family within the broader tribal structure of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Surur is a family/clan name found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. They are documented as part of the broader tribal fabric of the GCC states, though specific historical details about their origins and migrations remain unclear. The family maintains presence in modern Gulf societies as part of the established tribal communities. Their exact tribal affiliations and historical role require further genealogical research.
history: The Al-Surur family emerges from historical records in connection with the Al-Buraimi oasis region. According to documentation from the pre-Saudi era governance of Al-Buraimi, the Al-Surur family held authority over Hamasah, one of the nine villages that comprised the Tawam oasis (modern Al-Buraimi/Al-Ain area). This places them as established local rulers in the northern Oman/UAE border region, specifically controlling Hamasah village while other families governed neighboring settlements - the Al-Zar'a family governed the broader Al-Buraimi area, Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and Al-Azaznah tribe ruled Al Hailah village. The family's documented presence in this strategically important oasis, which served as a crucial waypoint on overland trade routes between Sohar and the Persian Gulf, indicates their historical significance in regional commerce and governance. Over time, the Al-Buraimi region came under the influence of Al Saud, marking a transition in the political landscape that would have affected the Al-Surur family's authority. The oasis region's complex history of tribal alliances, power struggles, and eventual incorporation into modern state boundaries reflects the broader political transformations that shaped Arabian Peninsula families like Al-Surur. Their association with Hamasah village specifically connects them to the agricultural and trading networks that sustained the Tawam oasis communities for centuries.

timelineEvents.title: Authority over Hamasah
timelineEvents.description: Al-Surur family documented as holding authority over Hamasah village in the Al-Buraimi oasis during the pre-Saudi era

name: Al-Suwait
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and local leadership in Najd region
description: Al-Suwait is a small tribal clan historically based in the Najd region of central Arabia. They are part of the broader Adnani tribal network and have maintained their traditional pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has limited historical documentation but continues to exist as part of the modern Saudi tribal structure.
history: Al-Suwait is a branch of the larger Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to multiple historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes trace back to al-Dhaghn, a place opposite Medina in the Hejaz. Al-Suwait forms part of the Ahl al-Buwait division of Al-Dhafeer, which earned this designation due to their habit of defending refugees. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration, becoming known as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' for their practice of raiding alongside women and children. They historically inhabited the desert of north Najd and north Bahrain, later moving to areas of southern Iraq, northern Saudi Arabia, and northern Kuwait. In 1926, when Ibn Saud conquered much of Arabia, the Saudi branch of the tribe was allowed to settle in permanent settlements after the roads were secured. Al-Suwait, along with other Al-Dhafeer branches, participated in significant historical events including supporting the House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain in 1782 and the Battle of Qaysar in 1924 where they defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from ancestral homes near Medina to central Arabian desert

migrationPath.to: North Najd and North Bahrain region
migrationPath.description: Settlement in northern desert regions spanning Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait

timelineEvents.description: Al-Dhafeer forces assisted House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain at request of Kuwait's Emir Abdullah I

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Qaysar

timelineEvents.description: Ibn Saud allowed Al-Dhafeer branches in Saudi Arabia to establish permanent settlements after securing regional roads

name: Al Tarayma
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on genealogical descent and customary leadership
description: Al Tarayma is a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific territorial base or major historical role. Like many smaller tribal branches, they likely practiced traditional Bedouin pastoralism and maintained genealogical connections within the broader tribal framework of the region. Their current status and specific GCC connections require further historical research to establish definitively.
history: Al Tarayma is documented as one of the sections within the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged as a powerful alliance in the 18th century under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh. The Albu Salih confederation originated from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq and transformed into a warfare alliance due to opposition against Ottoman Empire influence in the region. As part of this confederation, Al Tarayma would have participated in the traditional lifestyle that combined settled agriculture near the great marshes of southern Iraq with seasonal nomadic migrations. The nomadic branches historically migrated south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, where they traded with local villages and towns before returning north for summer. During the early 20th century, Al Tarayma, as part of the Albu Salih, were involved in resistance against British forces during World War I, including participation in the Battle of Shaiba in 1915 alongside Ottoman forces. By the 1940s and early 1950s, most nomadic sections had abandoned the nomadic lifestyle and settled in various locations. Today, members of the broader Albu Salih confederation, including Al Tarayma, are located across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal nomadic migration south during autumn for livestock herding

migrationPath.to: Kuwait territories and Arabian Gulf coast
migrationPath.description: Continuation of seasonal migration for trading with local villages

timelineEvents.title: Formation of Albu Salih Confederation
timelineEvents.description: Al Tarayma becomes part of the Albu Salih tribal confederation under Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh's leadership

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Shaiba
timelineEvents.description: Al Tarayma participates as part of Albu Salih forces fighting alongside Ottomans against British Empire near Basrah

timelineEvents.title: End of Nomadic Lifestyle
timelineEvents.description: Majority of Al Tarayma nomads settle permanently in Iraq, Kuwait, Iran and Saudi Arabia

name: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Tayy tribe with traditional pastoral authority
ancestorStory: Descended from Tha'laba, a prominent figure within the Tayy tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the ancient Tayy who were known for their presence in northern Arabia.
description: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) is a tribal branch of the larger Tayy confederation, traditionally based in northern Arabia. As part of the Qahtani lineage, they were among the pastoral nomadic tribes that moved across the Arabian Peninsula. While historically significant within the Tayy structure, their influence diminished over time as other tribal groups gained prominence in the Gulf region. They are now considered part of the historical tribal landscape rather than an active political entity.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Tayy tribal confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches sharing common ancestry

history: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) represents a significant sub-branch of the Al Jadila division of the ancient Tayy tribe, one of the largest and most influential Arab tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the Al Jadila branch was named after a woman of the Tayy named Jadilah, whose sons Hur and Jundub became progenitors of major sub-branches. The Banu Jundub specifically produced the numerous Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) subbranch, which itself generated the Banu La'm, a leading sub-branch of Al Jadila in northern Arabia. Some sources suggest that the Jarm (or Jurum) may have also been a branch of the Al al-Tha'alib. The Tayy as a whole traced their ancestry to Julhumah ibn Udad (known as 'Tayy'), descended from the Qahtani lineage through Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub. Originally based in Yemen, the Tayy migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, establishing themselves in the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges, which became known as Jabal Tayy. During the late 6th century Fasad War between the Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila factions, several Al Jadila clans, including elements of Al al-Tha'alib, migrated from northern Arabian plains to Syria, where they converted to Christianity under Ghassanid influence and established a military encampment called 'Hadir Tayyi' near Qinnasrin. The Al al-Tha'alib experienced the broader Tayy conversion to Islam in 629-630 CE when tribal leaders like Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr embraced the new faith. During the Islamic conquests, some Banu La'm leaders, including Thumama ibn Aws, initially joined the rebel Tulayha during the Ridda Wars before being diplomatically returned to the Muslim fold through Adi ibn Hatim's intervention. By the Mamluk period, the Tha'laba branch of the tribe had established significant presence in Egypt, particularly in the al-Sharqiyah district of the Nile Delta and along the Mediterranean coast extending to western Galilee, where they served as auxiliaries and maintained postal routes for the Mamluk state.

migrationPath.description: Original Tayy migration from Yemen to northern Arabian mountain ranges following Banu Azd dispersion
Al Jadila clans including Al al-Tha'alib elements migrate to Syria during Fasad War, establish Hadir Tayyi military encampment

name: Al-Thira'an
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
description: Al-Thira'an is a tribal branch found in the Arabian Peninsula, primarily in the eastern regions of Saudi Arabia. They are traditionally a Bedouin tribe engaged in nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal structure and customs. The tribe has adapted to modern sedentary life while preserving their tribal identity and traditional leadership systems.
history: Al-Thira'an is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes can be traced to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn. The Al-Dhafeer confederation, including Al-Thira'an, were Bedouin Arabs who lived in the deserts of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), spanning the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known for their nomadic lifestyle and was nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children. Al-Thira'an belongs to the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. Around 1926, when Emir Ibn Saud conquered large portions of Arabia, the tribe's branches in Saudi Arabia were allowed to settle in various settlements after the roads were secured. Today, members of the broader Al-Dhafeer confederation, including Al-Thira'an, live primarily in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from ancestral homes near Medina to central Najd desert regions
Migration to desert regions of north Najd, north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia and north Kuwait

name: Al-Ubaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Gulf region
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Ubaid, the tribal branch established itself in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are known for their traditional nomadic lifestyle and later settlement patterns.
description: Al-Ubaid is a tribal branch found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including regions of modern-day Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states. They traditionally practiced nomadic pastoralism before many members settled in towns and cities. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and customs while adapting to modern Gulf society. They are dispersed across different areas and have integrated into the social fabric of various Gulf communities.
history: Al-Ubaid (Al-Obaidi) is an Arab tribe with complex origins traced to the ancient Yemenite tribe Madh'hij through the Zubaid branch. According to genealogical records, they descend from Amru bin Ma'adi Yakrib Al-Zubaidi Al-Madh'hiji Al-Qahtani Al-Arabi, known as 'Fares Al Arab' (knight of the Arabs). The tribe's more recent history centers on their rule as sultans in Najd for over 800 years prior to the 1750s. The Al-Ubaid established a powerful sultanate in Najd under the Zubaidi branch, with a documented lineage of rulers including Sultan Maktoum, Sultan Laheeb, Sultan Mahjoub, and others extending back through 20 generations. Their political prominence brought them into direct rivalry with the emerging House of Saud during the establishment of the first Saudi State in the mid-18th century. This rivalry proved decisive when the Al-Ubaid were defeated by Al-Saud forces and subsequently banished from their ancestral Najdi territories circa 1750s. The final Zubaidi Sultan in Najd, Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi, led the forced migration of his people to Iraq. His eldest son, Sultan Obaid, became the eponymous founder of the Al-Obaidi family and the basis for the tribal name. In Iraq, the Al-Ubaid initially settled in Al Jazira region of Mesopotamia between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, establishing themselves as a significant tribal presence. However, their dominance in this region was later challenged and eventually displaced by the larger Shammar tribe, which took control of the Jazirah plain after decisively defeating the Al-Ubaid in the early 19th century. Despite these territorial losses, Al-Ubaid descendants remain dispersed across Iraq and other parts of the Arabian Peninsula, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to modern circumstances.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration led by Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi after defeat by Al-Saud forces and banishment from Najd

timelineEvents.title: Defeat and Banishment from Najd
timelineEvents.description: Al-Ubaid sultanate defeated by Al-Saud forces, ending over 800 years of rule in Najd

name: Al Yosha'a
legitimacyNotes: Religious authority based on claimed Hashemite descent and scholarly tradition
ancestorStory: Al Yosha'a trace their lineage to Yosha (Joshua), claiming descent through the Hashemite line. They established themselves as a scholarly and religious family in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Yosha'a (Sada Al Yosha) are a Sayyid family claiming Hashemite descent, historically prominent in religious scholarship and Islamic jurisprudence across the Arabian Peninsula. They have been particularly influential in areas of present-day Saudi Arabia and have maintained their status as religious authorities. The family has produced notable Islamic scholars and judges throughout their history. They continue to maintain their religious and social prominence in modern Gulf society.

relations.context: Religious support and scholarly services to Saudi state
Historical marriages between noble families

history: Al Yosha'a (Sada Al Yosha) are a Sayyid family claiming Hashemite descent through Yosha (Joshua), traditionally prominent in religious scholarship across the Arabian Peninsula. According to tribal genealogical records, they form one of the main sections of the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged in the 18th century as a powerful alliance in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate in southern Iraq. The Albu Salih confederation was formed during the 18th century under opposition to Ottoman influence, transforming from a single tribe into a multi-tribal alliance that included various Arab tribal origins. Al Yosha'a joined this confederation as the Bani Hashem section, maintaining their distinct Sayyid status and religious authority within the broader tribal structure. The confederation's territory centered around the great marshes near present-day Al Islah district in Dhi Qar province, Iraq, with seasonal migrations extending into Kuwait and northeastern Arabia. During the early 20th century, Al Yosha'a members, like other Albu Salih sections, were involved in resistance against British occupation of Mesopotamia, participating in battles including the 1915 Battle of Shaiba alongside Ottoman forces. By the 1940s-1950s, many nomadic members settled permanently across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with Al Yosha'a continuing to maintain their religious and scholarly prominence in modern Gulf society.

migrationPath.description: Annual autumn migrations of nomadic branches through Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and Arabian Gulf coast
Settlement of majority of nomadic members across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran and Saudi Arabia

name: Al-Zar'a family
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Al-Zar'a trace their lineage to an ancestor named Zar'a, though specific genealogical details vary among different branches of the family.
description: Al-Zar'a is a family name found among various tribal groups across the Arabian Peninsula. They are present in different Gulf states as part of the broader tribal fabric of the region. The family maintains traditional tribal customs while adapting to modern Gulf society. Their current status and specific regional concentrations vary across different GCC states.
history: According to the research provided, the Al-Zar'a family held governance over Al-Buraimi in the pre-Saudi era, representing one of the established ruling families in this strategic oasis region of northern Oman. The family controlled Al-Buraimi before the area came under Saudi influence, suggesting they were among the traditional local authorities in the Tawam region (as Al-Buraimi was historically known). During their rule, other families controlled different villages within the greater oasis - the Al-Surur family governed Hamasah, the Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and the Al-Azaznah tribe ruled Al Hailah village. The Al-Zar'a governance ended when the region fell under Al Saud influence, after which a complex period of political struggles ensued involving various local leaders. Interestingly, the name 'Zar'a' appears in another historical context in the research - Abd el-Krim, the Moroccan revolutionary leader, claimed in his memoirs to trace his ancestry to a patriarch named Zar'a who originated from Yanbu in the Hejaz and belonged to an Arab tribe called Ouled Si Mohammed ben Abd el-Krim. This Zar'a allegedly immigrated to Morocco's Rif region and settled among the Beni Ouriaghel in the 10th century, though the connection between these two Zar'a references is unclear from the available evidence.

timelineEvents.title: Al-Zar'a Rule in Al-Buraimi
timelineEvents.description: Al-Zar'a family governed Al-Buraimi oasis in the pre-Saudi era, controlling this strategic location before Saudi influence expanded into the region

name: Alhamamada
history: The Alhamamada are a Palestinian branch of the larger Mahamid tribe, an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to their tribal biography, when population growth led to overcrowding in their original territories of Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, the tribe gathered to decide their future. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were fellow Harb clans), they chose migration. The tribe moved northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then continued toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they settled in large numbers. In Karak, they established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily ruled by the Al-Amru clan, which lasted for an extended period. However, historical accounts suggest their rule was harsh, leading to united resistance from other clans in the region. From their Levantine settlements, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the region, with the Alhamamada specifically establishing themselves in Palestine as one of several Palestinian branches including the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, and others.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration from overcrowded original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region northward to Dumat Al-Jandal
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Expansion and branching from Levantine settlements into Palestine, forming the Alhamamada branch

name: Alhasasana
legitimacyNotes: descended from Qahtan through recognized genealogical lines
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Hassan, a descendant of the Qahtani tribal confederation. They are considered part of the broader southern Arabian tribal network.
description: Al-Hasasana is a Qahtani tribal branch found primarily in the Arabian Peninsula, with presence in various Gulf regions. They maintain traditional Bedouin customs and are part of the larger network of southern Arabian tribes. The tribe has historically been involved in pastoral activities and seasonal migrations across tribal territories. They remain active as a recognized tribal entity within the contemporary Gulf social structure.

relations.context: part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation

history: According to the research, Alhasasana is documented as a branch that emerged from the Mahamid tribe in Palestine. The Mahamid themselves trace their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation, with their ancestral homeland in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. The Mahamid underwent extensive migrations due to territorial constraints - when their population grew too large for their original areas in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, tribal councils decided to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans. Their migration path took them first to Dumat Al-Jandal in northern Arabia, then toward the Levant, eventually settling in the Karak region of Jordan where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. This emirate lasted for an extended period before various clans united against their rule. From their Levantine settlements, the Mahamid dispersed across multiple regions, with various branches including Alhasasana documented specifically in Palestine alongside other sub-tribes such as Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, and others. The research indicates the tribe maintained connections across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant, with branches also documented in Syria, Iraq, and North Africa.

migrationPath.description: Migration from ancestral homeland due to population growth and territorial constraints
Movement toward the Levant and settlement in Karak where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Dispersal and branching into various sub-tribes including Alhasasana in Palestine

name: Aljeayisa
description: Al-Jeayisa is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They appear to maintain their tribal identity in contemporary times as part of the broader tribal structure of the Gulf region.
history: The Aljeayisa are documented as a branch of the Mahamid Arab tribe, according to Palestinian tribal genealogies. The Mahamid trace their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb and originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. When their population grew and their original territories became insufficient, the Mahamid tribe made the collective decision to migrate northward, ultimately reaching Palestine where they established multiple branches including the Aljeayisa. The research indicates that Aljeayisa developed as one of several Palestinian branches of the Mahamid, alongside other groups such as the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, and others. However, the specific details of when the Aljeayisa branch was established, their particular territorial holdings within Palestine, or their subsequent migrations to other regions of the Arabian Peninsula are not documented in the available sources.

name: Alkhudur
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and genealogical recognition
description: Al-Khudur is a tribal branch found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintains traditional tribal structures and genealogical connections. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to trace their specific origins or major historical roles. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern era.
history: Alkhudur is documented as a branch of the Mahamid tribe in Palestine, according to genealogical records. The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with their ancestral homeland in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. The tribe's historical narrative describes a period when their population grew beyond what their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz could sustain. After tribal consultation, they rejected conquest of neighboring tribes (many of whom were related Harb clans) and instead chose migration. The Mahamid undertook a significant northward migration, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually established themselves in the Karak region of Jordan, where they became numerous enough to form alliances with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. Historical sources suggest their rule was eventually challenged due to harsh treatment of subject populations. The Alkhudur branch specifically emerged as part of the Mahamid's spread into Palestine, representing one of multiple Palestinian branches that include kiawan, alsawalima, alhamamada, and others.

name: Almadhakir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and local recognition
description: Al-Madhakir is a tribal name found in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though specific historical details about their origins, territories, and activities remain unclear in available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated over time. Their current status and geographical distribution require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The Almadhakir are documented as one of the branches of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. According to tribal records, when the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original territories in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, tribal leaders gathered to decide their course of action. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were related clans from the Harb tribe), they chose migration. Some branches of the tribe moved north to Dumat Al-Jandal before continuing toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid. Today, the Almadhakir branch is documented as being present in Saudi Arabia as part of the broader Mahamid tribal structure, which also includes the branches alrtuei, almatiei, almuhabida, and alrathea.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories when tribal lands became too narrow
Continued migration toward the Levant and settlement in Karak

name: Almatiei
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and local consensus
description: Al Matiei is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins and traditional territories. They maintain their tribal identity and social structure within the contemporary Gulf context. Further genealogical research would be needed to establish their detailed lineage connections and historical significance.
history: The Almatiei are documented as one of the main branches of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), which traces its lineage to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb tribal confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. According to tribal records, when the Mahamid grew large and their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient, the tribe deliberated on their options. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring Harb clans, they chose migration, with those united by the fifth grandfather making this collective decision. The tribe's migration led them northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan's leadership. This emirate lasted for an extended period, though historical sources suggest their rule was harsh, eventually leading to unified resistance from other regional clans. Today, the Almatiei remain one of the recognized branches of the Mahamid in Saudi Arabia, maintaining their distinct tribal identity within the broader Mahamid confederation that has spread across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, and beyond.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region northward to Dumat Al-Jandal due to territorial constraints
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid

name: Almuhabida
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and consensus
description: Al Muhabida is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula, traditionally engaged in pastoralism and desert life. They maintain traditional tribal structures and customs typical of Arabian Bedouin communities. The tribe has presence in various parts of the Gulf region, though their specific historical prominence and territorial control remains limited in documented sources. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity within the broader Arabian tribal system.
history: Al Muhabida is a branch of the Mahamid tribe, an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When the tribe grew large and their original territories became insufficient, tribal councils were held to determine their future. Rather than conquering neighboring Harb sub-clans, they chose migration as their path forward. The tribe's documented migration took them northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant where they settled in the Karak region. There they established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily led by the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period before facing opposition from other clans due to their harsh governance. In modern times, Al Muhabida represents one of five primary branches of the Mahamid tribe found in Saudi Arabia, alongside alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir, and alrathea. The broader Mahamid tribal network extends throughout the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and North Africa, with various sub-branches and alliances formed across these regions.

migrationPath.description: Migration northward when original territories became insufficient for expanding tribe
Continued migration toward the Levant and settlement in Karak region

name: Almusaeada
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established lineage and consensus
description: Al Musaeada is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their precise origins or major historical role. They appear to maintain traditional tribal structures and identity. Their current status and specific geographic concentration within the GCC region requires further verification.
history: The Almusaeada are a branch of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to tribal biography, when the tribe grew large and their areas of residence in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to decide their course of action. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were fellow Harb clans), they chose to migrate. The Mahamid subsequently moved north to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant, eventually settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid. The Almusaeada emerged as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid, representing part of this tribe's extensive spread across the Arab world. The research indicates their connection to this larger tribal confederation but provides limited specific details about the Almusaeada branch's particular history or current status.

name: Alrathea
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
description: Al-Rathea is a tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe appears to have had connections to the broader tribal networks of the region but lacks substantial historical records regarding their specific territories, alliances, or significant historical events. Their current status and activities remain unclear from available historical sources.
history: The Alrathea are documented as one of the branches of the larger Mahamid tribal confederation. The Mahamid trace their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with their ancestral homeland in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. According to tribal accounts, when the Mahamid confederation grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, the tribal council deliberated on their options. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring Harb clans and sub-clans, they chose migration, with those united by a common fifth grandfather departing together. The main Mahamid migration led northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then continued toward the Levant, ultimately reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. This emirate lasted for an extended period, though historical accounts suggest their rule was harsh, eventually leading to rebellion by subject clans. Today, the Alrathea branch is found primarily in Saudi Arabia as part of the broader Mahamid tribal network that has spread across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and North Africa.

migrationPath.description: Northern migration of Mahamid confederation including Alrathea branch due to territorial constraints
Continued migration toward the Levant where Mahamid established their emirate

name: Alrtuei
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Rtuei is a tribal group historically present in the eastern Arabian Peninsula region. They are part of the broader tribal fabric of the Gulf states, maintaining traditional tribal structures and customs. Like many Arabian tribes, they have adapted to modern state systems while preserving their tribal identity. Their current status and specific historical role in Gulf politics requires further documentation.
history: The Alrtuei are documented as a tribal branch of the Mahamid (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its lineage to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. According to historical sources, the Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When their population grew and their original territories became insufficient, the tribe made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans and sub-clans. The Alrtuei represent one of the primary branches of the Mahamid that remained in the Arabian Peninsula, specifically documented as being present in Saudi Arabia. While their ancestral tribe's main migration led northward through Dumat Al-Jandal toward the Levant, where they established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region, the Alrtuei branch appears to have maintained their presence in the Gulf region. The broader Mahamid tribal network established various branches across multiple countries, with the Alrtuei being one of six major branches in Saudi Arabia alongside almatiei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea. Their tribal identity remains connected to the larger Mahamid confederation while maintaining their distinct regional presence in the eastern Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region due to population growth and territorial constraints

name: Alsawalima
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical settlement patterns
ancestorStory: The Alsawalima trace their lineage to an ancestor named Salem. They are considered part of the broader tribal network in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Alsawalima is a tribal branch found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are known for their pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has historical presence in the region with connections to other tribal groups. Their current status involves maintaining tribal identity while adapting to modern Gulf state structures.
history: The Alsawalima are documented as a branch that emerged from the Mahamid tribe in Palestine. The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with their majority originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to tribal records, when the Mahamid tribe grew too large for their original areas in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz, they made collective decisions about expansion, ultimately choosing migration over conquest due to their kinship ties with neighboring Harb tribe clans. The Mahamid followed an extensive migration pattern, moving from their original homeland to Dumat Al-Jandal in the north, then toward the Levant, eventually reaching and settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. From their settlements in the Levant, particularly in Palestine, various branches emerged including the Alsawalima, along with other branches such as Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alhamamada, Almusaeada, Alhasasana, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad. The specific circumstances of the Alsawalima's emergence as a distinct branch and their subsequent migrations to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula are not detailed in available records.

Secondary migration of tribal segments into northern Oman and southeastern Arabian Peninsula

name: `Amarat
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Al Amarat is a tribal name found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. The name literally means 'the buildings' or 'the constructions' in Arabic, suggesting possible historical association with settlement or construction activities. Due to the common nature of this tribal designation, specific historical documentation and current status vary by location and particular branch.
history: The 'Amarat represent a northern branch of the great Anizah tribe, one of the most prominent tribal confederations in Arabian Peninsula history. The Anizah, to which 'Amarat belongs, traces its genealogical origins to the Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Historical records show that Anizah existed as an autonomous tribal group predating the rise of Islam in the seventh century. According to British intelligence officer Gerard Leachman's early 20th century accounts, the 'Amarat branch was centered in the deserts of Iraq, where they operated as semi-nomadic Bedouins. Leachman, who served as a political officer in Mesopotamia during World War I, had close relationships with Fahad Bey Ibn Haddal, chief of the Amarat Bedouins. The 'Amarat fought alongside the Muntafiq Bedouin federation during this period. Like other Anizah branches, the 'Amarat were affected by the establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century, which severely impacted traditional Bedouin lifestyle patterns that had involved movement across vast areas spanning multiple modern states. The tribe's presence in Iraq during the early 1900s suggests they were part of the broader Anizah expansion northward into Mesopotamia and the Levant that occurred during the Ottoman era.

name: al-'Amasib
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based authority through descent from Qahtan
ancestorStory: Al-'Amasib trace their lineage to an ancestor named Masib, who was part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabia.
description: Al-'Amasib is a Qahtani tribal branch historically based in the Arabian Peninsula with presence in various Gulf regions. They are part of the broader network of southern Arabian tribes that maintained traditional pastoral economies. The tribe has maintained its identity through genealogical connections and continues to exist as an active tribal group. Their historical significance lies primarily in their role within the larger Qahtani tribal confederation system.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins

history: The al-'Amasib appear in historical records as one of the riverine clans of the Manasir tribe of Northern Sudan, specifically listed among the Manasir al-Nil (riverine Manasir) who inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in an area called Dar al-Manasir. According to Manasir genealogical traditions, they trace their lineage to multiple possible origins: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the southern Arabian Qahtaniin, while others trace their ancestry to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or to descendants of King Sabir through the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab. Modern scholarship suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-'Amasib, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted a common genealogical framework for mutual recognition and resource claims. As riverine people, al-'Amasib would have pursued small-scale agriculture along the Nile, particularly date cultivation, while maintaining the traditional tribal social structures that characterize Sudanese riverine communities. The connection between this Sudanese al-'Amasib clan and the Arabian Peninsula al-'Amasib mentioned in the existing data cannot be established from the available research, though both groups claim Qahtani origins.

Settlement expansion into Omani mountain valleys and interior regions

name: 'Amayer
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established lineage and customary authority
description: The 'Amayer is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula, though specific historical documentation about their origins and movements is limited. They appear to have traditional pastoral economic activities typical of Arabian tribes. Their current status and specific territorial associations within the GCC region require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The 'Amayer are one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. According to historical sources, the 'Amayer were among the principal subdivisions of Bani Khalid alongside the Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood clans. The Bani Khalid confederation controlled vast territories extending from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south at their greatest extent, ruling over Al-Hasa, Al-Qatif, and wielding political influence in Najd. Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid clan (the traditional leaders of Bani Khalid), the confederation expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabian cities in 1670 and established the Bani Khalid Emirate with its capital at Al-Mubarraz. The 'Amayer, as part of this confederation, would have participated in the pastoral and political activities that characterized Bani Khalid rule over the region's deserts and oases. The confederation's power declined following defeats by the First Saudi State in 1793, when the Al-'Ura'yir rulers were deposed from Al-Hasa. Despite brief restoration under Ottoman and Egyptian protection in the early 19th century, Bani Khalid influence permanently ended after battles with tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830.

Northern migration to Kuwait region during Ottoman expansion

name: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation based on genealogical descent from Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya
ancestorName: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was a prominent leader of the Hawazin tribal group in pre-Islamic Arabia. His descendants formed one of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was one of the largest tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, descended from the Hawazin group. The confederation included numerous sub-tribes and was known for their pastoral nomadic lifestyle across Najd and surrounding regions. They played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal warfare and early Islamic conquests. Many modern tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula, including the GCC region, trace their lineage back to various branches of this ancient confederation.

relations.context: Some branches of Al Muntafiq claim descent from Amir ibn Sa'sa'a
Genealogical connections through Adnani lineage

history: The Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was one of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, descended from the Hawazin group and tracing their lineage to Adnan. Their original homeland was the border area between Najd and Hejaz in Khurmah and Ranyah. The confederation consisted of several major branches: Banu Kilab (descendants of Kilab ibn Rabi'ah ibn 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a), who were Bedouins living in western Nejd and led the confederation prior to Islam; Banu Numayr, who lived on the western borders of al-Yamamah; and Banu Ka'b, the largest section divided into Banu Uqayl, Banu Ja'dah, Banu Qushayr, and Al Harish. The tribe engaged in the Fijar War against the Quraysh before Islam and gave late allegiance to Muhammad. During Muhammad's era, the tribe was involved in military conflicts, including the incident where Abu Bara guaranteed safety for Muslim missionaries to Najd, leading to the tragic Bi'r Ma'una massacre. The confederation produced notable poets including Labid ibn Rabi'ah (author of one of the Seven Hanged Poems) and the protagonists of Layla wal Majnun saga. After Islam's rise, various branches migrated extensively: Banu Kilab later moved north to Syria and established the Mirdasid dynasty, Banu Numayr left for Iraq after 9th-century Abbasid campaigns, Banu Uqayl established the Uqaylid dynasty in Mosul and later returned to Arabia to found the Usfurid and Jabrid dynasties in Bahrain province. The Bani Yas of the UAE and Bani Amer of UAE/Oman both trace their descent to Yas bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah and Amer bin Rabia bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah respectively. Modern tribes including Subay', Suhool in Nejd, and sections of Bani Khalid also trace their lineage to this ancient confederation, demonstrating the enduring influence of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a across the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Anaza
legitimacyNotes: One of the largest and most influential Bedouin confederations, tracing noble Adnani lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Anaza ibn Asad, tracing back to the ancient Rabi'a tribal group. The confederation formed through alliances of related clans sharing this ancestral connection.
description: Anaza is one of the largest Bedouin confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, historically ranging across northern and central Arabia. They are renowned for their camel breeding and played significant roles in Arabian politics and warfare. The confederation includes numerous sub-tribes and branches that migrated across the region. Today, Anaza descendants are found throughout the GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia, where they maintain their tribal identity and continue to be influential in regional affairs.

relations.context: Historical alliances and intermarriage with Saudi ruling family
Traditional Bedouin territorial and grazing disputes
Cooperation in nomadic migrations and trading routes

history: The Anaza (Anizah) confederation represents one of the most significant Bedouin tribal movements in Arabian history, with documented existence predating Islam in the 7th century. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Originally, two main branches existed: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary clans in the wadis of Al-Yamama district in eastern Najd. The Anizah participated in the Battle of Siffin supporting Ali ibn Abi Talib, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the tribe settled in Khaybar at the end of the 10th century AD, expelling the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region. During the Ottoman era, Anizah became masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while migrating seasonally to southern Syria where they collected khuwwa tribute from Hawran region farmers. The first Anizah subtribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. The Wuld Ali entered Hawran simultaneously, followed by the Ruwallah in the early 19th century, while the Fad'an and Sba'a settled in central Syrian plains. The Ruwallah branch became so powerful they practically developed into an independent tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe. In Nejd, 19th-century chronicles record Anizah's penetration into northern and western regions, with Ibn La'bun documenting settlement of several families in the 14th century CE. The 15th century saw major Anizah migration into Al-Qassim region, and by the early 18th century, Anizah Bedouins reached Riyadh's gates, killing ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. Under Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal's leadership, Anizah unified against tribal enemies at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely disrupted the Bedouin lifestyle, forcing most Anizah members to settle within new nation-states and adopt Saudi, Kuwaiti, Iraqi, Lebanese, Syrian, or Jordanian citizenship while retaining tribal appellations like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili.

description: Anazzah is one of the largest and most prominent Bedouin tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, historically ranging across northern Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Syria, and Iraq. Known for their extensive camel herding and warrior traditions, they played a significant role in Arabian politics and were instrumental in various historical conflicts. The confederation includes multiple major branches and maintains strong tribal identity across GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait. They remain active today with members integrated into modern Gulf societies while preserving tribal customs.
