name: Chegini
history: The Chegini (چەگینی/چگنی) are a mostly sedentary Kurdish tribe residing primarily in Iran's Lorestan province, with scattered populations in Qazvin and Fars provinces, as well as in Iraq's Kurdistan Region and Kirkuk. Charles MacGregor classified them as Kurdish in his 1872 topographical work, though the Encyclopædia Iranica notes that some non-Kurdish tribes in Iran share the same name. The tribe's origins remain obscure, having migrated to Lorestan from Northwestern Iran. During Tahmasp I's reign, the Chegini Kurds settled in Khorasan while traveling to India, where they prospered under royal protection. By 1597, Sharafkhan Bidlisi documented that the Chegini lived by brigandage and highway robbery, notably lacking any emir or mirza to lead them—unusual among Kurdish tribes. In early 20th century Lorestan, the Chegini played a significant role in tribal resistance against Reza Shah's conquest. In late May 1924, they joined a unified tribal alliance with the Beiranvand, Papi, Baharvand, and half the Judaki tribe to attack Khorramabad, besieging the city for 38 days. The Chegini notably defeated General Shah Bakhti's forces, killing around 120 of his men and forcing his retreat. However, by June 1924, disputes over captured weapons and provisions led to the alliance's collapse, with the Chegini withdrawing from the rebellion along with other tribes. Today, the Chegini are among the Kurdish Lak subtribes populating northern Lorestan, particularly in Delfan, Dowreh, and Selseleh counties, where Kurds constitute over 65% of the regional population.

migrationPath.description: Migration during Tahmasp I's reign while traveling to India

name: Dahaminah
description: The Dahaminah are a minor tribe from Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the United Arab Emirates (UAE). They originally inhabited the area of Wadi Qor and at the turn of the 19th century had a population of approximately 150 members who co-habited with members of the Manai'i tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor.

relations.context: Co-habited in settlements along Wadi Qor at turn of 19th century

history: The Dahaminah are a minor tribe from Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the United Arab Emirates, with their traditional homeland centered in the mountainous region of Wadi Qor in the Hajar Mountains. This wadi, running from the mountain village of Al Qor near Hatta through villages including Huwaylat, Rafaq and Al Nasla before crossing into Oman, formed one of the oldest caravan routes from the interior of the Gulf to the Batinah Coast. At the turn of the 19th century, the Dahaminah had a population of approximately 150 members who co-habited with members of the Manai'i tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor. The tribe also occupied areas around Wadi Al Munay, the main tributary of Wadi Al Qor, alongside other tribal groups including the Bani Kaab, Maharzah, and Quwayd. The villages throughout these wadis were traditionally settled by the Dahaminah along with the Washahat and other tribes, taking advantage of the aflaj daudi (underground waterways) and falaj systems that distributed water for irrigation. Like many mountain communities in the region, Dahaminah settlements have experienced depopulation as younger generations migrate to the UAE's cities, though seasonal occupation continues during celebrations like Eid when family members return to the ancestral villages.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in the mountain valleys of the Hajar range as part of broader tribal movements into the Trucial States region

timelineEvents.title: Dahaminah Population Census
timelineEvents.description: At the turn of the 19th century, the Dahaminah population was recorded at approximately 150 members co-habiting with Manai'i tribe members in Wadi Qor settlements

name: Dar Alearabi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Al-Arabi, part of the broader tribal migrations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Dar Al-Arabi is a tribal branch with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's name suggests Arab lineage and traditional nomadic pastoral lifestyle. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific activities or territorial control. Their current status appears to be largely historical with possible integration into larger tribal confederations.
history: Dar Alearabi is identified in the research as a Palestinian branch of the larger Mahamid tribe. The Mahamid are an Arab tribe tracing their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. According to tribal biography, when their population grew and their original territories became constrained, the tribe gathered to decide their future. Rejecting conquest of neighboring tribes (many being fellow Harb clans), they chose migration instead. The tribe moved northward, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they established significant presence, becoming numerous enough to outnumber local tribes and forming alliances with Karak clans, particularly Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. The Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid, ruled primarily by the Al-Amru clan for an extended period, though historical sources suggest their rule was harsh, eventually leading to unified opposition from other clans. From their Levantine base, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the region, with Dar Alearabi becoming one of the Palestinian branches alongside other groups like Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, and others.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration from overcrowded tribal territories in Yemen and Hijaz northward to Dumat Al-Jandal
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Expansion and branching into Palestine, forming Dar Alearabi and other Palestinian branches

name: Daramikah
description: The Daramikah are an Arabian tribe and a branch of the widespread Dhawahir of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman. They have given their name to a small watchtower located in the Qattara Oasis in Al Ain, traditionally held by the semi-nomadic Dhawahir. The tower protected the falaj irrigation system that gave life to the oasis. In the latter part of the 19th century, the Al bu Falah of Abu Dhabi bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir, who continued to cultivate the land in various oases.

relations.context: Daramikah are a branch of the widespread Dhawahir
Al bu Falah bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir in the latter part of the 19th century

history: The Daramikah (Arabic: درامكة, singular Darmaki) are an Arabian tribe and a branch of the widespread Dhawahir of the United Arab Emirates and Oman. The tribe's early history suggests they originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah, in what is today Oman, before settling in the Buraimi Oasis region. By the turn of the 20th century, the Dhawahir tribe, numbering 4,500, consisted of three subsections: the Daramikah, who populated Hili, Qattara, and Mutared; the Jawabir in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi. The Daramikah were semi-nomadic, staying in the villages for the summer date season and moving throughout the Trucial States in winter, keeping large flocks of sheep, herding camels and trading in charcoal. They owned much of the cultivated land and property in the Al Ain oasis, with the exception of Buraimi village itself. The Daramikah have given their name to a small watchtower located in the Qattara Oasis in Al Ain, which protected the falaj irrigation system that gave life to the oasis. Archaeological finds at Qattara include Wadi Suq era chlorite jugs and bowls, late Bronze Age short swords and daggers, and a notable Bronze Age pendant made from electrum depicting entwined horned animals. In the latter part of the 19th century, Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') increased his hold on Al Ain by buying date groves and water rights, predominantly from the Dhawahir. When the Dhawahir rebelled against this policy in 1877, Zayed went to war with them. After a month-long conflict, Zayed prevailed and took two Dhawahir Sheikhs hostage. In 1891, he marched on the oasis again and took the Dhawahir's main settlement. The Al bu Falah of Abu Dhabi subsequently bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir, who continued to cultivate the land in various oases including Al Ain, Jimi, Hilli, Qattarah and Mu'tirid. Today, the Daramikah maintain their identity as part of the larger Dhawahir confederation, which has long had a strong alliance with the ruling family of Abu Dhabi, the Al Nahyan, and the Bani Yas confederation.

migrationPath.description: Early migration of Dhawahir tribe from Al Dhahirah in Oman to settle in the Buraimi Oasis region, with Daramikah specifically settling in Hili, Qattara, and Mutared areas

name: Darawishah
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal structure with established lineage claims
description: The Darawishah are a tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their precise origins, traditional territories, or major historical role. They appear to maintain tribal identity in contemporary times though their specific influence and activities in GCC regional affairs remain unclear from available sources.
history: The Darawishah are a subsection of the Al Bu Shamis division of the Na'im tribe, a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. By the early 20th century, the Darawishah had established themselves as the dominant population in several coastal settlements along the Trucial Coast. In the small port of Hamriyah, a dependency of Sharjah, the headman and around 250 of its 300 houses were members of the Darawishah subsection. This community also dominated Hirah (Al Heera), another Sharjah dependency, with approximately 250 Na'im households. In Sharjah town itself, the Darawishah formed a significant portion of the settled population, including 100 Darawishah houses. The Al Bu Shamis, including the Darawishah, had become virtually independent from the main Na'im tribal authority and were closely associated with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, though their sheikhs periodically affirmed continued recognition of the tamimah (supreme authority) of the Na'im as a whole. Notable Darawishah leaders included the Al Shamsi family, who ruled Al Heera and Hamriyah. Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi was a particularly influential figure in early 20th century Trucial Coast politics, ruling Al Heera until his death in 1942. The Darawishah communities were involved in the pearling industry and maintained their tribal identity while adapting to settled coastal life. Modern members of this subsection are associated with the Emirati family name Alshamsi.

name: Daws
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with well-documented pre-Islamic lineage and Islamic period prominence
ancestorName: Daws ibn Udathah ibn Zayd al-Lat
ancestorStory: Daws traces its lineage to Daws ibn Udathah, a descendant of Zayd al-Lat from the Azd tribal confederation. The tribe was established in the mountainous regions of the Hejaz before the rise of Islam.
description: Daws was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Azd confederation, historically based in the mountainous regions between Mecca and Yemen. They are most famous for producing Abu Hurairah, one of the most prominent companions of Prophet Muhammad and narrator of hadith. The tribe converted to Islam during the Prophet's lifetime and played a role in early Islamic conquests. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history, their direct relevance to modern GCC tribal politics is limited as they were primarily based in the Hejaz region.

relations.context: Both trace ancestry to Qahtani Arab origins through Azd confederation

history: The Daws (Banu Daws) was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Azd confederation, historically located in the mountainous regions south of Mecca in the Tihama region. According to genealogical sources, Daws traces its lineage to Daws ibn Udathah, a descendant of Zayd al-Lat from the Azd tribal confederation. The tribe was established in the mountainous regions of the Hejaz before the rise of Islam and is noted as being a branch of the Zahran tribe. The Daws are most famous for producing two prominent companions of Prophet Muhammad: Tufayl ibn Amr, who served as their chief and was an early convert to Islam around four years before the hijra in 622 CE, and Abu Hurairah, one of the most significant narrators of hadith in Islamic tradition. Tufayl ibn Amr's conversion story is particularly noteworthy - when he traveled to Mecca for Hajj, he was initially warned by the Meccans not to approach Muhammad, with some claiming Muhammad was a magician. Tufayl was so concerned that he placed cotton wool in his ears to avoid hearing Muhammad. However, during his circumambulation (tawaf), he encountered Muhammad reciting the Quran, and his curiosity overcame his fears. Reasoning that as the head of his tribe and an intelligent man, he should be able to resist any magical influence, he removed the cotton wool and listened to the Quran, ultimately converting to Islam. The tribe played a role in the early Islamic conquests, with Tufayl ibn Amr leading a contingent of his people against the impostor Musaylima during the Ridda wars. Tragically, Tufayl ibn Amr fell as a martyr in the Battle of Yamama in 633 CE. The tribe also features in Islamic eschatological prophecies, with Abu Hurairah narrating a hadith about the end times that references Dhu l-Khalasah, an idol that was worshipped by the Daws during the pre-Islamic period (Jahiliyyah). While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history and early Islamic development, their direct relevance to modern GCC tribal politics is limited as they were primarily based in the Hejaz region rather than the territories that would become the modern Gulf states.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Dawsi families led by Tufayl ibn Amr and Abu Hurairah to join Prophet Muhammad
Some Dawsi warriors settled in Basra during Islamic conquests of Iraq

name: Dhahuriyiin
description: The Dhahuriyiin appear to be a smaller tribal group or clan historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records suggest they maintained alliance relationships with other tribal groups, particularly the Habus. Their current status and specific geographic distribution within the GCC region remains unclear from available sources.

relations.context: Associated as fellow Hajar Mountain tribes with shared cultural similarities

history: The Dhahuriyiin are a tribal group historically associated with the Hajar Mountain region of the northern UAE, particularly around Ras Al Khaimah. They are closely linked with the Shihuh and Habus tribes, sharing cultural similarities and traditions with these neighboring mountain tribes. The Dhahuriyiin appear to have been part of the broader tribal confederation that inhabited the rugged Hajar Mountains, where they likely maintained traditional mountain lifestyles. Like their associated tribes the Shihuh and Habus, they would have been involved in the complex territorial arrangements of the region, where tribal boundaries were defined by wadis and mountain ranges. The Dhahuriyiin's connection to the Habus suggests they may have shared similar cultural practices and possibly the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic that has Himyarite Yemeni origins dating back to the second century BCE, though this cannot be definitively confirmed from available sources.

name: Dhawahir
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with historical continuity in the Al Ain/Buraimi region, not a political confederation
traditionalEconomy: date cultivation, sheep herding, camel breeding, charcoal trading
description: The Dhawahir is a prominent tribe of the United Arab Emirates with their main centre at the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain. They have historically maintained a strong alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation. At the turn of the 20th century, they owned much of the cultivated land in Al Ain oasis (except Buraimi village itself) and numbered around 4,500 people. The tribe was traditionally involved in date cultivation, sheep herding, camel breeding, and charcoal trading. Their fertile woodlands, irrigated by an effective falaj system, supported their wood-burning industry.

The tower protected the falaj irrigation system that gave life to the oasis. In the latter part of the 19th century, the Al bu Falah of Abu Dhabi bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir, who continued to cultivate the land in various oases.

history:
The Dhawahir is a prominent tribe of the United Arab Emirates whose traditional area of influence centered on the Oasis of Al Ain and Buraimi. Early tribal history suggests they originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah, today in Oman, before settling in the Buraimi region. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 4,500 people and owned much of the cultivated land in Al Ain oasis (excluding Buraimi village itself). The tribe consisted of three main subsections: the Daramikah, who populated Hili and Qattara; the Jawabir in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi. Traditionally, they stayed in villages during the summer date season and moved throughout the Trucial States in winter, maintaining large flocks of sheep, herding camels, and trading in charcoal from their wood-burning industry supported by the oasis's falaj irrigation system.

The Dhawahir developed a strong and longstanding alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation, which was relatively unusual among interior tribes who typically asserted independence. However, this relationship faced significant strain under Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great'), who systematically purchased date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir, changing ownership patterns throughout the oasis. This policy led to rebellion in 1877, resulting in a month-long conflict where Zayed prevailed and took two Dhawahir sheikhs hostage. In 1891, Zayed again marched on the oasis with Dubai support, capturing the main Dhawahir settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now Al Ain) with 30 horsemen and 300 camel riders. He built a fort to establish dominion and appointed Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hilal Al Dhahiri as his loyal headman. The tribe maintained an often uneasy relationship with the Na'im, who represented a later wave of settlers in Buraimi and were frequently in dispute with the Dhawahir over territorial control.

migrationPath.description: Original migration from Al Dhahirah area in present-day Oman to the Buraimi Oasis region

name: Dhu Asbah
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Himyarite tribal legitimacy through South Arabian genealogical traditions
ancestorStory: Descended from Asbah ibn Dhu Raydan, a branch of the ancient Himyarite kings of Yemen. The tribe traces its origins to the pre-Islamic Himyarite kingdom in southern Arabia.
description: Dhu Asbah was an ancient South Arabian tribe belonging to the Himyarite confederation that ruled much of Yemen before Islam. They were part of the broader Dhu Raydan tribal complex associated with the Himyarite royal house. The tribe was primarily based in the highlands of Yemen and played a role in the political structure of pre-Islamic South Arabia. Like many ancient Yemeni tribes, their direct lineage became absorbed into later tribal formations following the Islamic conquests and subsequent migrations.

relations.context: Both trace descent to Qahtani South Arabian origins

history: Dhu Asbah was one of the two principal aristocratic families of the ancient Himyarite Kingdom in Yemen, alongside Dhu'l-Kala. The family traced its lineage to the Himyarite confederation that ruled much of Yemen before Islam, being part of the broader Dhu Raydan tribal complex associated with the Himyarite royal house. Following the Islamic conquests of the 630s, the Dhu Asbah played a prominent role in early Islamic Syria. Their chief Kurayb ibn Abraha Abu Rishdin led the Himyar of Homs after the Muslim conquest of Syria, but later moved to Egypt with most of the Dhu Asbah family members. Notable family members Abraha ibn Sabbah and his son Abu Shamir had participated in the Muslim conquest of Egypt in 640-641. During the First Muslim Civil War (656-661), the Himyarites including Dhu Asbah supported Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan against Caliph Ali. Their influence began to wane with their defeat at the Battle of Marj Rahit against the Quda'a confederation and Umayyad caliph Marwan I in 684, and practically diminished with the death of their allied leader at the Battle of Khazir in 686. Despite this decline, members of Dhu Asbah continued to play important roles throughout Umayyad rule (661-750) as governors, commanders, scholars, and pietists. During the Third Muslim Civil War, Dhu Asbah tribesmen who had remained in South Arabia supported the Kharijite leader Abu Hamza. A possible family member in Syria, Nadr ibn Yarim, led a military expedition against the Byzantines under the early Abbasid caliph al-Saffah (750-754). Kurayb's cousin Ayyub ibn Shurahbil ibn Sabbah later served as governor of Egypt under Caliph Umar II (717-720).

name: dhū-Raydān
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian royal dynasty that ruled parts of Yemen and southern Arabia
ancestorStory: Dhū-Raydān derives its name from Raydan, referring to the royal palace or capital of the Himyarite kings. The dynasty claimed descent from ancient South Arabian nobility.
description: Dhū-Raydān was an ancient South Arabian royal dynasty associated with the Himyarite kingdom that controlled much of Yemen and southern Arabia. They ruled from their capital at Zafar and later Sanaa, controlling important trade routes between the Indian Ocean and the Mediterranean. The dynasty is known from numerous South Arabian inscriptions where rulers styled themselves as 'King of Saba, Dhū-Raydān, Hadramawt and Yamanat.' The dynasty declined in the 6th century CE with the rise of Islamic conquests and has no direct connection to modern GCC tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace descent to ancient Qahtani lineages of South Arabia

history: Dhū-Raydān was an ancient South Arabian royal dynasty that emerged from the Himyarite Kingdom, controlling much of Yemen and southern Arabia from approximately 110 BCE to the 6th century CE. The dynasty derived its name from Raydan, referring to the royal palace and capital at Zafar (near modern Sanaa), from which Himyarite kings ruled. After establishing independence from the Qatabanian kingdom in 110 BCE, the Himyarite rulers styled themselves as 'King of Saba, Dhū-Raydān, Hadramawt and Yamanat,' reflecting their control over unified South Arabian territories. The kingdom was a major hub of international trade, linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, and India through frankincense and myrrh exports. Himyarite power eventually shifted to Sanaa in the fifth century as populations increased. The dynasty conquered neighboring kingdoms: Saba around 25 BCE (definitively by 280 CE), Qataban around 200 CE, and Hadhramaut around 300 CE. Originally polytheistic, the ruling class converted to Judaism under Malkikarib Yuhamin (c. 375-400 CE), with inscriptions referencing 'Rahmanan' (The Merciful) and Hebrew phrases. The Jewish period ended with the Aksumite invasion in 525-530 CE following persecutions by King Dhu Nuwas, leading to Christian rule under Abraha. The kingdom finally collapsed when conquered by the Sasanian Empire in 575-578 CE. After the Islamic conquests, Himyarite noble families like Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah played prominent roles in early Islamic Syria, leading South Arabian military contingents and settling in Homs, though their political influence waned after defeats in 684-686 CE.

name: Dhu'l-Kala
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Himyarite royal lineage with historical sovereignty in Yemen
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor who ruled parts of ancient Yemen and held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress'. They were among the noble houses of the Himyarite kingdom.
description: Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani tribe from the Himyarite nobility of Yemen, known for their royal lineage and territorial control in pre-Islamic Arabia. They were among the established ruling houses of South Arabia before Islam and participated in the early Islamic conquests. The tribe's influence was primarily centered in Yemen rather than the modern GCC states. Their historical significance lies in their role as one of the ancient Arab royal families that bridged the pre-Islamic and Islamic periods.

relations.context: Both descend from Qahtani lineage

history: Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani tribe of the Himyarite nobility from Yemen, named after their ancestor who held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress'. During the early 7th century, members of this illustrious family participated in the Muslim conquest of Syria under the Rashidun Caliphate. The most prominent figure was Dhu al-Kala Samayfa ibn Nakur al-Himyari, who led the Dhu al-Kala family as the most powerful clan in Yemen during the time of Prophet Muhammad and Caliph Abu Bakr (632-634). Samayfa participated in the conquest of Syria in the 630s and, like many other Yemenite tribesmen, settled in Homs after its capture in 637, where he became one of the two principal leaders of the Himyar tribe alongside Dhu al-Asbah Kurayb ibn Abraha. Samayfa was referred to by early Muslim sources as the 'king of Himyar' and originally intended to settle in Damascus to establish Himyarite dominance over Syria, though this conflicted with Qurayshite interests. During Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan's governorship of Syria in the 640s, Samayfa was known to act counter to Mu'awiya's orders in Homs, where the Yemenite troops followed his command rather than the official governor's. At the Battle of Siffin in 657 during the First Fitna, Samayfa commanded the Himyarite troops in Mu'awiya's army against Caliph Ali and was killed in combat on 27 July while leading his forces against the Rabi'a contingent. His death, along with that of Ubayd Allah ibn Umar, marked the beginning of Himyarite decline in Syria. Samayfa was succeeded by his son Shurahbil, who continued as a leading Umayyad commander until his death at the Battle of Khazir in 686, after which the Himyar in Syria 'sank to military insignificance' according to historian Wilferd Madelung.

migrationPath.description: Samayfa ibn Nakur and Dhu'l-Kala family participated in Muslim conquest of Syria, settling in Homs after its capture in 637

name: al-Diqeisab
description: Al-Diqeisab is a tribal name found in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about this specific tribal group is limited in widely available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they may represent a sub-lineage of a larger tribal confederation. Further research would be needed to establish their specific geographical distribution, historical role, and current status within the GCC region.
history: Al-Diqeisab (الدقيساب) is documented as one of the Riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in an area called Dar al-Manasir. According to Sudanese genealogical records, they are part of a larger tribal confederation that traces its origins to a historical ancestor named Mansur, with varying accounts placing him either as a descendant of al-'Abbas (the uncle of Muhammad), as Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), or as connected to other tribal lineages including the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Diqeisab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. The clan is classified among the riverine Manasir who pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop. As of 1993, the broader Manasir population was approximately 30,000-33,000 residents. The relationship, if any, between this Sudanese al-Diqeisab clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal groups of similar name remains undocumented in available sources.

name: Drisah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Al Drisah is a tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally associated with nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal identity in the modern era. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and major historical events. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in contemporary times.
history: The Drisah are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation operating in Oman and the United Arab Emirates, particularly in areas around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike other Bani Kaab subsections that had settled by the turn of the 20th century, the Drisah maintained their nomadic lifestyle along with the Shwaihiyin subsection, while the broader confederation numbered approximately 7,250 people with 1,150 remaining Bedouin. In 1844, the Bani Kaab confederation, including the Drisah, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. The alliance dynamics shifted over time, with the Bani Kaab later aligning with Saudi Arabia in the 1940s under Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah, who declared their territories belonged to the Saudis. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Drisah, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and engaged in combat against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

The text discusses 'Duru' as a geographical location - a ward and village in Tanzania - and 'Durrës' as an Albanian city. Neither relates to Arabian tribal lineages or Gulf tribal structures.

history: The Duru (Arabic: الدرعي singular Al Darai الدروع) is a relatively small but historically significant Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates. Despite their limited numbers, the Duru managed to involve themselves in numerous territorial conflicts throughout the Trucial States during the 20th century, particularly around the strategically important Buraimi and Liwa oases. The tribe maintained their traditional dar (territory) in these areas while engaging in complex alliances and rivalries with neighboring tribes. In 1877, the Duru joined forces with the Awamir tribe to conduct raids against Dubai, while northern Bani Qitab simultaneously raided Abu Dhabi, before peace was eventually brokered. Although the Duru considered themselves dependents of Dubai and maintained good relations with the Al Bu Shamis, they found themselves in persistent conflict with other tribes, most notably the warlike Awamir who encroached upon their territory, and the Rashid. During the 1920s, major fighting erupted between Bedouin tribes in the Liwa and Buraimi regions, with the Duru forming part of a southern confederation alongside the Awamir and Al Bu Shamis against the northern alliance of Manasir and Bani Yas of Dhafra. This conflict led to some Manasir and Mazari seeking protection from Ibn Jaluwi, the headman of Hasa, until Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan eventually brokered an uneasy truce. However, renewed fighting broke out in the Buraimi oasis between the Duru, Manasir and Bani Qitab, presenting an early challenge for the new Ruler of Abu Dhabi, Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan. In December 1923, Sultan marched against the Duru, prompting them to raise a force with the Awamir and Bani Qitab of Falayah that successfully defeated a party of 20 riders from Abu Dhabi, killing seven and capturing the rest. The escalating conflict saw both sides conducting retaliatory raids, with the Duru targeting Dubai in revenge for its support of Sultan's forces. Seeking external protection, the Duru, along with the Al Bu Shamis, turned to Ibn Jaluwi who was eager to extend Saudi influence among the Trucial States tribes. This decision contributed to the territorial arguments that would later form part of the Buraimi Dispute between Saudi Arabia and the Trucial States, as portions of various tribes, including elements influenced by Duru conflicts, became Saudi citizens while others remained under British protection.

name: Du'um
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and customary authority
description: Du'um is a tribal grouping found in the Arabian Peninsula, historically engaged in traditional pastoral activities. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and customs in the modern era. Limited historical documentation makes detailed reconstruction of their specific role in Gulf regional politics difficult to establish with certainty.
history: Du'um is identified as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid, including the Du'um branch, ruled southern Iraq, Kuwait, and Eastern Arabia (al-Hasa and al-Qatif) from the 15th century to the 18th century. The confederation's main branches included Al Humaid, Al Asfour, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces and established their emirate with Al-Mubarraz as capital. The Du'um, as part of this confederation, participated in the political dominance of eastern Arabia during this period. The Bani Khalid confederation, including Du'um, maintained staunch opposition to the rising Saudi-Wahhabi alliance in Najd. When the emir of Uyayna adopted Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's ideas, the Khalidi chief ordered cessation of support, leading to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's move to Dir'iyyah and alliance with Al Saud. The confederation's efforts to stop Saudi expansion through invasions of Najd and Diriyyah ultimately failed. The Saudis conquered the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa in 1793, ending their rule. After Ottoman restoration in 1818, the confederation was weakened by encroachment from Ajman, Dawasir, Subay', and Mutayr tribes, along with internal leadership disputes. Final defeats came in battles against Mutayri-'Ajmani alliance in 1823 and against Subay' and Saudis in 1830, ending Bani Khalid rule permanently.

name: Fad'an
legitimacyNotes: Major branch of Anizah confederation with traditional Bedouin leadership structure
ancestorStory: Descended from Fad'an ibn Rabi'ah of the Anizah tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the Adnani Arab genealogical tradition.
description: The Fad'an are a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, historically based in northern Arabia and the Syrian desert. They were among the most powerful Bedouin tribes during the 18th and 19th centuries, controlling extensive grazing territories and trade routes. The tribe is known for their camel herding traditions and warrior culture. Today, Fad'an members are found across Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Syria, and Iraq, with some branches having settled in Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliances during Saudi state formation
Traditional rivalry over grazing territories and tribal leadership in northern Arabia

history: The Fad'an are a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, with historical presence in northern Arabia and the Syrian desert. By the late 18th and early 19th centuries, the Fad'an emerged as a dominant force in the Syrian steppes, following earlier Anaza migrations to the region. The tribe gained particular prominence after other Anaza branches like the Hasana had already established themselves in Syria from 1757. In the early 19th century, the Fad'an and their subordinate tribe the Siba'a began challenging the established Hasana control over the plains east of Hama and Homs. In 1814, large numbers of Fad'an and Siba'a forces entered the plains east of the Orontes River, prompting the Hasana to ally with local Ottoman troops against them. Despite initial resistance lasting two weeks, the Fad'an eventually retreated but continued to return periodically to raid Hasana territories west of Hama. Following the Mawali tribe's devastating defeat in 1786 and their displacement by Anaza tribes, the Fad'an were among those granted the former Mawali rights to collect tolls from caravans traversing through Palmyra. The Hadidiyin tribe, enemies of the Mawali, were noted to have migrated to the Hama region from areas near Aleppo and the Euphrates valley in the mid-18th century specifically due to pressures from the advancing Fad'an. This demonstrates the Fad'an's significant territorial influence and military capacity during the Ottoman period, as they displaced established tribes and reshaped the tribal geography of central Syria.

migrationPath.description: Fad'an expansion into Syrian territories as part of broader Anaza tribal migrations, pressuring established tribes like the Hadidiyin

name: al-Farei'ab
history: Al-Farei'ab (الفريعاب) is a clan within the Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir), one of the Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. The clan is part of the larger Manasir people who inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in their homeland called Dar al-Manasir. According to Manasir oral tradition, they trace their origins back to an ancestor named Mansur, with multiple genealogical theories proposed: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others suggest descent from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, while another theory traces them to cousins from the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Farei'ab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. As a riverain clan, al-Farei'ab would have been involved in small-scale agriculture along the Nile, particularly date cultivation, and shared the material culture and Islamic practices common to the Manasir people. The clan was among those affected by the Merowe Dam construction which impacted all villages in Dar al-Manasir.

name: al-Ga'al
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through shaykhs and lineage-based authority
description: Al-Ga'al is an Arabian tribe with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally involved in pastoral activities and maintain tribal social structures. The tribe has branches that have settled in various parts of the Gulf region over time. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity with maintained lineage records and traditional leadership.
history: The al-Ga'al tribe represents one of the significant tribal entities within the broader Sudanese riverine Arab tribal complex, with documented presence as part of the Manasir confederation in Northern Sudan. According to documented tribal genealogies, al-Ga'al (الجعل) constitutes one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile. The tribe forms part of a complex amalgamation that developed over centuries through voluntary association between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab migrants, and other tribal minorities. Like other Manasir clans, al-Ga'al adopted Islam and Arabic as their primary language while maintaining their distinct tribal identity and territorial claims in what is known as Dar al-Manasir. The tribe's integration into the Manasir confederation provided them with established systems of mutual rights, obligations, and territorial claims against neighboring tribes, while maintaining their specific clan identity within the larger tribal structure.

name: Ga'ali
legitimacyNotes: branch of larger tribal confederation with traditional pastoral territories
description: The Ga'ali are a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula, particularly known for their traditional pastoral lifestyle. They maintained trade partnerships with other tribes including the Manasir, reflecting the interconnected nature of tribal economic networks in the region. Like many smaller tribal groups, they operated within the broader framework of Arabian tribal confederations and alliances.

relations.context: Some Manasir migrate to grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali Country during dry season.

history: The research provided focuses entirely on the Manasir tribe of Northern Sudan, not the Ga'ali tribe of the Arabian Peninsula. The Sudanese Manasir are described as a Sunni Arab riverine tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile, with their homeland called Dar al-Manasir. They trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or descendants of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and tribal minorities. The tribe is divided into Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah), with one riverain clan called al-Ga'al mentioned among their subdivisions. During dry seasons, some clans migrate to grazing grounds in 'Ga'ali Country' (الجعليين), suggesting historical connections between these Sudanese Manasir and a Sudanese Ga'ali group. However, this research provides no direct information about the Arabian Peninsula Ga'ali tribe that is the subject of this genealogical entry.

name: Ghafalah
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine Bedouin tribal lineage based on their traditional nomadic lifestyle and territorial claims
traditionalEconomy: selling firewood and charcoal in coastal towns, livestock (700 camels, 1,000 sheep and goats, cattle and donkeys), carrying goods across inland routes
description: The Ghafalah (singular Al Ghafli) is a Bedouin Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates. An entirely Bedouin tribe at the turn of the 20th century, they roamed the Jiri Plain inland of Ras Al Khaimah and particularly Umm Al Quwain, but did not extend their territory into the Hajar Mountains. Numbering about 500 at the turn of the 20th century, their population had dwindled to 197 by 1968. As a Ghafiri tribe, they were close to the Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and often supported them in conflict. They claimed precedence over a number of wells inland, often sharing these with the Khawatir.

history: The Ghafalah (singular Al Ghafli) is a Bedouin Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that roamed the Jiri Plain inland of Ras Al Khaimah and particularly Umm Al Quwain, though they did not extend their dar (roaming territory) into the Hajar Mountains or their foothills. As a Ghafiri tribe, they were closely aligned with the Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and often supported them in conflict. The tribe found themselves at odds with Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi in 1843, ten years after his bloody succession had triggered the 1833 flight of the Al Bu Falasah to found Dubai. Egged on by Dubai's ruler Maktoum bin Butti bin Suhail, Khalifa went to war against the Bani Qitab, Na'im and Ghafalah. His riders raided across the interior, looting the oasis town of Dhaid and taking camels. The Ghafalah hit back at Maktoum in November 1843, and he attacked them in turn, putting them to flight but losing his eye in the conflict. By 1844, Khalifa had established primacy over the interior tribes. An entirely Bedouin tribe at the turn of the 20th century, the Ghafalah numbered about 500 in strength but by 1968 their population had dwindled to 197 people living in the five northern Emirates. They subsisted mainly by selling firewood and charcoal in coastal towns, and by the produce of their 700 camels, 1,000 sheep and goats as well as cattle and donkeys. They also carried goods such as dates across inland routes to service coastal traders. The Ghafalah were among the tribes opposed to oil exploration taking place inland of the Trucial States, particularly in the late 1940s, frequently allying with the Khawatir tribe who frustrated the coastal Qawasim rulers in granting oil concessions and access to the interior. They claimed precedence over a number of wells inland, often sharing these with the Khawatir.

name: Ghamid
legitimacyNotes: Documented blood lineage tracing back through Azd Shanu'ah to Qahtan, with clear genealogical records and tribal presence maintained from pre-Islamic times to present
ancestorStory: The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage back to their forefather Ghamid, who is Amr ibn Ka'b — and some say Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ka'b — ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr, who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. There are different etymological explanations for the name, with Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stating it derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water.
traditionalEconomy: Agriculture and trade (benefited from Arabia Felix region with more rainfall and productive fields)
description: The Ghamd (also transliterated as Ghamid) is an Azd Arab tribe of the Hejaz Region, predominantly Sunni, and considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Ghamid people are thought to be closely related to the neighboring tribe of Zahran. They have maintained their presence in their historical homeland from pre-Islamic times to the present day, and were significantly involved in early Islamic expansion and various historical conflicts.

history: The Ghamid tribe is one of the oldest and largest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula, tracing their lineage to Ghamid (Amr ibn Ka'b ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr), who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. They belong to the Qahtani lineage and are closely related to the neighboring Zahran tribe, both being part of Azd Shanu'ah. The tribe's historical homeland is in the Al-Bahah region of southwestern Hejaz, in what was known as Arabia Felix due to its favorable climate and rainfall. The name Ghamid derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water, according to Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i. In pre-Islamic times, when Abraha the Abyssinian marched toward Mecca to destroy the Kaaba, Ghamid confronted and defeated his cavalry forces. The tribe sent two delegations to Prophet Muhammad - first in Mecca under Abi Dhibyan's leadership, and later in Madinah during the 10th year after Hijrah, where they embraced Islam and received written guarantees of their rights. Throughout Islamic history, Ghamid participated extensively in military campaigns and tribal conflicts. In the 17th century, they engaged in battles against Banu Al-Harith, supported the Sharif of Mecca in various campaigns including the famous battle of Al-Dhafa'a in 1678, and fought alongside Sharif Muhammad Al-Harith against the Dhafir tribes in Al-Qassim. During the 18th-19th centuries, they resisted Ottoman-Egyptian forces, notably defeating a Turkish army of twenty thousand under Abdin Bey in the Quraish Valley in 1814, and participating in numerous campaigns alongside various rulers including Imam Faisal bin Saud. The tribe maintained their presence in their ancestral homeland while many members migrated during Islamic expansions, with portions settling in Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen. Today, many Ghamdis have adopted the surname Al-Ghamdi and have migrated to Saudi Arabia's major metropolitan centers of Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dammam, while maintaining their tribal identity and cultural heritage.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic expansion - many Ghamdis left Hejaz for jihad and settled in Islamic cities east and west

name: Ghassanid
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arab dynasty claiming descent from Azd, ruled Byzantine client kingdom
ancestorStory: Descendants of Jafnah ibn Amr from the Azd tribe who migrated from Yemen after the collapse of Marib Dam. They established a powerful kingdom in southern Syria as Byzantine clients.
description: The Ghassanids were a prominent pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled a powerful client kingdom under Byzantine patronage in southern Syria and Jordan from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. Originating from the Qahtani Azd tribe of Yemen, they migrated north and became key players in the complex geopolitics between the Byzantine and Sassanian empires. They served as buffers against rival Arab tribes and the Lakhmids, while maintaining Arab cultural identity and eventually converting to Christianity. The dynasty declined with the Islamic conquests in the 7th century, though some branches may have integrated into later tribal structures.

relations.context: They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
Descended from Qahtani Azd tribal origins

history: The Ghassanids were a powerful Arab Christian dynasty that emerged from the Azd tribe of South Arabia in the 3rd century CE, following the legendary migration northward after the collapse of the Marib Dam. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Jafnah ibn Amr, though recent scholarship by Christian J. Robin suggests they may have first dominated the Hejaz region around Medina in the 3rd-4th centuries before migrating to Syria. By 473 CE, they had established themselves as Byzantine foederati under their chief Amorkesos, controlling parts of Palestine and eventually ruling a vast confederation from their capital at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights. Under rulers like al-Harith ibn Jabalah (r. 529-569), who received the highest imperial titles from Emperor Justinian I, they reached their zenith as the primary Arab allies of Byzantium, controlling territory from southern Syria to the northern Hijaz and commanding all Arab confederates of the empire. The Ghassanids were fervent Miaphysite Christians who played crucial roles in church politics, sponsoring the ordination of Jacob Baradaeus and other bishops while maintaining extensive monastic networks. Their military power was demonstrated in constant warfare against the Persian-allied Lakhmids and in major campaigns during the Byzantine-Sassanian War of 602-628. However, their Christian identity and theological disputes with Chalcedonian Byzantium led to periodic tensions, including the dissolution of their confederation under Emperor Maurice (582-585), though they were later restored due to military necessity. The dynasty's end came with the Islamic conquests - their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham led Byzantine forces at the decisive Battle of Yarmuk in 636, and after briefly converting to Islam, ultimately withdrew with his followers to Byzantine Anatolia in 639. Significant Ghassanid remnants remained in Damascus and the Ghouta region, many gradually converting to Islam and serving the Umayyad caliphate as governors, commanders, and scholars, with prominent families like those of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani and Abu Mushir continuing as respected Islamic scholars into the 9th century.

name: Ghassanids
legitimacyNotes: Christian Arab dynasty that ruled as foederati under Byzantine Empire
ancestorStory: Jafnah ibn Amr led the Azd migration from Yemen to the Levant after the collapse of the Marib Dam. His descendants established the Ghassanid kingdom in southern Syria.
description: The Ghassanids were a Christian Arab dynasty of Qahtani origin that ruled a kingdom in southern Syria and Jordan from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. They migrated from Yemen as part of the Azd tribal confederation and became Byzantine foederati, serving as a buffer against Sassanian-allied Lakhmids. The dynasty reached its zenith under al-Harith ibn Jabalah in the 6th century, controlling trade routes and maintaining sophisticated court culture. They were conquered by the Islamic expansion in the 7th century, with remnants scattering across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant.

history: The Ghassanids were a Christian Arab dynasty of Qahtani origin that migrated from Yemen to the Levant in the 3rd century CE as part of the Azd tribal confederation. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Jafna ibn Amr, who led his people northward following the collapse of the Marib Dam. They first established themselves in the Hejaz region around Medina before migrating further north into Syria, where they became the dominant Arab foederati of the Byzantine Empire by the early 6th century. Their capital was established at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, and their authority extended through tribal alliances from southern Syria to the northern Hijaz. The Ghassanids reached their zenith under al-Harith ibn Jabalah (529-569 CE), who received the highest imperial title ever bestowed upon a foreign ruler by Emperor Justinian I and was granted authority over all Arab allies of Byzantium. They were staunch Miaphysite Christians and played a crucial role in reviving the Syrian Miaphysite Church despite Orthodox Byzantine opposition. The dynasty served as a buffer against the Sassanian-allied Lakhmids and participated in the final Byzantine-Sasanian War (602-628). Their power ended with the Islamic conquests, suffering decisive defeat at the Battle of Yarmuk in 636 CE under their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham. Many Ghassanids subsequently converted to Islam and served the Umayyad Caliphate as governors, commanders, and court officials, while others maintained their Christian faith and scattered throughout Syria and beyond.

name: Ghatafan
legitimacyNotes: Ancient pre-Islamic tribal confederation with established genealogical legitimacy through Adnanite lineage
ancestorName: Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays Aylan
ancestorStory: Ghatafan was descended from Qays Aylan through Sa'd, forming one of the major tribal confederations of northern Arabia. The tribe was known for its warrior culture and extensive grazing territories in the Najd region.
description: Ghatafan was a major pre-Islamic Adnanite tribal confederation based in the Najd region of central Arabia, particularly around the areas of Khaybar and northeastern Hijaz. They were known as fierce warriors and skilled poets, playing significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal conflicts including the famous Dahis and Ghabra wars. The confederation included several important sub-tribes and was involved in early Islamic history, with some branches initially opposing then joining the Muslim community. While historically significant in central Arabian politics, their direct influence in modern GCC states is primarily through descendant lineages that merged into other tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace Adnanite origins through Qays Aylan lineage
Ghatafan descends from Adnan through Qays Aylan branch

history: Ghatafan was a major pre-Islamic Adnanite tribal confederation descended from Ghaṭafān ibn Saʾd ibn Qays ʿAylān, making it part of the larger Qays tribe. The confederation was based in the Wadi al-Rumma area of Najd between the Hejaz mountains and Jabal Shammar, with territory extending northeast of Medina. The main branches included Banu Abs (descendants of ʿAbs ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān), Banu Dhubyan (from Dhubyān ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān, including subtribes Banu Murra, Banu Fazara, and Tha'laba), Banu Ashja in the westernmost territory, and the Anmar in the Qassim region. During early Islamic history, Ghatafan played a significant role in conflicts with Muhammad, participating in several military engagements including the Invasion of Dhi Amr (September 624 CE), the Expedition of Dhat al-Riqa, the Invasion of Dumatul Jandal (July 626), and notably allying with the Quraysh in the Battle of the Trench. The tribe was involved in the events surrounding the Battle of Khaybar (628 CE), where they had been expected to reinforce the Jewish settlement against Muslim forces, having been bribed by Banu Nadir with half their harvest to provide 2,000 men and 300 horsemen. However, during the actual battle, the Ghatafan forces failed to provide the promised reinforcements, with various accounts suggesting they were either bought off by Muslims or turned back due to rumors of Muslim attacks on their own strongholds. In the 6th century, the legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab of the Banu Kalb tribe destroyed a Ghatafan sanctuary called 'Buss' that rivaled the Ka'aba in Mecca, either to assert tribal dominance or as a favor to the Quraysh. This demonstrates the significant religious and political influence Ghatafan wielded in pre-Islamic Arabia, as their sanctuary was considered important enough to challenge Meccan religious supremacy.

name: Grusha
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in northern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al Grusha (Qurosha) is a historical tribal group with roots in the northern Arabian Peninsula. They were traditionally involved in nomadic pastoralism and had connections to the broader tribal networks of the region. Historical records suggest they were part of the complex tribal movements that characterized pre-modern Arabia. Their current status as an active tribal entity in the GCC region is unclear from available historical sources.
history: Al Grusha (Qurosha) is identified in historical sources as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, alongside the Al Humaid, Jubur, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood clans. The Bani Khalid confederation itself claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, though Arab genealogists have questioned this lineage and suggested alternative origins including descent from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe. As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, the Grusha participated in the political dominance of Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. Under the leadership of the Al Humaid clan, particularly Barrak ibn Ghurayr, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif in 1670 and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The Grusha, like other Bani Khalid branches, were involved in the confederation's conflicts with the emerging Saudi-Wahhabi alliance in the 18th century. The Bani Khalid opposed Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's movement and attempted to prevent Saudi expansion, but were ultimately defeated when the Saudis invaded their domain in Al-Hasa and deposed the Al 'Ura'yir rulers in 1793. Following the collapse of Bani Khalid power in the early 19th century after defeats by tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, many members of the confederation, including the Grusha, dispersed throughout the region or settled in Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, and other areas.

migrationPath.description: Bani Khalid branches including Grusha consolidated control over Eastern Arabia under Barrak ibn Ghurayr

name: Guzayah
legitimacyNotes: Recognized tribal lineage with traditional authority structures
description: Al-Ghaziya is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records exist regarding their specific territorial claims or major political activities. They appear to have been part of the broader tribal landscape of pre-modern Arabia. Their current status and activities in the modern GCC states require further historical documentation.
history: The Guzayah represents a crucial ancestral branch in Arabian tribal genealogy, serving as the direct ancestor of the major Otaibah tribe. According to 8th-century historian Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's manuscript 'The Great Ancestry', verified in 1988, Guzayah was the son of Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, placing the tribe within the broader Hawazin confederacy that traces back through Qays ʿAylān to the Adnanite lineage. Al-Kalbi's work 'The Abundance of Kinship' specifically identifies Guzayah as one of three sons of Jusham (alongside Oday and Ouseema), and notes that 'The sons of Guzayah are Juda'aa, Hami, Otaibah, and Outwara.' This genealogical position makes Guzayah the father of Otaibah, whose descendants would become one of the largest Arabian tribes. The Guzayah lineage extends back through the classical Arabian genealogical chain: Hawāzin, Manṣūr, ʿIkrima, Khasafa, Qays ʿAylān, Mudar, Nizar, Ma'add, and ultimately to Adnan of the Ishmaelites. While Guzayah itself appears to have been absorbed into or superseded by its descendant tribes, particularly the Otaibah, its genealogical significance remained preserved in medieval Arabic historical sources. The tribe's legacy continued through its descendants who would play major roles in Arabian Peninsula history, from pre-Islamic times through the formation of modern Saudi Arabia.

name: Habus
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine blood lineage tribe with territorial base and distinct cultural identity, speaking Shehhi dialect with ancient Himyarite origins
ancestorStory: Their name is said to be derived from the location of their original holdings of land on the Jebel Hibs, a mountain in Ras Al Khaimah
traditionalEconomy: agriculture and trade - cultivating wheat and dates, trading agricultural products, honey, livestock and dairy products
description: The Habus is an Arab tribe primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

history: The Habus is an Arab tribe of Qahtani lineage primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. Their name derives from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain in Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe speaks the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, which has Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE, shared with neighboring Shihuh and Dhahuriyiin tribes. As an agrarian mountain people, the Habus cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The area around Wadi Naqab was originally held by the Naqbiyin tribe, but was gradually acquired by the Habus after the Naqbiyin fell out of favor with local rulers in the pre-Qawasim period (1695-1740), with many Naqbiyin relocating to Dibba and Khor Fakkan. By 1800, Wadi Naqab was considered Habus territory. The tribe is also associated with Wadi Sal and other mountain settlements. Fiercely independent, the Habus successfully resisted taxation attempts by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi, ruler of Ras Al Khaimah (1921-1948), leading to a brief conflict and subsequent agreement between Habus Sheikh Hamdan bin Malik and Sultan bin Salim. A copy of their peace agreement was lodged with Sultan Qaboos of Oman upon Hamdan's death. In 1951, the Habus became involved in territorial disputes when the Wali of Bukha claimed their areas as Omani territory. They initially accepted this but later agreed to Ras Al Khaimah rule under Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi in 1955, though continued resistance into the 1960s included holding up mineral survey parties and attempting to assert independence in 1963. The tribe traded wheat, honey, goats, dairy products and firewood in Ras Al Khaimah town, purchasing metal tools, coffee, salt and clothing. They expanded their date plantations around Khatt in the 1950s-1960s through purchases and rentals. Though many mountain settlements are now abandoned, younger tribe members have returned to build summer houses, and a tribal festival established in 2010 showcases their traditions, handicrafts and perfumery.

name: Haḍramaut
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation with historical kingdoms and established genealogical lineages
ancestorStory: Haḍramaut was the son of Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of the southern Arabian tribes. The tribe established one of the most prosperous ancient kingdoms in southern Arabia.
description: Haḍramaut is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation based in the Hadhramaut valley of southern Yemen and eastern Arabia. They established one of the great pre-Islamic kingdoms, controlling the lucrative frankincense trade routes between India and the Mediterranean. The tribe produced numerous merchant families who spread throughout the Indian Ocean, establishing trading communities from East Africa to Southeast Asia. Many Hadhrami families later settled in the Gulf states, particularly in the UAE and Oman, where they remain influential in commerce.

relations.context: Conquered by Himyar c. 300 CE after Haḍramaut had previously conquered Qataban
Al Kathiri are a major branch of the Hadhrami confederation

history: The research provided does not contain specific information about the Haḍramaut tribe's genealogy, migrations, or historical development. The Wikipedia articles focus on the Ten Lost Tribes of Israel (which is unrelated to Arabian Peninsula tribes) and general information about Yemeni tribal structure, mentioning that tribes in Yemen have long histories with some tracing roots to the Kingdom of Sheba era. The Tribes of Yemen article confirms that many Yemeni tribes formed alliances and confederations over time, and that tribal divisions stabilized after Islam into four main federations including Himyar, but does not provide specific details about Haḍramaut's origins, migrations, political history, or internal structure. Without concrete historical information about this specific tribe in the provided research, a detailed narrative cannot be constructed.

name: Hafaitat
legitimacyNotes: Branch of larger Huwaitat confederation with traditional territorial claims
ancestorStory: Hafaitat are descended from Hafeet, a branch of the larger Huwaitat tribal confederation. They trace their lineage through the Judhami branch of Quda'a.
description: The Hafaitat are a tribal branch of the larger Huwaitat confederation, traditionally based in the northern Arabian Peninsula and southern Jordan. They are known for their role in controlling trade routes between the Arabian interior and the Levant. The tribe maintained semi-nomadic lifestyle with camel herding as their primary economic activity. Today they remain active with members distributed across Saudi Arabia and Jordan.

relations.context: Hafaitat are a branch of the larger Huwaitat confederation

history: The Hafaitat are a major tribal section of the Sharqiyin tribe, the dominant Ghafiri-aligned confederation along the East Coast of the Trucial States. According to the research, the Hafaitat are one of three notable sections of the Sharqiyin, alongside the Yammahi and Hamudiyin, with the ruling Al Sharqi family of Fujairah deriving from the Hafaitat section. The Sharqiyin, numbering around 7,000 by the turn of the 20th century, settled extensively along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. The territory of Jabal Yibir was historically populated by the Sharqiyin tribe, mainly by the Hafaitat and Yammahi sections. The Hafaitat played a central role in the independence struggle of Fujairah, with Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi, head of the Hafaitat, leading multiple insurrections against Sharjah's control starting in 1879. After years of rebellions and conflicts, the Sharqiyin successfully declared independence from Sharjah in 1901, with Hamad bin Abdullah establishing the independent emirate of Fujairah, though British recognition only came in 1952. The Al Sharqi ruling family has governed Fujairah continuously since 1879, with Hamad bin Mohammed Al Sharqi currently serving as ruler since 1975.

name: al-Hamamir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on Qahtani lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from the ancient Himyarite kingdom of Yemen, tracing lineage to the Qahtani Arab tribes. The tribe migrated northward from Yemen in pre-Islamic times.
description: Al-Hamamir is a Qahtani tribe with ancient roots tracing back to the Himyarite kingdom of Yemen. They are part of the broader network of southern Arabian tribes that migrated northward into the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times. The tribe has maintained its tribal identity and structure throughout Islamic history, with branches found across the Arabian Peninsula. Today they remain an active tribal entity with members distributed across various Gulf states.

Both are Qahtani tribes from southern Arabian origins

history: The research provided focuses exclusively on the al-Hamamir as a clan within the Sudanese Manasir tribe, not the Arabian Peninsula al-Hamamir referenced in the existing data. According to this Sudanese context, al-Hamamir (الحمامير) appears as one of the Bedouin clans of the Manasir al-Badiyah (مناصير البادية), the desert-dwelling branch of the Manasir people of Northern Sudan. These Bedouin Manasir, including al-Hamamir, share grazing grounds in Abisba' (ابسباع) and Sani (سانى) areas alongside other clans such as al-Khabra, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab. During dry seasons, some of these clans migrate to various desert areas including the Kababish territory to the west, Wad Hamid grazing grounds in Ga'ali country, or Rubatab territory. However, the research does not provide any genealogical connection, migration patterns, or historical details that would link this Sudanese al-Hamamir clan to the Arabian Peninsula tribe of the same name mentioned in the existing data. The two appear to be entirely separate tribal entities sharing only a name.

name: Hamdan
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani confederation with established genealogical lineage and traditional leadership structure
ancestorName: Hamdan ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Awsalah ibn Rabi'ah ibn al-Khiyar ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan
ancestorStory: Descended from Hamdan ibn Malik, a prominent figure in the Qahtani lineage. The confederation formed in pre-Islamic times in the highlands of Yemen.
description: Hamdan is a major Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the northern highlands of Yemen, particularly around Sanaa and Sa'ada. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests and Yemen's medieval history. Some branches migrated to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula over the centuries. While primarily Yemen-based, they maintain connections with Gulf Qahtani tribes through shared lineage and historical alliances.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani ancestry
Fellow Qahtani tribes with historical connections

history: Hamdan is an ancient and prominent Arab tribal confederation with deep roots in northern Yemen, first mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE. The confederation stems from the eponymous ancestor Awsala (nicknamed Hamdan), whose lineage is traced through the Qahtani line to Kahlan and ultimately to Saba. In ancient Musnad texts, Hamdan was referred to as 'Ardam Hamdan,' meaning 'the land of Hamdan,' indicating their territorial control in the northern highlands extending from Sanaa toward Marib, Najran, Saada, and the Red Sea coast. From the 4th century BCE, Hamdan kings began controlling the Kingdom of Sheba, with leadership alternating between the two main branches: Hashid (first mentioned as 'Hashdum' in 4th century BCE texts) and Bakil (referenced from 6th century BCE). Yarim Ayman of Hashid monopolized the Sabaean kingdom around the 2nd century BCE, before power shifted to Bakil in the 1st century BCE. Bakil's rule ended when the Himyarites under Dhamar Ali Yahbar I triumphed in 100 CE. During the Islamic period, Hamdan played crucial roles in early Muslim history. In 631 CE, Hamdan delegations led by poet Malik ibn Namat and prince Abu Thawr Dhu'l-Mashar met Prophet Muhammad seeking alliance. More Hamdani tribesmen converted during Ali ibn Abi Talib's expedition to Yemen in 631-632, with Muhammad appointing a Bakil tribesman as his deputy over Hamdan. The tribe remained loyal during the Ridda wars following Muhammad's death in 632. Hamdan soldiers who settled in Kufa became fervent supporters of Ali during his caliphate (656-661), contributing 12,000 men to Ali's army at the Battle of Siffin in 657 under their leader Sa'id ibn Qays al-Hamdani. This close association with Ali led Hamdan to embrace Shi'a Islam, initially following Isma'ilism before becoming supporters of Zaydism. The Banu Yam branch of Hamdan settled north of Bakil in Najran and established the Isma'ili Hamdanid dynasty at Sanaa in the 12th century, later shifting toward Zaydi acceptance. Today, Hamdan comprises over one million members across Hashid and Bakil confederations, historically known as 'the wings of the Zaydite imamate' while maintaining wide-ranging autonomy. They continue to dominate northern Yemen from Sanaa to Saada, wielding significant political influence since the 1962 revolution despite the confederation's internal diversity and periodic shifts in tribal loyalties.

name: al-Hamdatiab
history: The al-Hamdatiab are classified as one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, but are specifically noted to have Shaiqi origins. According to the research, they are among the ten riverain clans of the Manasir people who inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in what they call Dar al-Manasir. The Manasir as a whole trace their origins to multiple possible lineages: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniin, others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, while a third tradition connects them to the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab through descendants of King Sabir. However, critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Hamdatiab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities. The al-Hamdatiab, like other riverain Manasir, pursue small-scale agriculture along the Nile and are renowned for date cultivation and mud house construction. Their population was estimated at around 30,000-33,000 in the 1990s, though this figure encompasses all Manasir clans collectively.

name: al-Hamsab
history: The al-Hamsab (الهامزاب) are a clan within the Manasir tribal confederation of Northern Sudan, specifically classified among the Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil). According to Manasir oral tradition, they trace their lineage back to a historical ancestor named Mansur, though multiple genealogical accounts exist. Some Manasir claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniin, others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, and still others trace their ancestry to Mansur as a descendant of King Sabir through the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Hamsab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities. Early travelers documented Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and their burial customs show strong similarities to Nubian practices. The al-Hamsab, like other Riverain Manasir clans, inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Dar al-Manasir, pursuing small-scale agriculture and date cultivation. Their population was affected by the Merowe Dam project which impacted all villages in their traditional homeland.

name: Hamudiyin
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah
ancestorStory: The Hammudids trace their lineage to Hamud ibn Maymun, a descendant of Ali ibn Abi Talib. They established themselves as a ruling dynasty in Al-Andalus during the early 11th century.
description: The Hammudids were a Hashemite dynasty that ruled parts of Al-Andalus (Islamic Spain) during the 11th century taifa period. They claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and established caliphates in Málaga and Algeciras. While primarily associated with Iberian history, some branches of Hashemite families later dispersed across the Islamic world including the Arabian Peninsula. Their historical significance lies in their role during the fragmentation of the Umayyad Caliphate in Al-Andalus.

relations.context: Both claim Hashemite descent through different lines

history: The Hamudiyin are a distinct section of the Sharqiyin tribe of the United Arab Emirates, historically concentrated along the eastern coast in areas including Wadi Sharm and Wadi Zikt in what is now Fujairah Emirate. As part of the broader Sharqiyin confederation, they were traditionally aligned with the Ghafiri tribal faction and were among the dominant groups along the Trucial States' eastern coastline. The Sharqiyin, including the Hamudiyin section, were originally dependencies of Sharjah but engaged in numerous rebellions throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries seeking independence. Under leaders like Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi of the Hafaitat section, they successfully resisted Sharjah's control, culminating in practical independence from 1901 and eventual British recognition of Fujairah as a separate Trucial State in 1952. The Hamudiyin subsisted primarily through agriculture, pearling, and fishing, living in barasti palm frond houses as late as the 1960s. Historical records from 1908 describe Hamudiyin settlements like Sharm with 40 houses and Dadna with 50 houses, complete with detailed accounts of their livestock and date palm cultivation along the wadis.
