history: The Harb tribe is an Arab tribe of Qahtanite origin in the Arabian Peninsula, with genealogical sources tracing their lineage to Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan. The tribe dates to the 2nd century of the Islamic calendar (8th century CE), when Qahtani tribes emigrated from southern Arabia to Hejaz around 131 AH (748-749 CE) seeking water and land after conflicts with their cousins Banu Ar-Rabi'ah bin Saad. This migration sparked territorial conflicts with established Adnani Arab tribes including Juhaynah, Anazzah, Muzaynah, and Sulaym over land and water resources. After approximately three centuries of settlement in Hejaz, the Harb tribe established dominance in the heart of Hejaz with territories surrounding the holy city of Medina. Their traditional lands extend from the Red Sea coast in Tihamah to the heart of Najd in central Saudi Arabia, and from Madinah in the north to Al Qunfudhah in the south. The tribe is organized into two main sections: Masrooh and Banu Salim, with Banu Salim further divided into Maymoon and Al-Marawihah subsections. In the 21st century, Harb has evolved into a federation of tribes and families, with many members migrating to major Saudi metropolitan centers including Riyadh, Jeddah, Al-Qassim, and Dammam. Beyond the Arabian Peninsula, significant Harb populations established themselves in Lebanon's Bekaa Valley during the 7th century, becoming one of the most prominent Arab tribes there, particularly around Baalbek and Zahle. Other branches spread to Jordan, where the Mahamid from Harb formed an emirate in Karak governorate, and to Palestine, Sudan, and Maghreb countries through various migrations and alliances.

They are historically found across various Gulf regions, particularly in areas where Hasanid families settled. The tribe has maintained their status through religious scholarship and participation in trade networks. They remain active today as part of the broader network of Hasanid descendants in the GCC region.

history: The research reveals a critical distinction: the existing data refers to Al-Hasana, a Hasanid tribe claiming descent from Al-Hasan ibn Ali (grandson of Prophet Muhammad), while the Wikipedia sources discuss the Hasana tribe of Syria, which is actually a branch of the Anaza confederation with no relation to Hasanid lineage. The Syrian Hasana are documented as the first Anaza tribe to migrate from Najd to Syria in 1757, establishing themselves in the plains east of Hama and Homs. Under their leader Muhanna al-Fadil, they became the dominant Bedouin force in the region by the 1780s, driving out the established Mawali tribe and gaining Ottoman sanction to collect tolls from Palmyra trade caravans. The tribe faced challenges from other Anaza branches like the Fad'an and Sba'a in the early 19th century, leading to territorial conflicts. During the French Mandate period, their leader Trad al-Milhim received substantial landholdings - some twenty villages in the 1930s. The tribe adapted to Syrian independence, with the al-Milhim family gaining parliamentary representation under Hafez al-Assad's government in 1977 and 1994, maintaining loyalty during the Syrian civil war. They continue to inhabit villages east of Homs and Hama with headquarters in Buwaydah. The existing data about Al-Hasana as Ashraf families appears to reference an entirely different tribal entity from the Arabian Peninsula Hasanid lineage, not the documented Syrian Anaza branch.

name: Hashid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal confederation leadership and historical territorial control in northern Yemen
ancestorStory: Descended from Hashid ibn Jusham ibn Khayran, considered one of the major tribal ancestors of the Bakil-Hashid confederation. The tribe traces its lineage to the ancient Himyarite and Sabaean kingdoms.
description: Hashid is one of Yemen's largest and most influential tribal confederations, historically based in the northern highlands around Sanaa. Together with their traditional rivals Bakil, they form the backbone of Yemen's tribal structure and have been described as 'the two wings of Yemen.' The confederation has played a crucial role in Yemeni politics throughout history, including significant influence during the Imamate period and modern republican era. While primarily Yemeni-based, some Hashid sections have historical connections to southern Arabian Peninsula regions.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani tribal origins

history: The Hashid is one of Yemen's most powerful tribal confederations, with origins tracing back over two millennia to the ancient Sabaean period. First mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions as 'Hashdum' in the 4th century BCE, the tribe was part of the Sum'ay confederation alongside Humlan and Yarsum tribes, worshipping the god Ta'lab. According to medieval Yemeni genealogies, Hashid and their traditional rivals Bakil were sons of Jashim bin Jubran bin Nawf, descended from the ancient Hamdan tribe. The Banu Hamdan served as qayls (chiefs) of Hashid in Sabaean times and later acquired control over parts of Bakil, giving their name to the broader Hamdan confederation that included both Hashid and Bakil tribes. In the late 3rd century, the Hamdan confederation switched allegiance to the Himyarite kingdom. The tribe's conversion to Islam came in 622 CE when Muhammad sent Ali ibn Abi Talib to Yemen after Khalid ibn al-Walid's unsuccessful mission. Unlike many Arabian tribes, the Hamdan remained loyal to Islam after Muhammad's death and did not join the ridda (apostasy) movement. They maintained allegiance to Ali ibn Abi Talib throughout his conflicts with the Umayyads, and by the 10th century had invited Imam al-Hadi Yahya bin al-Hussain to establish Zaidi rule in northern Yemen at Sa'da in 893-897 CE. This established the Hashid and Bakil as the 'two wings' of the Zaidi imamate, a relationship that would define their political role for centuries. Throughout Islamic history, Hashid warriors participated in major conquests and battles, with prominent figures like Saeed bin Qais Al-Hamdani carrying the Hamdan banner in the battles of the Camel and Siffin. The confederation played crucial roles in Yemen's medieval politics, supporting various Zaidi imams while maintaining significant autonomy in their northern highland territories around Sanaa. In modern times, the Hashid confederation was dominated for decades by the powerful al-Ahmar family, particularly Sheikh Abdullah ibn Husayn al-Ahmar who formed a crucial alliance with President Ali Abdullah Saleh from 1978 until his death in 2007. This alliance made al-Ahmar Yemen's second most powerful figure as Speaker of Parliament. However, following the Arab Spring in 2011, his sons led the Hashid in an insurgency against Saleh, playing an essential role in the revolution that forced the president to step down in 2012. The subsequent conflict with the Houthis and loss of Saudi support led to internal divisions within the confederation, with many Hashid tribes reaching separate peace agreements or switching sides, marking a significant decline in their unified political influence.

history: The Hawamil are documented as a section of the Bani Yas tribal confederation of the United Arab Emirates, though historical sources indicate they may have originally been part of the Al Ali tribe before incorporation into the Bani Yas. As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Hawamil are associated with the tribal movement from Najd in central Arabia to the coastal regions of what is now the UAE, though specific details of their individual migration are not documented in available sources. The Bani Yas confederation, to which the Hawamil belong, originated from Najd and settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700, later establishing themselves in Abu Dhabi in 1795 following the discovery of fresh water. The Hawamil are also mentioned as a subdivision within the Mutayr tribe structure, specifically as part of the Al-Hawamil section under the Birayh division, suggesting possible tribal connections or shared lineage across different confederations. The contemporary Bani Yas confederation recognizes more than 20 sections, with the Hawamil (Al Hameli) being one of the constituent groups, represented by the family name Al Hameli in modern UAE society.

name: Hawazin
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnani confederation with documented pre-Islamic nobility and descent from Hawazin ibn Mansur
ancestorStory: Hawazin was descended from Mansur ibn Ikrima, establishing one of the major Adnani tribal confederations. The tribe became renowned for their eloquent Arabic speech and fierce independence.
description: Hawazin was one of the most powerful pre-Islamic tribal confederations of central Arabia, centered in the Najd and eastern Hijaz regions. They were famous for their linguistic prowess and provided wet nurses to noble families, including the Prophet Muhammad's nurse Halima. The confederation was decisively defeated by Muslim forces at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE, after which many branches scattered and integrated into other tribal groups. Several modern Gulf tribes trace partial descent from Hawazin sub-tribes.

history: The Hawazin was a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation originally based in western Najd and around Ta'if in the Hejaz, forming part of the larger Qays tribal group. According to traditional genealogy, they descended from Hawazin ibn Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa ibn Qays Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Adnanite Arabs. The confederation consisted of subtribes including Banu Sa'd, Banu Jusham (known collectively as 'Ujz Hawazin or 'the rear of Hawazin'), and the powerful Banu Thaqif and Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, which were often counted separately. Beginning around 550 CE, the Hawazin became vassals of the Banu 'Abs of Ghatafan under chieftain Zuhayr ibn Jadhima, but discontinued tribute after his death, leading to sporadic conflicts with Ghatafan. During the late 6th-century Fijar War, the Hawazin fought alongside much of the Qays confederation against the Quraysh and Kinana tribes over four years, achieving several victories including at Nakhla, Shamta, and Ukaz. The Hawazin had minimal contact with the Prophet Muhammad until 630 CE, though his wet nurse Halima bint Abu Dhu'ayb came from their Banu Sa'd subtribe. After Muhammad's conquest of Mecca, Malik ibn Awf of Banu Nasr mobilized Hawazin and Thaqif forces, but they were decisively defeated at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE. Many Hawazin were captured and their property seized, though Muhammad reconciled with them by returning captives and recognizing Malik's chieftainship after his conversion. Following Muhammad's death in 632, the Hawazin joined the Ridda Wars against the early Muslim state but eventually returned to Islam. After their defeat, many branches scattered and migrated to various regions including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia. Modern tribes including the Otaibah of Saudi Arabia claim descent from Hawazin lineages, with the Otaibah specifically tracing ancestry through Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin according to Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century genealogical work.

name: Himyar
legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

relations.context: In the pre-Islamic era, Mudar was usually politically dominated by Himyar, until the collapse of the latter
Himyar conquered Saba' multiple times, first in c. 25 BCE, with political fortunes changing frequently until final conquest around 280 CE
Initially integrated into Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE; later conquered Qataban in c. 200 CE

Himyar overpowered and forced Kahlan tribes out of Yemeni highlands

history: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and confederation of tribes that dominated southern Yemen from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom, it achieved independence in 110 BCE and was subsequently ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe, with their capital at the ancient city of Zafar near modern Sanaa. The kingdom systematically conquered neighboring realms, taking Saba' around 25 BCE (and definitively around 280 CE), Qataban around 200 CE, and Haḍramaut around 300 CE, thereby uniting southern Arabia for the first time. Himyar established a sophisticated trading empire that linked the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa, with Pliny the Elder describing it as one of 'the richest nations in the world.' The kingdom underwent dramatic religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin (c. 375–400 CE), making it a unique Jewish kingdom in Arabia. This conversion was possibly motivated by a desire to distance themselves from the Christianizing Roman Empire. The Jewish period reached its climax under Dhu Nuwas, who persecuted Christians in Najran, leading to the Aksumite invasion of 530 CE that ended Jewish rule. Christianity then became the official religion under Aksumite rule until the Sasanian conquest in 575-578 CE. After the rise of Islam, Himyarite noble families, particularly the Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah, played crucial roles in early Islamic conquests, leading South Arabian contingents in the conquest of Homs in 638 and becoming influential in Umayyad politics, though their power declined after defeats at Marj Rahit (684) and Khazir (686).

migrationPath.description: Himyarite noble families, led by Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah, migrated to Syria as part of Muslim conquest armies and established themselves as the dominant South Arabian tribes in Homs
Himyarite contingents including Abraha ibn Sabbah and Abu Shamir participated in the Muslim conquest of Egypt and helped plan the new city of Fustat

name: Hourani
legitimacyNotes: Local clan status based on settlement and integration in Gulf communities
description: The Hourani is a small clan found in various Gulf communities, likely deriving their name from the Hauran region of southern Syria. They appear to be descendants of migrants or settlers who integrated into existing tribal structures in the Arabian Peninsula. Their presence in the Gulf is documented but limited, with members scattered across different emirates and regions. They maintain clan identity while being integrated into larger tribal confederations.
history: The Hourani clan derives its name from the Hauran region of southern Syria, with documented origins tracing to the Arab al-Halqiyyin tribe. According to historical records of the prominent Al-Hourani family, they moved from the town of Jasim in the southern Hawran region to Hama in central Syria around the 1510s, establishing their distinctive surname Al-Hourani based on their regional origin. The family claimed descent from Muhammad through genealogical records displayed in Hama's museum. The research indicates connections between Hourani Bedouins and the broader Mahamid tribal confederation, with the Hourani listed as a branch that emerged from the Mahamid's migrations from the Arabian Peninsula through the Levant. Syrian sources specifically identify Hourani Bedouins in the Daraa and Quneitra governorates, noting their close relationships with Jordanian tribes. The clan appears to represent descendants of migrants who integrated into existing tribal structures throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf regions, maintaining clan identity while becoming part of larger tribal confederations. Their presence in Gulf communities reflects the broader pattern of Levantine migration to the region, though their numbers remain limited and scattered across different emirates.

migrationPath.description: Al-Halqiyyin tribal migration from southern Hawran region to central Syria

timelineEvents.title: Hawran to Hama Migration
timelineEvents.description: Al-Hourani family moves from Jasim in southern Hawran to Hama, establishing regional surname

name: Hudhayl
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented genealogy tracing to pre-Islamic era
ancestorStory: Hudhayl ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar, eponymous ancestor of the tribe. The tribe descended from this figure and became one of the prominent tribes of the Hijaz.
description: Hudhayl was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe based in the mountainous regions between Mecca and Medina in the Hijaz. They were known for their poetry, archery skills, and as fierce warriors who controlled strategic routes in central Arabia. The tribe played a significant role during the early Islamic conquests and produced notable poets and scholars. While historically powerful in the Arabian Peninsula, their direct influence in the modern GCC states is limited compared to other tribal groups.

relations.context: The Bakr sub-tribe was at war with the Hudhayl, resulting in casualties
Historical movement patterns from Arabian Peninsula to Gulf region

history: Hudhayl (بنو هذيل) was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Hudhayl ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar, making them part of the Adnani lineage. The tribe was closely related to the ancestors of Prophet Muhammad through their common ancestor Mudrikah, whose son Khuzaymah became the progenitor of Banu Kinana. According to Ibn Ishaq, their full genealogical line traces back through Mudar, Nizar, Ma'ad, and Adnan to Ishmael and Abraham. The tribe inhabited the mountainous regions between Mecca and Medina in the Hijaz and became renowned throughout Arabian history for their talented poets, intellectuals, and fierce warriors who controlled strategic routes in central Arabia. During the early Islamic period, Banu Hudhayl played a significant role in defending the sacred sites of Islam. In 930 CE, during the infamous Qarmatian Sack of Mecca, the tribe demonstrated their martial prowess when they ambushed the retreating Qarmatian forces who had desecrated the Kaaba and stolen the Black Stone. The Hudhayl warriors successfully liberated many prisoners and recovered much of the stolen loot before the Qarmatians managed to escape back to Bahrain. This action was part of a larger ongoing conflict between the tribe and the radical Ismaili sect. The tribe produced notable companions of the Prophet, including Abdullah ibn Masud, as well as distinguished poets like Abū Dhuʾayb al-Hudhalī who was contemporary with Muhammad. In pre-Islamic times, specific sub-groups of the tribe, such as those under the Banu Hudhayl, worshipped the god Suwa' according to Islamic sources. Today, the descendants of Banu Hudhayl are found primarily in Saudi Arabia, with populations also present in Tunisia, Jordan, and Egypt, maintaining their historical reputation for scholarship and cultural contributions.

migrationPath.description: Small groups of Hudhayli warriors settled in Basra during Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Ambush of Retreating Qarmatians
timelineEvents.description: Banu Hudhayl warriors ambushed the Qarmatian forces retreating from Mecca after the Sack of Mecca, liberating many prisoners and recovering stolen loot
timelineEvents.location: Arabian Peninsula route between Mecca and Bahrain

name: Huwala
legitimacyNotes: Persian Gulf Arab merchants and traders with historical maritime and commercial authority
description: The Huwala are Arab tribes who migrated across the Persian Gulf between the Arabian Peninsula and Persian coast, particularly active in trade and pearling. They established communities on both sides of the Gulf, with significant presence in what is now UAE, Bahrain, and southern Iran. Historically they were prominent maritime merchants and pearl traders, often maintaining dual loyalties and commercial networks across Gulf waters. Many Huwala families remain influential in modern Gulf states, particularly in business and trade sectors.

relations.context: Conflicts over control of Bahrain, Huwala served Persian interests against Utub expansion
Historical maritime and trade competition in lower Gulf
Intermarriage and commercial relationships in Bahrain

history: The Huwala are Arabian Iranian tribal confederations who migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Persian Gulf coast of Iran beginning in the 13th-14th centuries, with continued migrations through the 19th century. They settled primarily in Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, particularly around Bandar Abbas, Qishm, and the mainland near Bandar Lengeh. Unlike most Persians who are Twelver Shia, the Huwala follow Sunni Islam like most Peninsular Arabs. The term specifically refers to actual Arab Huwala tribes including the Qawasem, Hammadi, Al Nasur, Obaidli, and Bani Tamim - not to be confused with Sunni Larestani Achomi families who sometimes adopt Huwala identity. In the 18th century, the Arab Al Qasimi tribal confederation took control of southern Iranian coasts and islands around Bandar Lengeh, with the Iranian Zand dynasty recognizing a Qasimi as local ruler in 1779. The Qasimis remained in control until 1887 when defeated by the British in their 'anti-piracy' campaign. Most Huwala re-migrated back to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the imposition of restrictive economic policies by Reza Shah in the 1930s - including centralization, conscription, and forced unveiling of women - leading to the final exodus of most remaining Huwala back to Arabia. Contemporary historians like Niebuhr and Lorimer documented that true Huwala Arabs lived in the specific Shibkoh region from Bandar Kanj in the south to Bandar Kangan in the north. Today, many Huwala families remain influential in modern Gulf states, particularly in business and trade sectors.

migrationPath.description: Initial Arab tribal migrations to Iranian coast, 13th-14th centuries

migrationPath.to: Iranian coast between Bushehr and Bandar Abbas
migrationPath.description: Continued migrations of sailors, traders, fishermen, pearl divers, and cultivators

name: Huwayla
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Huwayla is a minor Arabian tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe appears in some genealogical records but lacks extensive documentation regarding their specific territorial holdings, political influence, or economic activities. Their current status and activities in the modern GCC states remain unclear due to limited historical sources.
history: The Huwayla tribe is documented as establishing itself in Sharjah around 1727 as part of the Qawasim clan. In the early 18th century, the Huwayla tribe of the Qawasim clan declared Sharjah independent, marking a significant political development in the region. This establishment made Sharjah one of the wealthiest towns in the region at that time, with the settlement having existed for over 5000 years prior to Huwayla control. The tribe's rule continued into the 19th century when Sheikh Sultan I signed the General Maritime Treaty with Britain on 8 January 1820, accepting a protectorate status to keep the Ottoman Turks out. Like neighboring emirates Ajman, Dubai, Ras Al Khaimah, and Umm Al Quwain, Sharjah's strategic position on the route to India made it important enough to be recognized as a salute state, albeit of the lowest class with 3 guns. The Huwayla's governance through the Al Qasimi dynasty has continued since the 18th century, with Sharjah eventually joining the United Arab Emirates in 1971.

name: Ilyās
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical authority as ancestor of major Adnani tribal groups
ancestorStory: Ilyās ibn Muḍar is the legendary ancestor of several major Arab tribal confederations. He is considered the progenitor of tribes including Hawazin, Ghatafan, and other northern Arabian groups.
description: Ilyās ibn Muḍar represents a major genealogical branch within the Adnani Arab lineage system. As an ancestral figure, Ilyās is credited as the progenitor of several significant tribal confederations that spread across the Arabian Peninsula. His descendants include the Hawazin and Ghatafan tribal groups, among others, who played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian history. While Ilyās himself is a genealogical construct, the tribal groups claiming descent from him have had historical presence in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including regions that are now part of the modern GCC states.

relations.context: Ilyās is descended from Adnan in the genealogical system

history: The provided research contains information about Ilyas Kashmiri (a modern Pakistani militant) and Kurds as an ethnic group, but does not contain any relevant information about the Arabian tribal genealogy figure Ilyās ibn Muḍar. While there is a brief Wikipedia entry about Ilyas ibn Mudar mentioned, it only provides basic genealogical information stating he was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribal chief, son of Mudar ibn Nizar, and progenitor of Khindifite tribes including the Quraysh. The research notes he ruled over the Hijaz, maintained the Kaaba in Mecca, and promoted monotheism, but provides no specific dates, migration patterns, or detailed historical events that would allow for construction of a meaningful timeline or migration path. The existing tribal data already contains the core information available about this genealogical figure as an ancestral construct within the Adnani Arab lineage system.

name: Jadis
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with legendary status in pre-Islamic genealogy
ancestorName: Jadis ibn Lud ibn Sam ibn Nuh
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Jadis was descended from Lud, son of Sam (Shem), son of Noah. They were considered one of the ancient Arab tribes that inhabited the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times.
description: Jadis was an ancient Arabian tribe mentioned in pre-Islamic genealogical traditions and early Islamic historical accounts. They were traditionally placed among the early Qahtani Arab tribes that inhabited parts of the Arabian Peninsula in antiquity. The tribe is primarily known through genealogical literature rather than detailed historical records. They are considered extinct as a distinct tribal entity, having disappeared or been absorbed into other tribal groups before the Islamic period.
history: Jadis was an ancient Arabian tribe that, along with their neighbors the Tasm, dominated the region of Al-Yamama in the Arabian Peninsula from approximately 110 BCE until their extinction in the 6th century CE. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Jadis descended from either Lud or Aram, both sons of Shem (Sam), son of Noah, though the 11th-century historian Miskawayh stated both Jadis and Tasm were sons of Lud. The tribe settled in Al-Yamama where they built palaces and fortresses alongside the Tasm, creating an independent kingdom and civilization that was later taken over by the Banu Hanifa tribe. By the 6th century CE, both tribes were ruled by King Imliq of Tasm, who implemented the policy of droit du seigneur, deflowering Jadis virgins before marriage and controlling trade routes. The Jadis eventually assassinated Imliq during a special performance, revealing hidden swords from the sand to kill him. Despite constant warnings from a woman of their own tribe about incoming Himyarite armies, the Jadis ignored the threat. The Himyarite forces entered Al-Yamama and completely exterminated the unprepared Jadis tribe, killing all adults and enslaving the children. This genocide marked the end of Jadis as a distinct tribal entity, earning them classification among the al-Arab al-Ba'ida (extinct Arabian tribes). The tribe's legacy survived in Arabian oral traditions and was later adapted into the 2005 Jordanian television series 'Akher Ayyam Al Yamama' (The Last Days of Al Yamama). The legendary Zarqa al-Yamama, a blue-eyed woman with exceptional eyesight who could see enemies from a week's distance, belonged to the Jadis tribe and warned of the final Himyarite attack before being killed and crucified by Hassan al-Himyari's forces.

name: Jawabir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and customary authority
description: The Jawabir are a tribal group found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are present in several Gulf states as part of the broader tribal structure of the region. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain traditional social organization while adapting to modern Gulf society. Their historical activities and current status vary by location within the GCC states.
history: The Jawabir are documented as one of three subsections of the Dhawahir tribe, which held traditional dominance over the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain region. According to historical records, the Dhawahir tribe numbered approximately 4,500 people and consisted of three distinct groups: the Daramikah who populated Hili, Mutared and Qattara; the Jawabir who settled specifically in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi. The tribe followed a semi-nomadic lifestyle, staying in their villages during the summer date season and moving throughout the Trucial States during winter months. They maintained large flocks of sheep, herded camels, and engaged in charcoal trading from the fertile woodlands of the oasis, which were sustained by an effective falaj irrigation system. The Jawabir, like other Dhawahir subsections, had a long-standing alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation. This relationship became strained when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') began purchasing date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir in the late 19th century. The changing ownership patterns led to rebellion, and in 1877 Zayed went to war with the Dhawahir. After a month-long conflict, he captured their main settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now simply Al Ain) and built a fort to establish his dominion, appointing a Dhawahir member as his headman. Contemporary records also reference a separate Jawabir group that acquired territory in Samawah during the 19th century Ottoman redistribution of Khaza'il lands in Iraq, though the connection between these groups requires further research.

name: Juboor
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal legitimacy through established lineage and territorial presence
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its descent to an ancestor named Jabr, from whom they derive their name. They are considered part of the larger tribal confederation with historical presence in Iraq and Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Juboor is an Arab tribe with historical presence primarily in Iraq and parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally known as a nomadic pastoral tribe with branches that migrated across various regions. The tribe has maintained its traditional structure and identity, with some sections settling in Gulf states. They remain active today with members distributed across Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and other Arab countries.

relations.context: Both tribes migrated across similar routes in Arabian Peninsula
Historical cooperation in Iraqi tribal confederations

history: The Juboor are a major branch of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, one of the most powerful Arab tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula. According to the research, the Juboor were among the main branches of Bani Khalid alongside Al Humaid, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, Mahashir and Nahood. The Bani Khalid confederation dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries, with the Jabrids (Banu Jabr) serving as their ruling class and establishing an emirate that controlled Eastern Arabia and Najd from the 15th to 16th century. Under the leadership of the Al Humaid branch, particularly Barrak ibn Ghurayr, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif in 1670 and established their emirate with Al-Mubarraz as capital. The Juboor, as part of this confederation, participated in the political and military activities of the broader Bani Khalid state. The confederation's power declined following defeats by the First Saudi State in 1793-1796, and later conflicts with tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823 and 1830. After their military defeats, many Bani Khalid members, including the Juboor, settled across Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, and other Arab countries, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to new political realities.

migrationPath.description: Bani Khalid, including Juboor branch, established control over Eastern Arabia under Barrak ibn Ghurayr

name: Jubur
legitimacyNotes: Established through tribal confederacy and historical settlement patterns in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Jubur trace their lineage to Jabir, from whom the tribal name derives. They are considered part of the larger Zubayd tribal confederation.
description: The Jubur are a major tribal confederation primarily based in eastern Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and parts of Iraq. They are part of the larger Zubayd tribal group and have historically been involved in pastoral nomadism and inter-regional trade. The tribe played significant roles in the political dynamics of the northern Arabian Peninsula during the 18th and 19th centuries. Today they remain active with members distributed across several GCC states, particularly in Kuwait and eastern Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Historical alliances in Iraq and eastern Arabia
Competition over grazing lands and trade routes in eastern Arabia

history: The Jubur are a major Iraqi Arab tribe that traces its lineage to the Zubaidi tribal confederation, part of the larger Madhhij group. They are primarily based in Iraq, with significant presence in Hawija and Kirkuk since the 18th century. The tribe forms part of the Zubaydi confederation alongside the Azza, Dulaim, Janabi and Obaidi federations. The Jubur claim descent from the legendary pre-Islamic warrior Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi (died 642 CE), a cavalry commander who converted to Islam and served in major battles including Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahavand under the Rashidun Caliphate. Originally entirely Sunni Muslim, some Jubur began converting to Shia Islam in the 19th century, particularly in the mid-Euphrates region of southern Iraq. The tribe has maintained its martial traditions through modern times, playing significant roles in Iraqi politics and military affairs. During the Armenian genocide of 1915, the Jubur sheltered Armenian deportees in the al-Jazira desert. Since 2014, the tribe has actively fought against ISIS, participating in operations to retake Tikrit and other Iraqi cities. Some Jubur members joined the Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF), while tribal leaders like Ahmed Abdullah al-Jubouri and Najim Abdullah al-Jubouri have held prominent positions as governors and military commanders in modern Iraq.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Hawija and Kirkuk regions during the 18th century

timelineEvents.title: Death of Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib
timelineEvents.description: Legendary ancestor killed at Battle of Nahavand

timelineEvents.title: Religious Conversion
timelineEvents.description: Some Jubur begin converting from Sunni to Shia Islam

timelineEvents.title: Armenian Shelter
timelineEvents.description: Jubur tribe shelters Armenian deportees during Ottoman genocide

timelineEvents.title: Anti-ISIS Campaign
timelineEvents.description: Jubur tribe begins fighting Islamic State and joins PMF operations

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Tikrit
timelineEvents.description: Jubur forces participate with Iraqi Armed Forces in Second Battle of Tikrit

name: Junb
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani tribal legitimacy through descent from Junb ibn Sa'd al-Ashira
ancestorStory: Junb is descended from Sa'd al-Ashira, a major branch of the Qahtani tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient South Arabian kingdoms.
description: Junb is a Qahtani tribe historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Yemen and Najran regions. They are part of the larger Sa'd al-Ashira tribal confederation and have maintained their tribal identity through centuries. The tribe has branches that have migrated to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula over time. Today, members of Junb can be found across the Arabian Peninsula, including in Saudi Arabia's southern regions.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins through Sa'd al-Ashira

history: Junb is one of the three main tribes that compose the Qahtan tribal confederation, alongside Sanhan and Rufaida. The tribe is part of the larger Qahtani lineage tracing back to ancient South Arabian origins. According to historical accounts, the Qahtan confederation settled in Al-Quwai'iyah region of Najd and expanded its influence throughout southern Najd during a period when the area came under the rule of Ibn Saud in the second Saudi state. Historians describe Qahtan as having become one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a time before eventually returning to their original homeland in the south. Today, members of Junb and the broader Qahtan confederation are distributed across multiple countries including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe has produced notable figures across various fields, with many prominent individuals bearing the Al-Qahtani and Al-Hajri surnames serving in government positions, sports, and other public roles throughout the Gulf region.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Junb warriors and families northward following Islamic conquest participation, establishing new settlements in Najd
Expansion into Asir region during medieval period, establishing mountain settlements and trading posts

timelineEvents.title: Tribal Formation
timelineEvents.description: Establishment of Junb as distinct tribal entity within Sa'd al-Ashira confederation in Yemen highlands

timelineEvents.title: Islamic Conversion
timelineEvents.description: Junb tribe accepts Islam and pledges allegiance to Prophet Muhammad, maintaining autonomy over traditional territories

timelineEvents.title: Iraq Campaign Participation
timelineEvents.description: Junb warriors join Khalid ibn al-Walid's forces in conquest of Iraq, establishing tribe's military reputation

timelineEvents.title: Ottoman Integration
timelineEvents.description: Junb territories incorporated into Ottoman administrative system, tribe maintains local governance under imperial oversight

name: Jusham
legitimacyNotes: descent from Hilal ibn Amir lineage
ancestorName: Jusham ibn Muawiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Jusham was a branch of the Hilalian confederation that participated in the great Bedouin migrations. They trace their lineage through the Hawazin tribal group to Mudar.
description: Jusham was a tribal branch of the larger Hilalian confederation, part of the Banu Hilal migration that moved westward across North Africa in the 11th century. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who participated in the great Bedouin tribal movements. As part of the Hilalian tribes, they were primarily active in the western Arabian Peninsula and North African regions rather than the eastern Arabian Gulf. Their historical significance relates more to the broader Arab tribal migrations than to specific GCC regional politics.

relations.context: both trace Adnani Hawazin ancestry

history: The Banu Jusham were a prominent Arabian tribe that traced their lineage to Jusham ibn Muawiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, making them part of the larger Hawazin confederation and the Qays Aylan tribal group descended from Mudar and ultimately the Adnanites. During the pre-Islamic era, the Jusham were pastoral nomads inhabiting the steppes between Mecca and Medina, and engaged in frequent conflicts with rival tribes including the Banu Fazara. They participated in the Fijar War in the late 6th century alongside other Hawazin tribes against the Quraysh and Kinana. The tribe had little contact with Prophet Muhammad until 630 CE when they were defeated at the Battle of Hunayn, after which they reconciled with the Muslims. Following Muhammad's death in 632, the Jusham joined other Hawazin tribes in discontinuing tribute to Medina and participated in the Ridda Wars against the early Muslim state, but ultimately returned to the Islamic fold. The Banu Jusham later became part of the great Hilalian migration westward across North Africa in the 11th century. After their defeat by the Almohads, they were settled alongside the Riyah in regions of present-day western Morocco by the Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur, with the Jusham specifically settling in Tamasna. According to genealogical sources, the Jusham also formed one of the largest divisions of the Banu Taghlib's al-Araqim group, where they were known as one of 'al-Rawkan' (the two horns) alongside the Malik division due to their size and strength. The modern Saudi tribe of Otaibah claims descent from the ancient Hawazin confederation, which would include Jusham lineages. Through various tribal genealogies, the name Jusham appears connected to multiple Arabian tribal branches, reflecting the complex intertribal relationships and migrations that characterized Arabian Peninsula tribal history.

migrationPath.description: Hawazin tribes including Jusham inhabited pastoral regions between the holy cities
Participation in the great Hilalian Bedouin migration westward
Settlement by Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur after defeat

timelineEvents.title: Fijar War Participation
timelineEvents.description: Jusham fought with Hawazin confederation against Quraysh and Kinana tribes in the war of sacrilege

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Hunayn
timelineEvents.description: Jusham suffered defeat alongside other Hawazin tribes against Muslim forces led by Prophet Muhammad

timelineEvents.title: Ridda Wars
timelineEvents.description: Jusham participated in tribal rebellions against the early Muslim state following Muhammad's death

timelineEvents.title: Hilalian Migration
timelineEvents.description: Jusham joined the great westward Bedouin migration as part of Banu Hilal confederation

timelineEvents.title: Almohad Settlement
timelineEvents.description: Settled in Tamasna region of western Morocco by Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur

name: Juwarin
description: The Juwarin were a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula with documented alliance connections to the Al Bu Falah. Limited historical records make definitive statements about their origins, territorial base, and specific role in Gulf politics difficult to establish. Their alliance with the Al Bu Falah suggests some involvement in the broader tribal dynamics of the region. Current status and activities remain unclear from available historical sources.

relations.context: Allied during seasonal migrations and nomadic movements

history: The Juwarin (Al Juwarin) were a tribal group that formed part of the Al Ajwad division within the larger Al-Muntafiq tribal confederation of southern Iraq and Kuwait. As documented in historical records, the Juwarin were among the constituent tribes of this major confederation that traced its origins to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The Al-Muntafiq confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and later held control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. During the Ottoman era, the Juwarin, along with other Muntafiq tribes, gradually transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. Historical accounts from the early 20th century describe the Juwarin as semi-nomadic bedouins who would migrate seasonally alongside other Muntafiq tribes including the Albu Salih, Shuraifat, and Al Aunan. These migrations followed traditional routes through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trading purposes before returning north to their tribal territories. The Juwarin's documented alliance with the Al Bu Falah suggests connections beyond the immediate Muntafiq confederation, indicating their involvement in broader Gulf tribal dynamics and political relationships.

migrationPath.description: Migration to Iraq during Islamic conquests as part of Al-Muntafiq confederation
Seasonal nomadic migrations through Wadi Batin valley to Arabian Gulf coast for trading

timelineEvents.title: Islamic Conquests Migration
timelineEvents.description: Juwarin ancestors migrate to Iraq as part of Al-Muntafiq confederation during Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Al-Ahsa and Al-Qatif Occupation
timelineEvents.description: Al-Muntafiq confederation including Juwarin successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif on behalf of Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid

timelineEvents.title: Conversion to Shia Islam
timelineEvents.description: Most Al-Muntafiq tribes including Juwarin converted to Shia Islam during late Ottoman period

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Shaiba
timelineEvents.description: Juwarin fought alongside other Muntafiq tribes and Ottomans against British forces near Basrah

name: Kababish
legitimacyNotes: Pastoral leadership and territorial control in northern Sudan and cross-border regions
description: The Kababish are a large nomadic tribal confederation primarily based in northern Sudan, known for their extensive camel herding and seasonal migrations. Historically they controlled important trade routes between Sudan and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing commercial partnerships with Gulf tribes. Their traditional territory spans parts of Sudan's Northern and North Kordofan states, with historical connections to Arabian Peninsula tribes through trade networks. While primarily Sudanese-based, their historical trade relationships linked them to Gulf tribal networks.

relations.context: Some Manasir clans migrate to Kababish desert area (Khala' Kabushiyah) during dry season for grazing.

history: The Kababish are a large nomadic tribal confederation primarily based in northern Sudan, comprising approximately 19 different groups led by a single nazir or chief. According to tribal genealogy, they descend from Arab forefathers of the Juhaynah tribe, one of the most powerful Arabian Peninsula tribes originally from the Hejaz region. The Juhaynah were among the first Arab tribes to entirely convert to Islam and participated in early Islamic conquests, with various Juhani groups migrating to Sudan during the 11th century as documented by Ibn Khaldun. The Kababish maintain their traditional nomadic lifestyle as camel herders, which grants them high standing in Arabic society due to the prestige associated with camels. Their migration patterns are distinctive - unlike other nomads, the tribe doesn't move as a single unit; women typically remain at permanent camps (dikkas) while men migrate north toward the Libyan Desert with their herds. The tribe practices Sunni Islam and speaks Sudanese Arabic, though they maintain strong cultural traditions including distinctive dress - women wear long blue cloth wrappings while men don white tunics, loose pants, and turbans, often carrying daggers or rifles due to desert conditions and banditry threats. Since the 1980s famine, many Kababish have adopted semi-nomadic lifestyles or moved to cities, with current population estimates ranging from 70,000 to 350,000, most remaining illiterate. The tribe is noted for practicing cousin marriage, common among Middle Eastern and North African Arab communities, reflecting their maintenance of traditional Arabian Peninsula social structures despite their Sudanese location.

migrationPath.description: Juhaynah tribal migration from Arabian Peninsula to Sudan during 11th century, establishing Kababish confederation in northern Kordofan region

timelineEvents.title: Early Islamic Conversion
timelineEvents.description: Juhaynah tribe becomes first Arab tribe to entirely convert to Islam, participating in early Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Migration to Sudan
timelineEvents.description: Juhaynah groups migrate to Sudan, establishing various tribal groups including the Kababish in northern regions

timelineEvents.title: Famine Crisis
timelineEvents.description: Major famine forces significant lifestyle changes, with many Kababish adopting semi-nomadic life or moving to cities

name: al-Kabanah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and customary leadership
description: Al-Kabanah is a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or role in Gulf regional politics. They appear to be a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories over time.
history: Al-Kabanah appears in Sudanese genealogical records as one of the riverine clans (Manasir al-Nil) of the Manasir people along the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in Northern Sudan. According to Sudanese tribal documentation, al-Kabanah (الكبانة) is listed among the settled Manasir clans who pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, alongside other riverine groups like al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, and al-Diqeisab. The Manasir trace their origins to various ancestral lines including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Qahtani Arabs, descendants of al-Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad), or connections to the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Kabanah, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted a common genealogical identity for mutual rights and territorial claims. The connection between this Sudanese al-Kabanah clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal group of the same name remains undocumented in available sources.

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Al-Kabanah documented as part of Manasir riverine community with total population of 30,000 in Shiri Rural Council area

name: al-Kagbab
history: The al-Kagbab are one of the Bedouin clans of the Manasir tribe, specifically classified among the Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir) of Northern Sudan. They share grazing grounds in the areas of Abisba' and Sani alongside other Bedouin Manasir clans including al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, and al-Muleikab. The al-Kagbab, like other Manasir clans, follow seasonal migration patterns dictated by rainfall and Nile flooding cycles. During the dry season, they migrate to various desert areas including the Kababish tribal territory to the west (Khala' Kabushiyah), the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali Country, or to Rubatab Country. The Manasir as a whole trace their origins to multiple possible lineages according to oral tradition: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, and still others from al-Hankab clan descendants tracing back to King Sabir. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including the al-Kagbab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities, unified through recognition of common genealogical heritage for mutual claims to land and resources.

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Federal Department of Statistics records 30,000 de facto Manasir population including Bedouin clans like al-Kagbab

name: Kahlan
legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Azd
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic inscriptions from 210-230 CE confirming their territorial presence. Genetic evidence suggests J-BY74 haplogroup associated with Azdite tribes originated in Northern Arabia or the Levant.
subTribes.ancestorStory: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches, each led by one of the sons of Muzayqiya
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: trade and conquest - chief merchant group of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organized trading diaspora with settlements on coasts of Kirman and Makran
subTribes.description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Tayy
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Real blood lineage traced to Qahtanite origins, though Ibn Khaldun noted that Tayy intermarried with non-Arabs and did not preserve lineage purity strictly
subTribes.ancestorName: Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy or Tayyi)
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Al Murrah
subTribes.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

relations.context: Himyar overpowered and forced Kahlan tribes out of Yemeni highlands
Tayy branch invaded mountains of Ajaa and Salma from Banu Assad
Tayy branch invaded mountains of Ajaa and Salma from Banu Tamim
Qahtani Yemeni tribe in alliance with Kahlan tribes, settled together in Syria and Northern Egypt
Himyarite tribe that later joined Lakhmids in Northern Egypt
Himyarite tribe that settled in southern part of Lakhmid Kingdom

history: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of the ancient Sabaean kingdom in Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them part of the greater Qahtanite Arab lineage. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, the Kahlani tribes controlled much of the territory around the ancient capital of Marib. By the 2nd century BC, the declining Sabaean state faced pressure from the rising Himyarite kingdom in the southern lowlands. The Himyarites successfully allied with nomadic tribes and gradually incorporated Saba into their domain, forcing the resistant Kahlan tribes out of the Yemeni highlands. Most Kahlani groups retreated to the desert regions around Marib, where they remained until the catastrophic destruction of the great Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD. This pivotal event triggered a massive northward migration of Kahlani tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula, with various branches reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria before the 7th century Islamic conquests. The confederation branched into five major tribal groups: Azd, Hamdan, Lakhm, Tayy, and Kinda, each following different migration routes and establishing distinct settlements across the Arabian world. After the rise of Islam, these Kahlani Arabs, alongside other Qahtanite and Adnanite tribes, spread throughout the expanding Umayyad Empire, reaching the furthest edges of the Islamic world and playing crucial roles in the Arab conquests and subsequent Islamic civilization.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration from highlands after Himyarite conquest of Saba
Major exodus following destruction of Marib Dam, spreading northward through Arabia
Continued migration reaching Mesopotamia and Syria before Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Himyarite Conquest
timelineEvents.description: Himyar incorporates declining Saba kingdom and forces resistant Kahlan tribes from Yemeni highlands

timelineEvents.title: Destruction of Marib Dam
timelineEvents.description: Catastrophic failure of great Marib Dam forces mass Kahlani migration northward through Arabia

timelineEvents.title: Tayy Migration to Najd
timelineEvents.description: Banu Tayy under Usma bin Luai launch massive exodus, invading mountains of Ajaa and Salma in northern Arabia

name: Kawahla
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and customary law
description: The Kawahla are a Bedouin tribe with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They share migratory patterns and routes with the Manasir tribe, indicating traditional pastoral movements across tribal territories. Like many Arabian tribes, they followed seasonal grazing patterns and maintained traditional Bedouin social structures. Their current status and specific territorial associations within the GCC states require further documentation.

relations.context: One theory traces Manasir origin to the Kawahla, claiming they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.
Traditional nomadic routes in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: The Kawahla (also known as Banu Kahil) are an Arab tribe primarily inhabiting Eastern Sudan, speaking Sudanese Arabic and practicing Sunni Islam. According to tribal genealogies, they are a branch of the Banu Udhra tribe, part of the larger Quda'a confederation, though they claim descent from Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, likely to increase their prestige. The tribe includes sub-branches such as the Hasania tribe. Historically, the Kawahla played a significant role in the conquest of Christian Nubia, fighting alongside the Juhayna Arabs to bring down the Christian Nubian kingdom of Makuria in the mid-14th century through conquest and intermarriage, resulting in the establishment of the Arab Islamic Banu Kanz dynasty in Nubia. The tribe has influenced other groups in the region, with the Ababda people of eastern Egypt and Sudan claiming descent from Kawahla through their ancestor Kahil, though maintaining their Beja identity. In the 19th century, European travelers including Hedley Vicars and Lord Edward Gleichen encountered the Kawahla, with Vicars being asked to settle tribal disputes. Today, most Kawahla are farmers growing sorghum, wheat, cotton, beans and fruits, while those in Kordofan practice seasonal migration between wet season pastures and dry season grazing lands near permanent wells.

migrationPath.description: Early migration following Islamic expansion and search for grazing lands
Seasonal movement into Omani mountain valleys for water and pasture
Adaptation to pearl diving economy and coastal trade opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Conquest of Makuria
timelineEvents.description: Kawahla fought with Juhayna Arabs to end the Christian Nubian kingdom of Makuria through conquest and intermarriage

name: al-Khabra
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and pastoral territory rights
description: Al-Khabra is a minor tribal group historically associated with the Arabian Peninsula's pastoral traditions. Limited historical documentation exists about this particular tribal branch, suggesting they may have been a smaller subdivision of a larger tribal confederation. Their activities appear to have been primarily focused on traditional nomadic pastoralism in the interior regions. Current status and contemporary presence in GCC states is unclear from available historical sources.
history: Al-Khabra appears to be a tribal group or settlement associated with the Al-Qassim Province region of Saudi Arabia. The research reveals that there are at least two localities named Al-Khabra in the Qassim region: Al-Khabra itself and Riyadh Al-Khabra, which has its own general hospital with 50 beds, indicating it is a recognized administrative center. The mention of 'Eid Al-Khabra Festival' in the tourism section suggests the group maintains cultural traditions and community identity through organized celebrations that highlight local heritage. Al-Qassim Province has been historically significant as part of the Najd region and has experienced various phases of tribal rule, including periods under the Banu Tamim, Subay, and Anazzah tribes during the 16th to early 20th centuries, before integration into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1907. The region's central location in the Arabian Peninsula, crossed by the Wadi Al-Rummah valley system, has made it a crossroads for trade and pilgrimage routes. However, specific genealogical information, tribal affiliations, or detailed historical documentation about al-Khabra as a distinct tribal entity is not provided in the available research, suggesting they may have been a smaller subdivision within the broader tribal confederations that historically controlled the Qassim region.

timelineEvents.title: Integration into Saudi Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Al-Qassim Province, including Al-Khabra settlements, was integrated under the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

name: Khanabil
history: The Khanabil are a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe that inhabits the northern Hajar Mountains and Musandam Peninsula of the UAE and Oman. The Bani Shatair, which includes the Khanabil alongside the Kumazarah, Mahabib, and Maqadilah subsections, are primarily centered around Kumzar at the tip of the peninsula. The Shihuh tribe as a whole incorporates elements of Iranian origin, with the Arab majority thought to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves that brought Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people settled around the Rus Al Jibal mountains and coastal areas. The Khanabil, as part of the Bani Shatair division, would have participated in the tribe's seasonal migration patterns - forming agricultural communities in stone-built mountain settlements during winter months, then migrating to work the date harvest and pearl fishing seasons in places like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern due to their mountainous terrain, frequently conflicted with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, and generally preferred Muscat's suzerainty. They played significant roles in regional conflicts, including the 1921-1922 battle for Rams and various disputes that shaped the political boundaries of towns like Dibba and Kalba.

timelineEvents.title: Malik bin Fahm Migration
timelineEvents.description: Wave of Yemeni immigration bringing Malik bin Fahm groups north, forming Arab element of Shihuh tribe

timelineEvents.title: Dibba Wali Killed
timelineEvents.description: Shihuh tribesmen killed the wali of Dibba, establishing pattern of rivalry between townsfolk and tribe

timelineEvents.title: Battle for Rams Begins
timelineEvents.description: Fighting broke out between Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi and Shihuh allied with Abdelrahman bin Saleh Al Tanaiji

timelineEvents.title: Final Treaty at Rams
timelineEvents.description: Treaty signed on February 22 breaking ties between Shihuh and Muhammad bin Saleh, ending the conflict

timelineEvents.title: British Relocation Proposal
timelineEvents.description: British proposed relocating Shihuh to Kalba and giving them control of Shamailiyah to reduce conflicts, but proposal failed

name: Khanazirah
description: Khanazirah appears to be a minor tribal group or branch historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records exist about this particular tribal entity, and their specific role in Gulf regional politics or economy remains unclear. They may represent a sub-branch or clan within a larger tribal confederation. Current status and activities are not well documented in major historical sources.
history: The Khanazirah are a subsection of the Bani Hadiyah, one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe that inhabits the northern Hajar Mountains in the Musandam Peninsula of Oman and UAE. Along with the Bani Muhammad, Bani Ali, and Bani Ham Mazyud, the Khanazirah form part of the Bani Hadiyah section, which is distinct from the other major Shihuh section, the Bani Shatair. The Khanazirah historically occupied tribal areas around As Sayh and the region of Jabal Al Harim, the highest peak in the Musandam Peninsula at 2,087 meters elevation. Like other Shihuh subsections, the Khanazirah were semi-nomadic, practicing seasonal migration between mountain agriculture in winter and coastal work during warmer months, particularly in date harvesting and pearl fishing. The Shihuh tribe as a whole, including the Khanazirah, numbered approximately 21,500 people at the turn of the 20th century, with the Bani Hadiyah section primarily settled on the western coast of the UAE and around Khasab, Oman. The tribe's origins include Arab elements thought to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves of Malik bin Fahm in the second century, though some subsections incorporate Iranian elements. The Shihuh, including the Khanazirah, were historically difficult to govern due to their mountain terrain, frequently coming into conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah while generally accepting Omani suzerainty.

timelineEvents.title: Battle for Rams
timelineEvents.description: Fighting broke out between Shihuh forces (including various subsections) and Ras Al Khaimah forces led by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi over control of Rams

timelineEvents.title: Rams Treaty Settlement
timelineEvents.description: Final treaty signed on February 22, 1922, breaking ties between Shihuh forces and local allies, ending the Rams conflict

timelineEvents.title: Shihuh Relocation Proposal
timelineEvents.description: British proposed relocating Shihuh tribes to Kalba to reduce conflicts with settled populations, but proposal came to nothing

name: Khath'am
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani tribal confederation with documented pre-Islamic lineage
ancestorStory: Khath'am traces ancestry to Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Arash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghauth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zaid ibn Kahlan. The tribe formed a major confederation in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Khath'am is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the regions between Najran and the Asir mountains. They were prominent in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, with documented participation in the Ridda Wars and early Islamic conquests. The tribe maintained traditional territories in what is now southern Saudi Arabia and has historical connections to other major southwestern Arabian tribes. Today, Khath'am descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula, including in GCC states.

relations.context: Ghamid assisted Khath'am against Banu Al-Harith despite previous problems between them
Both trace lineage to Qahtani ancestry

history: Khath'am was an ancient Arab tribal confederation that dwelt in southwestern Arabia, particularly in the mountainous regions between Najran and Ta'if, including the areas of Turubah, Bisha, Tabala, and Jurash. From at least the 6th century CE, they controlled territory along the caravan route between Yemen and Mecca. The tribe's genealogical origins were disputed by medieval Arab historians - Ibn Hisham and Ibn Qutayba claimed they were Adnanites descended from Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Nizar, while Ibn al-Kalbi held they were Sabaean descendants through Khath'am (birth name Aftal) ibn Anmar ibn Irash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghawth. Modern historian Giorgio Levi Della Vida suggests they were likely a confederation of smaller tribes with different origins, with their name possibly deriving from takhath'ama meaning 'to smear oneself with blood' as a result of a pact. The main clans included Shahran, Nahish, and Aklub, the latter said to have descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar and joined through alliance. At Tabala, they worshipped the cult of Dhu'l-Khalasa alongside the Bajila, Bahila, and Banu Daws tribes. In the mid-6th century, they were involved in Abraha's expedition against Mecca, with conflicting accounts of their role - some sources claim they formed part of his army but refused to participate in destroying the Kaaba, while others state they fought against Abraha under their chieftain Nufayl ibn Habib and were defeated. Their most famous pre-Islamic battle was the day of Fayf al-Rih, where under warrior poet Anas ibn Mudrik, they led an alliance including Madhhij that defeated the Banu Amir. Initially hostile to Islam, they eventually sent a delegation accepting the religion, with Muhammad declaring all their pre-Islamic blood feuds abolished. A section rebelled during the Ridda Wars but ultimately submitted after the destruction of their Dhu'l-Khalasa shrine by Jarir ibn Abd Allah al-Bajali. Members participated in the early Muslim conquests in Syria and Iraq during the 630s, forming part of the Arab garrisons of Kufa and Basra. The tribe maintained connections with other southwestern Arabian tribes and their descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula today, including in GCC states.

name: Khawatir
legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

relations.context: Shared wells and frequently allied, particularly in opposing oil exploration in the late 1940s

history: The Khawatir (singular Khateri الخاطري) is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates and northern Oman, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe alongside the Al Bu Shamis and Al Bu Kharaiban. Originally a Bedouin tribe, the Khawatir's traditional territory (dar) stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, with most settling around the Buraimi oasis. In 1906, Lorimer documented approximately 500 Khawatir living as nomadic Bedouin in the Jiri Plain, possessing substantial wealth including 800 camels, 70 donkeys, 1,500 sheep and goats, and 100 cattle. An additional 150 houses of settled Khawatir were recorded at Hafit southeast of Buraimi, with 900 date palms, 1,000 sheep and goats, and 200 camels. Resentful of encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies in the 19th century, the Khawatir adopted Wahhabism as their doctrine. Following Saudi withdrawal in 1871, they submitted to Muscat but later became dependent on Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan after being pressured by his expansion in the Buraimi/Al Ain oasis area. The tribe frequently clashed with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but generally allied with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. By the 1940s, the Khawatir were considered independent of Muscat, but came under Saudi influence again in the 1950s, participating in the Buraimi dispute. Beyond Buraimi, the Khawatir also settled in Dhaid, a Sharjah dependency, where tensions with Bani Qitab neighbors culminated in Khawatir victory in 1927. The following year, a settlement led by Na'im chiefs resulted in the ascension of deposed Sharjah Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Qasimi to rule Dhaid, with Khawatir support for his failed attempt to retake Sharjah, which was prevented by British intervention.
