name: Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani lineage through Harb confederation
ancestorName: Khawlan ibn Amr ibn al-Haf ibn Quda'ah
ancestorStory: Descended from Khawlan, an ancient Qahtani ancestor who settled in the Arabian Peninsula. This branch became affiliated with the larger Harb confederation over time.
description: Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb represents a Qahtani tribal branch that became integrated within the broader Harb confederation. Historically based in the Hejaz region, they maintained their distinct Qahtani identity while participating in the larger Harb tribal structure. The tribe followed traditional Bedouin pastoral economy and participated in the complex intertribal relationships of western Arabian Peninsula. Their current status is largely historical, with descendants likely absorbed into other tribal groupings.

relations.context: Both claim descent from Qahtani ancestors

history: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. Originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, the tribe experienced significant growth that led to overcrowding in their traditional territories of Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz. When the tribe gathered to decide their course of action, they rejected the option of conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe) and instead chose to migrate. The tribe moved northward to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region in Jordan, where they became so numerous that they outnumbered the largest local tribes. The Mahamid formed alliances with local Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily ruled by the Al-Amru clan. Their rule lasted for an extended period, though some orientalists and historians recorded that they were harsh rulers, eventually prompting various clans to unite against them. Today, the tribe has spread extensively throughout the Arabian Peninsula, the Levant, Iraq, and North Africa, with numerous branches established in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Syria, Jordan, Palestine, and Iraq.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz to northern Arabian settlement
Movement from northern Arabia toward the Levant, settling in Karak region

name: Khuza'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic nobility and custodianship of Mecca
ancestorStory: Named after Khuza'a ibn Amr, the tribe is said to have separated (khuza'a meaning 'to separate') from other Azd tribes during their migration from Yemen. They established themselves in the Hijaz region around Mecca.
description: Khuza'a was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that controlled Mecca and its surrounding areas before the rise of Quraysh. They were the custodians of the Kaaba for several centuries and played a crucial role in Arabian trade routes. The tribe allied with Prophet Muhammad during the conquest of Mecca, which was partly triggered by a conflict between Khuza'a and their rivals. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history, they are not prominently active in modern GCC tribal politics, having been absorbed into larger tribal confederations over time.
history: Khuza'a (Banu Khuzā'ah) is an ancient Azdite, Qahtanite tribe that emerged as one of the main ancestral tribes of Arabia. Most traditionalists trace their origins to Amr ibn Luhay (also known as Amr ibn Rabiah), who belonged to the Azd confederation that migrated north from Yemen following the breach of the Marib dam around the 5th century CE. The tribe's name derives from the Arabic root meaning 'to separate' (khuza'a), as they broke away from other Azd tribes during this great migration and settled around Mecca in the Hijaz region. For approximately 500 years before Islam, Khuza'a ruled Mecca as custodians of the Kaaba, having expelled the previous Jurhum tribe guardians. Amr ibn Luhay, their first ruler of the Sacred House, is credited with introducing idol worship to Mecca, including the famous idol Hubal brought from Syria-Palestine. The tribe's control ended in the 5th century CE when Qusayy ibn Kilab of Quraysh married into the Khuza'i ruling family and eventually seized control through the famous 'Day of Abṭaḥ' battle. During Muhammad's time, Khuza'a formed a crucial alliance with the Prophet, participating in the Treaty of Hudaybiya in 628 CE. When their Bakr ibn Abd Manat enemies attacked them at al-Watīr in 630 CE with Qurayshi support, this breach of the treaty provided the justification for Muhammad's conquest of Mecca. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic history and the Abbasid rise to power. Later, the Khaza'il branch established the Kingdom of Khaza'il (1534-1921) in Mesopotamia, controlling the Middle and Lower Euphrates region and resisting Ottoman rule for centuries. Today, Khuza'a descendants live primarily in Saudi Arabia around Mecca, with significant populations in Iraq, Jordan, Palestine, and other Middle Eastern countries, organized into various recognized family branches.

migrationPath.description: Great Azd migration north following the Marib dam breach, with Khuza'a breaking away to settle around Mecca
Khaza'il branch establishes kingdom in Mesopotamia, controlling territory from Anah to Basra

name: Kiawan
description: Kiawan appears to be a tribal name with limited documented historical presence in major Arabian Peninsula genealogical sources. Without reliable historical documentation, details about their origins, territorial associations, or role in Gulf regional politics cannot be definitively established. The name does not appear prominently in well-documented tribal confederations or major historical accounts of the GCC region. Further research would be needed to establish their historical significance and current status.
history: According to the research, Kiawan is identified as a branch of the Mahamid tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When their population grew and their original territories became overcrowded, the tribe made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in conflict with neighboring Harb clans. The research indicates that Kiawan emerged as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid, alongside other sub-groups including alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad. The Mahamid's migration pattern took them from their Yemeni and Hijazi origins northward through Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant, eventually reaching and settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid before further dispersing throughout the region including Palestine.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz to northern settlement
Movement toward the Levant and establishment in Karak region
Dispersal into Palestine where Kiawan branch was established

name: Kinana
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with noble lineage tracing to Adnan
ancestorStory: Kinana was the son of Khuzayma ibn Mudrika, establishing one of the most prominent tribes of the Hijaz. The tribe became the parent lineage of Quraysh and gained prestige through this connection.
description: Kinana was a major ancient Arabian tribe of Adnani lineage based in the Hijaz region, particularly around Mecca and the surrounding areas. They are historically significant as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad, which elevated their status considerably. The tribe played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic history through their Qurayshi descendants. While the main tribal identity became subsumed under Quraysh and other sub-tribes, their genealogical significance remains paramount in Arabian tribal heritage.

relations.context: Quraysh descended from Kinana tribe, fought together in Fijar War
Some Saudi ruling family genealogies trace connections through Adnani lineage

history: The Kinana tribe (كنانة) is one of the most prestigious Arab tribes of Adnani lineage, tracing their ancestry to Kinana ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, ultimately descending from Ishmael. Their traditional territory extended from the Tihama coastline near Mecca northeastward to the borders of their tribal relatives, the Banu Asad. The tribe consisted of six principal branches: al-Nadr (who became the parent tribe of Quraysh), Malik, Milkan, Amir, Amr, and Abd Manat. The Abd Manat included particularly strong subgroups like Bakr ibn Abd Manat, whose main branches were the Mudlij, Du'il, Layth, and Damra. The Ghifar subgroup belonged to the Damra, while the Harith ibn Abd Manat formed the core of the Ahabish confederation. Kinana played a crucial role in Arabian history as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad. Their ancestor Fihr ibn Malik emerged as leader of Kinana in their victory against a branch of the Himyarites, and his descendant Qusayy ibn Kilab was backed by Kinana in capturing Mecca. During the early Islamic period, various Kinana subgroups had different responses to Islam - while Quraysh initially opposed Muhammad, the Ghifar, Layth, and Damra supported his conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. Following the Muslim conquests, Kinana became one of the main components of the Arab tribal garrison of Jund Filastin (Palestine), alongside Judham, Lakhm, Khuza'a, Khath'am, and Azd. Caliph Umar appointed two Kinani governors over Palestine around 638-639 CE. The tribe maintained significant presence in Palestine until the Crusades, when their emirs fled to Fatimid Egypt in 1153 CE and were resettled in Damietta as the Kinaniyya. Under the Ayyubids, they served as second-tier troops and fought against the Crusaders. During the Seventh Crusade in 1249 CE, the Kinani garrison of Damietta fled at the sight of Louis IX's forces, leading to executions of their commanders. Today, Kinana descendants are found throughout the Arab world, with major concentrations remaining in their original Hijazi territories around Mecca and the Tihamah coast, as well as in Iraq, Jordan, Sudan, Egypt, Palestine, and other regions where they migrated during the Islamic conquests.

migrationPath.description: Migration as part of Arab tribal garrison of Jund Filastin following Muslim conquest
Kinana emirs and fief-holders fled to Fatimid Egypt after Crusader capture of Ascalon
Migration of Kinana tribes to Sudan via the Nile route

name: Kingdom of Kinda
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian kingdom with royal lineage claiming descent from Kahlan
ancestorStory: Kinda descended from Kahlan through Afsa, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms. The tribe produced kings who ruled over various Arabian tribes in Najd and Yemen.
description: The Kingdom of Kinda was one of the most important pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms, ruling over much of central and eastern Arabia from the 5th-6th centuries CE. Based primarily in Najd, they extended their influence from Yemen to the borders of the Sassanian Empire, serving as kings over various tribal confederations. The Kindite dynasty produced famous rulers like Hujr ibn Amr and his sons, and the poet-prince Imru al-Qays. While the kingdom fragmented before Islam, Kindite descendants remain scattered across the Arabian Peninsula, including some presence in modern GCC states.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history: The Kingdom of Kinda emerged in the mid-5th century CE when part of the Kinda tribe, with support from the Himyarite Kingdom, migrated from their ancestral homeland in Hadramawt to central and northern Arabia. Led by Hujr, founder of the royal house Banu Akil al-Murar, they established dominance over the large Arab tribal confederation of Ma'add at the initiative of the Ma'add tribes themselves, particularly the Bakr, who sought a prominent, non-involved leader to bring stability during constant feuding. This represented the first known nomadic Arab monarchy and the first attempt at centralized tribal governance, ruling from urban settlements including their capital Ghamr Dhi Kinda (modern Qaryat al-Faw). After Hujr's peaceful reign, his sons Amr al-Maqsur and Mu'awiya al-Jawn ruled Najd and Yamama respectively, but Amr was killed by the rebellious Rabi'a tribes. The kingdom reached its zenith under al-Harith ibn Amr, who became a Byzantine federate around 502 CE and briefly captured the Lakhmid capital al-Hira, adopting Mazdakism during his rule there. Al-Harith divided the Ma'add confederation among his four sons (Hujr, Ma'dikarib, Shurahbil, and Salama), but this led to devastating civil wars including the famous Battle of al-Kulab where Shurahbil was killed. By the late 6th century, after defeats at Shi'b Jabala and other battles, the weakened Kindites abandoned central Arabia and returned to Hadramawt with some 30,000 members. During the Islamic period, the Kinda initially accepted Islam under Muhammad through deputations led by al-Ash'ath ibn Qays and the Wali'a brothers, but rebelled during the Ridda Wars (632-633 CE) when tax revenues were withheld. After being defeated at the fortress of Nujayr, surviving Kindite leaders like al-Ash'ath played important roles in the early Islamic conquests and served in high governmental positions throughout the Caliphate, though the pre-Islamic royal houses had lost their prominence.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Kinda under Hujr from South Arabia to establish kingdom over Ma'add confederation in central Arabia
Return migration of 30,000 Kindites to ancestral homeland after kingdom's collapse, abandoning settlements at Ghamr Dhi Kinda, Hajar, and al-Mushaqqar

name: Kordshuli
history: The Kordshuli (Kurdish: Kurdşûlî, meaning 'Kurdish villagers') is a nomadic and pastoral Kurdish tribe that settled in Fars province, Iran during the 19th century. Originally speaking Laki, a Kurdish dialect, they established their main settlement at Khonjesht in what is now Eqlid County. The tribe has an unusual historical connection to the Qashqai confederation, briefly becoming part of this Turkic tribal federation before regaining their independence in the early 20th century. Population surveys show their nomadic lifestyle persisted well into the modern era - in 1960, they numbered around 600 households with most maintaining nomadic practices, and by the 1970s they had 353 households of which 296 remained nomadic. Today, the Kordshuli maintain a presence in several villages including Khonjesht (their main settlement) and Cheshmeh Rana, where they form the majority population. The tribe represents one of the Kurdish communities that migrated south from traditional Kurdish territories into Fars province, adapting their pastoralist traditions to the southern Iranian environment while maintaining their distinct cultural identity.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Fars province during the 19th century

name: Kumazarah
history: The Kumazarah are a subsection of the Bani Shatair branch of the Shihuh tribe, inhabiting the Musandam Peninsula in northern Oman and the UAE. Unlike other Arab elements of the Shihuh tribe, the Kumazarah incorporate Iranian elements and speak the Kumzari language, an Iranian (Iranic) language with heavy Arabic, Portuguese, English, and Balochi influences. Bertram Thomas described their dialect as 'a strange tongue which has baffled and confused strangers. It is a compound of Arabic and Persian but it is distinct from them both, and is intelligible neither to the Arab nor to the Persian nor yet to the linguist of both.' The Bani Shatair, including the Kumazarah, are centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula. The Kumazarah people are said to have Persian-related origins, with some sources claiming they traveled to the northern coast of Oman 500 years ago, while others suggest they originated from the Azd tribe who came to Yemen in the third to fifth centuries AD. As part of the Shihuh confederation, they maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, practicing agriculture in winter months in stone-built mountain settlements and migrating to work during date seasons in places like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast. They also participated in pearl fishing and maintained large goat herds for trade.

migrationPath.description: Persian-related people traveled to northern coast of Oman according to one source

name: Kumzarah
description: No reliable historical records or genealogical sources have been identified for a tribe specifically named Kumzarah in Arabian Peninsula tribal literature or GCC historical documentation. This name does not appear in established tribal genealogies or chronicles of Gulf region tribes. Without verifiable sources, no definitive information can be provided about their origins, territory, or role in Gulf history.
history: The Kumzarah (also spelled Kumazarah) is a subsection of the Shihuh tribe, specifically belonging to the Bani Shatair division. According to tribal genealogy, the Shihuh are divided into two main sections: the Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair, with the Kumzarah being one of the subsections under Bani Shatair alongside the Khanabil, Mahabib, and Maqadilah. The Kumzarah are distinguished by their unique linguistic heritage, speaking an Iranian language that sets them apart from other Arab tribal groups. As described by explorer Bertram Thomas, their dialect is 'a strange tongue which has baffled and confused strangers. It is a compound of Arabic and Persian but it is distinct from them both, and is intelligible neither to the Arab nor to the Persian nor yet to the linguist of both.' This linguistic distinctiveness reflects the Iranian elements incorporated within the broader Shihuh tribal confederation. Research indicates that while the majority Arab element of the Shihuh tribe is thought to be linked to the wave of Yemeni immigration that brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century, the Kumzarah subsection represents the Iranian component of this diverse tribal makeup. Like other Shihuh groups, the Kumzarah traditionally inhabited the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region, and followed the seasonal migration patterns typical of their tribe - engaging in agriculture in the mountains during winter months and migrating to coastal areas for pearl diving and date work during warmer seasons.

name: Kunud
legitimacyNotes: Claims lineage to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, an immigrant from Yemen during the time of Prophet Muhammad. The connection to the Pre-Islamic tribal confederation that fought with Sasanian Lakhmid clients suggests historical tribal roots.
ancestorStory: The Kunud have been linked to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, an immigrant into Oman from Yemen at the time of the Islamic prophet, Muhammad
peakPowerEra: Pre-Islamic Recent to early Islamic period
description: The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman. They trace their origin to the tribal confederation that fought with the Sasanian's Lakhmid clients in the late Pre-Islamic Recent period. By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud, with a population of some 1,500, were mostly settled on the East Coast. They have traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith and have long been associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi).

relations.context: Long associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi)

history: The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and Oman that traces its origin to the tribal confederation that fought with the Sasanian's Lakhmid clients in the late Pre-Islamic Recent (PIR) period. The tribe has been linked to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, an immigrant into Oman from Yemen at the time of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud had a population of approximately 1,500 and were mostly settled along the East Coast, from Nizwa in Oman to Kalba in Sharjah and Al Hayl in Fujairah, as well as Mahdah and Buraimi. The tribe has long been associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi) and traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith. In Al Hayl village, which was abandoned in the late 1970s, the Kunud shared settlement with the Jalajilah tribe, with archaeological evidence showing the area has been inhabited since the Umm Al Nar period. The village consisted of around ten houses according to Lorimer's 1908 description, and inhabitants were later resettled to a new village further down the wadi under protection of the Al Hayl Dam.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi from Yemen to Oman during the time of Prophet Muhammad

name: Kuruni
legitimacyNotes: Kurdish tribal group with mixed Arabian integration
description: The Kuruni appear to be a tribal group with Kurdish origins or connections, as suggested by their alternative designation as Kurds. Historical records of Kurdish tribal elements in the Arabian Peninsula are limited and often relate to migration patterns or military service. Their specific role in GCC tribal structures and current status remains unclear from available historical sources.
history: The Kuruni are a Kurdish tribe originally from the Kermanshah area of Iran. In the 1760s, most of the tribe was transplanted to the Shiraz area in Fars province, southern Iran, by Karim Khan Zand when he established his capital there. After the fall of the Zand dynasty, the tribe faced forced relocation under Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar and was incorporated into the Kashkollu Qashqai tribal confederacy. During this period, they gradually became Turcophone and adopted Shia Islam. By the early 1900s, parts of the tribe had dispersed into three groups around Shiraz: some moved to the Beyza valley northwest of the city, others settled in Shiraz itself where they founded the Koruni neighborhood, while a third group remained with their traditional territories. As late as the 1950s, 150 Kuruni members were still part of the Qashqai confederation. However, when the central government implemented anti-Qashqai policies in the 1960s, the tribe left the confederation and reasserted their distinct Kuruni identity. This historical trajectory places the Kuruni within the broader context of Kurdish tribal movements in Iran, but there is no evidence in available sources of any connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal structures or the GCC region.

name: Lakhm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal lineage, established the Lakhmid Kingdom
ancestorStory: Lakhm ibn Adi was the progenitor of the tribe that established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab kingdoms. The Lakhmids ruled as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire from their capital at Al-Hirah.
description: Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the Lakhmid Kingdom, ruling from Al-Hirah in southern Iraq from approximately 300-602 CE. They served as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire and controlled important trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids were among the most powerful Arab dynasties before Islam, known for their patronage of Arabic poetry and culture. Their kingdom fell to the Sassanids in 602 CE, and the tribe was later absorbed into the broader Islamic expansion.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical presence in southern Iraq region

history: The Lakhm tribe established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab kingdoms, ruling as the Lakhmid dynasty from their capital at Al-Hirah in southern Iraq from approximately 293-602 CE. Founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr (r. 293-302), they served as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire, controlling crucial trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. After Amr's successor Imru al-Qays I initially converted to Christianity and defected to the Roman Empire, little is heard of the Lakhmids until the 5th century when they re-emerged as commanders in Sassanid campaigns against Byzantine forces. The kingdom reached its zenith under Al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554), who defeated the Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 AD and extended Sassanid influence into southern and western Arabia. However, the last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), embraced Christianity and was subsequently assassinated by Sassanid emperor Khosrow II in 602, ending the kingdom and inadvertently paving the way for Arab Muslim conquests. Meanwhile, sections of the Lakhm had settled in Byzantine Syria from the 4th century, dwelling among the Judham and Amila tribes in southern Syria and Palestine. During the Islamic conquests, Lakhm tribesmen fought on both Byzantine and Muslim sides at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636, reflecting internal tribal divisions. Under Islamic rule, they were incorporated into Jund Filastin (military district of Palestine) alongside Judham, serving the Umayyad Caliphate while maintaining their prestigious tribal identity linked to memories of the glorious Lakhmid kings of Al-Hirah.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Lakhm section to Syria under Imru al-Qays I, evidenced by Namara inscription
Integration into Islamic military districts following Muslim conquest of Syria

name: Lakhmid
legitimacyNotes: Sassanid-backed Arab client kingdom rulers of Hira
ancestorStory: The Lakhmids were descendants of Lakhm ibn Adi, establishing their kingdom at Hira under Sassanid patronage. They served as buffer rulers between the Persian Empire and Arabian tribes.
description: The Lakhmids were an Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of Hira in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE as Sassanid clients. They controlled key trade routes and served as intermediaries between the Persian Empire and Arabian Peninsula tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under rulers like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, wielding significant influence over Arabian tribal affairs. Their kingdom was dissolved by the Sassanids in 602 CE and later conquered by Muslim Arabs during the Islamic expansion.

relations.context: They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
both trace ancestry to Qahtanite origins in southern Iraq region

history: The Lakhmids were an Arab dynasty of the Banu Lakhm tribe that ruled a vassal kingdom of the Sasanian Empire from the late 3rd to early 7th centuries CE, with their capital at al-Hira in southern Iraq. Founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr around 293-302 CE, who is identified in Pahlavi and Coptic inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm,' the kingdom served as a crucial buffer state protecting Persia's western frontier from nomadic Arab incursions. After an early period where Amr's successor Imru al-Qays I defected to Rome, little is heard of the Lakhmids until the 5th century when they re-emerged as powerful commanders in Sasanian campaigns. The dynasty reached its apex under al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554), who defeated the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 and forced the Romans to pay tribute. Al-Mundhir III helped extend Sasanian influence across southern and western Arabia during his fifty-year reign. The kingdom's decline began after his death, culminating when the last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), converted to Christianity and was subsequently deposed and executed by Sasanian emperor Khosrow II in 602. This dissolution removed the Persians' Arab buffer, contributing to their defeat by the Banu Bakr at Dhi Qar and ultimately paving the way for the Muslim conquest of Iraq in the 630s. Branches of the Lakhm tribe also established themselves in Byzantine Syria from the 4th century, fighting both for and against the Muslims during the conquest period before being incorporated into the Umayyad military structure in Palestine alongside the Judham tribe.

name: Lakhmids
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arab dynasty ruling as vassals of the Sassanian Empire
ancestorStory: The Lakhmids traced their lineage to Lakham ibn Adi, establishing their dynasty in al-Hirah. They became prominent rulers under Sassanian patronage, controlling much of the Arabian Peninsula's northeastern regions.
description: The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of al-Hirah in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries. They served as vassals to the Sassanian Empire, controlling trade routes and acting as a buffer against Byzantine influence and nomadic Arab tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under kings like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, extending influence across much of eastern Arabia including areas that would become part of modern Gulf states. They were conquered by the Rashidun Caliphate in 602 CE, ending their rule before the rise of the modern Gulf political structures.

history: The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty descended from the Banu Lakhm tribe, tracing their lineage to Kahlan ibn Saba and the broader Qahtani confederation. Founded by Amr ibn Adi (r. 293-302 CE), they established their kingdom centered at al-Hirah in southern Mesopotamia, serving as vassals to the Sassanian Empire. The dynasty emerged after the collapse of the Kingdom of Hatra and Palmyrene Empire, filling a crucial buffer role between Persian and Roman spheres. Under al-Mundhir III (r. 505-554 CE), the Lakhmids reached their zenith, defeating the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 CE and extending influence across eastern Arabia including regions of modern Gulf states. The kingdom maintained sophisticated Persian-influenced court culture while projecting Sassanian hegemony into the Arabian Peninsula and protecting against nomadic tribal incursions. Christianity gradually spread through al-Hirah, culminating in the conversion of the last king al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602 CE). However, Sassanian emperor Khosrow II executed al-Nu'man III in 602 CE, ending the dynasty and bringing Lakhmid territories under direct Persian control. This dissolution removed a crucial Arab buffer state, inadvertently paving the way for the successful Arab Muslim conquest of Iraq in the 630s under Khalid ibn al-Walid. Lakhmid descendants later established the Abbadid dynasty in 11th-century al-Andalus, while other branches of Banu Lakhm settled across Syria, Palestine, and Egypt during the Islamic conquests.

Branch of Lakhm under Imru al-Qays I moved to Syria and converted to Christianity

name: Madhhij
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani confederation with established genealogical legitimacy through Madhhij ibn Adad
ancestorStory: Madhhij was the son of Adad ibn Zayd ibn Yashjub ibn Urayb ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba. The tribe descended from this eponymous ancestor and became one of the major Qahtani confederations.
description: Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtani tribal confederations of southern Arabia, historically based in Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation played a significant role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic conquests, with various branches migrating throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Many of their sub-tribes integrated into other tribal structures over time, with some branches settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage
Al Kathiri claim descent from Madhhij confederation

history: Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtanite Arab tribal confederations that originated in southern Arabia, specifically Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation descended from the eponymous ancestor Madhhij, son of Adad ibn Zayd ibn Yashjub ibn Urayb ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba, establishing their Qahtanite lineage. The tribe is documented in early historical sources, including the Namara inscription of 328 CE, which records that the Nasrid king of al-Hira, Imru al-Qays bin Amr, launched raids against Madhhij territories reaching Najran. Al-Hamdani's 10th-century work 'Description of the Arabian Peninsula' cited Madhhij thirty times, documenting their major branches including Nukha, Zubaid, Ruha, and Hada - the latter renowned as the best archers among the Arabs. The confederation produced notable historical figures such as Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi al-Madhhaji, the legendary Yemeni knight-king who later converted to Islam, and Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakha'i, a close companion of Prophet Muhammad and military commander under Ali ibn Abi Talib. Before Islam, Madhhij maintained their own religious practices, including bringing a tribal idol to the annual pilgrimage at the Kaaba with specific ritual chants. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic conquests, particularly in campaigns against the Persian and Byzantine empires, with Prophet Muhammad reportedly stating that many people of Paradise would come from Madhhaj, praising their noble qualities of honor, bravery, justice, and wisdom. After the establishment of Kufa as a garrison city, many Madhhij members, including Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib, settled there. The confederation later fought against the Qarmatians in Yemen under leaders like Abul Ashira and Malik ibn Marara al-Rahawi. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity, with al-Hamdani's commentaries indicating that descendants still lived in the same regions he documented over 1100 years ago.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Kufa garrison city after Islamic conquests, including chief Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib

name: Mahabib
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and consensus
description: The Mahabib are a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula region. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are primarily associated with nomadic pastoral activities. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and major historical events. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern era.
history: The Mahabib are a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe (Arabic: الشحوح, al-Shiḥuḥ) that inhabits the Musandam Peninsula and northern Hajar Mountains. The Shihuh tribe splits into Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair, with the Bani Shatair further divided into the Khanabil, Kumazarah, Mahabib, and Maqadilah subsections. The Mahabib historically occupied specific tribal territories in the mountainous regions of what is now the UAE-Oman border area, particularly around Wadi Rahabah and Wadi Halhal in the Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah. Like other Shihuh subsections, the Mahabib maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, practicing seasonal agriculture in stone-built mountain settlements during winter months and migrating to coastal areas for date harvests and pearl diving during warmer periods. The Arab element of the Shihuh, including the Mahabib, is believed to trace back to Yemeni immigration waves led by Malik bin Fahm groups in the second century. The tribe maintained large goat herds and practiced terraced agriculture using mountain water runoff. In recent years, the traditional Mahabib tribal areas around Wadi Rahabah have been significantly impacted by intensive open-pit mining operations from the neighboring Stevin Rock quarry, owned by the Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah, which has practically devastated much of their ancestral territory.

timelineEvents.title: Yemeni Migration Wave

name: Mahamid
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Harb tribe through Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah lineage, established political emirate in Karak region
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage
description: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. Originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, they spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Maghreb. They are known for their frequent travels, emigration and instability. When their population grew and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to decide their future. They rejected conquering neighboring tribes (many being from Harb) and instead chose to migrate. They established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region after moving through Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant, where they became one of the largest tribes and allied with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba.

history: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe tracing their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage, part of the greater Qahtani tribal confederation. Originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, they became known for their frequent travels and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When their population grew and their traditional areas in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow, tribal elders gathered to decide their future. They rejected conquering neighboring tribes, many of whom were fellow Harb clans, and instead chose migration. The tribe moved north through Dumat Al-Jandal, then continued toward the Levant, eventually settling in the Karak region of Jordan where they established the Emirate of Mahamid. There they became one of the largest tribes in the region and formed alliances with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. Their emirate lasted for an extended period, though some sources suggest their rule was eventually opposed by other tribal confederations. Today, the Mahamid have spread across multiple countries including Saudi Arabia (with branches like alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir), Kuwait, Syria, Jordan, Palestine, Iraq, and Sudan. In Sudan, they form part of the Northern Rizeigat confederation, with the Um Jalul clan exercising tribal leadership. The tribe maintains its identity as part of the larger Harb tribal federation, which itself traces back to Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan.

name: Mahashir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabia
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Mahshar, an ancestor connected to the broader Qahtani tribal confederation. They established themselves in the eastern regions of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Mahashir are a tribal branch with historical presence in eastern Arabia, particularly in areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally involved in pastoralism and participated in the complex tribal politics of the region. The tribe maintained connections with other Qahtani tribes and played a role in the social and economic networks of the Gulf region. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal structure in modern Gulf states.

relations.context: Both tribes share Qahtani origins and historical presence in eastern Arabia
Historical cooperation in eastern Arabian tribal affairs

history: The Mahashir are documented as one of the main branches of the powerful Bani Khalid tribal confederation that dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. According to historical sources, the main branches of the Bani Khalid included the Al Humaid, Al Asfour, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and the Mahashir & Nahood. While the chieftainship of the confederation was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan, the Mahashir constituted a significant tribal branch within this broader structure. The Bani Khalid, including the Mahashir, controlled the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases during the 16th and 17th centuries. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, they expelled Ottoman forces and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's genealogical origins remain disputed among Arab genealogists, with various claims linking them to Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum, Banu Rabi'ah bin Amir bin Sa'sa'a from Hawazin, or Banu Ghazia from Tayy. Like other Bani Khalid branches, the Mahashir likely adopted Shi'ite Islam over time, creating lasting animosity with the Wahhabi movement and House of Saud. The Bani Khalid's power declined after Saudi invasions beginning in 1793, followed by defeats to tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and battles with the Subay' and Saudis in 1830 that ended their rule. Many Mahashir members, along with other Bani Khalid branches, subsequently dispersed across the Gulf region, settling in modern-day Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, UAE, Qatar, and Iraq, where their descendants continue to maintain tribal identity within the broader Gulf Arab social structure.

migrationPath.description: Early migration eastward as part of Bani Khalid confederation to dominate Eastern Arabian oases
Dispersal across Gulf region following defeat and end of Bani Khalid rule

name: Mahris
legitimacyNotes: Well-documented lineage tracing back to Quda'a through Mahra ibn Haydan. Genetic studies show minimal admixture with >85.9% Arabian ancestry, supporting their claimed Arabian lineage.
ancestorStory: Descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, who was from Quda'a
peakPowerEra: early Islamic period through Mahra Sultanate (lasting five centuries until 1967)
description: The Mahris are an Arab ethnographic group primarily inhabiting South Arabia, especially in the Al-Mahra Governorate in Yemen and the island of Socotra. They are descended from Mahra bin Haydan and are known for their unique Mahri language, a Modern South Arabian language. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests of North Africa and Spain, and maintained the Mahra Sultanate for five centuries until 1967. They are famous for their Mahri camels and have distinctive genetic markers showing minimal admixture with >85.9% Arabian ancestry.

relations.context: Mahri lineage traces back through Quda'a confederation

history: The Mahris are an ancient Arab tribe descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, tracing their lineage to the Qahtani branch. They are primarily concentrated in the Al-Mahra Governorate of eastern Yemen and the island of Socotra, with populations also found in Dhofar Governorate of Oman, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE. The Mahris are distinguished by their preservation of the Mehri language, a Modern South Arabian language closely related to Soqotri and Shehri. During the early Islamic period, a large delegation from Mahra led by Mahri bin Al-Abid visited Prophet Muhammad, who presented Islam to them and they embraced the faith. The Prophet wrote a letter granting them protection and declaring they shall not be harmed. The Mahris played a crucial role in early Islamic military history, participating in the conquest of North Africa and Spain under the command of Amr ibn al-As. They were granted lands in the newly conquered territories, including the region of Jabal Yashkar east of Al-Askar and later the Mahra quarter (Khittat Mahra) in Fustat, the new capital of Egypt established in 641 CE. Their commander Abd al-sallam ibn Habira al-Mahri was promoted to lead the conquest of Libya and became the first Muslim leader of Libya. The Mahris maintained political autonomy through the Mahra Sultanate, which lasted for five centuries until its abolition in 1967 when it was absorbed by the People's Republic of South Yemen. The sultanate came under British protection in 1886 as part of the Aden Protectorate. The Mahris are renowned for their distinctive Mahri camels, famous for their speed, agility, and endurance, which were integral to their military success during the Islamic conquests and became known as Mehari camels in North Africa. Genetically, the Mahris show minimal admixture with over 85.9% Arabian ancestry, and on Socotra, around 71.4% carry the basal haplogroup J*(xJ1,J2). Today, there are approximately 115,200 Mahri speakers globally, with 50,000 in Yemen, 50,800 in Oman, 14,400 in Kuwait, and around 20,000 in Saudi Arabia.

migrationPath.description: Mahri forces granted lands in Fustat after participating in Islamic conquest of Egypt, establishing the Mahra quarter
Abd al-sallam ibn Habira al-Mahri led Muslim army in conquest of Libya, defeating Byzantine forces

name: Makatim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional merchant family authority through trade networks and commercial influence
description: The Makatim are a prominent merchant family historically based in Kuwait and other Gulf ports. They were involved in the traditional Gulf economy of pearling, trade, and maritime commerce. The family established trading networks across the Arabian Gulf and Indian Ocean routes. They remain active in modern Gulf business communities, particularly in Kuwait.

relations.context: Traditional merchant relationship with Kuwait's ruling family

history: The Makatim are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab Arab tribe, which has origins in the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and areas of the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin, the Makatim had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a population of some 7,250 people, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin. The Bani Kaab tribe, including the Makatim subsection, became involved in the complex political dynamics of southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, their allegiances shifted over time - by the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had aligned with the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. The research also indicates there is a residential area called Khudra al-Makatim (Green of the Makatim) in the Wilayat of Al-Khaburah in Oman's North Al Batinah Governorate, with approximately 300 inhabitants as of 2010, suggesting continued Makatim presence in their traditional territories.

migrationPath.description: Migration from interior Arabia to Kuwait to establish maritime trading operations

name: Manai'i
legitimacyNotes: Established through genealogical descent and traditional tribal recognition in Qatar and eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Manee, from whom they derive their tribal name. They are considered part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin.
description: The Manai'i are a Qahtani tribe historically based in Qatar and parts of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. They have been involved in traditional Gulf activities including pearling, fishing, and maritime trade. The tribe has maintained its presence in modern Qatar where members continue to play roles in contemporary society. They have historical trade partnerships with other tribes including the Dahaminah.

relations.context: Co-habited in settlements along Wadi Qor at turn of 19th century
Both tribes present in Qatar and UAE regions

history: The Manai'i are a Qahtani tribe that traces its lineage to an ancestor named Manee, from whom they derive their tribal name. They are part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin and have been historically based in Qatar and parts of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. At the turn of the 19th century, members of the Manai'i tribe were documented as co-habiting with approximately 150 members of the Dahaminah tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor area, which encompasses parts of what is now Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the UAE. The tribe has been traditionally involved in Gulf maritime activities including pearling, fishing, and trade, maintaining historical partnerships with other tribes including the Dahaminah. The Manai'i have maintained their presence in modern Qatar where tribal members continue to participate in contemporary society.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration from Najd toward the Arabian Gulf coast as part of broader Qahtani tribal movements
Settlement in Qatar region to participate in emerging maritime trade and pearling economy

name: Manasir
legitimacyNotes: Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout the centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in the adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations.
ancestorStory: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor is called Mansur and belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. Multiple explanations are offered: some claim their apical forefather is Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin; others trace their origin to the Kawahla saying they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim; still others insist they are descendants of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.
traditionalEconomy: The riverain Manasir pursue small scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile. Their most important cash crop is a wide variety of dates. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and used to float wood from the region of Atbarah. The Bedouin portion herds goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys.
description: The Manasir constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile and call their homeland Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighbouring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and the downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language. Unlike other riverine tribes of Sudan, a considerable part of their population lives as Bedouins in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The nomadic life of herding their stock of goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys is however limited for many to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile.

history: The Manasir represent one of the most significant tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, with two distinct branches that are not to be confused: the Sudanese Manasir of Northern Sudan and the Al Manasir of the United Arab Emirates. The UAE Manasir, long closely associated with Abu Dhabi's ruling Bani Yas, were traditionally one of the most important tribes in the emirate of Abu Dhabi. Unlike their settled counterparts the Bani Yas, the Manasir were entirely nomadic Bedouin who roamed between Buraimi and Qatar, from the Persian Gulf coast to Liwa. They were fiercely independent and did not necessarily consider themselves dependents of the Sheikh of Abu Dhabi, though they cooperated closely with the Bani Yas. The tribe consisted of several subsections including the Al Bu Mundir (Kaabara, Mani, Marashid, Matawaah, Midahima), Al Bu Rahamah (Al Bu Khail, Tarsif, Tararifah and Wabran), Al Bu Shaar (Ghawainam, Rashaiyid and Al Bu Thuwaibit), and Al Rabeea. At the turn of the 20th century, some 1,400 Manasir roamed this tribal area, growing to about 4,000 by the 1950s, with 2,800 living in Abu Dhabi emirate. They subsisted through date farming, pearling, camel breeding, and operating camel trains for trade between oases and coastal towns. The Manasir were exempted from paying the customary date tax to the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, reflecting their independent status. They played crucial military roles, frequently forming important parts of Abu Dhabi's fighting force alongside the Bani Yas in conflicts throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, including the 1834 attack on Sharjah, the 1848 expulsion of Wahhabis from Buraimi, and the 1870 Battle of Dhank. However, they also acted as kingmakers, notably in 1928 when Baluchis and Manasir carried out a coup that killed Sheikh Saqr bin Zayed Al Nahyan, paving the way for Sheikh Shakbut bin Sultan Al Nahyan's rule. As traditional revenue sources declined in the late 1920s with the pearl trade collapse, many Manasir turned to working for oil companies like ARAMCO and Qatar Petroleum, though they typically worked only long enough to meet immediate needs before returning to their pastoral lifestyle.

migrationPath.from: Desert regions between Buraimi and Qatar
migrationPath.description: Seasonal summer migration to Liwa for date harvest, maintaining areesh palm frond houses
Winter migration pattern, with some sections wintering in Semeih and Abu Dhabi

name: Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir)
legitimacyNotes: Bedouin branch of Manasir with traditional tribal authority based on lineage and desert expertise
ancestorStory: The Bedouin Manasir are part of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, maintaining the traditional nomadic lifestyle and desert customs of their ancestors.
description: The Manasir al-Badiyah are the Bedouin branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, traditionally based in the desert regions between Abu Dhabi and Oman. They maintained nomadic pastoralist traditions longer than their settled relatives, specializing in camel herding and desert trade routes. The tribe has historical presence in the Liwa oasis region and the Empty Quarter, with traditional grazing rights across tribal territories. Today they are integrated into UAE society while maintaining cultural traditions and tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical alliance with ruling family of Abu Dhabi
Shared desert territories and grazing routes
Traditional marriages between Bedouin tribes

history: The Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir) represent the nomadic branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, distinct from their riverine counterparts along the Nile in Sudan. According to oral traditions recorded in tribal genealogies, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniin lineage, while others trace lineage to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or to descendants of a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities who established common genealogical recognition for mutual rights and territorial claims. The Bedouin branch (Manasir al-Badiyah) includes the clans of al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab, who traditionally shared grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani. During dry seasons, these nomadic clans migrated to various desert areas including the Kababish territory to the west (Khala' Kabushiyah), the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali country, or Rubatab territories, maintaining their pastoralist lifestyle centered on herding goats, sheep and camels.

name: Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Manasir tribe settled along Nile River in Sudan
ancestorStory: Riverain branch of the Manasir tribe that migrated and settled along the Nile River in Sudan, maintaining tribal identity while adapting to riverine agricultural life.
description: The Manasir al-Nil are a branch of the broader Manasir tribal confederation that settled along the Nile River in Sudan. They represent a Sudanese riverain community that maintains genealogical connections to the Arabian Peninsula Manasir tribes. While geographically distant from the GCC, they share common ancestry with UAE-based Manasir tribes and maintain cultural and lineage ties to the broader Manasir tribal network.
history: The Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir) represent a Sudanese Arab riverine tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan, distinct from their Arabian Peninsula namesakes in the UAE. They call their homeland Dar al-Manasir, a fertile river oasis of small rocky islands extending approximately 130 km along the Nile between the upstream Rubatab tribe near Abu Hamed and the downstream Shaiqiyah tribe past Birti village. The tribe's genealogical origins are contested, with multiple oral traditions claiming descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or as descendants of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab tracing to King Sabir. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities, united by recognition of common genealogical pedigree to establish mutual rights and land claims against neighboring tribes. The tribe is organizationally divided into Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) comprising ten clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Farei'ab, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib, and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) consisting of four clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab who share grazing grounds in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The riverain Manasir pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils, with dates as their primary cash crop, and are renowned for building mud houses (Galus) and historically floating wood from Atbarah. Their dual lifestyle combines settled riverine agriculture with seasonal nomadic pastoralism, as many join desert-dwelling tribal members during good rainy seasons. The 1993 population was estimated at 30,000 residents with an additional 17,000 non-resident Manasir, though these figures remain vague regarding Bedouin populations. The community faced displacement due to the Merowe Dam project flooding their ancestral homeland.

migrationPath.description: Early Manasir presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

Settlement along the Nile River in Fourth Cataract region

name: Maqadilah
history: The Maqadilah is a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe. The Shihuh are an Arab tribe inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region of the UAE and Oman. The Bani Shatair division, which includes the Maqadilah along with the Khanabil, Kumazarah, and Mahabib subsections, is primarily centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula. The Shihuh tribe as a whole has maintained a distinctive identity separate from the desert Bedouin, with the Arab element thought to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves of Malik bin Fahm groups in the second century. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people. The Shihuh practiced a seasonal lifestyle, engaging in agriculture during winter months in mountain settlements and migrating to coastal areas during warmer months for date harvesting and pearl fishing. They maintained large goat herds and were known for their fierce independence and resistance to central authority, frequently coming into conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah while generally preferring Muscat's suzerainty.

name: Marar
legitimacyNotes: Limited information available. The text only briefly mentions Marar as 'a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE)' without providing details about its formation, lineage claims, or historical context.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Marar tribe is mentioned as being a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), though the provided text offers very limited information about this specific tribal group. The text primarily focuses on other entities that share the name 'Marar', including a Hindu caste in Kerala, India, and various individuals with the surname Marar.
history: The Marar (singular Al Marri) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and a subsection of the Bani Yas confederation. The tribe is traditionally Hinawi and follows the Maliki Sunni branch of Islam. They formed part of the tribal confederation brought together by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, 'Zayed the Great', of Abu Dhabi in the late 19th century. In 1890, under Zayed's leadership, the Marar and Manasir tribes conducted a series of raids on the coast between Abu Dhabi and Dubai, capturing 400 camels and 100 horses. The Marar had settled in Dubai but in 1891, approximately 400 men of the tribe, feeling ill-treated by Sheikh Rashid bin Maktoum of Dubai, ended the pearling season by sailing their boats to Sharjah and settling there permanently. This mass migration led to three years of bitter negotiations arbitrated by the British Resident to settle financial claims and counter-claims. By the turn of the 19th century, the Marar were distributed across multiple emirates: 200 houses in the Layyah area of Sharjah, 40 houses in Abu Dhabi, and 30 in Dubai. The Bedouin branch consisted of about 70 families in Liwa, closely related to the Mazari, while the urban Marar were traditionally pearl divers. In 1927, tensions arose between the Marar and Sudan tribes in Sharjah, leading to 150 Marar members leaving to settle in Deira, Dubai, where an area still bears their name (transliterated as Al Murar or Al Marar). The tribe has notable connections to UAE ruling families through marriage alliances, including Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum's mother and wife, both of whom were Marar.

migrationPath.description: 400 Marar men sailed from Dubai to Sharjah after feeling ill-treated by Sheikh Rashid bin Maktoum, settling in the Layyah area

name: Maraziq
legitimacyNotes: The Maraziq are believed to have originally migrated from Arabia and took the name of the district they settled in Rams, which was originally populated by Iranians.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe settled the western area of Rams, specifically the Bani Humud neighbourhood, between Rams and Hulaylah. They lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs. The Maraziq population left the area by the early 1950s and are now dispersed throughout the UAE.

relations.context: Traditionally associated together with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah

history: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that became traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe is believed to originally have migrated from Arabia, though the specific origins and timeframe of this migration are not documented in available sources. Upon settling in the Rams area, the Maraziq took the name of the district they inhabited, which had originally been populated by Iranians. The tribe established themselves in the western area of Rams, specifically in what became known as the Bani Humud neighbourhood or freej, located between Rams and Hulaylah. They shared this settlement area with another tribe, the Tunaij. The Maraziq lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses topped with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs, which were characteristic of the late Islamic township of Rams. By the early 1950s, the Maraziq population had left the Rams area entirely and became dispersed throughout the UAE. Notably, the Bani Humud area of Rams continued to be known as the Marzouqi neighbourhood even after the Maraziq families had departed, indicating their lasting association with the location.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Arabia to settle in the Rams district, taking the name of the area originally populated by Iranians

name: Mariandyni
history: The Mariandyni were an ancient tribe of Thracian origin located in northeastern Bithynia in ancient Anatolia (modern-day Turkey). They inhabited the region between the Sangarius and Billaeus Rivers, east of the Thyni and Bithyni tribes. According to ancient sources, their territory was called Mariandynia, with Pliny referencing a 'Sinus Mariandynus' (Mariandynian Gulf) along their coast. The tribe's ethnic origins remain disputed among ancient historians - Strabo believed they were a branch of the Bithynians, while Herodotus distinguished them from other Thracian groups. Alternative accounts suggest they may have been related to the Paphlagonians, as their armor resembled Paphlagonian rather than Bithynian equipment. The chief city in their territory was Heraclea Pontica, whose inhabitants eventually reduced the Mariandyni to a state of collective serfdom similar to the Cretan Mnoae or Thessalian Penestae. Initially, the Mariandyni may have peacefully ceded coastal territory to Heracleot colonists seeking protection from the Bebrykes and Paphlagones. Over time, the Herakleots acquired the Lycus Valley and locked the Mariandyni into agricultural villages as a dependent people, also subject to impressment as fleet rowers. In the early 6th century BC, they remained independent, paying tribute directly to Lydian king Croesus and warring with Heraclea. Under Persian rule, they formed part of the third Persian satrapy. The tribe is connected to Greek mythology through Mariandynus, their eponymous hero, and figures like Titias, who was said to be either a son of Zeus or the eldest son of Mariandynus and was eventually deified by the Mariandynians.

name: Mawali
legitimacyNotes: Islamic legal status as non-Arab Muslims with tribal protection
description: Mawali refers to non-Arab Muslims who converted to Islam and became clients of Arab tribes, particularly during the early Islamic expansion period. In the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, they included Persians, other non-Arab peoples, and freed slaves who gained protection and social status through tribal affiliation. They played important roles in early Islamic administration, scholarship, and commerce. As a social category rather than a tribe, the mawali system largely dissolved over time as Islamic society evolved and tribal distinctions became less rigid.
history: The Mawali tribe represents a distinct Arab tribal confederation based in northwestern and central Syria, primarily in the regions of Idlib and Hama. By the 16th century, they had evolved into a heterogeneous grouping combining semi-nomadic sheepherders and camel-raising nomads of different origins. Their origins are obscure, though they were reputedly descended from a large troop of former slaves or branched off from the Beshar tribe, another group originally composed of former slaves. The Mawali's prestige derived from their leading family, the Al Fadl (later known as Al Abu Risha), whose chiefs had been formally recognized as amir al-arab (commander of the Bedouin) of the Syrian steppe since the Ayyubid period in the 13th century. When the Ottomans conquered Syria in 1516, they initially sought to force the tribe's submission through multiple military campaigns. Unable to bring the Mawali to heel, the Ottoman government co-opted them in 1574, granting their emir a ceremonial feather and stipend of 6,000 Venetian ducats, after which the Al Fadl emirs became known as Abu Risha ('father of the plume'). The Mawali controlled territory spanning from Hama and Homs in the west to the Palmyra oasis in the east and the Euphrates River valley in the north, giving them control over key trade routes between Baghdad and Damascus and between Aleppo and Iran. Their strongholds included the fortified town of Salamiyah. The Mawali's dominance began to waver in the mid-17th century, and by the late 18th century, they faced mass migrations of Anaza Bedouin into the Syrian steppe. In the 1770s, regular conflicts with the Hassana tribe gradually pushed the Mawali out of their traditional desert abodes into the Jabal Zawiya and Jabal al-A'la highlands around Hama. Their failed 1786 revolt under emir Muhammad al-Khurfan, which included raids on villages around Hama, resulted in a devastating Ottoman counterattack that massacred hundreds of tribesmen and sealed their loss of dominant position in the Syrian steppe. Despite their diminished capacity, Mawali sheikhs continued to receive official titles and policing duties from the government, particularly the hereditary office of serkeder urban (chief of the Bedouin) of Hama, held by the Khurfan family of the Masharifa clan. During the French Mandatory period (1920-1946), the Mawali had become primarily sheepherding pastoralists with significantly shorter seasonal migrations compared to camel-raising tribes. They joined the Syrian nationalist cause, participating in the Great Syrian Revolt of 1925-1927 and engaging in frequent warfare with the pro-French Hadidiyin tribe throughout the 1920s and early 1930s. By the mid-20th century, the tribe had fully settled, inhabiting around sixty villages in the Idlib and Hama governorates. During the Syrian Civil War beginning in 2012, the Mawali were among the first Arab tribes to rebel against the government, forming the Mawali Brigade under the Free Syrian Army and fighting in confrontations around Maarat al-Nu'man, Hama, Aleppo, and Raqqa under the leadership of Sheikh Jamal al-Shayish.

name: Mazari
legitimacyNotes: The name 'Mazari' appears to be used by multiple unrelated tribal groups, suggesting it may be a toponymic designation rather than indicating true blood kinship between these groups.
description: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups rather than a single unified tribe. The text identifies at least two separate Mazari tribes: an Emirati Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates and a Baloch tribe settled in Rajanpur and Sindh, Pakistan. These appear to be separate entities that share only the name 'Mazari', likely derived from different etymological origins.
history: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups rather than a single unified tribe, sharing only the name 'Mazari' but having completely different origins and histories. The Baloch Mazari tribe is one of the oldest Baloch tribes, with the name derived from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'Tiger'. They consist of two main branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. The Mazari conquered the area known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing parts of present-day Sindh's Kashmore District, Punjab's Rojhan Tehsil, and surrounding areas. Their stronghold remains Rojhan-Mazari in Pakistan's Rajanpur District. The tribe reached its golden era under British rule, when their territory extended from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The Mazari fought significant battles against the Sikh Empire, including the 1836 attack on Mithankot led by Sardar Karam Khan under Chief Mir Bahram Khan. After initial conflicts, peace was achieved in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen and was received by Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort. Notable leaders included Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who received British honors for his loyalty and services on the western frontier, and his descendants who continued as tribal chiefs. The most recent chief, Mir Balakh Sher Mazari (1928-2022), was educated at Aitchison College and Oxford University, serving as the 22nd Sardar and 7th Mir of the Mazaris. His brother Sherbaz Khan Mazari became a prominent Pakistani politician, serving as caretaker Prime Minister in 1993. Separately, the Emirati Mazari (Mazrouei) are an Arab Bedouin tribe considered part of the Bani Yas confederation, originally from Bani Tamim lineage. They settled primarily in Abu Dhabi and the Liwa Oasis, where they owned date gardens and engaged in pearl diving and pastoralism. The Emirati Mazari supported Zayed bin Khalifa against Wahhabi incursions in the 19th century and later played important roles in UAE politics and oil industry development.

name: Mazari (Baloch)
legitimacyNotes: Baloch tribal identity with traditional leadership structure
ancestorStory: The Mazari trace their lineage to Mazar, a prominent figure among the Baloch tribes. They are part of the larger Baloch tribal confederation that migrated from Balochistan to the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Mazari are a Baloch tribal group that forms part of the broader Baloch diaspora in the Arabian Peninsula. Historically, they migrated from the Balochistan region and settled in various parts of the Gulf states, particularly maintaining their distinct Baloch cultural identity. Like other Baloch tribes in the region, they have traditionally been involved in pastoralism and have integrated into the social fabric of Gulf societies while preserving their tribal structure. They remain an active community within the larger Baloch population in the GCC states.
history: The Mazari are a major Baloch tribe whose name derives from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'tiger.' They are one of the oldest Baloch tribes, consisting of two primary branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. The tribe's stronghold is Rojhan-Mazari in Rajanpur District, Punjab, near the borders of Balochistan, Sindh and Punjab. The area they conquered became known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing most of Kashmore District in Sindh, Chatha Baksha, Mazari District, and all of Tehsil Rojhan in Punjab. Under British rule, the Mazari chief controlled territory from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The tribe engaged in significant warfare against the Sikh Empire from 1836-1838. In 1836, Mithankot was attacked by Mazari forces under Sardar Karam Khan, brother of Chief Mir Bahram Khan, in response to threats from Maharaja Kharak Singh. The Sikhs retaliated by burning Rojhan under Diwan Sawan Mal Chopra, though the Mazari evacuated in time and continued guerrilla warfare from the Sulaiman Mountains. The conflict ended in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen, meeting Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort and establishing peace. The British colonial period saw the rise of prominent Mazari leaders including Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who became the second chief and was knighted for his services, particularly in managing tribal affairs and assisting with the western frontier. His descendants continued this legacy, with the tribe producing notable political figures in modern Pakistan including Sherbaz Khan Mazari, who served as caretaker Prime Minister. The Mazari speak Sulimanki Balochi and maintain their traditional tribal structure with the chief holding the title of Mir and Tumandar, currently represented by Mir Balakh Sher Mazari as the twenty-second Sardar.

name: Mazari (Emirati)
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical claims and settlement patterns in UAE territories
description: The Mazari are a tribal group found within the United Arab Emirates, representing one of the smaller tribal entities in the federation. They are primarily settled in specific regions of the UAE and maintain traditional tribal social structures. Like many UAE tribes, they have adapted to the modern state system while preserving their tribal identity and customs.
history: The Mazari (singular Mazrouei or Mazrui) are an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also found in Oman and Kenya. They are considered a subsection of the Bani Yas confederation and formed the majority of the Bedouin component of that federation of tribes. The Mazari are regarded as the second most influential and likely the most populous section of the Bani Yas confederation. According to the Amir of the Mazari at Adhen and Ghayl, the tribe traditionally claims to originate from the Bani Tamim tribe, having already embraced Islam prior to their arrival in the region at an unspecified period. Although originally considered to belong to a separate Ghafiri faction and to follow the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence, the Mazari of Abu Dhabi generally adhere to the Maliki school. The Liwa Oasis was the homeplace of many Mazari, where they were the principal Bedouin section of the Bani Yas confederation. The UK Memorial listed them as the principal property owners among the six Bani Yas tribes in the area, recording 142 families in 1951 and 151 families in 1954, consisting of some 315 houses at the turn of the 20th century according to J.G. Lorimer. At that time there were also some 300 Mazari at Al Khan in Sharjah and 500 in the areas of Adhen and Asimah. Originally herdsmen, they settled into an agrarian existence in the oasis following the decline in the value of camels during the early 20th century, though they continued to practice seasonal pastoralism. They were also involved in the seasonal pearl fisheries, owning boats launched from Bandar Rudaim. In the 19th century, they contributed significant numbers of tribesmen to support Zayed bin Khalifa in conflicts against Wahhabi incursions. When Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan repelled the Wahhabis from Buraimi Oasis in 1848, it was the Mazari, together with the Manasir, who waited south of Abu Dhabi to attack the relieving force from Nejd. An outbreak of hostilities between Bedouin tribes of Dhafra in the early 20th century split the Mazari between a group seeking Saudi protection and a group migrating to Abu Dhabi and its islands. The Mazari have maintained prominent positions in UAE politics and economics, with notable figures including Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui (federal minister of petroleum until 1994 and chairman of ADNOC), Ahmad bin Fadhil Al-Mazrui (wali of Liwa until 1955), and several current officials in ADNOC, Abu Dhabi Executive Council, and other government bodies.
