name: Mazrui
description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

relations.context: Opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar in the 18th century

history: The Mazrui are an Omani Arab clan whose origins trace back through multiple branches across the Arabian Peninsula and East Africa. The research reveals two distinct but related Mazrui lineages: the East African branch that ruled parts of Kenya from the 18th-20th centuries, and the UAE branch that forms part of the Bani Yas confederation. The East African Mazrui governed Mombasa and other coastal areas from the 18th century, opposing the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled Zanzibar. They allied with the Portuguese on at least one occasion to attack Stone Town. When the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, the Mazrui actively resisted British rule alongside the Kikuyu and Kamba peoples. The UAE Mazari branch, found principally in Abu Dhabi but also in Oman and Kenya, are considered a major subsection of the Bani Yas confederation and reportedly trace their origins to the Bani Tamim tribe, having embraced Islam before settling in the region. Originally following the Hanbali school of jurisprudence as part of the Ghafiri faction, the Abu Dhabi Mazari generally adhere to the Maliki school. The Liwa Oasis served as the homeland for many UAE Mazari, where they owned significant date gardens and practiced seasonal pastoralism. In conflicts of the 19th and early 20th centuries, including Wahhabi incursions and tribal disputes in the Dhafra region, some Mazari sought Saudi protection while others migrated to Abu Dhabi, creating divisions that contributed to the later Buraimi Dispute. Today, the UAE Mazrui hold prominent positions in Abu Dhabi's government and economy, including senior roles in ADNOC, various authorities, and the ruling family connections.

name: Misaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and local consensus
description: Al Misaid is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula with presence in Gulf regions. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They maintain tribal identity and kinship structures typical of Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their current status and activities within the GCC context require further historical verification.
history: The Misaid are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation with presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and territories east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Misaid had settled (unlike some nomadic subsections like the Drisah and Shwaihiyin), forming part of a population of approximately 7,250 Bani Kaab members. In 1844, the tribe allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia were fluid - by the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted to support the Saudis, with their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring loyalty to Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the tribe supported the Saudi incursion and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. The Misaid, as part of this larger tribal structure, would have been involved in these political and military developments that shaped the modern borders and allegiances of the Gulf region.

name: Miyadilah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and pastoral leadership
description: The Miyadilah are a small Arabian tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Gulf region. Their exact origins, territorial base, and historical significance remain poorly documented in major tribal genealogical sources. They appear to have been associated with pastoral activities in the Arabian Peninsula but lack prominent historical records of major political or economic influence in the GCC states.
history: The Miyadilah are documented as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, which has significant presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, of which Miyadilah forms part, are associated with the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and territories in the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Miyadilah had settled along with most other Bani Kaab subsections, distinguishing them from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin branches. The broader Bani Kaab confederation, including the Miyadilah, became involved in significant regional conflicts and alliances. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. By the 1940s, their allegiances had shifted, with the tribe supporting Saudi interests during the period when oil concessions became a major regional issue. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab, presumably including the Miyadilah subsection, supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Miyalisah
history: The Miyalisah are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, which has historical roots in the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and in areas of the UAE east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin who remained nomadic, the Miyalisah had settled by the turn of the 20th century. As part of the Bani Kaab confederation, they became involved in the complex political alliances of southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, by the mid-20th century, the Bani Kaab including the Miyalisah had shifted their allegiance to support Saudi Arabia. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, they supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Mizahamiyin
ancestorStory: Descended from Muzahim, though specific genealogical details vary in tribal accounts.
description: The Mizahamiyin are a tribal group whose historical presence and exact lineage remain poorly documented in available sources. They appear to be connected to the broader tribal networks of the Arabian Peninsula, though their specific role in Gulf politics and economy is not well established in mainstream historical accounts. Their current status and territorial associations require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The Mizahamiyin are a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation present in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin who remained nomadic, the Mizahamiyin had settled by the turn of the 20th century. The Bani Kaab, including the Mizahamiyin, are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By 1844, the tribe allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a confederation that drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, allegiances shifted over time, and by the 1940s the Bani Kaab had aligned with the Saudis. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Mudar
legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Quraysh
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
subTribes.description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

relations.context: Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd

history: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the 'northern Arabs'. According to tradition, Mudar had two sons - Ilyās (also known as al-Yās) and ʿAylān (Qays ʿAylān) - from whom descended many influential tribes including Kināna, Quraysh, Tamīm, Hudhayl, and Asad. The earliest historical mention appears in the al-῾Irāfa 1 inscription (c. 440 CE) discovered at Zafar, capital of Himyar, which records a Mudarite chief traveling to Mas'al Jumh to meet the Himyarite king, indicating Mudar was then under Himyarite hegemony. A second inscription, Maʾsal 2 (521 CE), mentions Mudar providing reinforcements to a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory under the leadership of Banu Tha'labah. Mudar rose to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, who migrated to Syria. In pre-Islamic times, Mudar tribes occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions, and were at various times under the suzerainty of southern Arabian powers, particularly the Kingdom of Kinda whose rulers bore the title 'king of Maʿadd' or 'king of Mudar and Rabīʿa'. Following the decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule, Mudar tribes emerged as autonomous actors in central Arabia. During the later sixth century, sections of Mudar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the name Diyār Muḍar came to designate a distinct tribal territory. With the rise of Islam, many Mudar tribes entered the Muslim polity, though not simultaneously or uniformly. In early Islamic historiography, 'Mudar' increasingly functioned as a political label within the broader tribal dualism opposing Mudar to Yemen (Qahtan), a division that became especially prominent after the first Islamic civil wars.

name: Mudhar
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar, one of the primary divisions of Adnanite Arabs
ancestorStory: Mudhar was one of the sons of Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan, representing one of the major tribal confederations of northern Arabia. The Mudharis became one of the two main divisions of Adnanite tribes.
description: Mudhar was one of the great tribal confederations of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, comprising numerous tribes descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar. They were primarily based in the Najd and northern Arabian Peninsula, representing the northern Adnanite Arab division in opposition to the Rabiah confederation. The Mudhari tribes included major groups like Quraish, Tamim, and Qays, who played crucial roles in early Islamic conquests and later Arab expansion. While the confederation as a political entity is historical, many of its constituent tribes remain active throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region.

relations.context: The two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes

history: Mudhar (Arabic: مُضَر) was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia, tracing descent from Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan. Together with Rabi'a, it formed one of the two largest groupings of northern (Adnanite) Arabs, as opposed to the southern Qahtanite tribes. The confederation occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions in pre-Islamic Arabia. The earliest mention of Mudhar appears in the al-'Irafa 1 inscription (c. 440 CE) discovered at Zafar, capital of the Himyarite Kingdom, where a Mudhari chief is recorded traveling to Mas'al Jumh to meet the Himyarite king, indicating Mudhar was under Himyarite hegemony at the time. A second inscription, Ma'sal 2 (June 521 CE), mentions Mudhari reinforcements led by the Banu Tha'labah supporting a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory. According to genealogical tradition, Mudhar divided into two principal branches: Ilyas and Aylan (Qays Aylan), from which descended influential tribes including Kinana, Quraysh, Tamim, Hudhayl, and Asad. Following the decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule, Mudhar tribes emerged as autonomous actors in central Arabia. During the later sixth century, sections of Mudhar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the region became known as Diyar Mudhar. The confederation maintained close associations with Mecca and its sanctuary, with several religious offices connected to the pilgrimage attributed to Mudhari families. With the rise of Islam, Mudhar tribes entered the Muslim polity at different times, and 'Mudhar' increasingly functioned as a political label in the tribal dualism opposing northern Arabs (Mudhar) to southern Arabs (Yemen/Qahtan). This division became especially prominent during the Qays-Yaman wars, including major conflicts in Palestine and Transjordan between 793-796 CE, where Mudhari tribes led by Amir ibn Umara al-Murri fought against Yamani confederations and their Abbasid allies.

name: al-Muleikab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and established settlement patterns
description: Al-Muleikab is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific origins, traditional territories, or detailed genealogical connections. They appear to maintain their tribal identity in the modern era, though their current distribution and activities require further research to establish definitively.
history: Al-Muleikab is identified as one of the Bedouin clans (Manasir al-Badiyah) of the Manasir tribe in Northern Sudan. According to documented tribal genealogy, al-Muleikab belongs to the nomadic division of the Manasir people who inhabit the Bayuda Desert adjacent to the Fourth Cataract of the Nile. They share grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani with three other Bedouin Manasir clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, and al-Kagbab. The Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible lineages according to oral tradition - some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, and still others from al-Hankab clan descendants of King Sabir. Critical research suggests the Manasir, including al-Muleikab, represent a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities. During the dry season, al-Muleikab and other Bedouin Manasir clans migrate to various grazing areas including the Kababish territory to the west, Wad Hamid grazing grounds in Ga'ali country, or Rubatab territory, following traditional nomadic patterns of herding goats, sheep and camels.

name: Mutair (Ikhwan forces)
legitimacyNotes: Part of the Ikhwan religious-military movement under Ibn Saud's leadership
ancestorStory: Descends from Mutayr ibn Ghutayf of the Ghatafan tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the Hawazin branch of Qays Aylan.
description: Mutair is a major Najdi Bedouin tribe that became one of the most prominent members of the Ikhwan movement in early 20th century Saudi Arabia. Historically based in central Najd, they played a crucial role in Ibn Saud's military campaigns to unify Saudi Arabia between 1910-1930. The tribe later rebelled against Ibn Saud in the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930) before being subdued. Today, Mutair remains one of Saudi Arabia's largest and most influential tribes, with members across the Gulf states.

history: The Mutair (Ikhwan forces) were a major Najdi Bedouin tribe that became one of the most prominent members of the Ikhwan movement in early 20th century Saudi Arabia. The tribe's origins trace back to the northern Hejaz near Medina, but at some point in the 17th century, they began a large-scale migration eastwards into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes. By the early 20th century, Mutair had become the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem region. Led by Faisal Al-Duwaish (also spelled Al-Dewish), the tribe played a crucial role in Ibn Saud's military campaigns. In 1912, Ibn Saud undertook to settle the nomads in newly created villages (hijras) where they were indoctrinated into puritanical Islam and became warriors known as the Ikhwan. The most important Mutayri settlement was al-Artawiyya at the northern edge of the Dahna desert. Al-Duwaish led enthusiastic participation in the Ikhwan movement, providing Ibn Saud with crucial military support including the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, tensions arose when Ibn Saud forbade further raiding into British-protected territories. In late 1927, Mutayr tribesmen launched cross-border raids into Iraq, Transjordan, and Kuwait, challenging Ibn Saud's authority. This escalated into the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930). The main confrontation occurred at the Battle of Sabilla on March 29-31, 1929, where Ibn Saud's modernized forces with machine guns and vehicles defeated the traditional camel-mounted Ikhwan, killing some 500 rebels. Al-Duwaish fled to Kuwait in October 1929 but was detained by the British and handed over to Ibn Saud, dying in Riyadh prison on October 3, 1931. The rebellion was finally suppressed on January 10, 1930, and surviving Ikhwan were incorporated into regular Saudi units, later forming the National Guard.

migrationPath.description: Large-scale eastward migration from Hejaz highlands into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes

name: Mutayr

DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

history: The Mutayr tribe is a major Arab tribal confederation with origins in the northern Hejaz near Medina, belonging to Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. According to genealogist Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), Mutayr descends from Ghatafan, who are considered descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA testing has confirmed that male Mutayr participants share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-chromosome lineage from the J1 haplogroup network. The tribe's name 'Mutayr' derives from the al-Mutarinah section and means 'abundant in rain' or 'generous.' The tribe is organized into three main branches: Bani Abdullah (the largest, comprising over half the tribe), Al-Ulwa (also known as Alwa), and Braih (Birayh), each divided into numerous clans. During the 17th century, Mutayr underwent a major eastward migration from their original Hejaz highlands into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes like Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. This migration established Mutayr as the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem region. The tribe played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under the leadership of Faisal Al-Dewish (Al-Duwaish). In 1912, Ibn Saud undertook to settle nomadic tribes in newly created villages called hijras, indoctrinating them into puritanical Islam and creating the Ikhwan brotherhood. Mutayr's most important settlement was al-Artawiyya at the northern edge of the Dahna desert. Under Al-Dewish's leadership, Mutayr provided crucial military support to Ibn Saud, participating in the 1920 attack on Kuwait at al-Jahra (repelled under British pressure) and the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, Al-Dewish later led an Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud, claiming the king had abandoned true faith by refraining from attacking European-ruled Iraq and Syria. Ibn Saud defeated the rebels at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929, after which Al-Dewish fled to British-controlled Iraq but was handed back and imprisoned until his death. According to Bedouin oral tradition, Mutayr women are credited with creating the battoulah (Gulf burqa), a metallic-looking face mask worn by married women in the Persian Gulf region, around 1870 CE when a Mutayr woman used it to escape an unwanted marriage proposal. Originally nomadic Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in Saudi Arabian cities and towns, establishing up to 400 villages across the country, particularly in Riyadh, Medina, and the central region, with significant populations also in Kuwait.

name: Al-'Ulwa
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage connection to Mutayr confederation
description: Al-'Ulwa is a major branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the largest Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia and have historically been involved in nomadic pastoralism and tribal warfare. The tribe played significant roles in various historical conflicts and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Today they remain an active tribal group within Saudi Arabia with members also found in other Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliance during Saudi state formation

history: Al-'Ulwa is one of the three major branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Banu Abdullah and Braih. According to genealogical sources including Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan, descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA studies have confirmed that Mutayr members share a common ancestor and predominantly belong to the J1 haplogroup network. The tribe's original homelands were in the northern Hejaz near Medina, but in the 17th century, Mutayr began a large-scale eastward migration into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. This migration brought them into rivalry with the Utaybah tribe who inhabited similar areas. Al-'Ulwa is divided into seven main clans: Al-Muhahah, Al-Jiblan, Al-'Awanah, Al-Rukhman, Al-Mala'bah, Al-Sahabah, and Al-Bara'sah. The branch produced notable historical figures including Faisal al-Duwaish, one of the leaders of the Ikhwan movement and former Sheikh of the tribe. During the early 20th century conflicts between Al Saud and Al Rashid, Mutayr became dominant in Al-Qaseem region under Faisal Al-Dewish's leadership. The tribe joined Ibn Saud's Ikhwan movement enthusiastically, with their main settlement at al-Artawiyya. However, Al-Dewish later led a rebellion against Ibn Saud, which was defeated at the Battle of Sabilla. Today, nearly all Mutayr members, including Al-'Ulwa, are settled in Saudi cities and towns, making up to 400 villages across the country, with large populations also in Kuwait.

description: Bani Abdullah is a prominent branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in Najd and have historically practiced nomadic pastoralism across central and eastern Arabia. As part of Mutayr, they participated in the major tribal movements and conflicts of the 18th and 19th centuries, including interactions with the Saudi state. Today they maintain their tribal identity while many members have settled in modern GCC states, particularly Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.

history: The research provided contains no information about Bani Abdullah of the Mutayr tribal confederation. The documents focus on other Arabian tribes including Bani Shaiba (custodians of the Kaaba from Quraysh), Bani Kaab (from Oman and UAE), Banu Hanifa (from al-Yamama/central Saudi Arabia), Anizah (major Bedouin confederation), and Banu Hashim (Quraysh clan of Prophet Muhammad). While these sources confirm the existence of major tribal structures in the Arabian Peninsula and provide detailed histories of related groups, they do not contain any specific information about the origins, migrations, conflicts, or historical development of Bani Abdullah as a branch of Mutayr. Without direct research evidence about this specific tribal branch, no verified historical narrative can be constructed beyond what is already known from the existing data provided.

legitimacyNotes: Recognized sub-tribe within the Mutayr confederation with traditional grazing and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Braih is considered one of the founding branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the early divisions within Mutayr that established the major sub-tribal units.
description: Braih is a sub-tribe of the larger Mutayr confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribal groups of central Arabia. Historically based in the Najd region of Saudi Arabia, they participated in the traditional nomadic pastoral economy and tribal raids. As part of Mutayr, they played a role in the early Saudi state formations and the unification of Saudi Arabia. The tribe remains active today with members settled across Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

history: Braih (also spelled Birayh) is one of the three main divisions of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Bani Abdullah and 'Ulwa. The Mutayr tribe originates from the Ghatafan confederation, descendants of Ishmael according to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418). Originally based in the northern Hejaz highlands near Medina, Mutayr began a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, displacing tribes like Harb and Anizzah. Braih is subdivided into twelve major clans: Al-'Ibayat, Al-Dayahiyn, Al-Birzan, Al-Tha'lah, Al-Si'ran, Al-Muraykhat, Al-Bidana, Al-'Awaridh, Al-Hawamil, Al-'Afisah, Bani 'Abdillah, and Al-Wasama al-halfi. The Braih, as part of Mutayr, became dominant in Al-Qaseem region and played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian politics. They participated in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish, supporting Ibn Saud's unification campaigns including the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, relations soured when Al-Dewish led an Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud, culminating in the 1929 Battle of Sabilla where the rebels were defeated. The Braih have historically been nomadic Bedouins but today are largely settled across Saudi Arabia, with significant populations in Kuwait as well.

name: Mysians
history: The Mysians (Ancient Greek: Μυσοί) were an ancient people of northwestern Anatolia, not related to the Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy. Based on the research provided, the Mysians appear in the 'List of ancient Greek tribes' as a pre-Greek and non-Greek tribe that became Hellenized. They are listed among the pre-Greek peoples who later became part of the Greek cultural sphere, alongside other groups like the Pelasgians, Leleges, and Cadmeans. The research indicates they were among the populations that through 'absorption and assimilation of previous and neighboring populations into the Greek language and customs' became part of the broader Greek world. However, the provided research focuses primarily on Greek tribal genealogy and other Iranian, Baloch, Dardanian, and Pashtun peoples, with very limited specific information about Mysian origins, migrations, or political development. The research does not provide details about their tribal structure, leadership, conflicts, or relationship to Arabian Peninsula tribes.

name: Nahood
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Nahood is a tribal name mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation is limited. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, they were traditionally engaged in nomadic pastoralism across the desert regions. Their current status and specific historical role in Gulf region politics remains unclear due to limited documented presence in major historical events.
history: The Nahood are documented as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, appearing alongside clans such as Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir. As part of Bani Khalid, the Nahood participated in the confederation's rise to power in eastern Arabia during the 15th-18th centuries. Under the leadership of the Al Humaid clan, particularly Barrak ibn Ghurayr, Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions in 1670, establishing the Bani Khalid Emirate with its capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's territory extended from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south, wielding significant political influence over Najd in central Arabia. The Nahood, as part of this confederation, faced the same challenges that ultimately led to Bani Khalid's decline - conflicts with the rising Saudi state, internal leadership disputes, and encroachment by rival tribes such as the Ajman, Dawasir, and Subay'. The final defeat came in battles against tribal alliances in 1823 and 1830, ending Bani Khalid rule. Following these defeats, many members of the confederation, likely including Nahood families, dispersed to various regions including Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, and other parts of Saudi Arabia, where descendants continue to reside today.

name: Naqbiyin
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with clear territorial associations and historical presence in the region dating back at least 300 years in some locations.
description: The Naqbiyin is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been influential in the tribal politics of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates. By the early 20th century, they had settled throughout the region including Khor Fakkan, Kalba, Dibba, and Fahlain, with a population of approximately 1,800 people primarily engaged in agriculture. They are known for their strategic settlements and close relationship with the Qawasim rulers.

history: The Naqbiyin (النقبي, singular Al Naqbi) is a UAE tribe originally concentrated in the Wadi Naqab area of Ras Al Khaimah, from which they derive their name. The tribe dispersed from this agriculturally rich wadi after disputes with pre-Al Qawasim local rulers, likely between 1695-1740, with many members relocating to settlements in Sharjah emirate including Dibba, Khor Fakkan, and Kalba. By around 1800, their former territory in Wadi Naqab had been acquired by the Habus tribe. The Naqbiyin established strategic settlements throughout the region, including Khatt, Daftah in Wadi Ham, and Fahlain on the Jiri plain. In 1903, Lorimer documented Fahlain as consisting of 60 Naqbiyin houses with 2,000 date palms, primarily engaged in agriculture. The tribe maintained close relations with the Qawasim rulers, with their settlements in Khatt and Fahlain forming part of Hassan bin Rahma's sheikhdom when he signed the 1820 General Maritime Treaty with the British. On the east coast (Shamaliyah), the Naqbiyin were frequently in conflict with the neighboring Sharqiyin tribe but played influential roles in tribal politics. They were considered the 'powers behind the throne' when Kalba gained independence from Sharjah in 1937 under Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi. The Al Qasimi rulers strategically granted them lands as protection against the Sharqiyin, recognizing their presence in Khor Fakkan even before the Portuguese sack of 1506. The tribe's settlement in Khatt reportedly occurred over a 300-year period, and the Al Naqbi Tower still stands in the village today. By the early 20th century, their total settled population across the region numbered approximately 1,800 people.

migrationPath.description: Dispersal from agriculturally rich wadi after disputes with pre-Al Qawasim rulers

name: Nawaljiyin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established family lines
description: The Nawaljiyin are a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about their specific origins, territorial base, and role in Gulf history remains limited in widely available sources. Further research in local archives and oral traditions would be needed to establish their precise historical significance and current status.
history: The Nawaljiyin are a subsection of the Bani Kaab (بني كعب), an Arab tribe with presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The Nawaljiyin were among the settled sections of the Bani Kaab by the turn of the 20th century, distinguishing them from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections. By 1844, the Bani Kaab, including the Nawaljiyin, had allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia were fluid - by the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted to support Saudi Arabia, with their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring allegiance to the Saudi Eastern Province Governor. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and participated in fighting against British-backed Trucial Oman Levies forces.

timelineEvents.title: Alliance with Bani Yas
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in confederation to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi

timelineEvents.title: Settlement Period
timelineEvents.description: Nawaljiyin among the settled sections of Bani Kaab by turn of 20th century

timelineEvents.title: Saudi Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab shifted alliance to Saudi Arabia, with Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring territories as Saudi

timelineEvents.title: Buraimi Dispute
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab supported Saudi incursion and fought against Trucial Oman Levies

name: Northern Bani Qitab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage authority through Qitab ancestry
ancestorStory: Northern branch descended from Qitab, distinguished from southern Bani Qitab settlements through geographic and political divisions.
description: Northern Bani Qitab represents the northern territorial branch of the broader Bani Qitab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. They were traditionally distinguished from their southern counterparts through geographic settlement patterns and pastoral territories. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle through camel and sheep herding across northern Arabian territories. Their historical significance lies primarily in their role within the broader Qitab tribal structure rather than as independent political actors.

relations.context: Both tribes shared northern Arabian pastoral territories

history: The Northern Bani Qitab represents the nomadic northern territorial branch of the broader Bani Qitab tribal confederation, distinguished from the settled southern section through geographic settlement patterns and lifestyle. At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe consisted of some 2,100 nomadic Bedouin (of whom 600 were fighting men) and 2,700 settled people. The Northern branch's dar (district) stretched from south of the Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of the Hajar Mountains, the Jiri plain north of Sharjah, and the fertile area around Sharjah's inland oasis town of Dhaid. The Northern Bani Qitab mostly settled in the inland towns of Dhaid and Al Falayah. The tribe was highly influential in Trucial States politics, often involved in disputes between rulers. In 1905, they built a fort at Wadi Hatta and harassed caravans passing to the Omani Batina coast, leading to conflict with the Na'im tribe and intervention by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. They maintained strong loyalty to Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla of Umm Al Qawain, and when he died suddenly of pneumonia in 1922, the Bani Qitab under Sheikh Muhammed bin Ali Al Huwaidan were instrumental in ensuring the smooth succession of Sheikh Abdulla bin Rashid Al Mualla II, providing 100 men to secure the town. Throughout the 1920s-1940s, they remained involved in tribal conflicts across the interior, fighting in Buraimi and participating in raids with various tribes including the Awamir, Duru, Manasir and Bani Yas. The tribe supported Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi in his various political struggles, playing a key role in his accession as Ruler of Kalba in 1937. Effectively controlling the interior of the Northern emirates, they were courted by various rulers for oil concession access, maintaining a decisive force of 200-250 armed, mounted Bedouin even into the 1940s. The singular form Al Ketbi became a common family name in the Northern UAE, with notable members including Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi (third wife of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan) and various political figures and analysts.

migrationPath.description: Original migration from Yemen following Qahtani tribal movements northward into central Arabia
Temporary movement north during Islamic conquests to participate in campaigns in Iraq and Syria
Displacement during Qarmatian conflicts, seeking refuge in Mesopotamian borderlands

name: Obaidli
legitimacyNotes: Established merchant and pearling family with traditional leadership in coastal communities
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Obaid Allah, an ancestor who settled in the Gulf coast region. The name reflects their descent from this progenitor.
description: The Obaidli are a tribal group historically present in the Gulf coastal regions, particularly in areas that are now part of the UAE and Qatar. They were traditionally involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade activities. The tribe has maintained its presence in Gulf society and continues to be recognized in the modern GCC states. They represent one of the smaller but established tribal entities in the region's complex social fabric.

relations.context: Both tribes settled in similar Gulf coastal areas

history: The Obaidli are documented as one of the authentic Arab Huwala tribes, part of a tribal confederation that migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Iranian coast of the Persian Gulf between the 13th-19th centuries. According to historical sources, they were among the 'Arabs of the Eastern Coast' (عرب الساحل الشرقي) who settled primarily in the Shibkoh region of southern Iran, spanning from Bandar Kanj to Bandar Kangan. The Obaidli, alongside the Qawasem, Hammadi, Al Nasur, and Bani Tamim, formed the core of the actual Arab Huwala confederation, distinct from the Achomi Persian populations of the same region. They maintained their Sunni Islamic faith and Arabic tribal identity while residing under various Persian dynasties. During the 18th-19th centuries, they participated in the maritime trade networks of the Persian Gulf, particularly during the period when the Al Qasimi confederation controlled much of the southern Iranian coast around Bandar Lengeh. The restrictive economic policies imposed by Reza Shah Pahlavi in the 1930s, along with centralization efforts and social reforms, prompted most Huwala Arabs including the Obaidli to migrate back to the Arabian Peninsula. They settled across various Gulf states, particularly in the UAE and Qatar, where they integrated into the emerging modern state structures while maintaining their distinct tribal identity and heritage as authentic Arabs of Persian Gulf maritime tradition.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration as part of Huwala confederation to Iranian coast
Return migration due to Reza Shah's restrictive policies

timelineEvents.title: Qasimi Recognition
timelineEvents.description: Iranian Zand dynasty recognized Qasimi rule over Bandar Lengeh region where Huwala tribes including Obaidli were established

timelineEvents.title: British Anti-Piracy Campaign
timelineEvents.description: Qasimis defeated by British, affecting Huwala tribal territories in southern Iran

timelineEvents.title: Mass Huwala Migration
timelineEvents.description: Reza Shah's centralization policies and economic restrictions trigger return migration of Huwala Arabs to Arabian Peninsula

name: Otaibah
legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

relations.context: Otaibah is descended from and represents the largest remaining alliance of the Hawazin confederation
Conflicts over pastures in central Najd during 18th century, with Otaibah eventually superseding Anazzah
Mutayr aided by Qahtan fought against Anazzah in 18th century, later superseded by Otaibah
Centuries-long struggle in Najd and Hejaz, with conflicts in 1881-1882 when Otaibah plundered Harb camps
Muhammad's foster mother Halimah al-Sa'diyah was from Banu Sa'd, a subdivision of Hawazin

history: The Otaibah is one of the largest Arabian tribes, descended from the pre-Islamic Hawazin confederation and tracing their lineage through the Adnani branch to Ishmael. According to 8th-century genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, the name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance under the Otaibah banner. During the Ottoman period, the tribe engaged in the centuries-long struggle for control of Najd and Hejaz alongside various ruling families. In 1470, they were attacked by Sharif Muhammad ibn Barakat in eastern Ta'if. By the 18th century, the Otaibah had superseded the Mutayr and Qahtan tribes to become the dominant force in central Najd. During the 19th century, they were involved in multiple conflicts including supporting Ibrahim Mohammed Ali against Abdullah bin Saud in 1816, and engaging in warfare with Ibn Rashid throughout the 1870s-1880s. In the early 20th century, under leaders Sultan ibn Bjad and Eqab bin Mohaya, the Otaibah joined the Ikhwan movement and were instrumental in Ibn Saud's conquest of Al-Hasa, Ha'il, Al-Baha, Jizan, Asir, Ta'if, Mecca, and Jeddah. However, the tribe split during the 1928 Ikhwan revolt - Sultan ibn Bjad led the rebellious Barka branch while Eqab bin Mohaya and Omar bin Rubayan's Roug faction remained loyal to King Abdul Aziz. The rebellion was crushed at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929. The tribe established 19 Ikhwan settlements including Al-Ghata'at, Al-Sajir, and Sinam. In 1979, Otaibah member Juhayman al-Otaybi led the seizure of the Grand Mosque of Mecca with 500+ militants, holding it for two weeks before being defeated and executed. Today the tribe is divided into three main branches: Barga, Rog (including Al-Hafah), and Banu Saad, with many members serving in Saudi Arabia's armed forces.

migrationPath.description: After most Hawazin clans departed to other lands, remaining groups consolidated in central Najd under Otaibah leadership

name: Qahtan (Qahtanite lineage)
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical legitimacy as descendants of Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of southern Arabian tribes
ancestorStory: Identified by some with the biblical Joktan
description: Southern Arabs / Pure Arabs / al-Arab al-Ariba. Origin: South Arabia (Yemen, Hadhramaut).

subTribes.name: Himyar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
subTribes.description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

subTribes.name: Kahlan
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
subTribes.ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
subTribes.description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Qahtan (tribe)
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

relations.context: Direct descendant tribe maintaining the Qahtanite name and identity
Claims Qahtanite descent through the Saba lineage
Traditional genealogical division between Qahtanite (southern) and Adnanite (northern) Arab lineages

history: The Qahtanites (بنو قحطان), known as al-Arab al-Ariba (the Pure Arabs), are the southern Arabian tribes originating from modern-day Hadhramaut and Yemen. Arab tradition maintains that they descend from a semi-legendary ancestral figure named Qahtan and his 24 sons, who are considered the progenitors of Yemen and controllers of the Arabian Peninsula. In Judeo-Christian-Islamic traditions, some trace their lineage to Joktan (biblical Yoqtan), son of Eber, while others connect them to Jokshan, son of Abraham through Keturah. The Qahtanite people are traditionally divided into two major sub-groups: Himyar (the settled Arabs of the south) and Kahlan (their nomadic kinsmen). The Kahlan branch includes prominent tribes such as Azd (including Aus, Khazraj, Ghassan, and Khuza'a), Hamdan, Madhhij, Lakhm, Tayy (Shammar), and Kinda. Pre-Islamic migrations saw various Qahtanite groups moving northward, with the Ghassanids (ca. 250 CE) being the last major non-Islamic Semitic migration from Yemen, settling in the Hauran region and spreading to Palestine and Jordan. Following the rise of Islam in the 7th century, Qahtanites became heavily involved in the Arab conquests, migrating to newly conquered territories across the Levant, Mesopotamia, Egypt, and North Africa. During the Umayyad era, a significant blood feud erupted between the Qahtanites and the northern Adnanite tribes of Qays, known as the Qays-Yaman rivalry, which continued in various forms until the 19th century, fundamentally shaping Arabian tribal politics for over a millennium.

migrationPath.description: Ghassanid migration from Yemen to Syria, the last major pre-Islamic Qahtanite migration northward
Qahtanite participation in the Muslim conquest of Syria
Large-scale Qahtanite migration to Iraq following Muslim conquest
Qahtanite tribal migrations to Egypt under the Umayyad Caliphate
Banu Hilal and other Qahtanite tribes sent by Fatimids to North Africa

name: Qahtan (tribe)

description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

subTribes.name: Bani Hajer
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claimed Qahtanite lineage through Abida tribe, with documented leadership succession and migration patterns
subTribes.ancestorStory: Al Hawajer are a Qahtanite tribe from Abida, they resided near Sarat Abida also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub
subTribes.description: Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are an influential Qahtanite tribe in the Arabian Peninsula. Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

history: Qahtān is an ancient Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. The tribe traces its lineage to Qahtani origins, representing one of the two major divisions of Arab peoples alongside the Adnaniyah. According to genealogical tradition, Qahtan was among the earliest Arabs, with his descendants considered the authentic ancient Arabs of southern Arabia, as opposed to the northern Arabized Arabs. The tribe's original homeland was in Yemen, from where they later expanded northward. During the medieval period, the Qahtan tribe achieved significant prominence in the Najd region, particularly settling in Al-Quwai'iyah and expanding their influence throughout southern Najd. However, when Najd came under the rule of Ibn Saud during the second Saudi state, the tribe eventually returned to its original homeland in the south. Several historians describe this period as when Qahtan became one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a time before departing for their ancestral lands. Today, members of the Qahtan confederation and its sub-tribes are distributed across the Arabian Peninsula, including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe has produced numerous notable figures in modern times, including politicians, athletes, academics, and religious leaders. The confederation includes the Harb tribe, which traces its lineage as Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan, demonstrating the complex genealogical connections within the broader Qahtani tribal system.

migrationPath.description: Qahtan tribe settled in Al-Quwai'iyah region of Najd and expanded influence in southern Najd
Returned to original homeland in the south after Najd came under Ibn Saud's rule during the second Saudi state

name: Qataban
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian kingdom and tribal confederation based on control of frankincense trade routes
ancestorStory: Qataban was one of the four major kingdoms of ancient South Arabia, tracing lineage to Qahtanite origins. The tribe established a powerful kingdom controlling crucial incense trade routes between the 8th century BCE and 3rd century CE.
description: Qataban was one of the four major ancient South Arabian kingdoms, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. They dominated the frankincense trade routes from the 8th century BCE to 3rd century CE, with their capital at Timna. The kingdom declined due to changing trade patterns and pressure from Himyarite expansion. While historically significant to Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy, they have limited direct relevance to modern GCC tribal structures, existing primarily as an ancestral reference point for various Qahtanite lineages.

relations.context: Initially integrated into Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE; later conquered Qataban in c. 200 CE
Qataban represents one of the major ancient Qahtanite tribal kingdoms

history:
Qataban was one of the four major ancient South Arabian kingdoms, existing from approximately the 8th century BCE to the late 1st or 2nd centuries CE. The kingdom was centered around Wādī Bayhān with its capital at Timnaʿ, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. Originally part of Neolithic settlements dating to the 20th century BCE, the region later received waves of Semitic-speaking immigrants from the Levant and Mesopotamia, contributing to the emergence of ancient South Arabian culture. Qataban developed into a centralized state by the late 7th or early 6th century BCE, initially ruled by joint kings including Hawfiʿamm Yuhanʿim son of Sumhuʿalay Watar and Yadʿʾab son of Ḏamarʿali. The kingdom experienced complex relationships with neighboring powers, particularly Sabaʾ, alternating between alliance and conflict throughout its history.

Qataban regained independence in the late 5th century BCE and embarked on successful expansionist policies, conquering Maʿīn and challenging Sabaean supremacy by the 3rd century BCE. The kingdom's rulers adopted prestigious titles including mukarrib ('unifier') and malik ('king'), reflecting their regional hegemony. However, Qataban began declining in the 2nd century BCE when the Ḥimyarite tribal confederation seceded around 110 BCE and joined Sabaʾ to form the kingdom of Sabaʾ and Ḏū-Raydān. The kingdom's economy was based on irrigation agriculture, myrrh production, and most importantly, the lucrative frankincense trade routes connecting South Asia with the Mediterranean world. Qataban served as a crucial mediator in this international commerce, bringing significant wealth to its ruling classes. The kingdom finally ended in the late 1st century CE when Ḥaḍramawt and Ḥimyar divided and annexed its territories, marking the conclusion of over seven centuries of Qatabanian political independence.

timelineEvents.description: Qataban develops into a centralized state centered around Timnaʿ

timelineEvents.title: Sabaean Control
timelineEvents.description: Qataban comes under the control of Sabaʾ during the 6th century BCE

timelineEvents.title: Kingdom's End

name: Qawasem
legitimacyNotes: Maritime power and traditional ruling family with religious scholarship heritage
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Raziq, who established the family's presence in the region. They became prominent through their maritime activities and religious scholarship in the Gulf.
description: The Al Qasimi (Qawasem) are a prominent Arabian dynasty that has ruled Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in the UAE since the 18th century. Historically based along the Trucial Coast, they were major maritime powers controlling key trade routes in the Persian Gulf and engaging in both commerce and naval warfare. The British initially viewed them as pirates, leading to several military campaigns, but they later became key allies through the Trucial States system. Today they remain the ruling families of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates.

relations.context: Same tribal confederation, Al Qasimi is modern designation
Historical competition for territorial control in Trucial States
Modern UAE federation partners as ruling families

history: The Al Qasimi (Qawasem) trace their origins to Hashemite lineage, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim tribe are classified as Huwala - Arabian tribes who migrated to Iran in the 13th-14th centuries, maintaining connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia. By the 18th century, they had established control over both sides of the Persian Gulf, ruling from Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah on the Arabian coast while maintaining dominion over Lengeh, Qishm, and Bandar Abbas on the Iranian coast. In 1779, the Iranian Zand dynasty formally recognized a Qasimi as farmandar (local ruler) of Bandar Lengeh. The Qawasem became strong adherents of the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and allied themselves with the Emirate of Diriyah. This alliance, combined with their control of Gulf trade routes, brought them into conflict with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself. The British branded them as 'pirates,' leading to two major military campaigns - the Persian Gulf campaign of 1809 and a more decisive campaign in 1819 under William Keir Grant. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty established peace, transforming the 'Pirate Coast' into the Trucial States. The Qawasem lost their Iranian territories in 1887 when defeated by the British, retreating to focus on their Arabian domains. Today, the Al Qasimi continue to rule Sharjah under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi and Ras Al Khaimah under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, making them one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Qays Aylan
legitimacyNotes: Major Adnanite lineage branch with extensive sub-tribal divisions
ancestorStory: Qays ibn Aylan was a major Adnanite ancestor whose descendants formed numerous influential tribes. His lineage produced many prominent Arabian tribes that spread across the peninsula.

history: Qays Aylan emerged as a major Arab tribal confederation branching from the Mudar group, with its progenitor Qays ibn Aylan traditionally considered either son of Aylan (al-Nas ibn Mudar) or bearing the epithet 'Qays Aylan' himself according to Ibn Khaldun. As descendants of Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, they formed one of the two main subdivisions of Mudar (alongside Khindif) and became so numerous that by the 7th century, the term Qaysi referred to all North Arabians. The confederation comprised three main branches: Khasafa (including Hawazin, Sulaym, and Muharib), Sa'd (including Ghatafan with its Abs and Dhubyan sub-tribes, and A'sur with Ghani and Bahila), and Amr (including Adwan and Fahm). Originally inhabiting the Tihamah lowlands of western Arabia, by Muhammad's time they had spread across central and eastern Arabia, Lower Mesopotamia, and parts of Syria. Initially hostile to Islam, participating in conflicts like the siege of Medina, most Qaysi tribes converted by 630 CE, apostatized during the Ridda Wars, then rejoined Islam after defeat at Buzakha. During the Umayyad period (661-750), they formed a major political faction opposing the Yamani tribes, suffering severe defeats at Marj Rahit (684) but later dominating government under certain caliphs. Their political influence peaked under Marwan II, who relocated the capital to Harran in Qaysi territory, but declined after the Abbasid victory at the Battle of the Zab (750). Many Qaysi tribes subsequently migrated to North Africa and al-Andalus, with only remnants remaining in their central Arabian homeland by the 14th century.

legitimacyNotes: Established sub-tribe of the larger Bani Yas confederation

history: The Qubaisat (Al Qubaisat/Al Qubaisi) are a descendant branch of the ancient Banu Abs tribe, which according to research traces back through the Ghatafan confederation to Adnan. The modern Al Qubaisat tribe in the UAE specifically descends from the Banu Abs through this documented genealogical line: Abs ibn Baghid ibn Rayth ibn Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. In 1869, a critical event reshaped their political status when the Qubaisat, described as 'a subordinate branch of the Bani Yas tribe of Abu Dhabi,' seceded under Sheikh Butay bin Khadim at the end of the pearl-fishing season. This secession occurred amid unsettled debts in Abu Dhabi, and the tribe relocated to Khor Al Adaid, a disputed inlet on the southeast Qatari Peninsula. The secession created immediate diplomatic tensions, as the Qubaisat renounced their allegiance to their hereditary chief in Abu Dhabi. Sheikh Mohammed bin Saeed Bukawarah and Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani of Qatar both claimed debts against the Qubaisat - 70,000 krohns and 4,000 krohns respectively. Jassim obtained an Ottoman flag for the Al Adaid settlers, attempting to transfer their allegiance to the Ottoman Empire and away from Abu Dhabi's sphere. When Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi received British permission to reassert authority over Khor Al Adaid in 1878, the Qubaisat fled to Al Bidda (Doha) where they were welcomed by Qatar's leadership. This displacement became a major catalyst for the Qatari-Abu Dhabi War (1881-1893), with the Qubaisat's territorial dispute serving as one of the primary causes of the prolonged conflict. The tribe's settlement at Khor Al Adaid was seen as a direct challenge to Abu Dhabi's territorial claims and became a focal point of Ottoman-British imperial rivalry in the Gulf region. Today, the Al Qubaisi maintain close relations with the UAE's ruling families, having evolved from their 19th-century displacement into an established presence within the modern UAE's tribal structure.

name: Quraysh
legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

history: The Quraysh are an ancient Arabian tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam, tracing their lineage to Adnan the Ishmaelite through their progenitor Fihr ibn Malik. According to traditional sources, Fihr led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen, though the sanctuary remained under control of the Yemeni Khuza'a tribe. Fihr's descendants lived scattered among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them around 464 CE and established Quraysh dominance over Mecca. Qusayy divided the clans between Quraysh al-Bitah (those settled around the Kaaba) and Quraysh al-Zawahir (those in the outskirts), assigning religious and administrative duties among his sons. By the 7th century, the Quraysh had become wealthy merchants controlling trade routes between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean, running summer caravans to Gaza and Damascus and winter caravans to Yemen. The tribe was divided into ten main clans, most notably the Banu Hashim into which Muhammad was born around 570 CE. Initially showing little concern when Muhammad began preaching Islam in Mecca, their opposition grew as he challenged the prevalent polytheism. This led to the Muslim-Quraysh War (624-630 CE), beginning with Muhammad's migration to Medina in 622 CE and his subsequent raids on Qurayshi caravans. Major battles included Badr (624 CE) where the Quraysh suffered significant losses including the death of Abu Jahl, Uhud (625 CE) where they achieved a tactical victory, and the Battle of the Trench (627 CE) where a Quraysh-led confederation of 10,000 men failed to capture Medina. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in 628 CE established a ten-year truce, but was broken when Qurayshi allies attacked the Banu Khuza'ah who were allied with Muhammad. This led to Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE with 10,000 men, after which the Quraysh converted to Islam. Following Muhammad's death in 632 CE, Islamic leadership traditionally remained with the Quraysh, as seen with the Rashidun, Umayyad, and Abbasid caliphs. The tribe's organizational and commercial skills proved crucial in the early Islamic state's administration and the subsequent Islamic conquests across the Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Qusayy ibn Kilab gathered scattered descendants of Fihr ibn Malik and unified them in Mecca, establishing Quraysh control over the city and the Kaaba

name: Qurra
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Hilal confederation with recognized lineage
ancestorStory: Qurra was a son of Hilal ibn Amir, from whom this branch of the Banu Hilal takes its name. The tribe migrated westward with other Hilali branches during the great Arab migrations.
description: The Qurra were a branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin. They participated in the great Hilali migrations westward across North Africa in the 10th-11th centuries. As part of the larger Hilal tribal movement, they were primarily Bedouin pastoralists who played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb. Their direct presence in the modern Gulf region is limited, as most Qurra branches settled in western territories during the medieval migrations.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Hawazin tribal confederation

history: The name 'Qurra' appears in multiple distinct historical contexts in Arabian Peninsula genealogy, creating complexity in establishing a unified tribal identity. The research reveals three separate entities: the Qurra branch of Banu Hilal, the Banu Qurayza Jewish tribe of pre-Islamic Medina, and Abu Qurra of the Berber Banu Ifran. The Qurra as a branch of Banu Hilal were descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin confederation and participated in the great Hilali migrations westward across North Africa during the 10th-11th centuries CE. As Bedouin pastoralists, they played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb but maintained limited presence in the modern Gulf region, as most branches settled permanently in western territories. The Banu Qurayza, while sharing phonetic similarity in name, were an entirely different entity - a Jewish tribe of Yathrib (Medina) who claimed descent from Aaron and were eliminated in 627 CE following the Battle of the Trench. Abu Qurra represented yet another distinct group, being a member of the Berber Banu Ifran who founded Tlemcen between 767-778 CE. The Qurra associated with Banu Hilal maintained their Adnani Arab lineage and tribal structure throughout their westward migrations, becoming integrated into the broader tribal confederations of North Africa rather than establishing independent settlements in the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Rabīʿa
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār, major Adnanite tribal confederation
ancestorStory: Rabīʿa was one of the sons of Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān, representing one of the major divisions of northern Arabian tribes. The Rabīʿa confederation emerged from his lineage and became one of the most significant tribal groupings in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Rabīʿa was one of the major Adnanite tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia, claiming descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār. The confederation included prominent tribes such as Bakr ibn Wāʾil and ʿAbd al-Qays, and controlled territories across northeastern Arabia and parts of Iraq. They were known for their conflicts with their Mudari cousins and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. While the confederation as a unified entity is historical, many of its constituent tribal branches continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region today.

relations.context: Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd
ʿAbd al-Qays was a major constituent tribe of the Rabīʿa confederation

history: Rabīʿa ibn Nizār represents one of the two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, alongside Mudhar, tracing descent from Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. According to classical Arab genealogists, the confederation encompassed major tribal branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Bakr ibn Wa'il (with sub-tribes Banu Hanifa, Banu Shayban, Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba), Taghlib, and al-Nammir ibn Qasit. Legendary accounts place their original homelands in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which they migrated northwards and eastwards across the Arabian Peninsula and Mesopotamia. Abd al-Qays settled in Eastern Arabia including modern Bahrain and were largely sedentary, while Bakr's territories stretched from al-Yamama (around modern Riyadh) to northwestern Mesopotamia. Taghlib inhabited the eastern banks of the Euphrates, and Anizah divided between sedentary sections in southern Yamama and nomadic groups further north. Many Rabīʿa tribes, particularly Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, al-Nammir, and sections of Bakr, were predominantly Christian before Islam, with Taghlib maintaining Christian identity for some time after the Muslim conquests. During the 7th century, Mu'awiya settled Rabīʿa tribes in Diyar Rabi'a, the easternmost province of Jazira (Upper Mesopotamia), centered around Mosul and encompassing the upper Khabur River region and both shores of the Tigris from Jazirat ibn Umar to Tikrit. The confederation participated in early Islamic conquests and played significant roles in the Rashidun and Umayyad periods. During the Abbasid era, members of Bani Hanifa and related Bakr tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, dominating gold mines near Aswan and intermarrying with local Beja peoples, giving rise to tribes like the Banu Kanz. The region came under various dynastic controls including the native Hamdanid dynasty in the 10th century, followed by Buyids and Uqaylids until the Seljuq conquest. Modern descendants of Rabīʿa tribes continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula, Gulf region, and beyond, with several royal families including Al Saud, Al Khalifa, and Al Sabah tracing ancestry to various branches of the confederation.

ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar was one of the sons of Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. The tribe descended from him became one of the major northern Arabian tribal confederations.
description: Rabi'a was one of the major ancient Arabian tribal confederations, descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. They were prominent in northern and eastern Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The confederation included several important sub-tribes that played significant roles in Arabian history. Many of their branches later settled in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including areas that are now part of the GCC states, though the original confederation structure dissolved over time.

history: The Rab'ia tribe was one of the major ancient Arabian tribal confederations, descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them part of the Adnani lineage of northern Arabian tribes. They were prominent in northern and eastern Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. According to historical sources, Arab settlements in Bahrain may have begun around 300 B.C., with control of the island maintained by the Rab'ia tribe that eventually converted to Islam in 630 A.D. The confederation included several important sub-tribes that played significant roles in Arabian history. Most notably, the Baharna people of Bahrain - regarded by some scholars as the original inhabitants of Eastern Arabia - claim descent from various branches of the Rab'ia confederation, particularly the Abd al-Qays who were mostly Nestorian Christians before the seventh century, and others trace ancestry to related tribes like the Anizah (a cousin tribe of the Abd Al-Qays) and Bakr Bin Wail. The Abd al-Qays were among the major tribes of pre-Islamic Bahrain and were encountered by early Arab Muslim commanders like Salman ibn Rab'ia al-Bahili around 650 CE. The tribal confederation's influence extended throughout Eastern Arabia, including areas that are now part of Bahrain, Qatif, and al-Ahsa, though the original confederation structure dissolved over time as individual branches settled in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.
