name: Rabi'ah
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - bedouin pastoralism and sedentary agriculture, with some branches being mostly sedentary while others remained nomadic
description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

history: Rabi'ah ibn Nizar represents one of the two main patriarchal branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, alongside Mudhar. According to classical Arab genealogists, Rabi'ah's original homeland was in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which the tribe migrated northwards and eastwards across the Arabian Peninsula and into Mesopotamia. The tribe's major branches include Abd al-Qays (who settled in Eastern Arabia including modern Bahrain and were mostly sedentary), Banu Bakr (whose lands stretched from al-Yamama around modern Riyadh to northwestern Mesopotamia), Anizah (divided between sedentary sections in southern Yamama and bedouin sections further north), Taghlib (residing on the eastern banks of the Euphrates), and al-Nammir ibn Qasit. Many of these branches, including Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, al-Nammir, and sections of Bakr, were predominantly Christian before Islam, with Taghlib remaining Christian for some time after the Islamic conquests. During the Abbasid era, significant numbers of Bani Hanifa and related Bakr tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, particularly the Wadi Allaqi region near Aswan, where they dominated gold mining operations and intermarried with local Beja peoples. These migrants collectively identified as 'Rabi'a' in Egypt and became ancestors of the Banu Kanz (Kunooz) tribe, named after their Fatimid-era leader Kanz al-Dawlah of Bani Hanifa. The Rabi'ah legacy continues through numerous royal dynasties including the Al Saud of Saudi Arabia, Al Khalifa of Bahrain, Al Sabah of Kuwait, and the Hamdanid, Uyunid, and Dulafid dynasties of medieval times.

name: Ramzan (Qatar section)
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority within larger Na'im confederation structure
description: The Ramzan are a sub-tribal section of the larger Na'im tribe with presence in Qatar. As part of the Na'im confederation, they share the broader tribal identity and Adnani lineage. The Qatar section represents families who settled in the peninsula, contributing to Qatar's tribal demographic structure. They maintain traditional tribal customs while adapting to modern Qatari society.

relations.context: Both are sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation

history: The Ramzan are a sub-tribal section of the larger Na'im tribe, one of the most significant traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribes in the Gulf region. According to J.G. Lorimer's Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf published in 1904, the Na'im were described as 'a Bedouin tribe who grazed their cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah in 1873.' The tribe were reported as being one of the most powerful tribes in Qatar in an 1890 British government report, with 60 or 70 of the tribe's branch in Qatar having a hereditary attachment to the Bahraini emir. In 1937, a critical dispute over Zubarah took place between Bahrain and Qatar that fundamentally affected the Ramzan section. The Na'im in Qatar split into two sections during this conflict: the Ramzan, who supported the Qatari emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, and the main section Al Jabr, who had pledged allegiance to the ruler of Bahrain. This political division marked the Ramzan's distinct identity within the broader Na'im confederation. As part of the Na'im's traditional territory, the Ramzan shared in the tribe's historical presence around the Buraimi oasis and their role as one of the numerically strongest tribes in the region, with the combined Na'im population estimated at approximately 13,000 at the beginning of the twentieth century.

migrationPath.description: Na'im tribe including Ramzan section grazing cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah

name: Rashid
legitimacyNotes: No tribal information is provided in the source text. The name 'Rashid' appears only as a disambiguation term referring to various places, people, and concepts, but not as a tribal entity.
description: The text provided does not contain information about a tribal entity called 'Rashid'. The source material appears to be a disambiguation page listing various meanings of the name 'Rashid/Rachid/Rasheed' (meaning 'rightly guided' in Arabic), followed by biographical information about Rashida Tlaib, a Palestinian-American politician. No tribal lineage, structure, or traditional Arabian tribal characteristics are mentioned.
history: The Rashid tribe represents multiple distinct tribal entities across the Arabian Peninsula, each with separate origins and histories. The most prominent is the Rashid tribe of the UAE, documented as a small Bedouin tribe numbering approximately 300 members according to Thesiger. This tribe had extensive migration patterns stretching from Buraimi to the Abu Dhabi coast and as far south as Hadhramaut, centered in the Dhafrah area west of Liwa. They belonged to the Hinawi tribal confederation and were frequently involved in raiding activities, maintaining particular enmities with the Ghafiri-aligned Saar and Duru tribes. Thesiger described them as 'among the most authentic of the Bedu, the least affected by the outside world.' The tribe was renowned for breeding hardy camels and, unlike many Trucial States tribes, depended entirely on animal husbandry rather than date cultivation or settlement. A separate Rashid entity was the House of Rashid (Al Rashid), a Shammar dynasty that ruled the Emirate of Ha'il from 1836-1921. Founded by Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid, this dynasty became the most formidable enemies of the House of Saud in Najd. Centered in Ha'il, a city that derived wealth from Hajj pilgrimage routes and commerce, the Rashidi emirs cooperated closely with the Ottoman Empire. The dynasty faced recurring succession disputes and violent internal conflicts, with six Rashidi leaders dying violently in the late 19th century. In 1890, Al Rashid occupied Riyadh and defeated the Saudi tribes, forcing them into exile. However, by 1921, Ha'il was captured by Abdulaziz Al Saud, ending Rashidi rule. Some family members went into voluntary exile to Iraq, Pakistan, Oman, Kuwait, and the UAE.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal migration to summer at Buraimi

name: Remeithi

history: The Al Remeithi (Arabic: الرميثات/الرميثي) are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. According to historical records, the Bani Yas originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. The confederation, including the Remeithi section, settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700 in present-day Abu Dhabi territory under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. As part of the Bani Yas confederation, the Remeithi were among approximately 20 tribes that formed what was called the Bani Yas Coalition, which extended from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. The confederation's population was estimated by J.C. Lorimer at around 12,000 in the early 20th century, with about 10,000 settled members distributed between Liwa Oasis, Abu Dhabi town, Dubai, and other coastal and inland settlements. By 1968, the first Abu Dhabi census recorded 5,884 Bani Yas members within the emirate, though tribal affiliations were not recorded in subsequent censuses. The Remeithi, like other Bani Yas sections, experienced population decline during the late 1950s due to emigration for employment opportunities in Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia, with return migration occurring only after accelerated development under Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan.

name: Riyah
legitimacyNotes: Established Hilalian lineage with documented genealogy
ancestorStory: Descended from Riyah, son of Hilal ibn Amir, as part of the great Hilalian tribal confederation. The tribe traces its origins to the broader Hilalian migration patterns.
description: Riyah is a branch of the larger Hilalian tribal confederation, descended from the Adnani lineage through Hilal ibn Amir. Historically known as part of the great Hilalian migrations that spread across North Africa and parts of Arabia in the medieval period. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin pastoral lifestyle and tribal organization. Their current status in the Gulf region is largely historical, with descendants having integrated into various modern tribal structures.

relations.context: Both tribes share Adnani origins and historical nomadic patterns

history: The Riyah tribe represents one of the most powerful and historically significant sub-tribes of the Banu Hilal confederation, tracing their lineage through the Adnani genealogical line to Riyah, son of Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Originally from the Najd region of central Arabian Peninsula, the Riyah participated in the massive 11th-century Hilalian migration to the Maghreb, dispatched by the Fatimid Caliphate as punishment against the rebellious Zirid dynasty of Ifriqiya. Under the leadership of Munis ibn Yahya of the Mirdas family during this migration, the Riyah were among the estimated 200,000 to 1 million Arab nomads who transformed the demographic and cultural landscape of North Africa. By Ibn Khaldun's era (1332-1406), the Riyah had established themselves primarily in eastern Algeria around Constantine. The tribe demonstrated significant military prowess, notably in 1217 when Riyah warriors reinforced the Almohad army against the Marinids, killing their leader Abd al-Haqq I and earning recognition from Ibn Abi Zar as the strongest tribe in the Maghreb. However, their resistance to Almohad authority led to their devastating defeat at the Battle of Sétif in 1153, where their leader Mahrez ibn Ziyad was killed and the tribe was subsequently forced to relocate to Morocco. The Riyah played a crucial role in the Arabization of the Maghreb, spreading Bedouin Arabic culture and nomadic lifestyle across North Africa, fundamentally altering the region's linguistic and cultural identity. Their descendants became integrated into various North African tribal structures, with their historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula becoming largely ceremonial as the tribe's center of power shifted permanently to the Maghreb.

name: Rubatab
legitimacyNotes: Riverine Nubian tribe with historical presence in northern Sudan
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Rabat, the Rubatab are part of the Ja'alin confederation of tribes. They trace their lineage through Arab ancestry mixed with indigenous Nubian populations.
description: The Rubatab are a Sudanese tribe primarily based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, part of the broader Ja'alin tribal confederation. They are not traditionally associated with the Arabian Peninsula or Gulf region, though some members may have migrated to Gulf states in modern times. Their historical territory centers around the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile. The tribe has limited direct relevance to GCC tribal politics or history.

relations.context: Neighboring upstream riverine tribe with similar culture and lifestyle. Some Manasir clans migrate to Rubatab Country during dry season.

history: The Rubatab are a Sudanese Arab tribe inhabiting the region around the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in northern Sudan, specifically in the Abu Hamad District stretching north of Berber until Abu Hamad. With a population of approximately 60,000 as of 2012, they constitute a sub-group of the larger Ja'alin tribal confederation, one of the three prominent Sudanese Arab tribes in northern Sudan. The Rubatab trace their origins to their ancestor Rabat (also spelled Robat), who according to local legend was the eldest son of a sheikh who once owned all the land between Khartoum and Dongola. When dividing their father's inheritance, Robat chose the rocky lands south of Abu Hamed as an example of peaceful coexistence, which explains why the Rubatab occupy some of the more challenging terrain in the region. Like other Ja'alin tribes, the Rubatab claim Arab ancestry tracing back to Ibrahim Ja'al, an alleged Abbasid noble from the Hejaz whose clan married into the local Nubian population. However, scholars classify them as 'Afro-Arab hybrid,' representing a mix of indigenous Africans and Arabs who came to Sudan between the 9th and 14th centuries. The tribe speaks an archaic form of Arabic and maintains traditional riverine culture along the Nile, with customs documented in 1918 by British administrator John Winter Crowfoot including specific marriage practices, inheritance laws, and religious beliefs. They neighbor the Ababda people, Bishari tribe, and Manasir tribe, and like their neighbors, have no direct historical connection to the Arabian Peninsula or Gulf region, though some members may have migrated to Gulf states in modern times.

name: Rufaida
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and descent from Qahtani origins
ancestorStory: Descended from the ancient South Arabian Qahtani tribes. The tribe traces its lineage through various branches that settled in different parts of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Rufaida is a Qahtani Arab tribe with ancient South Arabian origins that has branches distributed across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Historically, they were involved in the traditional Bedouin lifestyle of pastoralism and participated in the tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia. The tribe has maintained its tribal identity through genealogical preservation and continues to exist in modern times with members found in several Gulf states. Their historical significance lies in their participation in the broader tapestry of Arabian tribal politics and migration patterns.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins

history: Al-Rufaida is a Qahtani Arab tribe that forms one of the three main branches of the Qahtan tribal confederation, alongside Sanhan and Junb. According to Wikipedia sources, the modern Qahtan confederation has members distributed across Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. Historically, the Qahtan tribe settled in the Najd region known as Al-Quwai'iyah and expanded its influence in southern Najd during the era of the second Saudi state under Ibn Saud's rule. Several historians described Qahtan as becoming one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a period before returning to their original homeland in the south. The tribe's name appears to derive from the historical figure Rufaida Al-Aslamia (born approximately 620 AD), an Arab medical practitioner recognized as the first female Muslim nurse in Islam, who belonged to the Bani Aslem tribe of the Khazraj confederation in Medina and was among the Ansar who supported Prophet Muhammad. However, the research does not establish a direct genealogical connection between the historical Rufaida Al-Aslamia and the modern Rufaida branch of Qahtan. The tribal structure suggests ancient South Arabian origins consistent with Qahtani lineage, with the modern confederation maintaining traditional Bedouin pastoral practices while adapting to contemporary Gulf state citizenship and economic systems.

name: Rulers of Ras Al Khaimah
legitimacyNotes: hereditary ruling family established through tribal confederation and British recognition
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi dynasty traces its origins to Qasim bin Rashid who established their rule in the 18th century. The family became prominent maritime rulers controlling both Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah before the territories were divided.
description: The ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, part of the Al Qasimi dynasty that historically controlled significant portions of the Arabian Gulf coast. Based in Ras Al Khaimah emirate in the northern UAE, they were prominent maritime traders and rulers who controlled shipping routes in the Gulf. The family split from their Sharjah cousins in the 19th century, establishing separate emirates. Today they remain the hereditary rulers of Ras Al Khaimah emirate as part of the United Arab Emirates federation.

relations.context: The relationship was often fractious, with villages seceding several times before 1921
both branches of the Al Qasimi dynasty
part of the broader Qawasim tribal confederation

history: The Al Qasimi dynasty that rules Ras Al Khaimah traces its origins to the Qawasim tribe, which are of Huwala origin with Hashemite lineage, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated and maintained connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. The first recorded ruler was Sheikh Rahma Al Qasimi (1708-1731), followed by his son Sheikh Matar bin Butti Al Qasimi (1731-1747). The dynasty established itself as a significant maritime power in the early 18th century, with Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi described by the Dutch as the 'Emir of Julfar' and one of the richest Arab merchants. During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they emerged as a maritime power controlling territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah, Sharjah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their control of trade routes led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself, resulting in the British labeling them as 'pirates' and the southern Gulf coast as the 'Pirate Coast'. The British mounted two major military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. The 1819 campaign was particularly devastating, with Major-General William Keir Grant leading 3,000 soldiers in the bombardment and capture of Ras Al Khaimah and the siege of Dhayah Fort, where the last Al Qasimi resistance surrendered on December 22, 1819. This led to the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, signed by Sheikh Sultan Bin Saqr Al Qasimi, establishing British protectorate over the Trucial States. In 1869, Ras Al Khaimah became fully independent from Sharjah under Sheikh Humaid bin Abdullah Al Qasimi, though it was re-incorporated into Sharjah from 1900 to 1921. Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi ruled from 1948 to 2010, initially declining to join the UAE upon its formation in 1971 due to disputes over the Iranian seizure of Abu Musa and the Tunb islands. However, on February 10, 1972, under his leadership, Ras Al Khaimah became the seventh emirate to join the United Arab Emirates. The current ruler, Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, has governed since 2010, with Mohammed bin Saud Al Qasimi as the appointed heir presumptive.

migrationPath.description: Qawasim tribe established themselves in Ras Al Khaimah, maintaining connections with Persian territories including Lengeh

name: Rumaithat
legitimacyNotes: No tribal entity named 'Rumaithat' is described in the provided text. The text discusses individuals and places with similar names but no tribal structure.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: Based on the provided text, there is no information about a tribe called 'Rumaithat'. The text contains information about three distinct entities: 1) Rumaitha Al Busaidi - a contemporary Omani activist and scientist, 2) Rumaithiya - a residential area in Kuwait, and 3) Rumaythah ibn Abi Numayy - a historical Emir of Mecca from the 14th century. None of these appear to be references to a tribal entity called 'Rumaithat'.
history: The Rumaithat (Al Remeithi) are one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia. As part of the broader Bani Yas, they descend from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, along with the Awamir tribe. The Bani Yas confederation, including the Rumaithat, settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 in present-day Abu Dhabi territory under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. The Rumaithat were among the sections that formed part of this powerful confederation that would come to dominate much of what is now the United Arab Emirates. As members of the Bani Yas, they were part of the migration to Abu Dhabi island in 1793-1795 following the discovery of fresh water, and the subsequent establishment of Abu Dhabi town as the seat of the emirate. The Rumaithat, like other Bani Yas sections, experienced population decline through the mid-20th century due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment opportunities, particularly in oil and construction sectors during the late 1950s, before experiencing return migration following the accession of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and the emirate's oil-driven development.

name: Ruwallah
legitimacyNotes: Major branch of Anizzah confederation with recognized leadership through traditional sheikhs
ancestorStory: Descended from Rwailah, son of Rabiah of the Anizzah tribal confederation. The tribe emerged as a distinct branch that became one of the most powerful Bedouin groups in northern Arabia.
description: The Ruwallah (Rwala) are one of the largest and most prestigious branches of the Anizzah tribal confederation, traditionally ranging across northern Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Syria, and Iraq. Historically known as one of the most powerful Bedouin tribes, they controlled vast territories and trade routes in the Syrian desert and northern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe is famous for their camel breeding, warrior traditions, and complex political alliances. Today, members of the Ruwallah are found across the GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, though many have settled while maintaining tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical alliances and conflicts during Saudi state formation
Traditional competition for territory and resources in northern Arabia

history: The Ruwallah (Arabic: الرولة, Rwala) emerged as a distinct tribal branch sometime in the 16th century or shortly thereafter, belonging to the Dhana Maslam branch of the large Anizah tribal confederation. The tribe was traditionally centered in the region of al-Jawf and Sirhan Valley in northern Arabia, with their tribal territories extending southwards to al-Qasim and northwards to Damascus before the demarcation of Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century. The leadership of the tribe rests with the House of Sha'lan (Al Sha'lan), whose most notable leader was Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan (1847-1942), who ruled the tribe between 1904 and 1942. Nuri secured his rule by killing his brother Sattam and another brother, and became known as Emir of Jawf after occupying the Al Jawf region in 1909 from Al Rashid forces. Initially allied with the Ottomans until his exile to Spain in 1910, he later joined the Arab Revolt against the Ottoman Empire in 1917 under the influence of T.E. Lawrence and Emir Faisal, though only after his son Fawwaz was arrested. His forces were among the Arab units that entered Damascus on October 1, 1918. The Ruwallah were known for adopting modern technology, with Al Shalaan owning multiple vehicles and creating a fleet of three 'armoured cars' with machine guns in 1926. The tribe developed significant political connections through strategic marriages with the Al Saud family - Nuri's granddaughters married multiple Saudi princes including Abdullah bin Abdulaziz. The Ruwallah consist of five major branches: Al-Kwakbah, Al-Doughman, Al-Murre'eth, Al-Frrejah, and Al-Ga'a'gaah. Most tribe members have settled into sedentary or urban life in Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Syria, while maintaining their tribal identity.

Establishment of control over al-Jawf oasis and surrounding territories

name: Saba'
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Yemeni kingdom and dynasty with claimed descent from biblical/Quranic Queen of Sheba
ancestorName: Saba ibn Yashjub ibn Yarub ibn Qahtan
ancestorStory: Saba is considered the progenitor of the Sabaean people and the founder of the ancient kingdom of Saba in Yemen. According to Arab genealogical tradition, he was a descendant of Qahtan through Yashjub ibn Yarub.
description: Saba was an ancient Arabian kingdom and dynasty based in Yemen, famous for the legendary Queen of Sheba mentioned in biblical and Quranic accounts. The Sabaean kingdom controlled important trade routes between Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean, accumulating wealth through incense and spice trade. Their capital was at Marib, known for its great dam and advanced irrigation systems. While historically centered in Yemen, Sabaean influence extended throughout southern Arabia and their genealogical legacy is claimed by various Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Himyar conquered Saba' multiple times, first in c. 25 BCE, with political fortunes changing frequently until final conquest around 280 CE
Saba is considered ancestor of Qahtani tribal confederation

history: Saba' (Sheba) was an ancient South Arabian kingdom that existed in Yemen before 275 CE, likely beginning between 1000-800 BCE. The kingdom's heartland was located around Marib and Sirwah, with Marib serving as its primary capital and featuring the famous Marib Dam mentioned in the Quran. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Saba is considered the progenitor of the Sabaean people and descended from Qahtan through Yashjub ibn Yarub, making it a foundational Qahtani lineage. The first major phase lasted from the 8th-1st centuries BCE, reaching its height under rulers called 'mukarrib' (federator), particularly during the conquests of Karib'il Watar who extended Sabaean territory from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden and eastward to the Hadhramaut foothills. The kingdom controlled crucial trade routes for frankincense and myrrh, accumulating enormous wealth through the spice trade with Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean. After declining due to Roman expansion disrupting trade routes, Saba was annexed by Himyar around the 1st century BCE, but re-emerged from the 1st-3rd centuries CE with a secondary capital at Sanaa before final conquest by the Himyarites around 275 CE. The Sabaeans also established presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE. The biblical and Quranic accounts of the Queen of Sheba's visit to King Solomon, though considered legendary by historians, reflect the kingdom's legendary wealth and international reputation. Modern Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula trace their genealogical heritage to this ancient Sabaean legacy.

migrationPath.description: Sabaean migration across the Red Sea to establish the kingdom of D'mt in modern-day Ethiopia, building temples to Almaqah and spreading Sabaean culture

timelineEvents.title: Kingdom Foundation

name: Sabaeans
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian kingdom with divine kingship and control of incense trade routes
ancestorName: Saba ibn Yashjub ibn Yarub ibn Qahtan
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Saba was descended from Qahtan and became the progenitor of the great South Arabian civilization. The kingdom is also associated with the biblical Queen of Sheba.
description: The Sabaeans were an ancient South Arabian civilization centered in present-day Yemen, famous for their advanced irrigation systems and control of the lucrative incense trade. They established one of the most powerful kingdoms in pre-Islamic Arabia, with their capital at Ma'rib and the famous dam that sustained their agricultural economy. Their trade networks extended throughout the Arabian Peninsula and connected to the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean routes. While the kingdom declined by the 6th century CE, Sabaean tribal descendants dispersed throughout Arabia and are considered ancestors of many Qahtani tribes in the modern GCC states.

relations.context: The Azd bordered Sabaeans in modern-day Yemen
Sabaeans are considered major ancestors of Qahtani tribal confederation
Kathiri claim descent from ancient South Arabian Sabaean origins

history: The Sabaeans were an ancient South Arabian civilization that existed from approximately 1200 BCE until 275 CE, centered around Ma'rib in modern-day Yemen. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Saba, son of Qahtan, making them part of the Qahtani Arab lineage. The kingdom experienced two distinct periods: the first Sabaean kingdom (8th-1st centuries BCE) and a second resurgent kingdom (1st-3rd centuries CE). During their peak under rulers like Karib'il Watar (8th-7th centuries BCE), they controlled vast territories extending from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden, dominating the lucrative frankincense and myrrh trade routes. The Sabaeans used the title 'mukarrib' (federator) during their imperial phase, indicating hegemony over neighboring kingdoms, before reverting to 'malik' (king) during periods of decline. Their advanced irrigation system, centered on the famous Ma'rib Dam, supported a 10,000-hectare oasis and sustained their agricultural economy for over a millennium. The first kingdom fell to Roman economic pressure and Himyarite expansion around the 1st century BCE. After briefly re-emerging as an independent kingdom around 100 CE with Sanaa as a secondary capital, the Sabaeans were permanently conquered by the Himyarites around 275 CE. Following the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam and subsequent migrations known as 'Sil al-Aram,' Sabaean tribal descendants dispersed throughout Arabia, with many settling in various regions and becoming ancestors to numerous Qahtani tribes. The Sabaeans also established a significant presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE and maintaining trade networks and colonies along the East African coast.

migrationPath.description: Following the final Himyarite conquest and the eventual collapse of the Ma'rib Dam, Sabaean populations dispersed throughout Arabia in what became known as the Sil al-Aram migration
Sabaean migration and colonization to establish the kingdom of D'mt in the Ethiopian highlands, building temples to Almaqah and spreading Sabaean culture

name: Sakasic

relations.context: Himyarite tribe that later joined Lakhmids in Northern Egypt

history: The Sakasic (also spelled Sakasek) were a Himyarite tribe that emerged during the period of Himyarite dominance in southern Arabia. As a Himyarite tribe, they were part of the southern Arabian confederation that eventually conquered and incorporated the declining Sabaean kingdom by the 2nd century BC. The Sakasic were contemporaries of other major Himyarite and Qahtani tribal groups including the Lakhmids, Banu Judham, and various Kahlan tribes. Around the 3rd century AD, coinciding with the destruction of the Marib Dam and the subsequent northward migrations of many Yemeni tribes, the Sakasic migrated from their homeland in Yemen to northern Egypt. They established themselves in the ancient town of Bubastis in Egypt, which they renamed Zaqaziq after their tribal name. This settlement became so significant that Zaqaziq not only became the modern name of the town but also gave its name to one of Egypt's provinces, demonstrating the lasting impact of Sakasic settlement in the region.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen to northern Egypt around 3rd century AD, settling in ancient Bubastis

timelineEvents.title: Settlement of Bubastis
timelineEvents.description: Sakasic tribe settles in ancient Egyptian town of Bubastis, renaming it Zaqaziq

name: Salalat
description: The Salalat were a tribal group mentioned in historical Arabian Peninsula records. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or major role in Gulf regional politics. They appear to have been a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories.
history: The Salalat are documented as one of the subsections of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation with territories around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and areas of the UAE east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some nomadic sections of the Bani Kaab such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin, the Salalat had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a population of approximately 7,250 total Bani Kaab members. In 1844, the Salalat, as part of the broader Bani Kaab confederation, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal coalition that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab confederation, including the Salalat, supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and participated in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

migrationPath.description: Northern migration of some Salalat clans seeking new grazing lands and trade opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Alliance Against Wahhabis
timelineEvents.description: Salalat as part of Bani Kaab allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi

timelineEvents.title: Settlement Period
timelineEvents.description: Salalat had settled as part of Bani Kaab confederation by turn of 20th century

timelineEvents.title: Buraimi Dispute Support
timelineEvents.description: Participated in Saudi-supported resistance against British forces during Buraimi Dispute

name: Salihids
legitimacyNotes: Claimed legitimacy through Ismaili religious authority and military conquest
ancestorStory: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Sulayhi established the dynasty in the 11th century as an Ismaili ruler in Yemen. The dynasty claimed descent from noble Arab lineage and religious authority through Fatimid connections.
description: The Salihids (Sulayhids) were an Ismaili Shia dynasty that ruled parts of Yemen from 1047 to 1138 CE. Based in the Yemeni highlands with their capital at Sana'a, they controlled important trade routes and agricultural regions. The dynasty was notable for the prominent role of Queen Arwa al-Sulayhi, who ruled effectively for several decades. Their influence extended into parts of the Arabian Peninsula but they are primarily significant to Yemeni rather than Gulf history, eventually being overthrown by the Ayyubids.
history: The Salihids (Arabic: بنو سليح, Banū Salīḥ) were a dominant Arab foederati tribe that served the Byzantine Empire in the 5th century CE, succeeding the Tanukhids and preceding the Ghassanids. Originally concentrated in Wadi Sirhan and Balqa regions of modern Jordan, they stemmed from the Quda'a tribal confederation according to genealogical sources. The tribe's royal house, known as the Zokomids (Ḍajaʿima), were founded by Zokomos (Ḥamāṭa) who converted to Christianity along with his subjects after a monk prophesied the birth of his son. The most notable Salihid phylarch was Dawud (David), a Christian king who built the monastery Dayr Dawud between Resafa and Ithriya in northern Syria. Initially a military commander, Dawud later embraced religious life, earning the nickname 'al-Lathiq' (the bedraggled) for carrying water and mortar on his back during monastery construction. His piety weakened his military position, and he was killed by Tha'laba ibn Amir of Banu Kalb and Mu'awiya ibn Hujayr of Banu Namir in battle between al-Qurnatayn and Mount Harib. The Salihids' decline accelerated after their participation in Emperor Leo I's failed 468 CE expedition against the Vandals in North Africa, which decimated their forces. They were ultimately defeated by the Ghassanids around 502 CE, becoming subordinate federate tribes. During the Islamic conquest of the Levant in the 630s, Christian Salihids fought alongside other Arab federate tribes against Muslim forces at Dumat al-Jandal and Ziza, refusing conversion offers at Qinnasrin in 638. The tribe largely dispersed throughout Syria and Iraq, joining the Quda'a confederation during the Umayyad period. Modern descendants may include the al-Salīḥāt tribe near Amman, Jordan, where ancient Salihi toponyms like Wādī al-Salīḥī still exist.

migrationPath.description: Original migration from northern Arabian valley to Transjordan as Byzantine foederati
Expansion into Syrian territories under Byzantine service

name: Sanhan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Yemeni tribal authority with historical presence in southern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Sanhan traces its lineage to Sanhan bin Yazid, considered one of the ancient Qahtani tribal ancestors. The tribe has maintained its identity and territorial base in Yemen for centuries.
description: Sanhan is a Qahtani Arab tribe historically based in the highlands of Yemen, particularly in the Sanaa region. They are known for their agricultural settlements and traditional tribal governance structures. The tribe has maintained its presence in Yemen throughout various political changes and continues to be active in tribal affairs. While primarily Yemen-based, some branches have historical connections to broader Arabian Peninsula tribal networks.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history: Sanhan is a Yemeni Arab tribe with deep historical roots in the highlands southeast of Sanaa. Ancient Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE consistently mention Sanhan alongside Khawlan, indicating their longstanding presence in the region. The tribe gained significant political prominence through Ali Abdullah Saleh, who was born in 1947 in Beit al-Ahmar village in Sanhan territory, approximately 20 kilometers southeast of Sanaa. Despite not being from a traditionally powerful sheikhly family, Saleh rose through military ranks after joining the Royal Armed Forces in 1958 and participating in the 1962 Nasserist coup that established the Yemen Arab Republic. After becoming president in 1978 following Ahmad al-Ghashmi's assassination, Saleh created an extensive patronage system centered on his Sanhan tribe. Approximately 70% of command positions in Yemen's security services and army were filled by Sanhan members, making the tribe the backbone of his three-decade rule. The research indicates that Sanhan maintained close relationships with neighboring Khawlan tribes and was part of broader Qahtani genealogical networks. Ancient Sabaean texts reference military campaigns in Wadi Dafa where Sanhan resided alongside Dadan tribes, and this location remains inhabited by Sanhan today. The tribe's political influence extended through Saleh's extended family network, with his brothers, sons, and nephews occupying key government positions. Even after Saleh's death in 2017 during fighting with Houthi forces in Sanaa, Sanhan remains an influential tribal group in Yemen's complex political landscape.

name: Sawalim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and consensus
description: The Sawalim are a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain traditional pastoral economic activities and social structures. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to trace their specific origins or major historical roles in Gulf region politics.
history: The Sawalim are documented as a Bedouin tribal group that played a significant role in the political conflicts of the early Ottoman period in the Levant and Arabian Peninsula. According to historical records, the Sawalim were among the rival Bedouin tribes of the Gaza and Ramla areas who supported the rebel governor Janbirdi al-Ghazali during his revolt against the Ottoman Empire in 1521. They allied with other tribal groups including the Banu Ata and Banu Atiyya in opposition to the Turabay dynasty and other tribes loyal to Ottoman rule. In the Gulf region, the Sawalim appear as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribal confederation, which dominated territories around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and areas of the UAE east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Sawalim had transitioned from nomadic to settled life, forming part of a larger Bani Kaab population of approximately 7,250 people. A small residential area called Falij al-Sawalim exists in the Wilayat of Al Khaburah in Oman's North Al Batinah Governorate, with a population of 14 residents as of 2010, suggesting continued settlement patterns in the region.

timelineEvents.title: Support for Janbirdi Revolt
timelineEvents.description: Sawalim tribal forces allied with Janbirdi al-Ghazali during his rebellion against Ottoman rule, alongside Banu Ata and Banu Atiyya tribes

timelineEvents.title: Transition to Settled Life
timelineEvents.description: Sawalim subsection of Bani Kaab completed transition from nomadic to settled lifestyle in UAE-Oman border region

name: Sba'a
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal confederation recognized by customary law and historical precedent
description: Al-Sba'a (The Seven) was a historical tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, likely consisting of seven allied tribes or tribal sections. The confederation appears in early Arabic poetry and historical accounts as part of the complex tribal alliances of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. Like many ancient confederations, the specific composition and territories of Al-Sba'a are not well-documented in surviving historical sources. The name suggests a formal alliance structure based on the significant number seven in Arabian tribal tradition.
history: The Sba'a tribe is a sub-tribe of the great Anizah confederation, part of the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The Anizah confederation, including the Sba'a, has pre-Islamic origins and was among the tribes that supported Ali ibn Abi Talib at the Battle of Siffin, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. In the mid-18th to early 19th centuries, the Sba'a entered the central Syrian plains as part of the broader Anizah migration northward, often conflicting with the Hasana tribe for territorial control. The Sba'a established themselves in the Syrian desert regions, with their traditional territories extending into areas around Salamiyah, where they formed loose alliances with Ismaili settlers in the mid-19th century to aid against other Bedouin groups. The tribe was led by various sheikhs including Ibn Murshid as Sheikh of Sba'a, with sub-sections like Al-Abedah led by Ibn Hudayb and Al-Rasaleen led by Ibn Aida. A significant political moment occurred in June 1930 when Sheikh Rakan bin Mershed of the Sba'a signed a peace treaty with Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan, the powerful chief of the Ruwallah tribe, ending conflicts between these two Anizah sub-tribes. The Sba'a's connection to broader regional politics was demonstrated through the marriage of Jane Digby, an English aristocrat, to Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab of the Mezrab section of the Sba'a in the 1850s, highlighting the tribe's integration into the complex social fabric of 19th-century Syria.

migrationPath.description: Sba'a entered central Syrian plains as part of Anizah confederation migration

name: Shaiqiyah
legitimacyNotes: Riverain Sudanese Arab tribe with claimed Ja'ali lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Shaiq, claimed to be part of the Ja'ali tribal confederation of northern Sudan. The tribe traces its origins to Arab migrants who settled along the Nile in medieval times.
description: The Shaiqiyah are a Sudanese Arab tribe historically based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region. They were known as skilled horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes and resisted Egyptian expansion in the early 19th century. The tribe has limited direct connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal politics but maintains cultural and genealogical ties to broader Arab tribal networks. Their alliance with the Manasir reflects historical connections between Sudanese and Gulf Arab tribal confederations.

relations.context: Neighboring downstream riverine tribe with similar culture. One theory suggests Manasir descended from Shaiqi clan al-Hankab.

history: The Shaiqiyah (also rendered Shaigiya, Shawayga or Shaykia) are an Arabized Nubian tribe primarily based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region and Dar al-Shayqiya stretching from Korti to the fourth Nile cataract. Despite claims to descent from a Hejazi Arab named Shaig, allegedly a descendant of Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad) who came from the Arabian Peninsula in the 7th century, they have been classified by scholars as Arabised Nubians who originated in the 15th century as a hybrid of various tribes settled in the area. By the early 20th century, tribal nobles themselves denied Arab origins, stating they were indigenous to Sudan. The tribe was historically bilingual in Arabic and Dongolawi (a Nubian language) until the late 19th century, with complete Arabization occurring by the early 20th century. The Shaiqiyah emerged as an independent political entity after the Makurian civil war of 1365, initially as subjects of the Funj Sultanate before breaking free around 1690 by defeating the Abdelab governor. They became renowned as fierce horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes and conducted raids as far as Wadi Halfa in the north and Shendi in the south. Their military prowess was noted by early 19th century travelers like James Bruce and John Lewis Burckhardt, who described them as perfectly independent people fighting on horseback in coats of mail with lances, targets, and sabres. The tribe's independence ended in 1820 when they were defeated by the Turco-Egyptian forces at the battle of Korti under Muhammad Ali's conquest, despite initial resistance led by Mihera Bint Abboud. Following their defeat, they were incorporated into Turkish ranks and used during the suppression of the Ja'alin revolt in 1822, receiving Ja'alin lands between Shendi and Khartoum as reward for their service. During the Mahdist period (1880s-1898), the tribe suffered severe persecution, with many leaders executed, reducing them to a few hundred families by the reconquest. They later recovered and served prominently in the Egyptian Army and Sudan Defence Force, with General Ibrahim Abboud becoming Sudan's president in 1964.

migrationPath.description: Migration from more southern regions to present homeland as noted by James Bruce

timelineEvents.description: Defeated by Turkish/Egyptian forces after refusing to submit, retreated southwards

name: Shammar
legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

relations.context: The Ruwallah became the arch-enemy of the large tribe of Shammar, who inhabited roughly the same area and dominated Nejd in the late 19th century. A 19th century oral poetic epic tells of rivalry between heroes from both tribes.
Long traditional rivalry as both tribes inhabited the same area from Ha'il to Syrian Desert frontiers
Shammar is the largest branch descended from Tayy tribe
Blood relations with Yemenite dynasties that ruled Syria, enabled Tayy expansion northward
Blood relations with Yemenite dynasties that ruled Iraq, enabled Tayy expansion to al-Hirah
Modern descendants of Tayy

history: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula in the second century CE. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE. These mountains subsequently became known as Jabal Shammar from the 14th century, when the tribe first appeared in literature under the name Shammar. The tribe consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah, with the historical seat of leadership in Hāʾil. The Shammar ruled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1836 under the Al Rashid dynasty, reaching peak power around the 1850s when they controlled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to Syria and Upper Mesopotamia. In the 17th century, large sections under Al Jarba leadership migrated to Iraq, eventually becoming one of Iraq's largest tribes with over 1.5 million members. The Saudi conquest of Hāʾil in 1921 ended the Rashidi emirate, with many Shammar subsequently fleeing to Syria and Iraq. Despite modernization, the Shammar maintain strong tribal identity across Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Jordan, with notable political influence including Ghazi al-Yawar becoming Iraq's interim president in 2004.

migrationPath.description: Tayy migration from Yemen to northern Arabian mountains under Usma bin Luai
First major exodus of Shammar Al Jarba to Iraq

name: Shammar al-Jarba
legitimacyNotes: Traditional leadership through the Al Jarba sheikhs, historically recognized authority over northern Arabian territories
ancestorStory: The Al Jarba trace their lineage to Jarba ibn Muhammad, who established the family's prominence within the Shammar confederation. They became the paramount sheikhs of Shammar through political acumen and tribal alliances.
description: The Shammar al-Jarba are a prominent branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation, serving as the traditional paramount sheikhs of Shammar. They are historically based in northern Arabia, particularly the Najd and extending into Iraq and Syria. The Al Jarba family has maintained political leadership over various Shammar sections for centuries, playing significant roles in Arabian politics and tribal alliances. While their main territories lie outside the GCC, they have historical connections and presence in northern Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Both are major branches of the broader Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal section under traditional Al Jarba leadership

history: The Shammar al-Jarba represent the paramount sheikhly branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation, tracing their lineage to Jarba ibn Muhammad who established their prominence within Shammar. The broader Shammar confederation originated as part of the Tayy tribe, which migrated from Yemen to the mountains of Aja and Salma in northern Arabia around 115 CE under Usma bin Luai. These mountains were initially called Jabal Tayy, later renamed Jabal Shammar in the 14th century when the tribe adopted the Shammar name. The Al Jarba clan emerged from the Al Sultah section of the Al Aslam branch of Shammar, becoming the traditional ruling family of the confederation. In the late 18th century, around 1791-1798, a massive exodus of Shammar occurred under Al Jarba leadership following conflicts with the expanding Saudi-Wahhabi state. Sheikh Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba was killed in 1791 in the Battle of Aladwa against Ibn Saud, prompting his successor Mutlag Al-Jarba to lead thousands of Shammar families to safety in southern Iraq. This migration established the Shammar al-Jarba as the dominant tribal force in the Mesopotamian region between the Tigris and Euphrates, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Al Jarba subsequently became one of Iraq's most powerful tribes, owning vast tracts of land and supporting the Hashemite monarchy. After 2003, Ghazi al-Yawar from the Al Jarba clan was chosen as Iraq's interim president. The family also established significant presence in Syria, where they continue to lead Syrian Shammar under Sheikh Humaydi Daham al-Hadi al-Jarba (1936-2022). The Al Jarba have maintained their traditional role as paramount sheikhs of Shammar across borders, with contemporary leadership including the current Sheikh of Sheikhs whose uncle is Ghazi al-Yawar. The family has historically allied with Ottoman authorities against Wahhabi expansion and later played significant roles in modern Middle Eastern politics, maintaining their influence through strategic alliances and tribal leadership across Iraq, Syria, and their ancestral lands in northern Saudi Arabia.

name: Shammar Al-Sayeh
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Shammar confederation with established genealogical lineage
ancestorStory: Al-Sayeh is considered a notable ancestor within the Shammar tribal genealogy. The branch derives its name and identity from this progenitor within the broader Shammar confederation.
description: Shammar Al-Sayeh is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups. Historically based in the northern Arabian Peninsula, they participated in the traditional nomadic lifestyle and tribal movements across the region. As part of the Shammar confederation, they have historical connections to various Gulf regions through migration and tribal alliances. The branch maintains its distinct identity while being part of the broader Shammar tribal structure.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal branches with common ancestry

history: The Shammar Al-Sayeh represents a tribal branch within the larger Shammar confederation, which traces its origins to the Tayy tribe that migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in 115 CE under the leadership of Usma bin Luai. The Tayy conquered the mountains of Ajā and Salma from the Banu Assad and Banu Tamim, with these territories later becoming known as Jabal Shammar in the 14th century. The broader Shammar confederation consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah. Research specifically identifies Shammar Al-Sayeh as 'a tribal confederation of tribes from Shammar' that maintained independence from the Al-Jarba authority that led major Shammar migrations to Iraq. While the main Shammar confederation became centered in Ha'il and later established the Emirate of Jabal Shammar under the Al Rashid dynasty, the Al-Sayeh branch appears to have maintained its distinct identity within the broader tribal structure. The research indicates that during various historical periods, different Shammar groups, including Al-Sayeh, migrated to Iraq and other regions, with some settling in areas like Najaf where they formed communities alongside other Shammar groups such as Al-Zuhairy and Al-Towej. The Al-Sayeh branch, like other Shammar divisions, would have participated in the complex tribal politics and conflicts that characterized Arabian Peninsula history, including the long rivalry with the Anazah confederation and the various Saudi-Rashidi wars of the 18th and 19th centuries.

name: Shammar Toga
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Shammar confederation with traditional pastoral rights
ancestorStory: Toga is considered the eponymous ancestor of this branch of Shammar. The lineage traces back through the broader Shammar tribal genealogy.
description: Shammar Toga is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups. They are traditionally nomadic pastoralists who have historically ranged across northern Arabia and into Iraq. As part of the broader Shammar tribal system, they maintain traditional social structures and continue to be present in modern Gulf states. The tribe follows the typical Shammar patterns of seasonal migration and livestock herding.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Sister branches within Shammar tribal structure

history: Shammar Toga is a Shia branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation that emerged from the broader Shammar tribal system. According to Wikipedia sources, the Shammar are a Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy tribe, which migrated into northern Arabia from Yemen in the second century CE. The Shammar confederation consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah, with Shammar Toga being a geographical rather than genealogical subdivision. In the 17th century, a large section of the Shammar left their traditional homeland of Jabal Shammar under the leadership of the Al Jarba clan and settled in Iraq, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Shammar became divided into two geographical branches in Iraq: the northern branch known as Shammar al-Jarba, which remained primarily Sunni, and the southern branch, Shammar Toga, which converted to Shia Islam around the 19th century after settling in southern Iraq. This religious conversion occurred during the period when many originally Sunni tribes living along the Euphrates and Tigris converted to Shia Islam, often through interaction with Shia missionaries from Najaf and Karbala who operated with relative freedom during Ottoman rule. The conversion was part of a broader pattern in which tribes adopted sedentary agricultural life in the hinterlands of the Shia shrine cities and frequently traded and interacted with their residents.

name: Shihuh
legitimacyNotes: The tribe incorporates elements of Iranian origin with the Kumazarah subsection speaking an Iranian language. The majority Arab element is linked to Yemeni immigration in the second century.
ancestorStory: The Arab element of the tribe's make-up is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen, which brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century.
traditionalEconomy: Agricultural communities in winter months with terraced mountain fields, seasonal migration for date harvests and pearl fishing, goat herding for trade
description: The Shihuh is an Arab tribe living in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman, inhabiting the northern part of the Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula). The tribe has long been influential in the affairs of both the east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman and has fiercely maintained both its identity and independence. They are essentially agricultural during winter months in mountain communities, migrating to coastal areas during warmer months for date season work and pearl fishing.

subTribes.name: Bani Hadiyah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal section of the larger Shihuh confederation
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - seasonal work in pearling industry and agriculture in mountain regions
subTribes.description: The Bani Hadiyah is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, originating in Ras Al Khaimah and also in areas of Northern Oman. They are one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, along with the Bani Shatair. The tribe is divided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. Traditionally, they practiced seasonal migration, working in Ras Al Khaimah during the pearling season and farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter.

subTribes.name: Bani Shatair
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - worked in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams during pearling season, farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter
subTribes.description: Bani Shatair is a tribal name originating in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE and areas of Northern Oman. They are one of two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, traditionally engaged in seasonal migration between coastal pearling work and mountain farming. The village of Sha'am was originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair.

relations.context: Frequent neighbors sharing cultural similarities and traditions, with common intermarriage especially with Al Haramsha of Bani Idaid Shihuh
Allied with Shihuh against Sharjah during independence struggles

history: The Shihuh is an Arab tribe inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) in the UAE and Oman. The tribe's Arab lineage traces to the wave of immigration from Yemen in the second century CE, specifically groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants who moved north and established the first Arabian settlements in Oman. This migration led to conflict with the Persians who claimed Omani territory, culminating in the great Salut Battle where the Persians were defeated, paving the way for Oman's Arabization. The Shihuh are divided into two main sections: Bani Hadiyah (found mainly on the western coast including Khasab) and Bani Shatair (centered around Kumzar). At the turn of the 20th century, they numbered approximately 21,500 people. The tribe incorporated Iranian elements, particularly the Kumazarah subsection who spoke Kumzari, a Southwestern Iranian language. Historically difficult to govern due to their mountainous terrain, the Shihuh maintained fierce independence and were frequently in conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, generally preferring Muscat's suzerainty. They played pivotal roles in regional politics, including the 1921-1922 battle for Rams and conflicts in Dibba and Kalba. The tribe practiced seasonal migration between mountain agriculture in winter and coastal work during date and pearl seasons. In 1970-71, following the British-backed Omani coup, British and Trucial Oman Scout forces invaded Musandam, ending local tribal rule and establishing permanent Omani military control.

name: Shuraifat
legitimacyNotes: Claims descent from Sharif lineage, traditional leadership in tribal confederation structures
description: The Shuraifat are a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula with documented alliances to the Al Bu Falah confederation. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are known for their pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has maintained its identity through centuries of regional political changes and continues to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern Gulf context.

relations.context: Allied during seasonal migrations and nomadic movements

history: The Shuraifat are a tribal group within the Al-Muntafiq confederation, specifically part of the Al Ajwad division. The Al-Muntafiq was a large Arab tribal confederation predominantly settled in southern Iraq's provinces and northern Kuwait, with most tribes tracing their genealogy to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The Shuraifat, alongside other Muntafiq tribes including the Juwarin and Al Aunan, participated in the seasonal nomadic migrations with the Albu Salih clans during the early-to-mid 20th century. These migrations involved moving south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn, traveling through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trade with local villages and towns, before returning north in spring to their tribal territories for summer. The confederation as a whole converted from Sunni to Shia Islam during the Ottoman era from the late eighteenth century onwards, and most members had transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life by the 1940s-1950s, settling in countries within or near the indigenous lands of the Muntafiq confederation.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal nomadic migration south through Wadi Batin valley to Arabian Gulf coast for trade, returning north for summer

timelineEvents.title: End of Nomadic Lifestyle
timelineEvents.description: Majority of nomadic Muntafiq tribes including Shuraifat settled permanently in countries within or near their indigenous lands

name: Shwaihiyin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal structure and lineage recognition
description: The Shwaihiyin are a smaller tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins and historical role in Gulf society. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are recognized within the broader Arabian tribal system. Their current status and activities require further research for comprehensive documentation.
history: The Shwaihiyin are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, with documented presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. According to early 20th century records, they were one of the nomadic sections of the Bani Kaab, alongside the Drisah, while other tribal sections had settled by that time. The tribe's traditional territories were associated with areas around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The Shwaihiyin became prominently involved in Gulf politics during the mid-19th century when a blood feud erupted between them and the Huwalah tribe in Sharjah in 1855. At this time, they were described as recent immigrants to Sharjah, numbering approximately 500 fighting men. Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi relocated the Shwaihiyin from Sharjah to Hamriyah to resolve the conflict, but this move triggered the first rebellion by Hamriyah against Al Qasimi rule. The resulting siege of Hamriyah in May 1855 saw fierce fighting, with the Shwaihiyin and local defenders holding out against a combined force from Sharjah and Ajman. British mediation eventually resolved the conflict, resulting in the Shwaihiyin being removed from Hamriyah. Like other sections of the Bani Kaab, the Shwaihiyin were involved in the complex tribal politics of southeastern Arabia, including alliances with various ruling families and involvement in territorial disputes that characterized the region during the 19th and 20th centuries.

migrationPath.description: Relocated by Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi due to blood feud with Huwalah tribe

name: Sinjarah
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Shammar confederation with traditional pastoral and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Descended from Sinjar, a prominent figure within the Shammar tribal lineage. The name is associated with the historic Sinjar region and their ancestral connections to Shammar.
description: The Sinjarah are a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the most prominent tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula. They traditionally practiced nomadic pastoralism across the northern Arabian Peninsula and have historical presence in areas that extend into modern Gulf states. As part of the Shammar, they share the confederation's Adnani lineage and have maintained their tribal identity while adapting to modern state structures. They remain active within the broader Shammar tribal framework in Saudi Arabia and neighboring regions.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal branches with common ancestry

history: The Sinjarah are one of the three main branches of the Shammar tribal confederation, alongside Abdah and Al-Aslam, descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in 115 CE under the leadership of Usma bin Luai. The Tayy invaded and conquered the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim, establishing themselves in what became known as Jabal Shammar by the 14th century. The Sinjarah branch includes clans such as Al Thabit, Al Ghafilah, Al Zameel (sometimes referred to as Al Souid or Al Faddaghah), and Al Zomail. According to 1932 population estimates, the Sinjarah comprised approximately 25% of the Badu Shammar population, which totaled 150,000-200,000 people. Like other Shammar branches, the Sinjarah were primarily nomadic camel-herders and horse-breeders in northern Najd, though some became city-dwellers in Ha'il or agriculturists in surrounding oases. The Sinjarah participated in the broader Shammar confederation's complex political history, including conflicts with the Saudi states, support for the Al Rashid emirate of Jabal Shammar (1836-1921), and eventual incorporation into modern Saudi Arabia following the conquest of Ha'il in 1921. Despite urbanization and modern state integration, the Sinjarah maintain their tribal identity within the larger Shammar framework.

name: Southern Bani Qitab
legitimacyNotes: Southern branch of Bani Qitab with traditional territorial claims in southern regions
ancestorStory: Southern branch of the Bani Qitab lineage that settled in the southern territories of the Arabian Peninsula. They maintained distinct identity while sharing common ancestry with northern Bani Qitab branches.
description: The Southern Bani Qitab represent a tribal branch of the larger Bani Qitab confederation that historically occupied southern territories of the Arabian Peninsula. As a Qahtani lineage, they were part of the ancient South Arabian tribal system that predated Islam. Their traditional economy was based on nomadic pastoralism, moving with their herds across the southern desert regions. The tribe's historical significance lies in their role as part of the broader Bani Qitab tribal network, though their specific influence in GCC politics appears to have diminished over time.

relations.context: Part of broader Qahtani tribal confederation

history: The Southern Bani Qitab represents a settled branch of the larger Bani Qitab tribal confederation that became geographically and politically distinct from their northern kinsmen. According to detailed tribal records from the turn of the 19th century, the Southern Bani Qitab comprised approximately 500 households settled around the village of Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area. Over time, this southern section separated from the northern nomadic Bani Qitab and eventually became incorporated into what was recognized as a governorate of Oman in the 20th century. Unlike their northern relatives who maintained nomadic Bedouin traditions and wielded significant political influence in the Trucial States, the Southern Bani Qitab adopted a more settled lifestyle in their southern territories. The broader Bani Qitab confederation was highly influential in early 20th century politics, with the northern branch controlling interior territories and maintaining armed forces of 200-250 mounted Bedouin as late as the 1940s. However, the southern branch's political significance appears to have been more limited, as they were not prominently mentioned in the major tribal conflicts and political negotiations that involved the northern Bani Qitab, such as the 1905-1907 disputes mediated by Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. The Southern Bani Qitab's integration into Oman's administrative structure marked their formal separation from the broader tribal network that operated across the Trucial States.

Expansion into oasis settlements for agricultural and trading opportunities
Further eastward movement into Omani territories during Abbasid period

name: Subay'
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Closely related family that roamed central Nejd together with Subay'
Encroached on Bani Khalid territories and fought final battle against them in 1830
Border dispute and battle in Ranyah Valley, ended in Ghamid victory

history: Subay' (Arabic: سبيع) is an Arabian tribe of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock tracing its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who dominated central Arabia in the medieval period. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe comprised both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections. The original grazing lands of the family's bedouins were in the region of Ranyah and Kurmah, on the border between Nejd and 'Asir, before they migrated to roam the areas of central Nejd around Riyadh alongside the closely related Suhool tribe. Some sections moved further north and established the town of Rumah. The sedentary members have resided in central Arabia for centuries, representing a possible legacy of Banu 'Amir's medieval heyday, with families belonging to Subay' found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. Historically, Al-Subaies were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and continued this martial tradition, serving as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. In the Al-Qassim Province's tribal conflicts from the 1600s to 1907, Unaizah was ruled by the Al Sulaim dynasty from the Subay tribe, demonstrating their political influence in the region. Today, the tribe is mostly found in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, United Arab Emirates, and Kuwait, maintaining both their bedouin heritage and urban presence across the Arabian Peninsula.

Some sections moved further north and established the town of Rumah

name: Al Sudairi
legitimacyNotes: Noble lineage within Najdi tribal structure, political prominence through intermarriage with Al Saud
ancestorStory: The Al Sudairi trace their lineage to Sudairi, an ancestor from the Najd region. They became particularly prominent through strategic marriages and alliances with the Al Saud family.
description: Al Sudairi is a prominent Najdi clan historically based in central Saudi Arabia. They are most famous for their strategic intermarriages with the Al Saud ruling family, particularly through Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi, who became the mother of several Saudi kings including Fahd, Sultan, and Salman. The clan has maintained significant political influence in modern Saudi Arabia through these royal connections. They remain an active and influential family within the Kingdom's political and social structure.

relations.context: Multiple strategic marriages including Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi to King Abdulaziz

history: The Al Sudairi are a prominent Najdi clan that forms part of the noble Dawasir tribe, historically settled in Al Ghat, an oasis town located in central Arabia approximately 250 kilometers northwest of Riyadh. The family's documented lineage traces through Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Turki bin Suleiman Al Sudairi, known as Ahmed Al Kabeer (The Great), who served as an administrator under Faisal bin Turki, ruler of the Second Saudi State, in various regions including Al Ahsa, Al Ghat, and Buraimi before his death in 1860. The Al Sudairi's political prominence emerged through strategic alliances with the Al Saud family, beginning with Sara bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (c.1860-1910), daughter of Ahmed Al Kabeer, who married Abdul Rahman bin Faisal and became the mother of King Abdulaziz, the founder of Saudi Arabia. This established a crucial matrimonial connection that would define the clan's future influence. The family's power reached its zenith through Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (1900-1969), great-granddaughter of Ahmed Al Kabeer, who became one of King Abdulaziz's most important wives. Born in Al Ghat, she was the daughter of Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi, a powerful tribal chief and early supporter of King Abdulaziz who later served as governor of Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj provinces. Hussa's marriage to King Abdulaziz produced twelve children, including seven sons known as the 'Sudairi Seven' - among them future kings Fahd and Salman, as well as Sultan and Nayef who both served as crown princes. Her influence extended beyond motherhood; she was described as combining the qualities of Margaret Thatcher and Mother Teresa, organizing daily dinner gatherings to maintain unity among her sons and actively participating in political decisions. The Al Sudairi family's integration into the royal structure was further strengthened through multiple marriages - Hussa's sisters Muhdi and Sultana married into the Al Saud family, while her brothers were appointed as governors across various Saudi provinces. Today, through King Salman's reign and the position of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the Al Sudairi continue to wield significant political influence in Saudi Arabia, with their lineage connections extending to contemporary figures like Princess Rajwa Al Hussein, whose mother Azza Al Sudairi links her to this influential Najdi clan.

timelineEvents.title: Death of Ahmed Al Kabeer
